topics.title: Carnot Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT502 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT ENGINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Carnot Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

The **Carnot Cycle** is a theoretical, ideal, fully **reversible thermodynamic cycle** that establishes the maximum possible thermal efficiency for any heat engine operating between two given thermal reservoirs. It was formulated by **Sadi Carnot (1824)** and consists of **four internally reversible processes**: two **isothermal** (constant temperature) processes and two **isentropic / adiabatic** (constant entropy) processes.

The cycle operates between:
- A **High-Temperature Reservoir (HTR)** at absolute temperature $T_H$ (in **Kelvin**, K)
- A **Low-Temperature Reservoir (LTR)** at absolute temperature $T_L$ (in **Kelvin**, K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The Carnot Cycle is the **benchmark** against which ALL real heat engines, refrigerators, and heat pumps are compared. The **Second Law of Thermodynamics (Kelvin-Planck & Clausius Statements)** is mathematically derived from the impossibility of a cycle exceeding Carnot efficiency.

### Four Constituent Processes

| State Transition | Process Type | Operation | Energy Interaction |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| $1 \rightarrow 2$ | Isothermal Expansion | Working fluid expands at $T_H$ | Heat $Q_H$ absorbed from HTR |
| $2 \rightarrow 3$ | Isentropic Expansion | Adiabatic expansion $T_H \rightarrow T_L$ | No heat transfer; Work done |
| $3 \rightarrow 4$ | Isothermal Compression | Working fluid compressed at $T_L$ | Heat $Q_L$ rejected to LTR |
| $4 \rightarrow 1$ | Isentropic Compression | Adiabatic compression $T_L \rightarrow T_H$ | No heat transfer; Work input |

### Conceptual Analogy — The Water Wheel of Heat

Imagine a perfectly designed **water wheel** placed between a high waterfall (hot reservoir) and a low pond (cold reservoir). Water (heat) flows from high to low potential. The wheel converts the *potential difference* into rotational energy (work).

- If you let the water fall **without a wheel**, all energy is wasted as turbulence (irreversibility).
- A **Carnot engine** is the *most perfect wheel ever built* — it extracts the **maximum possible mechanical work** from the temperature difference, with **zero losses**.
- No real engine can beat this limit — this is the Second Law of Thermodynamics in action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Used in Carnot Analysis:**
> - Universal Gas Constant: $R_u = 8.314 \text{ kJ/kmol·K}$
> - Absolute Zero: **0 K** = −273.15 °C
> - Temperatures **MUST** be in **Kelvin (K)** for all Carnot efficiency formulas.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why is the Carnot Cycle the Most Efficient?

The Carnot cycle achieves maximum efficiency because every process is **internally and externally reversible**:

1. **Isothermal processes** occur at *infinitely small temperature differences* between the working fluid and the reservoirs (no finite $\Delta T$ driving force = no irreversibility).
2. **Isentropic processes** involve *no friction, no turbulence, and no heat transfer* through a finite temperature difference.
3. The cycle is **closed** — the working fluid returns to its initial state, satisfying the First Law over one complete cycle: $\oint dU = 0$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kelvin-Planck Statement (Second Law):** It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no other effect than the extraction of heat from a single reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work. Hence, **no engine** can be more efficient than a Carnot engine between the same two reservoirs.

### 2.2 Logical Step-by-Step Operation

**Step 1 — Isothermal Expansion (1→2):**
The working fluid (typically an ideal gas) is placed in thermal contact with HTR at $T_H$. It expands slowly, doing work $W_{1-2}$ on the surroundings. Heat $Q_H$ is absorbed from HTR. Since temperature is constant, internal energy change is zero, so $Q_H = W_{1-2}$.

**Step 2 — Isentropic Expansion (2→3):**
The fluid is thermally insulated. It continues to expand, doing work $W_{2-3}$, while its temperature drops from $T_H$ to $T_L$. No heat transfer ($Q = 0$). Entropy remains constant.

**Step 3 — Isothermal Compression (3→4):**
The fluid is placed in contact with LTR at $T_L$. It is compressed isothermally, releasing heat $Q_L$ to the LTR. Work is done **on** the fluid: $W_{3-4} = Q_L$.

**Step 4 — Isentropic Compression (4→1):**
The fluid is again insulated and compressed back to its original state at $T_H$ and the original pressure. No heat transfer. Entropy constant. Work is done **on** the fluid.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory for KTU Board Exam 2024 Scheme:** Memorize the relationships below. These appear in **every module test** and **frequently in Part A and Part B questions**.

| S.No. | Quantity | Formula | Units | Application Context |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Thermal Efficiency (Heat Engine) | $\eta_{th,Carnot} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = 1 - \dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$ | dimensionless | Engine performance |
| 2 | Carnot Efficiency (Temperature Form) | $\eta_{th,Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | dimensionless | **Most used formula** |
| 3 | Net Work Output | $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$ | kJ | First Law over cycle |
| 4 | Heat Absorbed (Reversible) | $Q_H = T_H (S_2 - S_1)$ | kJ | From T-s diagram area |
| 5 | Heat Rejected (Reversible) | $Q_L = T_L (S_3 - S_4)$ | kJ | From T-s diagram area |
| 6 | COP of Refrigerator | $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | dimensionless | Cooling applications |
| 7 | COP of Heat Pump | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | dimensionless | Heating applications |
| 8 | Relationship | $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ | dimensionless | Quick verification |
| 9 | PMM2 Violation Check | $\eta_{real} \leq \eta_{Carnot}$ | dimensionless | Second Law check |
| 10 | Carnot Factor (decrement) | $\eta_{actual} = \eta_{Carnot} \times \eta_{relative}$ | dimensionless | Real engine analysis |

**Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
- $\eta_{th} \rightarrow 0$ when $T_L \rightarrow T_H$ (no temperature difference, no work)
- $\eta_{th} \rightarrow 1$ only when $T_L \rightarrow 0 \text{ K}$ (absolute zero, unattainable — **Third Law**)
- $COP_R \rightarrow \infty$ when $T_H \rightarrow T_L$ (degenerate case)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Use of Carnot Concept |
|:---|:---|
| **Power Plants** (Steam, Gas, Nuclear) | Upper bound for thermal efficiency — drives superheat/reheat strategies |
| **Refrigeration & HVAC** | Sets maximum theoretical COP for chillers, AC systems |
| **Internal Combustion Engines** | Otto & Diesel cycles compared against Carnot for the same T-limits |
| **Cryogenics** | Liquefaction of gases (LNG, air separation) uses reverse Carnot |
| **Renewable Energy** | Solar thermal plants designed to approach Carnot limits |
| **Heat Pump Heating** | Geothermal heat pumps designed using Carnot COP benchmarks |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Carnot Thermal Efficiency

We begin with the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to a closed system undergoing a complete cycle, assuming the working fluid is an **ideal gas**.

**First Law Statement (Cycle):**

$$
\oint dU = 0
$$

Therefore, the net work done equals the net heat transfer:

$$
W_{net} = Q_{net} = Q_H - Q_L
$$

**Process 1→2 (Isothermal Expansion at $T_H$):**
For an ideal gas, $\Delta U = 0$ (since temperature is constant). By First Law:

$$
Q_H = W_{1-2} = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \frac{m R T_H}{V} \, dV
$$

Integrating:

$$
Q_H = m R T_H \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)
$$

**Process 2→3 (Isentropic Expansion):**
No heat transfer occurs ($Q = 0$). The temperature drops from $T_H$ to $T_L$:

$$
Q_{2-3} = 0, \quad W_{2-3} = -\Delta U = m c_v (T_H - T_L)
$$

**Process 3→4 (Isothermal Compression at $T_L$):**
For an ideal gas at constant $T_L$, $\Delta U = 0$:

$$
Q_L = W_{3-4} = \int_{V_3}^{V_4} P \, dV = m R T_L \ln\!\left(\frac{V_4}{V_3}\right)
$$

Note that $V_4 < V_3$, so $Q_L$ is negative (heat rejected).

**Process 4→1 (Isentropic Compression):**
Again, $Q = 0$:

$$
Q_{4-1} = 0, \quad W_{4-1} = m c_v (T_L - T_H) = -m c_v (T_H - T_L)
$$

**Net Work over the Cycle:**

$$
W_{net} = W_{1-2} + W_{2-3} + W_{3-4} + W_{4-1}
$$

After cancellation of the isentropic work terms:

$$
W_{net} = m R T_H \ln\!\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right) + m R T_L \ln\!\left(\frac{V_4}{V_3}\right)
$$

**Applying Isentropic Relations (2→3 and 4→1):**

For isentropic process 2→3:

$$
\frac{T_H}{T_L} = \left(\frac{V_3}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma - 1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{V_3}{V_2} = \left(\frac{T_H}{T_L}\right)^{\frac{1}{\gamma-1}}
$$

For isentropic process 4→1:

$$
\frac{T_L}{T_H} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_4}\right)^{\gamma - 1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{V_1}{V_4} = \left(\frac{T_H}{T_L}\right)^{\frac{1}{\gamma-1}}
$$

This implies:

$$
\frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{V_3}{V_4}
$$

**Substituting into Thermal Efficiency Equation:**

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H} = \frac{m R T_H \ln(V_2/V_1) - m R T_L \ln(V_3/V_4)}{m R T_H \ln(V_2/V_1)}
$$

Since $V_2/V_1 = V_3/V_4$, the logarithms are equal. Let $\ln(V_2/V_1) = \ln(V_3/V_4) = K$:

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{T_H \cdot K - T_L \cdot K}{T_H \cdot K} = \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_H}
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{th,Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of COP for Reversible Refrigerator

A refrigerator extracts heat $Q_L$ from a cold space (at $T_L$) using work input $W_{net}$ and rejects $Q_H$ to a hot space (at $T_H$).

**Coefficient of Performance (COP) Definition:**

$$
COP_R = \frac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}}
$$

**Substituting $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$:**

$$
COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}
$$

**For a Reversible (Carnot) Cycle, Using $Q_H/T_H = Q_L/T_L$:**

This relation comes from the **Clausius Equality** for any reversible cycle: $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$. For the Carnot cycle, $Q_H/T_H = Q_L/T_L$.

Therefore:

$$
Q_L = Q_H \cdot \frac{T_L}{T_H}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
COP_R = \frac{Q_H \cdot (T_L/T_H)}{Q_H - Q_H \cdot (T_L/T_H)} = \frac{T_L/T_H}{1 - T_L/T_H}
$$

$$
\boxed{COP_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of COP for Reversible Heat Pump

A heat pump delivers heat $Q_H$ to a hot space (at $T_H$) using work input $W_{net}$, extracting heat $Q_L$ from a cold space (at $T_L$).

**COP Definition:**

$$
COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H - Q_L}
$$

Using $Q_L/Q_H = T_L/T_H$:

$$
COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{Q_H(1 - T_L/T_H)} = \frac{1}{1 - T_L/T_H}
$$

$$
\boxed{COP_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}}
$$

---

### 3.4 KTU Analytical Matrix — Cross-Question Patterns

| Given Data | Required Output | First Formula to Use | Final Answer Form |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| $T_H$, $T_L$ | $\eta_{th}$ | $\eta = 1 - T_L/T_H$ | Decimal or percentage |
| $\eta_{th}$, $W_{net}$ | $Q_H$ | $Q_H = W_{net}/\eta$ | Energy (kJ) |
| $Q_H$, $T_H$, $T_L$ | $Q_L$ | $Q_L = Q_H \cdot T_L/T_H$ | Energy (kJ) |
| $Q_L$, $W_{net}$ | $COP_R$ | $COP_R = Q_L/W_{net}$ | Dimensionless |
| $Q_H$, $W_{net}$ | $COP_{HP}$ | $COP_{HP} = Q_H/W_{net}$ | Dimensionless |
| $T_H$, $T_L$ | $COP_R$, $COP_{HP}$ | $COP_R = T_L/(T_H - T_L)$ | Dimensionless |
| $COP_{HP}$ | $COP_R$ | $COP_R = COP_{HP} - 1$ | Dimensionless |

---

### 3.5 Illustrative Numerical Example (Worked Out)

> **Problem:** A Carnot heat engine receives **1000 kJ** of heat from a source at **800 K** and rejects heat to a sink at **300 K**. Determine:
> (a) Thermal efficiency
> (b) Net work output
> (c) Heat rejected
> (d) COP if reversed as a refrigerator between the same limits

**Given:**
- $Q_H = 1000 \text{ kJ}$
- $T_H = 800 \text{ K}$
- $T_L = 300 \text{ K}$

**Part (a) — Thermal Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{800} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625
$$

$$
\eta_{th} = 62.5\%
$$

**Part (b) — Net Work Output:**

$$
W_{net} = \eta_{th} \times Q_H = 0.625 \times 1000 = 625 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Part (c) — Heat Rejected:**

$$
Q_L = Q_H - W_{net} = 1000 - 625 = 375 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Verification (via $Q_L = Q_H \cdot T_L/T_H$):**

$$
Q_L = 1000 \times \frac{300}{800} = 1000 \times 0.375 = 375 \text{ kJ} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Part (d) — COP as Refrigerator:**

$$
COP_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{300}{800 - 300} = \frac{300}{500} = 0.6
$$

**COP as Heat Pump:**

$$
COP_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{800}{500} = 1.6
$$

**Cross-check using $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$:**

$$
COP_{HP} = 0.6 + 1 = 1.6 \quad \checkmark
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Architecture of the Carnot Heat Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RESH["HIGH TEMP RESERVOIR T_H KELVIN"]
    RESL["LOW TEMP RESERVOIR T_L KELVIN"]
    ENG["CARNOT CYCLE WORKING FLUID IDEAL GAS"]
    
    QH["Q_H HEAT INPUT kJ"]
    QL["Q_L HEAT REJECTION kJ"]
    WN["W_NET WORK OUTPUT kJ"]
    
    RESH --> QH
    QH --> ENG
    ENG --> QL
    QL --> RESL
    ENG --> WN
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Heat $Q_H$ flows from the high-temperature reservoir into the Carnot engine. The engine produces net work $W_{net}$ (extracted as useful output) and rejects the remaining heat $Q_L$ to the low-temperature reservoir. The first law demands $Q_H = W_{net} + Q_L$.

---

### 4.2 State Transition Flow — The Four Processes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ST1["STATE 1 T_H HIGH PRESSURE V1"]
    ST2["STATE 2 T_H LOW PRESSURE V2"]
    ST3["STATE 3 T_L LOW PRESSURE V3"]
    ST4["STATE 4 T_L HIGH PRESSURE V4"]
    
    P12["PROCESS 1 TO 2 ISOTHERMAL EXPANSION Q_H ABSORBED"]
    P23["PROCESS 2 TO 3 ISENTROPIC EXPANSION T DROPS"]
    P34["PROCESS 3 TO 4 ISOTHERMAL COMPRESSION Q_L REJECTED"]
    P41["PROCESS 4 TO 1 ISENTROPIC COMPRESSION T RISES"]
    
    ST1 --> P12 --> ST2 --> P23 --> ST3 --> P34 --> ST4 --> P41 --> ST1
```

> **Cycle Closure:** The fluid returns from State 4 back to State 1 via isentropic compression, completing the closed loop. The net area enclosed by the cycle on a P-V diagram represents $W_{net}$.

---

### 4.3 Carnot Cycle on T-S Diagram — The Rectangular Representation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TSDIAG["T-S DIAGRAM RECTANGULAR CYCLE"]
        direction TB
        AXISX["ENTROPY S"]
        AXISY["TEMPERATURE T"]
        
        CR1["POINT 1 T_H S_1"]
        CR2["POINT 2 T_H S_2"]
        CR3["POINT 3 T_L S_2"]
        CR4["POINT 4 T_L S_1"]
        
        CR1 --- CR2
        CR2 --- CR3
        CR3 --- CR4
        CR4 --- CR1
    end
    
    AR_QH["AREA UNDER 1-2 REPRESENTS Q_H T_H TIMES DELTA S"]
    AR_QL["AREA UNDER 3-4 REPRESENTS Q_L T_L TIMES DELTA S"]
    AR_W["ENCLOSED RECTANGULAR AREA REPRESENTS W_NET Q_H MINUS Q_L"]
    
    CR1 --> AR_QH
    CR4 --> AR_QL
    CR1 --> AR_W
```

> **Why T-S Diagram is Powerful:** On a T-s diagram, the Carnot cycle appears as a **perfect rectangle** with the top edge at $T_H$ and the bottom edge at $T_L$. The width is $\Delta S = S_2 - S_1$. This makes:
> - $Q_H = T_H \cdot \Delta S$ (top rectangle area)
> - $Q_L = T_L \cdot \Delta S$ (bottom rectangle area)
> - $W_{net} = (T_H - T_L) \cdot \Delta S$ (enclosed rectangle area)
> - $\eta = (T_H - T_L)/T_H = 1 - T_L/T_H$

---

### 4.4 Carnot Cycle on P-V Diagram — Qualitative Shape

The Carnot cycle on a P-V diagram consists of two **isothermal curves** (hyperbolas, $PV = $ constant) and two **isentropic curves** ($PV^\gamma = $ constant):

```
PRESSURE P
  ^
  |   1 .----------. 2      (Isothermal at T_H: PV = mRT_H)
  |     \          \
  |      \          \
  |       \          \
  |        \          \
  |         \          \
  |          \          \
  |           4 .------. 3   (Isothermal at T_L: PV = mRT_L)
  |
  +--------------------------> VOLUME V
       V4   V1    V2   V3
```

> **Key Observation:** Isothermal curves (1-2 and 3-4) are **flatter** at higher temperatures. Isentropes (2-3 and 4-1) are **steeper** than isotherms. The **enclosed area** = $W_{net}$.

---

### 4.5 Reversed Carnot Cycle — Refrigeration/Heat Pump Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RESH2["HOT SPACE T_H"]
    RESL2["COLD SPACE T_L"]
    RVP["REVERSED CARNOT CYCLE"]
    
    QH2["Q_H REJECTED TO HOT SPACE"]
    QL2["Q_L ABSORBED FROM COLD SPACE"]
    WIN["W_IN WORK INPUT"]
    
    RVP --> QH2 --> RESH2
    RESL2 --> QL2 --> RVP
    WIN --> RVP
```

> **Mode Identification:** When the cycle direction is reversed:
> - **Refrigerator Mode:** $Q_L$ (cooling effect) is the desired output
> - **Heat Pump Mode:** $Q_H$ (heating effect) is the desired output
> - Both modes use the **same work input** $W_{in}$, but yield different COPs.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. Define the Carnot cycle and list its four processes. Why is it considered a reversible cycle?**

**Model Answer:**
The Carnot cycle is an ideal reversible thermodynamic cycle proposed by Sadi Carnot in 1824, consisting of **four internally reversible processes**:
1. Isothermal expansion at $T_H$ (heat $Q_H$ absorbed)
2. Isentropic expansion ($T_H \to T_L$)
3. Isothermal compression at $T_L$ (heat $Q_L$ rejected)
4. Isentropic compression ($T_L \to T_H$)

It is considered reversible because all processes are carried out **quasi-statically** (infinitesimally small deviations from equilibrium) with **no friction, no heat transfer across finite temperature differences, and no unrestrained expansion**. The cycle therefore satisfies the equality $\oint \delta Q/T = 0$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q2. State the Carnot theorem. Mention its two corollaries.**

**Model Answer:**
**Carnot Theorem:** No heat engine operating between two given thermal reservoirs can be more efficient than a reversible (Carnot) engine operating between the same two reservoirs. Additionally, all reversible engines operating between the same two reservoirs have the **same efficiency**, irrespective of the working fluid.

**Corollary 1:** The efficiency of a Carnot engine depends only on the temperatures of the reservoirs: $\eta = 1 - T_L/T_H$.

**Corollary 2:** No engine can have 100% thermal efficiency unless $T_L = 0 \text{ K}$, which is unattainable per the Third Law of Thermodynamics.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**A Carnot heat engine operates between a high-temperature reservoir at 600 °C and a low-temperature reservoir at 40 °C. The engine receives 2000 kJ of heat per cycle from the hot reservoir. Determine:**

**(a) [7 Marks] The thermal efficiency, the net work output per cycle, and the heat rejected to the cold reservoir per cycle.**

**(b) [7 Marks] If the same cycle is reversed to operate as a refrigerator and as a heat pump, calculate the COP in each case. Also compute the rate of heat removal and heat delivery if the work input is 50 kW.**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $T_H = 600 + 273 = 873 \text{ K}$
- $T_L = 40 + 273 = 313 \text{ K}$
- $Q_H = 2000 \text{ kJ}$ (per cycle)

**Part (a) — Heat Engine Analysis:**

**Step 1:** Compute thermal efficiency using the Carnot formula.

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{313}{873}
$$

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - 0.3585 = 0.6415
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{th} = 64.15\%}
$$

**Step 2:** Net work output per cycle.

$$
W_{net} = \eta_{th} \times Q_H = 0.6415 \times 2000
$$

$$
\boxed{W_{net} = 1283.0 \text{ kJ}}
$$

**Step 3:** Heat rejected to cold reservoir.

$$
Q_L = Q_H - W_{net} = 2000 - 1283.0
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_L = 717.0 \text{ kJ}}
$$

**Verification using $Q_L = Q_H \cdot T_L/T_H$:**

$$
Q_L = 2000 \times \frac{313}{873} = 2000 \times 0.3585 = 717.0 \text{ kJ} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

**Part (b) — Reversed Cycle (Refrigerator and Heat Pump):**

**Step 4:** COP as a refrigerator.

$$
COP_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{313}{873 - 313} = \frac{313}{560}
$$

$$
\boxed{COP_R = 0.5589}
$$

**Step 5:** COP as a heat pump.

$$
COP_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{873}{560}
$$

$$
\boxed{COP_{HP} = 1.5589}
$$

**Verification:** $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1 = 0.5589 + 1 = 1.5589$ ✓

**Step 6:** Rate of heat removal (refrigeration effect) at $W_{in} = 50 \text{ kW}$.

$$
\dot{Q}_L = COP_R \times \dot{W}_{in} = 0.5589 \times 50
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{Q}_L = 27.95 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Step 7:** Rate of heat delivery (heating effect) as heat pump.

$$
\dot{Q}_H = COP_{HP} \times \dot{W}_{in} = 1.5589 \times 50
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{Q}_H = 77.95 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Energy Balance Check:** $\dot{Q}_H = \dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_{in} = 27.95 + 50 = 77.95$ kW ✓

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**A Carnot refrigerator maintains a cold space at −10 °C by rejecting heat to surroundings at 35 °C. The refrigerator requires a cooling load of 5 kW. Determine:**

**(a) [7 Marks] The COP of the refrigerator, the power input to the compressor, and the rate of heat rejected to the surroundings.**

**(b) [7 Marks] If the same setup is operated as a Carnot heat pump for space heating, calculate its COP, the heating capacity, and the percentage improvement in COP compared to an electric resistance heater (which has COP = 1).**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $T_L = -10 + 273 = 263 \text{ K}$
- $T_H = 35 + 273 = 308 \text{ K}$
- $\dot{Q}_L = 5 \text{ kW}$ (cooling load)

**Part (a) — Refrigerator Analysis:**

**Step 1:** COP of the Carnot refrigerator.

$$
COP_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263}{308 - 263} = \frac{263}{45}
$$

$$
\boxed{COP_R = 5.844}
$$

**Step 2:** Power input to compressor.

$$
\dot{W}_{in} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{COP_R} = \frac{5}{5.844}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{W}_{in} = 0.8555 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Step 3:** Rate of heat rejected.

$$
\dot{Q}_H = \dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_{in} = 5 + 0.8555
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{Q}_H = 5.8555 \text{ kW}}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Heat Pump Analysis:**

**Step 4:** COP of the Carnot heat pump.

$$
COP_{HP} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{308}{45}
$$

$$
\boxed{COP_{HP} = 6.844}
$$

**Step 5:** Heating capacity (heat delivered to the hot space).

$$
\dot{Q}_{H,HP} = COP_{HP} \times \dot{W}_{in} = 6.844 \times 0.8555
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{Q}_{H,HP} = 5.8555 \text{ kW}}
$$

**Step 6:** Percentage improvement over electric resistance heater.

For a resistance heater, all electrical work is converted to heat: $COP_{resistance} = 1$.

$$
\% \text{ Improvement} = \frac{COP_{HP} - COP_{resistance}}{COP_{resistance}} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ Improvement} = \frac{6.844 - 1}{1} \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\% \text{ Improvement} = 584.4\%}
$$

**Conclusion:** A Carnot heat pump delivers **6.844 units of heat for every 1 unit of electrical work**, which is **584.4% more efficient** than direct electrical resistance heating.

---

### ⛔ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
>
> 1. **Temperature in Celsius:** Using 600 °C and 40 °C directly in the efficiency formula gives nonsensical results. **ALWAYS convert to Kelvin**: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273$.
>
> 2. **Confusing $Q_L$ and $Q_H$:** Students frequently invert the heat quantities. Remember: $Q_H$ is heat **input** (from hot reservoir), $Q_L$ is heat **rejected** (to cold reservoir). Both are positive magnitudes.
>
> 3. **Reversed Cycle Sign Errors:** When the Carnot cycle is reversed, $Q_H$ and $Q_L$ swap their role conceptually, but the magnitude formulas remain the same. The COP changes definition.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to Verify $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$:** Examiners award bonus marks for this cross-check.
>
> 5. **Rounding Errors:** Keep **at least 4 decimal places** during intermediate steps; round only the final answer to 2 decimal places.
>
> 6. **Confusing "Net Work" with "Cycle Work":** $W_{net} = W_{1-2} + W_{2-3} + W_{3-4} + W_{4-1}$, not just one of the isothermal terms.
>
> 7. **Missing Units in Final Answer:** Even though the COP is dimensionless, **always write it as a number** (e.g., "COP = 5.844"), not with units.
>
> 8. **Drawing the Wrong Cycle Direction:** A clockwise P-V or T-S diagram is a **heat engine**; a counter-clockwise one is a **refrigerator/heat pump**. This is a 1-mark question in KTU — don't lose it.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Carnot Cycle (Module 1)**

- ☐ The **Carnot cycle** is an **ideal, reversible** cycle operating between **two thermal reservoirs** at $T_H$ and $T_L$.
- ☐ Four processes in order: **Isothermal expansion (1→2) → Isentropic expansion (2→3) → Isothermal compression (3→4) → Isentropic compression (4→1)**.
- ☐ Heat is transferred **only** during isothermal processes ($Q_H$ at $T_H$, $Q_L$ at $T_L$).
- ☐ Work is transferred **only** across the system boundary during all four processes.
- ☐ **Master Formula:** $\eta_{th,Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ — temperatures in **Kelvin**.
- ☐ **Net Work:** $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$ (First Law over cycle).
- ☐ **Refrigerator COP:** $COP_R = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ — desired output is $Q_L$ (cooling).
- ☐ **Heat Pump COP:** $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ — desired output is $Q_H$ (heating).
- ☐ **Identity:** $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ (always check this in your solution).
- ☐ On a **T-s diagram**, the cycle is a **rectangle** — areas directly give $Q_H$, $Q_L$, and $W_{net}$.
- ☐ On a **P-V diagram**, the cycle is bounded by two isotherms and two isentropes.
- ☐ **Carnot Theorem:** No engine can be more efficient than a Carnot engine between the same $T_H$ and $T_L$.
- ☐ $\eta_{th} \rightarrow 100\%$ only if $T_L = 0$ K, which is **impossible** (Third Law).
- ☐ **Reversed Carnot Cycle** = Refrigerator/Heat Pump (counter-clockwise on P-V/T-S).
- ☐ For KTU 14-mark problems: **always show the conversion to Kelvin first**, then compute efficiency, then $W_{net}$, then $Q_L$, then COPs.
- ☐ Use the relationship $\dfrac{Q_H}{T_H} = \dfrac{Q_L}{T_L}$ for any reversible cycle (Clausius Equality).
- ☐ A **real engine's efficiency** is always **less than** Carnot efficiency — this is the Second Law in action.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reversed Carnot Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT502 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT ENGINES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Reversed Carnot Cycle # Module 1 — Reversed Carnot Cycle ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition The **Reversed Carnot Cycle** is the thermodynamic cycle obtained by executing the four processes of the standard **Carnot Cycle** in the exact opposite (reverse) direction. While the forward Carnot cycle functions as a **heat engine** (converting heat into work), the reversed Carnot cycle operates as a **refrigeration cycle** or a **heat pump cycle**. It consists of two **isothermal** processes and two **isentropic** (reversible adiabatic) processes executed in the sequence: *Isentropic Expansion → Isothermal Heat Absorption → Isentropic Compression → Isothermal Heat Rejection*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Reversed Carnot Cycle is the benchmark for the **maximum possible Coefficient of Performance (COP)** for any refrigeration system operating between two given temperature reservoirs. No real refrigerator operating between the same two temperatures can exceed the COP of a Reversed Carnot Cycle. > [!NOTE] > **Naming Convention Used in KTU Textbooks:** > - **$T_1$** = Temperature of the **HOT reservoir** (higher temperature, in **K**) > - **$T_2$** = Temperature of the **COLD reservoir** (lower temperature, in **K**) > - **$Q_1$** = Heat rejected to the hot reservoir (always **positive** in the KTU convention) > - **$Q_2$** = Heat absorbed from the cold reservoir (always **positive** magnitude) > - **$W_{in}$** = Net work input **TO** the cycle ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you have a **water pump**, but instead of pumping water, you are "pumping heat" from a cold place to a hot place. Nature does not allow heat to spontaneously flow from cold to hot (this is the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**), so you must **invest work** to force this transfer. **Think of a domestic refrigerator:** - Inside the fridge (cold space) is at a low temperature $T_2$. - The room outside the fridge (hot space) is at a higher temperature $T_1$. - The refrigerator **absorbs heat** $Q_2$ from the food (cold reservoir). - Your **electricity bill** pays for the work $W_{in}$ consumed by the compressor. - The fridge **rejects heat** $Q_1 = Q_2 + W_{in}$ to the kitchen (hot reservoir), which is why the back of a refrigerator feels warm. A **Reversed Carnot Cycle** is the **theoretically perfect refrigerator** — it is reversible, has no friction, no pressure drops, and uses perfect isothermal and isentropic processes. It tells us the **absolute best performance** physically possible. > [!NOTE] > **Constant Temperatures** — A key property of the Reversed Carnot Cycle is that both the heat absorption and heat rejection occur at **constant temperatures** ($T_2$ and $T_1$ respectively). This is precisely why it is the *ideal* benchmark. --- ### Real-World Identification Tags - **Used as a reference in:** Vapour Compression Refrigeration, Vapour Absorption Refrigeration, Air-Conditioning Systems, Heat Pumps, Cryogenic Liquefaction. - **Metric Used:** **COP (Coefficient of Performance)** — defined differently for refrigerators and heat pumps. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Processes of the Reversed Carnot Cycle The cycle is traversed in the **anti-clockwise** direction on the **P–V** and **T–S** diagrams (opposite of the forward Carnot cycle). Working from state point **1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 1**: ### Process 1 → 2: Isentropic (Reversible Adiabatic) Expansion - The refrigerant expands through a **reversible adiabatic turbine** (or expansion valve, ideally). - Entropy remains **constant**: $s_1 = s_2$ - Temperature **drops** from the hot reservoir temperature $T_1$ to the cold reservoir temperature $T_2$. - **No heat transfer** occurs ($Q_{1-2} = 0$). - Work is **produced** by the system (in the ideal Reversed Carnot turbine). ### Process 2 → 3: Isothermal Heat Absorption at $T_2$ - The refrigerant (now a low-temperature fluid) **absorbs heat** $Q_2$ from the cold space being refrigerated. - Temperature remains constant at $T_2$. - Entropy **increases** from $s_2$ to $s_3$ (since heat enters the system). - This is the **useful refrigeration effect**. ### Process 3 → 4: Isentropic (Reversible Adiabatic) Compression - The refrigerant is compressed by a **reversible adiabatic compressor**. - Entropy remains constant: $s_3 = s_4$ - Temperature **rises** from $T_2$ back to $T_1$. - **No heat transfer** occurs ($Q_{3-4} = 0$). - Work is **consumed** by the system. ### Process 4 → 1: Isothermal Heat Rejection at $T_1$ - The high-temperature refrigerant **rejects heat** $Q_1$ to the hot reservoir (surroundings). - Temperature remains constant at $T_1$. - Entropy **decreases** from $s_4$ to $s_1$. - This is the **waste heat** dumped to the environment. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Memory Trick:** *"Expansion cools, Absorption freezes, Compression heats, Rejection warms."* — follow this order: **E-A-C-R** for processes 1-2-3-4. --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Notes | |---|----------|---------|------------|-------| | 1 | Heat Rejected to Hot Reservoir | $Q_1 = T_1 \cdot (s_1 - s_4)$ | **kJ/kg** | Positive value; occurs at constant $T_1$ | | 2 | Heat Absorbed from Cold Reservoir (Refrigeration Effect) | $Q_2 = T_2 \cdot (s_3 - s_2)$ | **kJ/kg** | Positive value; occurs at constant $T_2$ | | 3 | Net Work Input to Cycle | $W_{in} = Q_1 - Q_2$ | **kJ/kg** | Equal to area enclosed in cycle on **P–V** or **T–S** | | 4 | Work Input (Alternate form) | $W_{in} = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot (s_1 - s_4)$ | **kJ/kg** | Since $s_1 - s_4 = s_3 - s_2$ | | 5 | **COP of Reversed Carnot Refrigerator** | $\text{COP}_{ref} = \dfrac{Q_2}{W_{in}} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2}$ | Dimensionless | Maximum possible COP | | 6 | **COP of Reversed Carnot Heat Pump** | $\text{COP}_{hp} = \dfrac{Q_1}{W_{in}} = \dfrac{T_1}{T_1 - T_2}$ | Dimensionless | Always $\text{COP}_{hp} = \text{COP}_{ref} + 1$ | | 7 | Relation between COPs | $\text{COP}_{hp} = \text{COP}_{ref} + 1$ | Dimensionless | Important derivation identity | | 8 | Heat Pump vs Refrigerator | $Q_1 = Q_2 + W_{in}$ | **kJ/kg** | Energy balance (1st Law) | | 9 | Isentropic Process Constraint | $s_1 = s_2$ and $s_3 = s_4$ | **kJ/kg·K** | Entropy constant for 1-2 and 3-4 | | 10 | Net Entropy Change (Per Cycle) | $\oint ds = 0$ | **kJ/kg·K** | Cycle is reversible, so $\Delta s_{universe} = 0$ | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL UNIT WARNING FOR KTU VALUATION:** Temperatures **MUST** be in **Kelvin (K)** in all COP formulas. Using °C will cause a full-mark deduction. Convert using: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$. --- ## 2.3 Why the COP Cannot Exceed the Carnot Value By the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** (Clausius Inequality for a reversible cycle): $$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$$ For the Reversed Carnot cycle, this expands to: $$\frac{Q_1}{T_1} = \frac{Q_2}{T_2}$$ This fundamental identity (often called the **Carnot Heat Ratio**) is the seed of every COP formula. It tells us that in a reversible cycle, the **ratio of heat exchanged to the absolute temperature** is constant. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Air-Conditioning & Refrigeration:** The COP of a Reversed Carnot cycle sets the **theoretical upper limit** for real refrigerators. A typical domestic refrigerator has a real COP of 1.5 to 3.0, whereas a Reversed Carnot cycle between the same temperatures might have a COP of 5 to 8. - **Heat Pump Design:** Engineers compare real heat pump COPs against $\text{COP}_{hp,Carnot}$ to gauge the **second-law efficiency** (or **exergy efficiency**) of the system. - **Cryogenics:** Liquefaction of gases (e.g., LNG, liquid nitrogen) targets performance approaching the Carnot limit. - **HVAC Building Design:** ASHRAE standards often quote Reversed Carnot COP as the maximum benchmark for chiller performance. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the COP of a Reversed Carnot Refrigerator ### Step 1: Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics to the Cycle For a closed system executing a complete cycle, the net change in internal energy is zero: $$\oint dU = 0$$ Therefore, the net work input equals the net heat transfer (with sign convention: work in is positive, heat in is positive): $$W_{in} = Q_1 - Q_2$$ *Logic:* The energy entering the system as work and as heat from the cold reservoir must equal the energy leaving as heat to the hot reservoir. ### Step 2: Apply the Second Law (Clausius Equality for Reversible Cycle) For a **reversible** cycle, the Clausius equality holds: $$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$$ Splitting this over the four processes of the Reversed Carnot cycle: $$\frac{Q_1}{T_1} + 0 - \frac{Q_2}{T_2} + 0 = 0$$ *Note on signs:* $Q_1$ is rejected from the system (negative in the strict $\oint$ convention but taken as positive magnitude in KTU notation). $Q_2$ is absorbed into the system (positive). Re-arranging: $$\frac{Q_1}{T_1} = \frac{Q_2}{T_2}$$ ### Step 3: Express the COP Definition By definition, the COP of a refrigerator is the **useful refrigeration effect** divided by the **work input**: $$\text{COP}_{ref} = \frac{Q_2}{W_{in}}$$ ### Step 4: Substitute $W_{in} = Q_1 - Q_2$ $$\text{COP}_{ref} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_1 - Q_2}$$ ### Step 5: Divide Numerator and Denominator by $Q_2$ $$\text{COP}_{ref} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} - 1}$$ ### Step 6: Use the Second-Law Relation $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{T_1}{T_2}$ $$\text{COP}_{ref} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} - 1}$$ ### Step 7: Simplify the Denominator Algebraically $$\frac{T_1}{T_2} - 1 = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_2}$$ ### Step 8: Final Result $$\boxed{\text{COP}_{ref} = \frac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2}}$$ This is the **most important formula in the entire topic** for KTU exams. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the COP of a Reversed Carnot Heat Pump ### Step 1: Definition of COP for a Heat Pump The "useful effect" of a heat pump is the **heat delivered** $Q_1$ to the hot space (e.g., a room being heated in winter), not the refrigeration effect: $$\text{COP}_{hp} = \frac{Q_1}{W_{in}}$$ ### Step 2: Substitute $W_{in} = Q_1 - Q_2$ $$\text{COP}_{hp} = \frac{Q_1}{Q_1 - Q_2}$$ ### Step 3: Divide Numerator and Denominator by $Q_1$ $$\text{COP}_{hp} = \frac{1}{1 - \dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1}}$$ ### Step 4: Use $\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}$ $$\text{COP}_{hp} = \frac{1}{1 - \dfrac{T_2}{T_1}}$$ ### Step 5: Simplify the Denominator $$1 - \frac{T_2}{T_1} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1}$$ ### Step 6: Final Result $$\boxed{\text{COP}_{hp} = \frac{T_1}{T_1 - T_2}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation of the Identity $\text{COP}_{hp} = \text{COP}_{ref} + 1$ ### Step 1: Subtract the COP Formulas $$\text{COP}_{hp} - \text{COP}_{ref} = \frac{T_1}{T_1 - T_2} - \frac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2}$$ ### Step 2: Common Denominator $$\text{COP}_{hp} - \text{COP}_{ref} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_2}$$ ### Step 3: Result $$\boxed{\text{COP}_{hp} - \text{COP}_{ref} = 1}$$ Therefore: $$\text{COP}_{hp} = \text{COP}_{ref} + 1$$ This identity is a **favourite 2-mark short question** in KTU exams. --- ## 3.4 Derivation of Net Work in Terms of Temperature and Entropy ### Step 1: From First Law $$W_{in} = Q_1 - Q_2$$ ### Step 2: Substitute Heat Expressions $$Q_1 = T_1 \cdot (s_1 - s_4) \quad \text{and} \quad Q_2 = T_2 \cdot (s_3 - s_2)$$ ### Step 3: Apply Isentropic Constraints For isentropic processes 1-2 and 3-4: $s_1 = s_2$ and $s_3 = s_4$. Hence: $$s_1 - s_4 = s_3 - s_2 = \Delta s$$ ### Step 4: Substitute $$W_{in} = T_1 \cdot \Delta s - T_2 \cdot \Delta s = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot \Delta s$$ ### Step 5: Final Compact Form $$\boxed{W_{in} = (T_1 - T_2)(s_1 - s_4)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** Whenever a problem states "Reversed Carnot cycle" without specifying process details, the *first thing* you should write is the COP formula. Marks are awarded for **stating the governing equation first**, even before the numerical substitution. --- ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Demonstration (For Concept Reinforcement) **Problem:** A Reversed Carnot cycle operates between a hot reservoir at $T_1 = 300$ K and a cold reservoir at $T_2 = 250$ K. If the refrigeration effect is $Q_2 = 100$ kJ/kg, determine: 1. COP of refrigerator 2. COP of heat pump 3. Net work input 4. Heat rejected to hot reservoir **Solution:** **(1) COP of Refrigerator:** $$\text{COP}_{ref} = \frac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2} = \frac{250}{300 - 250} = \frac{250}{50} = 5.0$$ **(2) COP of Heat Pump:** $$\text{COP}_{hp} = \frac{T_1}{T_1 - T_2} = \frac{300}{50} = 6.0$$ *Verification:* $\text{COP}_{hp} = \text{COP}_{ref} + 1 = 5 + 1 = 6$ ✓ **(3) Net Work Input:** $$W_{in} = \frac{Q_2}{\text{COP}_{ref}} = \frac{100}{5.0} = 20 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **(4) Heat Rejected:** $$Q_1 = Q_2 + W_{in} = 100 + 20 = 120 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ *Alternative verification:* $$Q_1 = \text{COP}_{hp} \times W_{in} = 6.0 \times 20 = 120 \text{ kJ/kg} \checkmark$$ > [!NOTE] > **Insight:** For every **1 kJ** of work input, the refrigerator removes **5 kJ** of heat from the cold space, and the heat pump delivers **6 kJ** of heat to the hot space. This is the **miracle of the heat pump** — it is more efficient than direct electrical heating! # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Reversed Carnot Cycle on a T–S Diagram (Block-Level Architecture) > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note:** The T–S diagram is the most useful for refrigeration cycles because **area = heat transfer**. The Reversed Carnot cycle appears as a **rectangle** (since both isotherms are horizontal lines and both isentropics are vertical lines). It is traversed **anti-clockwise** (opposite to the heat-engine Carnot cycle). ```mermaid flowchart TB state1["STATE 1: HIGH T HIGH S
Superheated Vapor at T1
Refrigerant leaving compressor"] state2["STATE 2: LOW T HIGH S
Saturated or Superheated Vapor at T2
After isentropic expansion"] state3["STATE 3: LOW T HIGHER S
Saturated Vapor at T2
After isothermal heat absorption"] state4["STATE 4: HIGH T LOWER S
Subcooled Liquid at T1
After isentropic compression"] process12["PROCESS 1 TO 2
ISENTROPIC EXPANSION
s1 equals s2 T drops from T1 to T2
Q equals 0"] process23["PROCESS 2 TO 3
ISOTHERMAL HEAT ABSORPTION at T2
Q2 equals T2 times delta s
REFRIGERATION EFFECT"] process34["PROCESS 3 TO 4
ISENTROPIC COMPRESSION
s3 equals s4 T rises from T2 to T1
Q equals 0"] process41["PROCESS 4 TO 1
ISOTHERMAL HEAT REJECTION at T1
Q1 equals T1 times delta s
DUMPED TO HOT RESERVOIR"] state1 --> process12 process12 --> state2 state2 --> process23 process23 --> state3 state3 --> process34 process34 --> state4 state4 --> process41 process41 --> state1 classDef stateNode fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef processNode fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class state1,state2,state3,state4 stateNode class process12,process23,process34,process41 processNode ``` --- ## 4.2 Physical System Block Diagram (Refrigeration Hardware Mapping) The Reversed Carnot cycle is **idealized**, but it maps to real hardware components as follows: ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph COLD["COLD RESERVOIR at Temperature T2"] COLD_OBJ["Refrigerated Space
e.g. Cold room, AC evaporator coil"] end subgraph HOT["HOT RESERVOIR at Temperature T1"] HOT_OBJ["Surroundings
e.g. Ambient air, condenser coil"] end subgraph CYCLE["REVERSED CARNOT CYCLE"] TURB["Reversible Adiabatic
EXPANDER
State 1 to 2"] EVAP["Isothermal
EVAPORATOR
State 2 to 3
Absorbs Q2"] COMP["Reversible Adiabatic
COMPRESSOR
State 3 to 4"] COND["Isothermal
CONDENSER
State 4 to 1
Rejects Q1"] end WIN["Work Input W_in
e.g. Electric Motor"] EVAP -.Heat Q2.-> COLD_OBJ COND -.Heat Q1.-> HOT_OBJ TURB --> EVAP EVAP --> COMP COMP --> COND COND --> TURB WIN --> COMP WIN --> TURB classDef reservoir fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef component fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef work fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class COLD_OBJ,HOT_OBJ reservoir class TURB,EVAP,COMP,COND component class WIN work ``` --- ## 4.3 P–V Diagram Schematic (Cycle Shape on Pressure–Volume Plane) On a P–V diagram, the cycle looks like a **curvilinear quadrilateral** (similar to the forward Carnot cycle, but traversed in the reverse direction): - **Top curve (4 → 1):** Isothermal compression of vapour at high $T_1$ — moves left (volume decreases). - **Right curve (1 → 2):** Isentropic expansion — drops down and right (T decreases, V increases). - **Bottom curve (2 → 3):** Isothermal expansion of vapour at low $T_2$ — moves right (volume increases). - **Left curve (3 → 4):** Isentropic compression — rises up and left (T increases, V decreases). > [!NOTE] > **Why a Rectangle on T–S, but Curved on P–V?** > On a T–S diagram, an isotherm is a horizontal line and an isentrope is a vertical line → **rectangle**. On a P–V diagram, isotherms are hyperbolas ($PV = \text{const}$ for an ideal gas) and isentropes are steeper curves ($PV^\gamma = \text{const}$). --- ## 4.4 Schematic P–H (Pressure–Enthalpy) Representation The P–H diagram is heavily used in practical refrigeration. On this diagram: - Isothermal processes are nearly **horizontal** lines (in the wet region, $h$ depends only on $T$ for an ideal two-phase mixture). - Isentropic processes appear as **nearly vertical** drops (isenthalpic throttling) or rises (ideal compressor). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse the **Reversed Carnot Cycle** with the **Vapour Compression Cycle** on a P–H diagram. The Vapour Compression cycle uses an **isenthalpic throttling** (4 → 1) instead of isothermal heat rejection, and an **irreversible compression**. The Reversed Carnot is purely theoretical. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define the Reversed Carnot Cycle and state its four processes. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The Reversed Carnot Cycle is an ideal reversible thermodynamic cycle that operates in the **reverse direction** of the Carnot cycle, functioning as a **refrigeration** or **heat pump** cycle. It consists of the following four reversible processes executed in sequence: 1. **Process 1 → 2: Isentropic Expansion** — The working fluid expands reversibly and adiabatically, with no change in entropy. Its temperature drops from the hot reservoir temperature $T_1$ to the cold reservoir temperature $T_2$. 2. **Process 2 → 3: Isothermal Heat Absorption at $T_2$** — The working fluid absorbs heat $Q_2$ from the cold space at constant low temperature $T_2$. This is the **refrigeration effect**. 3. **Process 3 → 4: Isentropic Compression** — The working fluid is compressed reversibly and adiabatically; entropy remains constant, and temperature rises from $T_2$ to $T_1$. 4. **Process 4 → 1: Isothermal Heat Rejection at $T_1$** — The working fluid rejects heat $Q_1$ to the hot reservoir at constant high temperature $T_1$. > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Key:** All four processes must be named in order. Marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for correctly listing the four processes. --- ### Question 2: What is the Coefficient of Performance (COP)? Why is COP preferred over efficiency for refrigeration cycles? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** The **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** of a refrigerator is defined as the ratio of the desired output (refrigeration effect $Q_2$) to the work input $W_{in}$ required to achieve it: $$\text{COP}_{ref} = \frac{Q_2}{W_{in}}$$ For a Reversed Carnot refrigerator: $$\text{COP}_{ref} = \frac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2}$$ **Why COP is preferred over thermal efficiency for refrigeration:** 1. **COP can exceed unity (1).** A COP of 5 means that for every 1 kJ of work input, 5 kJ of heat is removed. Thermal efficiency is bounded by 1 (or 100%) for all heat engines by the Second Law — a misleading comparison. 2. **Different "desired output":** In a heat engine, the desired output is **work produced**. In a refrigerator, the desired output is **heat extracted from the cold space**, not work. Hence a different metric (COP) is needed. 3. **Performance benchmarking:** COP directly answers the practical question: *"How much cooling do I get per unit of work I pay for?"* — which is what matters to the user. > [!NOTE] > **Valuation Key:** Mentioning that COP > 1 is possible and giving the formula carries full marks. Marks: 1.5 for definition + 1.5 for reasons. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) --- ### 🔵 Question A: Comprehensive Reversed Carnot Refrigerator Problem `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Question:** A Reversed Carnot refrigerator is used to maintain a refrigerated space at $-10°C$. The temperature of the surroundings is $30°C$. The heat load on the refrigerator is $2.5$ kW (i.e., $2.5$ kJ/s leakage into the cold space). Calculate: **(a)** The COP of the refrigerator and the COP of the equivalent heat pump. **(b)** The power input to the refrigerator (in kW) and the rate of heat rejected to the surroundings. **Model Solution:** **Given Data:** - Cold space temperature: $T_2 = -10°C = -10 + 273 = 263$ K - Hot space temperature: $T_1 = 30°C = 30 + 273 = 303$ K - Refrigeration load: $Q_2 = 2.5$ kW **Part (a) — Calculation of COPs:** **COP of the Refrigerator:** $$\text{COP}_{ref} = \frac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2} = \frac{263}{303 - 263} = \frac{263}{40} = 6.575$$ **COP of the Heat Pump:** $$\text{COP}_{hp} = \frac{T_1}{T_1 - T_2} = \frac{303}{40} = 7.575$$ *Verification using identity:* $$\text{COP}_{hp} = \text{COP}_{ref} + 1 = 6.575 + 1 = 7.575 \checkmark$$ **Part (b) — Power Input and Heat Rejection Rate:** **Power Input (Work Input Rate):** $$W_{in} = \frac{Q_2}{\text{COP}_{ref}} = \frac{2.5}{6.575} = 0.3802 \text{ kW}$$ **Rate of Heat Rejection to Surroundings:** $$Q_1 = Q_2 + W_{in} = 2.5 + 0.3802 = 2.8802 \text{ kW}$$ *Alternative calculation:* $$Q_1 = \text{COP}_{hp} \times W_{in} = 7.575 \times 0.3802 = 2.880 \text{ kW} \checkmark$$ **Final Answer:** - $\text{COP}_{ref} = 6.575$ (dimensionless) - $\text{COP}_{hp} = 7.575$ (dimensionless) - $W_{in} = 0.3802$ kW - $Q_1 = 2.880$ kW **Sub-part Marks Distribution (KTU pattern):** - Part (a) — 7 marks: 3 marks for formula, 2 marks for correct substitution, 2 marks for final answer. - Part (b) — 7 marks: 3 marks for work formula + answer, 2 marks for $Q_1$ formula, 2 marks for numerical answer. --- ### 🟢 Question B: Heat Pump Mode and Comparison `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Question:** A building is to be maintained at $22°C$ when the outside ambient temperature is $2°C$ using a Reversed Carnot heat pump. The rate of heat loss from the building to the surroundings is $1.5 \times 10^5$ kJ/hr. **(a)** Determine the COP of the heat pump and the power required to drive it. **(b)** If this heat pump were reversed to act as a Reversed Carnot refrigerator (cooling the building at $22°C$ when outside is $40°C$), calculate the new COP and the power required to maintain the same heat load. **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Heat Pump Mode:** **Given Data:** - Inside (hot) temperature: $T_1 = 22°C = 22 + 273 = 295$ K - Outside (cold) temperature: $T_2 = 2°C = 2 + 273 = 275$ K - Heat loss rate: $Q_1 = 1.5 \times 10^5$ kJ/hr **Convert to kW:** $$Q_1 = \frac{1.5 \times 10^5}{3600} = 41.667 \text{ kW}$$ **COP of Heat Pump:** $$\text{COP}_{hp} = \frac{T_1}{T_1 - T_2} = \frac{295}{295 - 275} = \frac{295}{20} = 14.75$$ **Power Required:** $$W_{in} = \frac{Q_1}{\text{COP}_{hp}} = \frac{41.667}{14.75} = 2.825 \text{ kW}$$ **Part (b) — Refrigerator Mode (Reversed Operating Conditions):** **New Operating Data:** - New hot (outside) temperature: $T_1' = 40°C = 40 + 273 = 313$ K - New cold (inside) temperature: $T_2' = 22°C = 295$ K - Heat load to be removed: $Q_2' = 41.667$ kW (same magnitude) **COP of Refrigerator (New Conditions):** $$\text{COP}_{ref} = \frac{T_2'}{T_1' - T_2'} = \frac{295}{313 - 295} = \frac{295}{18} = 16.389$$ **Power Required (Refrigerator Mode):** $$W_{in}' = \frac{Q_2'}{\text{COP}_{ref}} = \frac{41.667}{16.389} = 2.542 \text{ kW}$$ **Final Answer:** - Part (a): $\text{COP}_{hp} = 14.75$, $W_{in} = 2.825$ kW - Part (b): $\text{COP}_{ref} = 16.389$, $W_{in}' = 2.542$ kW **Observation:** Reversing the operating temperatures from $(295, 275)$ to $(313, 295)$ reduced the temperature difference from 20 K to 18 K, **increasing** the COP — but since the second case requires overcoming a larger temperature lift on average, real-world analysis requires care. **Sub-part Marks Distribution (KTU pattern):** - Part (a) — 7 marks: 2 marks for correct temperature conversion to K, 2 marks for COP formula, 3 marks for power calculation. - Part (b) — 7 marks: 2 marks for identifying the new $T_1$ and $T_2$, 2 marks for new COP, 3 marks for power. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **🔥 KTU EXAMINER'S PITFALL CALLOUT — READ CAREFULLY 🔥** > > **1. Forgetting to Convert °C to Kelvin (Most common mistake — loses 2 to 3 marks):** > If the problem gives temperatures in Celsius, **MUST** convert to Kelvin using $T(K) = T(°C) + 273$ (or 273.15 for higher precision). Using °C directly in $\text{COP} = T_2/(T_1 - T_2)$ is a fatal error and will result in a **negative COP**. > > **2. Mixing up $T_1$ and $T_2$:** > $T_1$ = **HOT** reservoir (higher temperature), $T_2$ = **COLD** reservoir (lower temperature). Swapping these will give an inverted, incorrect answer. Always sanity check: $T_1 > T_2$ must hold. > > **3. Confusing Refrigerator COP with Heat Pump COP:** > - $\text{COP}_{ref} = T_2 / (T_1 - T_2)$ (uses the cold temperature in numerator) > - $\text{COP}_{hp} = T_1 / (T_1 - T_2)$ (uses the hot temperature in numerator) > - The two differ by **exactly 1**, and an examiner will readily detect mix-ups. > > **4. Missing Units in the Final Answer:** > Always state units: COP is **dimensionless**, $W_{in}$ is in **kW or kJ/s**, $Q$ is in **kJ or kJ/kg**. Missing units = lost marks. > > **5. Not Stating the Governing Formula First:** > KTU examiners reward students who write the formula **before** substitution. Always follow: *Formula → Substitution → Answer*. > > **6. Forgetting the 1st Law Verification $Q_1 = Q_2 + W_{in}$:** > This is a great cross-check. If your $Q_1$ does not equal $Q_2 + W_{in}$, re-check your arithmetic. > > **7. Writing Negative or Zero Work:** > A Reversed Carnot cycle **requires** positive work input. If your $W_{in}$ comes out negative, you have a sign error. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ The **Reversed Carnot Cycle** is the **reverse** of the forward Carnot cycle — it operates as a refrigerator or heat pump. - ✅ The four processes are: **Isentropic Expansion → Isothermal Heat Absorption → Isentropic Compression → Isothermal Heat Rejection** (in that order, E-A-C-R). - ✅ On a **T–S diagram**, the cycle is a **rectangle traversed anti-clockwise**. - ✅ On a **P–V diagram**, the cycle is a **curvilinear quadrilateral** with two hyperbolic isotherms and two steeper isentropic curves. - ✅ **Refrigeration effect** $Q_2$ is absorbed at the **cold** temperature $T_2$ — this is the useful cooling. - ✅ **Heat rejection** $Q_1$ occurs at the **hot** temperature $T_1$ — this is the waste heat dumped to surroundings. - ✅ **Net work input** $W_{in} = Q_1 - Q_2 = (T_1 - T_2)(s_1 - s_4)$. - ✅ **COP of Reversed Carnot Refrigerator** = $\dfrac{T_2}{T_1 - T_2}$ — **the most important formula**. - ✅ **COP of Reversed Carnot Heat Pump** = $\dfrac{T_1}{T_1 - T_2}$. - ✅ **Identity:** $\text{COP}_{hp} = \text{COP}_{ref} + 1$ — derivable directly from the two formulas. - ✅ **Always** use **Kelvin** for temperatures in all COP and heat ratio formulas. - ✅ **Reversed Carnot COP is the maximum** achievable — real cycles are always less efficient. - ✅ A higher temperature difference $T_1 - T_2$ **lowers** the COP — this is why cooling in summer (large $T$ difference) is harder than in winter. - ✅ **A COP greater than 1 is normal** for refrigerators — it does not violate any law of physics. - ✅ The **Second Law** identity $\dfrac{Q_1}{T_1} = \dfrac{Q_2}{T_2}$ is the **foundation** of all COP derivations. - ✅ The Reversed Carnot cycle is **purely theoretical** — real cycles use isenthalpic throttling (Vapour Compression) or thermal compression (Vapour Absorption) instead of isothermal processes. - ✅ **KTU 2024 Scheme CO Mapping:** This topic directly addresses **CO1** (Apply the laws of thermodynamics to reversible and irreversible processes) and forms the foundation for refrigeration cycle analysis in later modules.

topics.title: Performance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT502 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT ENGINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Performance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance** in thermodynamics refers to the quantitative measure of how effectively a heat engine, refrigerator, or thermodynamic system converts input energy (heat, fuel) into useful output (work, cooling effect). It is mathematically expressed through various efficiencies and specific output parameters that benchmark actual cycles against the theoretical Carnot (reversible) limit.

The performance of a thermodynamic system is characterized by **three primary indices**:

1. **Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{th}$)** – The fraction of total heat input converted to net work output.
2. **Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_{m}$)** – The ratio of indicated (theoretical) power to brake (actual shaft) power.
3. **Overall Efficiency ($\eta_{overall}$)** – The product of thermal and mechanical efficiencies, representing the actual conversion of fuel energy to useful shaft work.

Auxiliary performance indicators include **Mean Effective Pressure (MEP)**, **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)**, **Specific Work Output**, and **Heat Rate**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy – The Fuel-Efficient Car:**
> Imagine two cars starting from Kerala to Delhi. Car A burns 50 L of petrol and reaches Delhi. Car B burns 35 L and reaches the same destination. Clearly, Car B is more "thermally efficient" — it converts more of the fuel's chemical energy into useful motion (work) and wastes less as heat. The "performance" metric in thermodynamics is exactly this — measuring how *cleverly* an engine uses its fuel. The **Carnot cycle** is the "perfect driver" — no car in the real world can match it, but it sets the benchmark every engineer chases.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are **standard references** for performance calculations:

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \ \text{kJ/(kmol·K)}$
- **Specific Heat Ratio for Air:** $\gamma = 1.4$ (constant pressure to constant volume)
- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \ \text{kPa}$
- **Carnot Efficiency Benchmark:** $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ (where temperatures are in **Kelvin**)
- **Performance Unit Standards:**
  - Efficiency → Dimensionless (or %)
  - SFC → $\text{kg/(kW·h)}$
  - MEP → $\text{kPa}$ or $\text{bar}$
  - Power → $\text{kW}$ or $\text{hp}$ ($1 \ \text{hp} = 0.7457 \ \text{kW}$)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework – Step-by-Step Logic

The performance evaluation of any heat engine follows a rigorous **3-step analytical protocol**:

**Step 1: Identify the Cycle Type**
Determine whether the system operates on a **Carnot, Otto, Diesel, Brayton, or Rankine** cycle. Each cycle has unique pressure-volume (P-v) and temperature-entropy (T-s) characteristics that dictate its efficiency formula.

**Step 2: Apply Energy Balance (First Law)**
For a cyclic device, $\oint dQ = \oint dW$. The net work output equals the net heat input:
$$W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$$

**Step 3: Compute Performance Indices**
Using the derived $W_{net}$ and known $Q_{in}$, calculate the efficiency parameters and compare them to the Carnot limit operating between the same temperature reservoirs.

### 2.2 Detailed Logic Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Performance Cannot Exceed Carnot:**
> The second law of thermodynamics (Kelvin-Planck statement) prohibits any engine operating between two thermal reservoirs from exceeding the efficiency of a reversible (Carnot) engine. This is the **"why"** behind the Carnot benchmark — it is the theoretical ceiling of performance.

- **Heat Engine Performance** → Higher $T_H$ and lower $T_L$ improve efficiency.
- **Refrigerator Performance** → Measured by **Coefficient of Performance (COP)**.
- **Heat Pump Performance** → Also measured by COP, but $COP_{HP} = COP_{Ref} + 1$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 Module-1 exam-essential. Memorize the table structure, not just individual equations.**

| # | Performance Parameter | Formula | Units | Key Condition |
|---|----------------------|---------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$ | Dimensionless | All cycles |
| 2 | Carnot Efficiency (Heat Engine) | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Dimensionless | Reversible cycle only |
| 3 | COP of Refrigerator | $COP_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Dimensionless | $T$ in Kelvin |
| 4 | COP of Heat Pump | $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | Dimensionless | $T$ in Kelvin |
| 5 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{W_{brake}}{W_{indicated}}$ | Dimensionless | Real engines |
| 6 | Overall Efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{th} \times \eta_m$ | Dimensionless | Combined losses |
| 7 | Carnot COP Relation | $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ | Dimensionless | Always true |
| 8 | Mean Effective Pressure | $MEP = \dfrac{W_{net}}{V_1 - V_2}$ | $\text{kPa}$ | Reciprocating engines |
| 9 | Specific Fuel Consumption | $SFC = \dfrac{\dot{m}_f}{W_{brake}}$ | $\text{kg/(kW·h)}$ | IC engines |
| 10 | Power from MEP | $W = MEP \times L \times A \times N \times n$ | $\text{kW}$ | $n$ = no. of cylinders |
| 11 | Heat Input Relation | $Q_{in} = m \cdot c_v (T_3 - T_2)$ | $\text{kJ}$ | Otto cycle |
| 12 | Otto Cycle Efficiency | $\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ | Dimensionless | Constant volume cycle |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in Industry:**
> - **Automotive Sector:** Engineers chase higher $\eta_{th}$ to meet BS-VI / Euro-7 emission norms and improve fuel economy.
> - **Power Plants:** Rankine cycle performance determines the **heat rate** (kJ/kWh) — lower heat rate means lower coal consumption per MWh generated.
> - **HVAC Industry:** COP guides the selection of air conditioners (a typical split AC has $COP \approx 3.0$ to $4.0$).
> - **Jet Propulsion:** Brayton cycle performance (with afterburner) determines the **thrust-specific fuel consumption (TSFC)** of fighter aircraft.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Carnot Thermal Efficiency (Foundation of All Performance)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most critical derivation** in KTU Module 1. It appears in nearly every university exam either directly or as a sub-part.

**Starting Point:** Consider a reversible Carnot cycle operating between a hot reservoir at $T_H$ and a cold reservoir at $T_L$.

**Step 1: Net Work Output from the Cycle**

For a cyclic heat engine operating between two reservoirs, the net work is the difference between heat absorbed from the hot source and heat rejected to the cold sink:

$$
W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L
$$

**Step 2: Apply the First Law to the Cycle**

Since the working fluid returns to its initial state after one complete cycle, the change in internal energy is zero ($\Delta U = 0$). The first law therefore gives:

$$
Q_{net} = W_{net}
$$

**Step 3: Write the Thermal Efficiency Definition**

By definition, the thermal efficiency is the ratio of net work output to heat input:

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute the Net Work Expression**

Substituting $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$ from Step 1:

$$
\eta_{th} = \frac{Q_H - Q_L}{Q_H}
$$

**Step 5: Simplify the Expression**

Expanding the numerator over the common denominator:

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H}
$$

**Step 6: Apply the Carnot Reversibility Condition (Second Law)**

For a **reversible** Carnot cycle, the ratio of heat transfer to absolute temperature is constant (Clausius theorem). Hence:

$$
\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L}
$$

**Step 7: Rearrange the Reversibility Relation**

Solving for the ratio $\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H}$:

$$
\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}
$$

**Step 8: Final Substitution into the Efficiency Expression**

Substituting Step 7 into Step 5:

$$
\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Since $T_L < T_H$ for any real engine, the term $\dfrac{T_L}{T_H} < 1$, ensuring $\eta_{Carnot} > 0$. As $T_L \to 0$ K, $\eta_{Carnot} \to 1$ (100%) — but absolute zero is unreachable (third law of thermodynamics), so $100\%$ efficiency is impossible.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: COP of a Refrigerator (Performance of Refrigeration Cycles)

**Step 1: Define the Objective Function**

A refrigerator's performance is measured by how much heat $Q_L$ it extracts per unit of work input $W_{net}$:

$$
COP_R = \frac{\text{Desired Output}}{\text{Required Input}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}}
$$

**Step 2: Apply Energy Conservation (First Law)**

For a cyclic refrigerator, the energy balance gives:

$$
Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}
$$

**Step 3: Express the Net Work**

Rearranging the energy balance:

$$
W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L
$$

**Step 4: Substitute into the COP Definition**

$$
COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{Q_H - Q_L}
$$

**Step 5: Apply the Reversible Carnot Relation**

For a reversible refrigeration cycle:

$$
\frac{Q_L}{T_L} = \frac{Q_H}{T_H} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H}
$$

**Step 6: Divide Numerator and Denominator by $Q_L$**

$$
COP_R = \frac{1}{\frac{Q_H}{Q_L} - 1}
$$

**Step 7: Substitute the Temperature Ratio**

Using $Q_H / Q_L = T_H / T_L$:

$$
COP_R = \frac{1}{\frac{T_H}{T_L} - 1} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{COP_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Relationship Between Heat Pump and Refrigerator COP

**Step 1: Write Both Definitions**

$$
COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} \quad \text{and} \quad COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}}
$$

**Step 2: Add the Two Numerator Energies**

Using $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$:

$$
Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}
$$

**Step 3: Divide Both Sides by $W_{net}$**

$$
\frac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}} + 1
$$

**Step 4: Substitute the COP Definitions**

$$
COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1}
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A heat engine operates between a hot reservoir at $T_H = 800 \ \text{K}$ and a cold reservoir at $T_L = 300 \ \text{K}$. It receives $Q_H = 1000 \ \text{kJ}$ of heat. Determine: (a) Carnot efficiency, (b) Net work output, (c) Heat rejected.

**Solution:**

**(a) Carnot Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{800}
$$

$$
\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625 = 62.5\%
$$

**(b) Net Work Output:**

$$
W_{net} = \eta_{Carnot} \times Q_H = 0.625 \times 1000
$$

$$
W_{net} = 625 \ \text{kJ}
$$

**(c) Heat Rejected to Cold Sink:**

$$
Q_L = Q_H - W_{net} = 1000 - 625
$$

$$
Q_L = 375 \ \text{kJ}
$$

**Verification (using temperatures):**

$$
\frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \frac{T_L}{T_H} = \frac{300}{800} = 0.375
$$

$$
Q_L = 0.375 \times 1000 = 375 \ \text{kJ} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture – Performance Evaluation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Identify System Type] --> stepB{Heat Engine or Refrigerator?}
    stepB -->|Heat Engine| stepC[Measure Q_in from Hot Source]
    stepB -->|Refrigerator| stepD[Measure Q_L from Cold Space]
    stepC --> stepE[Calculate Net Work W_net]
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF[Compute Thermal Efficiency eta_th]
    stepF --> stepG[Compute Carnot Benchmark eta_Carnot]
    stepG --> stepH{eta_th vs eta_Carnot}
    stepH -->|eta_th less than eta_Carnot| stepI[Valid Engine - 2nd Law Satisfied]
    stepH -->|eta_th greater than eta_Carnot| stepJ[Impossible - Reject Result]
    stepI --> stepK[Report Overall Performance]
    stepJ --> stepK
    stepK --> endA[Final Performance Report]
```

### 4.2 Performance Parameter Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Performance of Thermodynamic System] --> nodeB[Thermal Performance]
    rootA --> nodeC[Mechanical Performance]
    rootA --> nodeD[Economic Performance]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Thermal Efficiency]
    nodeB --> nodeF[COP for Refrigerator]
    nodeB --> nodeG[COP for Heat Pump]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Mechanical Efficiency]
    nodeC --> nodeI[Volumetric Efficiency]
    nodeD --> nodeJ[Specific Fuel Consumption]
    nodeD --> nodeK[Heat Rate kJ per kWh]
    nodeE --> nodeL[Compare with Carnot Limit]
    nodeH --> nodeM[Brake vs Indicated Power]
```

### 4.3 Carnot Cycle T-s Diagram (Schematic Representation)

```mermaid
graph LR
    state1[State 1 - High T High S] -->|Isothermal Expansion| state2[State 2 - T_H Constant S increases]
    state2 -->|Isentropic Expansion| state3[State 3 - Low T Low S]
    state3 -->|Isothermal Compression| state4[State 4 - T_L Constant S decreases]
    state4 -->|Isentropic Compression| state1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic:** The enclosed area on the T-s diagram represents the **net work output** of the cycle. The Carnot cycle forms a perfect rectangle on the T-s plane — any real cycle inscribed within this rectangle automatically has lower performance.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define the term **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** for a refrigerator. How is it different from the COP of a heat pump?

**Model Answer:**
The COP of a refrigerator is defined as the ratio of the desired cooling effect (heat extracted from the cold space) to the work input required:

$$
COP_R = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}}
$$

The COP of a heat pump uses the heat delivered to the hot space as the desired output:

$$
COP_{HP} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{net}}
$$

The two are related by: $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$. For a reversible cycle: $COP_R = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ and $COP_{HP} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** State and explain the **Kelvin-Planck statement** of the second law of thermodynamics.

**Model Answer:**
The Kelvin-Planck statement states that:
> *"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than the extraction of heat from a single reservoir and the performance of an equivalent amount of work."*

In other words, no heat engine can convert **all** the heat input from a single thermal reservoir into work — some heat **must** always be rejected to a lower-temperature sink. This is why the thermal efficiency of any real engine is always strictly less than 100%, and the Carnot efficiency is the theoretical maximum.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the thermal efficiency of a Carnot cycle operating between two thermal reservoirs at temperatures $T_H$ and $T_L$ (in Kelvin). **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A Carnot refrigerator operates between a cold space at $-10^\circ \text{C}$ and the surroundings at $35^\circ \text{C}$. The refrigerator extracts $1200 \ \text{kJ/min}$ of heat from the cold space. Calculate: (i) COP of the refrigerator, (ii) Power input to the compressor in kW, (iii) Heat rejected to the surroundings per minute. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Derivation of Carnot Thermal Efficiency** *(See Section 3.1 for full derivation)*

The final result is:

$$
\boxed{\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}}
$$

Key steps: (1) $W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$, (2) $\eta = W_{net}/Q_H$, (3) $\eta = 1 - Q_L/Q_H$, (4) Reversibility gives $Q_L/Q_H = T_L/T_H$.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $T_L = -10 + 273 = 263 \ \text{K}$
- $T_H = 35 + 273 = 308 \ \text{K}$
- $Q_L = 1200 \ \text{kJ/min} = 20 \ \text{kJ/s}$

**(i) COP of the Refrigerator:**

$$
COP_R = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263}{308 - 263}
$$

$$
COP_R = \frac{263}{45} = 5.844
$$

**(ii) Power Input to Compressor:**

Since $COP_R = Q_L / W_{net}$:

$$
W_{net} = \frac{Q_L}{COP_R} = \frac{20}{5.844}
$$

$$
W_{net} = 3.422 \ \text{kW}
$$

**(iii) Heat Rejected to Surroundings:**

Using energy balance $Q_H = Q_L + W_{net}$:

$$
Q_H = 1200 + (3.422 \times 60) = 1200 + 205.32
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_H = 1405.32 \ \text{kJ/min}}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) - Alternative Choice**

**(a)** State the **Carnot theorems** of thermodynamics. Explain why no real engine can have an efficiency greater than that of a Carnot engine operating between the same temperature limits. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A heat engine receives $500 \ \text{kJ}$ of heat from a source at $900 \ \text{K}$ and rejects $250 \ \text{kJ}$ to a sink at $300 \ \text{K}$. Determine: (i) Thermal efficiency, (ii) Carnot efficiency, (iii) Whether the engine violates the second law. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Carnot Theorems:**

**Theorem 1:** The efficiency of a reversible heat engine operating between two given thermal reservoirs is independent of the working substance and depends only on the reservoir temperatures.

**Theorem 2:** No irreversible heat engine operating between two given reservoirs can be more efficient than a reversible engine operating between the same two reservoirs.

**Explanation of Real-Engine Limit:** The Kelvin-Planck statement directly implies that heat rejection is mandatory. In any real (irreversible) engine, internal irreversibilities such as friction, turbulence, and heat leakage cause additional entropy generation, which manifests as additional heat rejection. Therefore, real engines always have $\eta_{real} < \eta_{Carnot}$.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $Q_H = 500 \ \text{kJ}$, $Q_L = 250 \ \text{kJ}$
- $T_H = 900 \ \text{K}$, $T_L = 300 \ \text{K}$

**(i) Thermal Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{Q_L}{Q_H} = 1 - \frac{250}{500} = 0.5 = 50\%
$$

**(ii) Carnot Efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{900} = 0.6667 = 66.67\%
$$

**(iii) Second Law Check:**

Since $\eta_{th} = 50\% < \eta_{Carnot} = 66.67\%$, the engine **does not violate** the second law. The engine is feasible (though it is operating below the reversible limit, indicating it has irreversibilities).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Temperature Units:** Students frequently use Celsius instead of Kelvin. **Always convert to Kelvin** before any efficiency or COP calculation. Marks are cut even for one missing conversion.
> 2. **Sign Convention:** $Q_L$ in Carnot cycle is heat *rejected* (out of system). Don't confuse it with $Q_{in}$.
> 3. **Power vs Energy:** In numerical problems, $Q_L$ may be given in kJ/min — convert to kW (i.e., kJ/s) before dividing.
> 4. **Verifying the Second Law:** Always explicitly state whether $\eta_{th} < \eta_{Carnot}$ and conclude whether the result is feasible. Examiners award a full mark for this concluding remark.
> 5. **COP vs Efficiency:** Refrigerator and heat pump do NOT have "efficiency" — they have "COP." Using "efficiency of a refrigerator" loses a mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "Performance":**

- **Thermal Efficiency Formula:** $\eta_{th} = W_{net} / Q_{in} = 1 - Q_L / Q_H$
- **Carnot Efficiency:** $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_L / T_H$ (Temperatures **must** be in Kelvin)
- **COP of Refrigerator:** $COP_R = T_L / (T_H - T_L)$ — dimensionless, typically between 2 and 6
- **COP of Heat Pump:** $COP_{HP} = T_H / (T_H - T_L)$
- **Key Identity:** $COP_{HP} = COP_R + 1$ (always)
- **Carnot Theorems:** (1) Efficiency is independent of working fluid, (2) No irreversible engine beats the Carnot limit
- **Second Law (Kelvin-Planck):** No engine can convert heat from a single reservoir entirely into work
- **Second Law (Clausius):** Heat cannot spontaneously flow from cold to hot without external work
- **Overall Efficiency:** $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{th} \times \eta_{m}$ — accounts for friction losses
- **Mean Effective Pressure (MEP):** A fictitious constant pressure that, if acted on the piston during the full stroke, would produce the same work as the actual cycle
- **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC):** Lower SFC = better performance; measured in $\text{kg/(kW·h)}$
- **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Real engines typically achieve $30\%$ to $50\%$ of the Carnot efficiency due to irreversibilities
- **Numerical Trap:** Always verify $\eta_{th} \le \eta_{Carnot}$ — if not, your calculation is wrong
- **Units Quick Reference:** $1 \ \text{kW} = 1 \ \text{kJ/s}$, $1 \ \text{hp} = 0.7457 \ \text{kW}$, $1 \ \text{bar} = 100 \ \text{kPa}$

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clausius Inequality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT502 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT ENGINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Clausius Inequality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clausius Inequality (KTU Board-Standard Definition):**
> For any thermodynamic system undergoing a **cyclic process** (whether reversible or irreversible), the cyclic integral of the heat transfer divided by the absolute temperature of the boundary at which the transfer occurs is **less than or equal to zero**. Mathematically:
> $$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_{boundary}} \le 0$$
> Equality holds if and only if the cycle is **completely reversible** (i.e., internally reversible with no dissipative effects).

This statement is the **mathematical backbone of the Second Law of Thermodynamics** and serves as the parent theorem from which the property of **Entropy** is formally derived.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are a **"heat accountant"** tracking energy flows in a cyclic process (a process that returns to its starting state, like an engine running in a loop).

- Every time heat $\delta Q$ crosses the system boundary at temperature $T$, you record it as a tiny entry in your ledger: $\frac{\delta Q}{T}$.
- The Clausius Inequality tells you that, **no matter what you do** (reversible or irreversible), when you sum up (integrate) all these entries over one complete loop, your net balance will **never be positive**.
- It can only be **zero (reversible)** or **negative (irreversible)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Broken Dam":**
> Water always flows downhill (Second Law). If you carry water uphill, the total "potential energy gained" is *less than or equal to* the energy you spent carrying it — you can never gain extra for free. Similarly, heat naturally flows from hot to cold, and the Clausius Inequality says that the net "weighted heat" transferred around any cycle can never come out as a net gain; nature always takes its toll through friction, turbulence, and unresisted expansion.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Absolute Temperature Scale:** Kelvin (**K**) — the *only* thermodynamically valid scale; Celsius or Fahrenheit will produce incorrect signs.
- **Sign Convention:** $\delta Q > 0$ for heat supplied **TO** the system (KTU 2024 standard).
- **Equality Conditions:** Both *internally reversible* **AND** *externally reversible* (i.e., heat transfer across finite temperature differences is zero).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The temperature in the denominator is the **boundary temperature** $T_b$ where heat crosses, **NOT** the system temperature. For a reversible process, $T_b = T_{system}$. For an irreversible process, the two may differ.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Breakdown — The "Why" and "How"

The Clausius Inequality emerges from a synthesis of the **Kelvin-Planck** and **Clausius statements** of the Second Law. Here is the structured logical flow:

### Step 1 — Start with Two Reversible Carnot Engines
- Take an **arbitrary cycle** operating between a series of reservoirs at temperatures $T_i$.
