- Between each reservoir and the system, place a **reversible Carnot engine** that absorbs $\delta Q_i$ from the reservoir and rejects $\delta Q_{0,i}$ to a common reference reservoir at $T_0$.

### Step 2 — Apply the Carnot Efficiency
- For each reversible Carnot engine:
$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_0}{T_i} = \frac{W_i}{\delta Q_i}$$

### Step 3 — Superpose All Engines
- The combined system (arbitrary cycle + all Carnot engines) operates in a cycle.
- By the Kelvin-Planck statement, it cannot produce net work while exchanging heat with a single reservoir.
- Therefore, the total work output of the composite system must be $\le 0$.

### Step 4 — Algebraic Reduction
- Summing the work outputs and applying the constraint $W_{total} \le 0$ directly yields the Clausius Inequality.

### Step 5 — Equivalence and Reversibility Condition
- If the original cycle is reversible, the inequality sign **reverses** by running it backward.
- The only way both directions can hold simultaneously is if the integral equals exactly **zero**.

## 2.2 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Power Plants:** Quantifies the maximum possible efficiency — you can never beat Carnot because any irreversibility makes the Clausius integral *strictly negative*.
- **Refrigeration & HVAC:** Sets the theoretical lower bound on compressor work.
- **Chemical Engineering:** Foundation of **exergy (availability) analysis** — the lost work is precisely the negative of the Clausius integral.
- **Entropy Generation:** The inequality is the direct statement of the **Second Law** in integral form.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Table 2.1 — High-Yield Formulas for Clausius Inequality**

| **Formula** | **Name / Condition** | **Units** | **Sign Convention** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b} \le 0$ | Clausius Inequality (general cycle) | Dimensionless (J/K per J) | $\le 0$ always |
| $\oint \left(\dfrac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{rev} = 0$ | For a **reversible** cycle | Dimensionless | Equals **0** |
| $\oint \left(\dfrac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{irr} < 0$ | For an **irreversible** cycle | Dimensionless | Strictly **< 0** |
| $\Delta S_{sys} = \displaystyle\int_{1}^{2} \left(\dfrac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{rev}$ | Entropy change (reversible path) | J/K | System property |
| $S_{gen} = \displaystyle\int_{1}^{2} \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b} + \Delta S_{sys}$ | Entropy generation (irreversible) | J/K | $\ge 0$ (2nd Law) |
| $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Carnot (reversible) efficiency | — | Upper bound |
| $T_b = T_{sys}$ | Reversible heat transfer condition | K | Equality holds |

## 2.4 Core Logical Insight

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Clausius Inequality is the **only** integral statement from which both the existence of entropy (as a property) and the direction of spontaneous processes can be rigorously derived. KTU frequently tests this through 3-mark "define" or "state" questions, and 14-mark derivation/numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Clausius Inequality

### 3.1.1 Setup of the Composite System

Consider an arbitrary system undergoing one cycle while exchanging heat $\delta Q_i$ with reservoirs at temperatures $T_i$ (where $T_i$ is the boundary temperature of the system). We introduce $n$ **reversible Carnot engines**, each operating between the $i$-th reservoir and a common low-temperature reservoir at $T_0$.

Each Carnot engine $i$:
- Receives heat $\delta Q_{0,i}$ from the common reservoir at $T_0$.
- Rejects heat $\delta Q_i$ to the system (or receives it from the system, depending on sign).
- Produces work $\delta W_i$.

### 3.1.2 Apply the Reversible Carnot Efficiency Relation

For each reversible Carnot engine, the ratio of heat transfers equals the ratio of absolute temperatures:

$$\frac{\delta Q_i}{T_i} = \frac{\delta Q_{0,i}}{T_0}$$

Rearranging to isolate the heat dumped into the reference reservoir:

$$\delta Q_{0,i} = \frac{T_0}{T_i} \, \delta Q_i$$

### 3.1.3 Sum Over All Engines

The total heat rejected to the reference reservoir at $T_0$ from the entire bank of $n$ Carnot engines is:

$$\delta Q_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \delta Q_{0,i} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{T_0}{T_i} \, \delta Q_i$$

Factoring out the constant $T_0$:

$$\delta Q_0 = T_0 \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\delta Q_i}{T_i}$$

### 3.1.4 Apply the Kelvin-Planck Statement

The composite system (original cycle + all Carnot engines) exchanges heat **only** with the single reservoir at $T_0$ (since the reservoirs at $T_i$ are balanced — the system and Carnot engine exchange equal and opposite $\delta Q_i$).

By the **Kelvin-Planck statement** of the Second Law, no system operating in a cycle can produce net work while exchanging heat with a single reservoir. Since the Carnot engines are reversible, they produce maximum possible work. The composite system as a whole cannot have positive work output:

$$W_{composite} = W_{original\,cycle} + \sum_{i} \delta W_i \le 0$$

Because each Carnot engine operates reversibly, its work is the difference between heat absorbed at $T_0$ and heat rejected at $T_i$:

$$\delta W_i = \delta Q_{0,i} - \delta Q_i = \frac{T_0}{T_i}\,\delta Q_i - \delta Q_i = \left(\frac{T_0}{T_i} - 1\right)\delta Q_i$$

### 3.1.5 Take the Limiting Case (Continuous Reservoirs)

Let the number of reservoirs $n \to \infty$ and the heat transfers $\delta Q_i \to \delta Q$ become infinitesimally small. The sum becomes a cyclic integral:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} = \frac{\delta Q_0}{T_0}$$

From the Kelvin-Planck constraint, $\delta Q_0 \le 0$ (no net heat can be extracted from a single reservoir and converted fully to work). Therefore:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} \le 0$$

This is the **Clausius Inequality**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Equivalence Proof: Reversible Cycle ⇒ Equality

**Claim:** If the original cycle is reversible, then $\oint \left(\dfrac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{rev} = 0$.

**Proof by Contradiction / Symmetry:**

Suppose the original cycle is reversible and produces $\oint \left(\dfrac{\delta Q}{T}\right) < 0$. Now run the **same** cycle in **reverse** direction. The heat transfers reverse sign, so:

$$\oint \left(\frac{\delta Q_{reverse}}{T}\right) > 0$$

But a reversed reversible cycle is itself a valid reversible cycle, and we just derived that **all** cycles must satisfy $\le 0$. This is a contradiction unless:

$$\oint \left(\frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{rev} = 0 \qquad \blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Entropy as a Property — Consequence of the Inequality

Since the cyclic integral vanishes for any reversible cycle, the integral $\int_{1}^{2} \left(\dfrac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{rev}$ is **path-independent**. This allows the definition of a new property:

$$S_2 - S_1 = \int_{1}^{2} \left(\frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{rev}$$

This is the **fundamental definition of entropy** (per unit mass: $s$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Matrix — How Each Thermodynamic Quantity Relates:**

| **Quantity** | **Definition** | **Inequality Form** | **Equality Form** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Clausius Integral | $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b}$ | $\le 0$ (general) | $= 0$ (reversible) |
| Entropy Change | $\Delta S = \int \dfrac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$ | Path-independent (state fn) | Always defined via reversible path |
| Entropy Generation | $S_{gen} = \sum \dfrac{Q_k}{T_k} + \Delta S_{sys}$ | $\ge 0$ (2nd Law) | $= 0$ (reversible process) |
| Lost Work | $W_{lost} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$ | $\ge 0$ | $= 0$ (reversible) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Logical Flow of the Clausius Inequality Derivation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Arbitrary Cyclic System] --> B[System exchanges heat dQ_i with reservoir i at T_i]
    B --> C[Insert Reversible Carnot Engine i between reservoir i and reference reservoir at T_0]
    C --> D[Apply Carnot Heat Ratio: dQ_i / T_i = dQ_0_i / T_0]
    D --> E[Sum over all i reservoirs: dQ_0 = T_0 * sum of dQ_i / T_i]
    E --> F[Composite system: original cycle + all Carnot engines]
    F --> G[Apply Kelvin-Planck Statement: W_composite is less than or equal to 0]
    G --> H[Deduce: dQ_0 is less than or equal to 0]
    H --> I[Take continuum limit: sum becomes cyclic integral]
    I --> J[FINAL: Cyclic integral of dQ over T_b is less than or equal to 0]
    J --> K{Reversible Cycle?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Cyclic integral = 0, Entropy is a property]
    K -- No --> M[Cyclic integral less than 0, Entropy is generated]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Reversible vs Irreversible Cycle on T–S Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REVCYC["REVERSIBLE CYCLE (Area enclosed = Net Heat = Net Work)"]
        R1[State 1] -->|Isothermal heat addition| R2[State 2]
        R2 -->|Reversible adiabatic expansion| R3[State 3]
        R3 -->|Isothermal heat rejection| R4[State 4]
        R4 -->|Reversible adiabatic compression| R1
    end
    subgraph IRRCYC["IRREVERSIBLE CYCLE (Same endpoints, larger area = more entropy generated)"]
        I1[State 1] -->|Irreversible heat transfer| I2[State 2 prime]
        I2 -->|Unresisted expansion| I3[State 3 prime]
        I3 -->|Irreversible heat rejection| I4[State 4 prime]
        I4 -->|Friction losses| I1
    end
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Relationship Between Clausius Inequality, Entropy, and Second Law

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SL[Second Law of Thermodynamics] --> CL[Clausius Inequality]
    CL --> EQ1[Equality Case: Reversible Cycle]
    CL --> EQ2[Inequality Case: Irreversible Cycle]
    EQ1 --> ENTP[Entropy defined as State Function]
    EQ2 --> SGEN[Entropy Generation is greater than 0]
    ENTP --> APPL1[Exergy / Availability Analysis]
    SGEN --> APPL2[Lost Work Quantification]
    APPL1 --> REAL[Real Power Plant Design]
    APPL2 --> REAL
```

## 4.4 Diagram Interpretation Notes

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematics:**
> - In the **reversible cycle**, the path on a T–S diagram is closed and the enclosed area represents net work done. The Clausius integral sums to exactly **zero** because positive contributions (heat in) are exactly cancelled by negative ones (heat out).
> - In the **irreversible cycle**, internal entropy generation makes the path "bulge" outward. The integral becomes **strictly negative**, quantifying the *irreversibility* or *lost work*.
> - The block diagram shows the **logical hierarchy**: the Second Law *implies* the Clausius Inequality, which *implies* entropy as a state function, which *enables* exergy analysis for real engineering design.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State the Clausius Inequality. What is its significance in thermodynamics?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The Clausius Inequality states that for any system executing a cyclic process, the cyclic integral of the heat transfer divided by the absolute temperature of the boundary is always less than or equal to zero:
> $$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} \le 0$$
> **Significance:**
> 1. It is the **mathematical statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics** in integral form.
> 2. It establishes the existence of **entropy as a thermodynamic property** (state function), since for reversible cycles the integral is exactly zero, making path-independent integration possible.
> 3. It provides the criterion for **distinguishing reversible from irreversible** processes: equality ⇒ reversible; strict inequality ⇒ irreversible with $S_{gen} > 0$.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Why is the temperature in the Clausius Inequality taken as the boundary temperature and not the system temperature?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The temperature in the denominator must be the **boundary temperature** $T_b$ at which heat actually crosses the system boundary. This is because:
> 1. For an **irreversible process**, the system itself may not have a single uniform temperature (e.g., during free expansion), but the boundary is where heat transfer physically occurs and is well-defined.
> 2. The Clausius Inequality is a statement about **real physical heat transfer events**, which take place at the boundary.
> 3. For a **reversible process**, the boundary temperature equals the system temperature, so the distinction vanishes and entropy change can be computed using system temperature.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Clausius Inequality for a general cyclic process using the Kelvin-Planck statement of the Second Law. Clearly state all assumptions.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A reversible heat engine operates between a hot reservoir at **800 K** and a cold reservoir at **300 K**. The engine rejects **500 kJ** of heat to the cold reservoir per cycle. Calculate:
- (i) The efficiency of the engine.
- (ii) The heat supplied from the hot reservoir.
- (iii) The work output per cycle.

Verify that the Clausius Integral for this cycle equals zero.

---

#### Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation [7 Marks]:**

Refer to **Section 3.1** above for the exhaustive step-by-step derivation. Key checkpoints for the examiner:

1. **Setup (1 mark):** Arbitrary cycle + $n$ reversible Carnot engines + reference reservoir at $T_0$.
2. **Carnot relation (2 marks):** $\dfrac{\delta Q_i}{T_i} = \dfrac{\delta Q_{0,i}}{T_0}$, hence $\delta Q_{0,i} = \dfrac{T_0}{T_i} \delta Q_i$.
3. **Summation (1 mark):** $\delta Q_0 = T_0 \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{\delta Q_i}{T_i}$.
4. **Kelvin-Planck application (2 marks):** $W_{composite} \le 0 \Rightarrow \delta Q_0 \le 0$.
5. **Limiting case + final inequality (1 mark):** $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b} \le 0$.

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 Marks]:**

Given: $T_H = 800$ K, $T_L = 300$ K, $Q_L = 500$ kJ (heat rejected).

**(i) Efficiency of the reversible (Carnot) engine:**

$$\eta_{rev} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{800} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{rev} = 62.5\%}$$

**(ii) Heat supplied from the hot reservoir:**

For a reversible engine, $\dfrac{Q_L}{Q_H} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$:

$$Q_H = Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H}{T_L} = 500 \times \frac{800}{300} = 500 \times 2.6667$$

$$\boxed{Q_H = 1333.33 \text{ kJ}}$$

**(iii) Work output per cycle:**

By the First Law applied to a cyclic heat engine, $W = Q_H - Q_L$:

$$W = 1333.33 - 500 = 833.33 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\boxed{W = 833.33 \text{ kJ per cycle}}$$

**Verification of the Clausius Integral:**

For a reversible cycle, the integral over the hot leg (heat in) and cold leg (heat out) is:

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = \frac{Q_H}{T_H} - \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = \frac{1333.33}{800} - \frac{500}{300}$$

$$= 1.6667 - 1.6667 = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

This confirms the **equality case** of the Clausius Inequality for a reversible cycle.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Show that for a reversible cyclic process, the Clausius Integral equals zero, and use this result to establish that **entropy is a thermodynamic property (state function)**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An irreversible heat engine operates between the same two reservoirs (800 K and 300 K) as in Question A. It produces **600 kJ** of work per cycle while receiving **2000 kJ** of heat from the hot reservoir. Determine:
- (i) The efficiency of this irreversible engine.
- (ii) The Clausius Integral for this cycle.
- (iii) The total entropy generation per cycle, and interpret the result.

---

#### Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Reversibility ⇒ Entropy Property [7 Marks]:**

1. **Statement of equality (2 marks):** For a reversible cycle,
$$\oint \left(\frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{rev} = 0$$

2. **Path-independence argument (3 marks):** Consider two reversible paths A and B between the same states 1 and 2. Construct a composite cycle: 1 → 2 via path A, then 2 → 1 via path B (reversed). Apply the Clausius Integral:
$$\int_{1}^{2} \left(\frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{A} + \int_{2}^{1} \left(\frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{B} = 0$$
Reversing the limits of the second integral:
$$\int_{1}^{2} \left(\frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{A} = \int_{1}^{2} \left(\frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{B}$$
This shows the integral is **path-independent** ⇒ entropy is a state function.

3. **Definition of entropy (2 marks):**
$$S_2 - S_1 = \int_{1}^{2} \left(\frac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{rev}$$
Since the right side depends only on states 1 and 2, $S$ is a property.

**Part (b) — Numerical on Irreversible Engine [7 Marks]:**

Given: $T_H = 800$ K, $T_L = 300$ K, $W = 600$ kJ, $Q_H = 2000$ kJ.

**(i) Efficiency of the irreversible engine:**

$$\eta_{irr} = \frac{W}{Q_H} = \frac{600}{2000} = 0.30 = 30\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{irr} = 30\%}$$

**Comparison:** $\eta_{irr} = 30\% < \eta_{rev} = 62.5\%$ — the Second Law is **not violated** because the engine is irreversible.

**(ii) Clausius Integral for this cycle:**

First, find $Q_L$ from the First Law: $Q_L = Q_H - W = 2000 - 600 = 1400$ kJ.

$$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = \frac{Q_H}{T_H} - \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = \frac{2000}{800} - \frac{1400}{300}$$

$$= 2.500 - 4.6667 = -2.1667 \text{ kJ/K}$$

$$\boxed{\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = -2.1667 \text{ kJ/K} < 0}$$

This confirms the **strict inequality case** of the Clausius Inequality.

**(iii) Total entropy generation per cycle:**

$$S_{gen} = -\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T_b} = -(-2.1667) = 2.1667 \text{ kJ/K}$$

$$\boxed{S_{gen} = 2.1667 \text{ kJ/K} > 0}$$

**Interpretation:** The positive entropy generation is the *thermodynamic cost* of irreversibility (friction, finite temperature difference heat transfer, unresisted expansion, etc.). The lost work is:

$$W_{lost} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen} = 300 \times 2.1667 = 650 \text{ kJ}$$

This lost work represents the gap between the actual work (600 kJ) and the **maximum possible** (reversible) work (1250 kJ, which is $62.5\% \times 2000$).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 1. **Using system temperature instead of boundary temperature** in the Clausius integral for irreversible processes — *deduct 1 mark*.
> 2. **Forgetting the sign convention**: $Q_L$ should appear with a **negative sign** in the integral since heat is *rejected* (leaves the system). Incorrect sign handling is the #1 reason for wrong numerical answers.
> 3. **Confusing entropy change with entropy generation**: $\Delta S_{sys}$ is a property change (can be +ve, −ve, or zero); $S_{gen} \ge 0$ is **always non-negative** by the Second Law.
> 4. **Not taking the continuum limit** in the derivation — many students stop at the sum and lose 1 mark for incomplete final step.
> 5. **Using °C instead of K** — this is an instant **zero** for the numerical answer because the ratio $T_L/T_H$ is dimensionally meaningless otherwise.
> 6. **Drawing a Carnot cycle on a P–V diagram instead of a T–S diagram** when asked to verify the Clausius integral — marks deducted for not using the most appropriate diagram.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Clausius Inequality**

- **Core Statement:** $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b} \le 0$ for any cycle.
- **Equality** holds **iff** cycle is **completely reversible**.
- **Strict inequality** ($< 0$) indicates **irreversibility** with $S_{gen} > 0$.
- **Temperature in denominator = boundary temperature** (not system temperature for irreversible cases).
- **Only Kelvin (K)** is valid in the formula — never Celsius or Fahrenheit.
- **Derivation hinges on Kelvin-Planck statement** + bank of reversible Carnot engines + reference reservoir.
- **Entropy defined** as $dS = \left(\dfrac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_{rev}$ — a direct consequence of the equality case.
- **Entropy generation:** $S_{gen} = -\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b} \ge 0$.
- **Lost work:** $W_{lost} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$ — the gap between actual and reversible (maximum) work.
- **Carnot efficiency** is the **upper bound** on any heat engine operating between two reservoirs: $\eta_{th,max} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$.
- **KTU 2024 hot topics**: State the inequality (3M), derive it (14M), verify via numerical (14M), distinguish reversible vs irreversible using the integral sign (3M or 7M).
- **Always show units** in numericals: heat in kJ, entropy generation in kJ/K, work in kJ.
- **Trick question to watch:** "Is the Clausius Inequality valid for open systems?" — No, the standard form applies to **closed systems executing a cycle**. For open systems, the inequality is generalized using **flow exergy** concepts (covered in Module 4).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of Increase of Entropy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT502 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT ENGINES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principle of Increase of Entropy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PRINCIPLE OF INCREASE OF ENTROPY (Clausius Statement)**
> *"The entropy of an isolated system undergoing any process (reversible or irreversible) always increases, or in the limiting case of a reversible (ideal) process, remains constant. It can never decrease."*

Mathematically, for any process occurring in an isolated system:

$$\Delta S_{isolated} \geq 0$$

Where the equality sign ($\Delta S = 0$) holds **only for a totally reversible (frictionless, quasi-static) process**, and the strict inequality ($\Delta S > 0$) holds for **any spontaneous, real (irreversible) process** such as friction, unrestrained expansion, heat transfer across a finite temperature difference, mixing, or chemical reaction.

The principle is also commonly known as the **Second Law of Thermodynamics in Entropy Form**, and is universally applied in KTU board problems to determine the **direction of natural processes** and to calculate the **entropy generation number** $N_s = \dfrac{S_{gen}}{m}$ or the **lost work** $W_{lost} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Milk in Coffee" Experiment**
> Imagine dropping a single drop of black ink into a glass of clear water. Within seconds, the ink spreads spontaneously and uniformly throughout the water. You will **never** observe the ink spontaneously re-collecting into a single drop, no matter how long you wait. The system's *disorder* (entropy) has increased. To restore order, you would need external work (e.g., a complex filtration process), which itself creates even more disorder elsewhere in the universe.

| Analogy Element | Thermodynamic Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Glass of water + ink drop | Isolated system (no mass/energy exchange) |
| Ink spreading spontaneously | Irreversible process |
| Final uniform mixture | State of maximum entropy (equilibrium) |
| Reverse (concentrating ink) | Violates Second Law; impossible naturally |

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following standard values are universally used in KTU 2024 numerical problems:

- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ kJ/(kmol·K)}$
- **Standard Reference Temperature:** $T_0 = 298.15 \text{ K}$ (for dead-state problems)
- **Entropy Units:** $\text{kJ/K}$ (absolute) or $\text{kJ/(kg·K)}$ (specific)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Tested):**
> - $\Delta S \geq 0$ applies **only to isolated systems** or equivalently to the **universe** (system + surroundings).
> - For a **non-isolated system**, $\Delta S_{system}$ can be **positive, negative, or zero** (e.g., a refrigerator's working fluid loses entropy while the surroundings gain more).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Building from First Principles

The Principle of Increase of Entropy is a direct consequence of the **Clausius Inequality**, which itself is derived from the **Second Law of Thermodynamics**. The logical chain is:

1. **Clausius Statement of Second Law:** *"Heat cannot, of itself, flow from a colder body to a hotter body."*
2. **Mathematical Encoding (Clausius Inequality):** For any cyclic process, $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T} \leq 0$.
3. **Equality holds for reversible cycles; inequality for irreversible cycles.**
4. Defining entropy via a reversible path: $dS = \dfrac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$.
5. Combining the definition with the inequality yields the **Principle of Increase of Entropy**.

### Step-by-Step Logical Deduction

- **Step 1 — Define entropy differential:** For any infinitesimal reversible change of a closed system, $\delta Q_{rev} = T \, dS$.
- **Step 2 — Compare actual heat to reversible heat:** For an irreversible process, the actual heat transfer is **less than** the reversible heat transfer at the same states: $\delta Q_{actual} < \delta Q_{rev}$.
- **Step 3 — Form the inequality:** $\dfrac{\delta Q}{T} \leq dS \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad dS \geq \dfrac{\delta Q}{T}$.
- **Step 4 — Specialize to isolated system:** An isolated system exchanges **no heat** with surroundings, so $\delta Q = 0$. Therefore $dS \geq 0$.
- **Step 5 — Conclusion:** $\Delta S_{isolated} \geq 0$. This is the Principle of Increase of Entropy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in engineering?**
> The principle is the foundation for calculating the **maximum possible work**, **exergy (availability)**, and **lost work** in real engineering systems such as turbines, compressors, nozzles, and heat exchangers. Every KTU thermodynamics problem involving "irreversibility" or "lost work" uses this principle.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — every row is a board-exam scoring opportunity.**

| # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Boundary Condition / Process | SI Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Clausius Inequality | $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b} \leq 0$ | Any cyclic process | kJ/K |
| 2 | Entropy Definition | $dS = \dfrac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$ | Reversible path only | kJ/K |
| 3 | General Entropy Inequality | $dS \geq \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b}$ | Any process (rev. or irrev.) | kJ/K |
| 4 | **Principle of Increase of Entropy** | $\Delta S_{isolated} \geq 0$ | Isolated system only | kJ/K |
| 5 | Entropy of Universe | $\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_{sys} + \Delta S_{surr} \geq 0$ | Always true | kJ/K |
| 6 | Entropy Generation | $S_{gen} = \Delta S_{sys} - \sum \dfrac{Q_k}{T_k}$ | $S_{gen} \geq 0$ | kJ/K |
| 7 | Reversible Process (Tds Equations) | $T \, dS = dU + P \, dV$ | Closed system, reversible | kJ |
| 8 | Ideal Gas — T-V relation | $\Delta s = C_v \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right) + R \ln\!\left(\dfrac{v_2}{v_1}\right)$ | Ideal gas | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 9 | Ideal Gas — T-P relation | $\Delta s = C_p \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | Ideal gas | kJ/(kg·K) |
| 10 | Lost Work (Exergy Destruction) | $W_{lost} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$ | Adiabatic or any process | kJ |
| 11 | Isentropic Process | $\Delta s = 0$ | Reversible adiabatic | kJ/(kg·K) |

> **Note on boundary temperatures:** $T_b$ denotes the *boundary temperature* at which heat $\delta Q$ crosses the system boundary, while $T$ (without subscript) denotes the *thermodynamic temperature* of the system at the point where $dS$ is evaluated.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Use of Principle |
|---|---|
| **Steam Power Plants** | Quantifies irreversibility in boiler, turbine, condenser — used to compute thermal efficiency penalty |
| **Refrigeration & HVAC** | Verifies minimum compressor work via entropy balance; COP limits |
| **Internal Combustion Engines** | Determines actual cycle efficiency vs. ideal Otto/Diesel cycle |
| **Chemical Processes** | Predicts spontaneity of reactions; basis for Gibbs free energy |
| **Cryogenics & Liquefaction** | Sets theoretical minimum work for gas liquefaction (Linde process) |
| **Nozzles & Diffusers** | Calculates entropy rise due to friction; exit velocity reduction |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — From Clausius Inequality to the Principle

We derive the Principle of Increase of Entropy rigorously starting from the Clausius Inequality.

**Starting Premise (Clausius Inequality for any cycle):**

$$\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b} \leq 0$$

The equality holds for a completely reversible cycle and the strict inequality for any irreversible cycle.

**Step 1:** Consider a closed system undergoing a process from state 1 to state 2 along an irreversible path $I$, and then returning from state 2 to state 1 along a reversible path $R$. The combined path $I + R$ constitutes a cycle.

**Step 2:** Apply the Clausius inequality to this composite cycle:

$$\int_1^2 \left(\dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b}\right)_I + \int_2^1 \left(\dfrac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_R \leq 0$$

**Step 3:** Rearranging to isolate the integrals:

$$\int_1^2 \left(\dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b}\right)_I \leq -\int_2^1 \left(\dfrac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_R$$

**Step 4:** Reverse the limits of the right-hand-side integral:

$$\int_1^2 \left(\dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b}\right)_I \leq \int_1^2 \left(\dfrac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_R$$

**Step 5:** By the defining property of entropy along a reversible path:

$$\int_1^2 \left(\dfrac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_R = S_2 - S_1 = \Delta S_{system}$$

Therefore:

$$\int_1^2 \left(\dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b}\right)_I \leq \Delta S_{system}$$

**Step 6:** Taking the differential (infinitesimal) limit:

$$dS \geq \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b}$$

This is the **general entropy inequality** valid for any process (reversible or irreversible) on a closed system.

**Step 7:** Now specialize to an **isolated system**. By definition, an isolated system exchanges neither heat ($\delta Q = 0$) nor work ($\delta W = 0$) nor mass with its surroundings.

Substituting $\delta Q = 0$:

$$dS_{isolated} \geq 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{\Delta S_{isolated} \geq 0}$$

This completes the derivation. **Q.E.D.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Entropy Generation Expression

Starting from the First Law for a closed system:

$$dU = \delta Q - \delta W$$

For an internally irreversible process, the actual work is less than the reversible work:

$$\delta W_{actual} < \delta W_{rev} = P \, dV$$

Therefore the heat transfer is also less than the reversible heat transfer:

$$\delta Q_{actual} < \delta Q_{rev} = T \, dS$$

The deficit appears as internal dissipation, captured by an entropy generation term $S_{gen}$:

$$\Delta S_{system} = \int \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b} + S_{gen} \quad \text{where } S_{gen} \geq 0$$

Equivalently:

$$S_{gen} = \Delta S_{system} - \sum_k \dfrac{Q_k}{T_{b,k}}$$

This is the **fundamental equation** used in nearly every KTU entropy-balance problem.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Ideal Gas Process

> **Problem:** 2 kg of air ($C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K) is heated reversibly at constant pressure from 300 K to 600 K. Calculate the change in entropy and verify the principle. Take surroundings at $T_0 = 300$ K.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the process:** Constant pressure ($P = \text{const}$), reversible.

**Step 2 — Apply the ideal gas T-P entropy formula** (from Formula Sheet Row 9):

$$\Delta S = m \left[ C_p \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right) - R \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:** Since pressure is constant, $P_2 / P_1 = 1$, so $\ln(1) = 0$:

$$\Delta S = 2 \times 1.005 \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{600}{300}\right) - 0$$

$$\Delta S = 2 \times 1.005 \times \ln(2)$$

$$\Delta S = 2 \times 1.005 \times 0.6931$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S = +1.393 \text{ kJ/K}}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the Principle:** Since this is a reversible heat-addition at constant $P$, the entropy of the system increases, but the entropy decrease of the surroundings ($-Q/T_{surr} = -1.393$ kJ/K) exactly balances. Thus $\Delta S_{univ} = 0$, consistent with reversibility.

> **Engineering Interpretation:** Positive $\Delta S_{system}$ indicates energy dispersal within the gas. Reversibility ensures zero entropy generation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example II — Irreversible Heat Transfer

> **Problem:** A 50 kg block of iron ($C = 0.45$ kJ/kg·K) initially at 500 K is immersed in a large lake at 300 K. Determine the total entropy change of the universe.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Final equilibrium temperature** (lake is huge, so lake temperature unchanged at 300 K):

$$T_{final} = T_{lake} = 300 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Entropy change of iron (system):**

$$\Delta S_{iron} = m C \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_f}{T_i}\right) = 50 \times 0.45 \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{300}{500}\right)$$

$$\Delta S_{iron} = 22.5 \times (-0.5108)$$

$$\Delta S_{iron} = -11.493 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**Step 3 — Heat transferred to lake:**

$$Q = m C (T_i - T_f) = 50 \times 0.45 \times (500 - 300) = 4500 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Entropy change of lake (surroundings):**

$$\Delta S_{lake} = \dfrac{Q}{T_{lake}} = \dfrac{4500}{300} = +15.000 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**Step 5 — Entropy change of universe:**

$$\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_{iron} + \Delta S_{lake} = -11.493 + 15.000$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S_{univ} = +3.507 \text{ kJ/K} > 0}$$

**Step 6 — Verification:** $\Delta S_{univ} > 0$ confirms the **Principle of Increase of Entropy** — the process is spontaneous and irreversible.

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The positive $\Delta S_{univ}$ represents irreversibility — here, heat transfer across a finite temperature difference of 200 K. This is the most commonly tested KTU problem type.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Entropy Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System + Surroundings Boundary] --> B{Is the System Isolated?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Apply dS greater or equal 0]
    B -- No --> D[Compute dS_sys and dS_surr Separately]
    C --> E{S greater or equal 0 satisfied?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Process is Allowed by Second Law]
    E -- No --> G[Process is Impossible]
    D --> H[Sum: dS_univ = dS_sys + dS_surr]
    H --> I{S_univ greater or equal 0?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Allowed]
    I -- No --> K[Violates Second Law]
    F --> L[Compute S_gen and W_lost]
    J --> L
    L --> M[Engineering Decision: Acceptable Irreversibility or Redesign]
```

## 4.2 Classification of Processes by Entropy Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Process Type] --> Q{Reversible?}
    Q -- Yes --> R1[Isentropic if Adiabatic]
    Q -- Yes --> R2[delta S equals integral of dQ rev over T]
    Q -- No --> S1[Internally Irreversible]
    Q -- No --> S2[Externally Irreversible]
    Q -- No --> S3[Both Internal and External]
    S1 --> T1[S_gen greater than 0]
    S2 --> T2[S_gen greater than 0]
    S3 --> T3[S_gen greater than 0]
    R1 --> U1[S_gen equals 0]
    R2 --> U2[S_gen equals 0]
```

## 4.3 System–Surroundings Entropy Interaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SYS[System Boundary] -->|Heat Transfer Q| SUR[Surroundings at T0]
    SUR -->|Entropy Outflow Q divided by T0| ENV[Universe]
    SYS -->|Entropy Generation S_gen| ENV
    ENV --> T{Entropy Balance Check}
    T --> RES1[S_univ greater than 0: Irreversible]
    T --> RES2[S_univ equals 0: Reversible]
    T --> RES3[S_univ less than 0: Impossible]
```

## 4.4 Engineering Interpretation Schematic — Lost Work Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN[Input Energy] --> RV[Reversible Work Maximum]
    IN --> AC[Actual Work Delivered]
    RV --> DIFF{Difference}
    AC --> DIFF
    DIFF --> LW[Lost Work equals T0 times S_gen]
    LW --> DEC[Engineering Design Improvement]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the Principle of Increase of Entropy.**

**Model Answer:**
The Principle of Increase of Entropy states that the entropy of an isolated system can never decrease; it either remains constant (for a reversible process) or increases (for an irreversible process).

$$\Delta S_{isolated} \geq 0$$

This principle, attributed to Clausius, is the mathematical embodiment of the Second Law of Thermodynamics. Equality holds for an idealized reversible process (zero entropy generation), and the strict inequality for any real, spontaneous, irreversible process. The principle dictates the *direction* of all natural processes and confirms that the universe is irreversibly evolving toward a state of maximum entropy (thermodynamic equilibrium).

*(Examiner's Note: Many students forget to explicitly state "isolated system." This is mandatory for full marks.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between entropy change of a system and entropy generation.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Entropy Change of System ($\Delta S_{sys}$) | Entropy Generation ($S_{gen}$) |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Property of the system; a state function | A path-dependent quantity; **not** a property |
| **Sign** | Can be $+, -, \text{or } 0$ | Always $\geq 0$ (by Second Law) |
| **Reversibility** | Same for any path between two states | Zero only for reversible processes |
| **Formula** | $\Delta S = \int_1^2 \dfrac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$ | $S_{gen} = \Delta S_{sys} - \sum \dfrac{Q_k}{T_k}$ |

The entropy *change* describes the system's initial-to-final state transition, whereas entropy *generation* quantifies the irreversibility created during the process.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing $\Delta S \geq 0$ without specifying the *isolated* system — you will lose **2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the units (kJ/K or kJ/kg·K) in the final numerical answer — KTU deducts **0.5 mark** per missing unit.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing entropy of the *system* with entropy of the *universe*. The principle applies to the universe/isolated system, **not** to the system alone.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Treating heat transfer across a finite $\Delta T$ as reversible — it is **always irreversible**.

---

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the Principle of Increase of Entropy from the Clausius Inequality. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rigid, insulated tank of volume $0.5 \text{ m}^3$ initially contains air at 400 K and 200 kPa. A paddle wheel does 30 kJ of work on the air. Assuming air as an ideal gas with $C_v = 0.718 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ and $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, determine:
  (i) The final temperature of air.
  (ii) The entropy change of the air.
  (iii) Verify the Principle of Increase of Entropy. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Closed system with finite mass.
2. Quasi-equilibrium states for entropy definition.
3. Internally reversible paths exist between any two equilibrium states.

**Derivation:**

Consider a closed system undergoing an irreversible process $I$ from state 1 to state 2, followed by a reversible process $R$ from state 2 back to state 1. The composite forms a cycle, and applying the Clausius inequality:

$$\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b} = \int_1^2 \left(\dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b}\right)_I + \int_2^1 \left(\dfrac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_R \leq 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\int_1^2 \left(\dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b}\right)_I \leq \int_1^2 \left(\dfrac{\delta Q}{T}\right)_R = \Delta S$$

Thus $dS \geq \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b}$. For an isolated system, $\delta Q = 0$, hence:

$$\boxed{\Delta S_{isolated} \geq 0}$$

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $V = 0.5 \text{ m}^3$ (rigid), $T_1 = 400 \text{ K}$, $P_1 = 200 \text{ kPa}$, $W_{in} = 30 \text{ kJ}$.

**Step 1 — Mass of air:**

$$m = \dfrac{P_1 V}{R T_1} = \dfrac{200 \times 0.5}{0.287 \times 400} = \dfrac{100}{114.8} = 0.8711 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 2 — First Law (rigid + insulated):** All paddle work converts to internal energy:

$$W_{in} = \Delta U = m C_v (T_2 - T_1)$$

$$30 = 0.8711 \times 0.718 \times (T_2 - 400)$$

$$T_2 - 400 = \dfrac{30}{0.6254} = 47.96 \text{ K}$$

$$T_2 = 447.96 \text{ K} \approx \boxed{448 \text{ K}}$$

**Step 3 — Entropy change of air (ideal gas, constant volume):**

$$\Delta S_{air} = m C_v \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right) = 0.8711 \times 0.718 \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{448}{400}\right)$$

$$\Delta S_{air} = 0.6254 \times \ln(1.12) = 0.6254 \times 0.1133$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S_{air} = 0.0709 \text{ kJ/K}}$$

**Step 4 — Verification:** The tank is rigid and insulated, so it is an isolated system. $\Delta S_{air} = 0.0709 \text{ kJ/K} > 0$. The Principle of Increase of Entropy is verified. ✓

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE

**(a)** Explain the concepts of entropy, entropy generation, and lost work. How are they interrelated? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two identical blocks of copper, each of mass 5 kg and specific heat $C = 0.386$ kJ/kg·K, are initially at 500 K and 300 K respectively. They are brought into thermal contact in an insulated container. Calculate:
  (i) The final equilibrium temperature.
  (ii) The total entropy change of the universe.
  (iii) The entropy generated.
  **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Entropy ($\Delta S$):** A thermodynamic property measuring molecular disorder or energy dispersal at a specified temperature. Mathematically, for a reversible process, $dS = \dfrac{\delta Q_{rev}}{T}$. It is a *point function* and *exact differential*.

**Entropy Generation ($S_{gen}$):** The entropy produced *within* a system due to irreversibilities (friction, mixing, unrestrained expansion, finite $\Delta T$ heat transfer). It is *not* a property — it is path-dependent and always $\geq 0$.

**Lost Work ($W_{lost}$):** The difference between the maximum (reversible) work obtainable and the actual work delivered. It represents the useful energy potential destroyed by irreversibility.

**Interrelation:**

$$W_{lost} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$$

where $T_0$ is the dead-state (environment) temperature. Thus, every unit of entropy generated in a process at environment temperature $T_0$ corresponds to $T_0$ units of work potential permanently lost.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:** $m_1 = m_2 = 5$ kg, $C = 0.386$ kJ/kg·K, $T_1 = 500$ K (hot), $T_2 = 300$ K (cold).

**Step 1 — Final equilibrium temperature** (energy conservation, both identical):

$$T_f = \dfrac{T_1 + T_2}{2} = \dfrac{500 + 300}{2} = \boxed{400 \text{ K}}$$

**Step 2 — Entropy change of hot block:**

$$\Delta S_{hot} = m C \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_f}{T_1}\right) = 5 \times 0.386 \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{400}{500}\right)$$

$$\Delta S_{hot} = 1.93 \times \ln(0.8) = 1.93 \times (-0.2231) = -0.4307 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**Step 3 — Entropy change of cold block:**

$$\Delta S_{cold} = m C \ln\!\left(\dfrac{T_f}{T_2}\right) = 1.93 \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{400}{300}\right)$$

$$\Delta S_{cold} = 1.93 \times \ln(1.333) = 1.93 \times 0.2877 = +0.5553 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**Step 4 — Total entropy change of universe:**

$$\Delta S_{univ} = \Delta S_{hot} + \Delta S_{cold} = -0.4307 + 0.5553$$

$$\boxed{\Delta S_{univ} = +0.1246 \text{ kJ/K}}$$

**Step 5 — Entropy generated:** Since the universe (insulated container) is an isolated system, $S_{gen} = \Delta S_{univ}$:

$$\boxed{S_{gen} = +0.1246 \text{ kJ/K}}$$

**Verification:** $\Delta S_{univ} > 0$ confirms the Principle of Increase of Entropy. The process is irreversible (heat transfer across finite $\Delta T$).

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU Board Exam)**

- [ ] **Core Principle:** $\Delta S_{isolated} \geq 0$ — *equality for reversible, strict inequality for irreversible.*
- [ ] **Scope:** Applies to *isolated system* or *universe*, NOT to the system alone.
- [ ] **Clausius Inequality:** $\oint \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b} \leq 0$ — the parent result from which the principle is derived.
- [ ] **General Entropy Inequality:** $dS \geq \dfrac{\delta Q}{T_b}$ — valid for closed systems under any process.
- [ ] **Entropy Generation:** $S_{gen} = \Delta S_{sys} - \sum \dfrac{Q_k}{T_k} \geq 0$ (always non-negative).
- [ ] **Lost Work Relation:** $W_{lost} = T_0 \cdot S_{gen}$ (engineering consequence of the principle).
- [ ] **Ideal Gas Entropy Formulas:** Always keep $C_p$, $C_v$, and $R$ consistent for the chosen gas; air values: $C_p = 1.005$, $C_v = 0.718$, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K.
- [ ] **Always State Units:** kJ/K (absolute) or kJ/(kg·K) (specific) — missing units = lost marks.
- [ ] **Reversible Adiabatic = Isentropic:** $\Delta S = 0$ is the gold-standard reversible benchmark.
- [ ] **For Universe/Isolated System:** $\Delta S \geq 0$ always; can never be negative.
- [ ] **Common Exam Trap:** If a problem says "insulated container" — that means *isolated system* → apply $\Delta S \geq 0$ directly.
- [ ] **Engineering Connection:** Every lost-work problem, every exergy/availability problem, every cycle-efficiency problem rests on this principle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of Pure Substances: Steam - Formation and its Thermodynamic Properties, P-V Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT502 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT ENGINES > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Properties of Pure Substances: Steam - Formation and its Thermodynamic Properties, P-V Diagram # MODULE 2 | PROPERTIES OF PURE SUBSTANCES: STEAM ## 1.1 Definition of a Pure Substance A **pure substance** is defined as a substance which is homogeneous and has a fixed (invariant) chemical composition throughout its mass. Even though it may consist of more than one phase, the chemical composition of the substance as a whole remains uniform. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A pure substance is one whose chemical composition is identical in all phases. Water (H₂O) is the most common engineering pure substance, and it can exist as a compressed liquid, saturated liquid, wet mixture, saturated vapor, or superheated vapor — but its chemical identity remains H₂O throughout. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a pure substance like a sealed glass jar containing only distilled water. Whether the water inside is in the form of solid ice, liquid water, or invisible water vapor (steam), it is *still* chemically the same H₂O molecule. If you add a pinch of salt to the water, it is no longer a pure substance — it becomes a *mixture*. The jar may have liquid at the bottom and vapor at the top (two phases), yet it is still classified as a single pure substance because every molecule in every phase is H₂O. In steam engineering applications — such as power plants (Rankine cycle), refrigeration, and process industries — we routinely deal with water undergoing phase transitions at constant pressure, so the concept of a pure substance is foundational. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric:** The most important physical constant associated with steam thermodynamics is the **critical point of water**: $P_{cr} = 22.064 \text{ MPa}$ and $T_{cr} = 373.946 \text{ °C}$ (≈ **647.096 K**). At this point, the saturated liquid and saturated vapor phases become indistinguishable. --- ## 1.2 Phase Change Process — Physical Overview When heat is added to a pure substance at a constant pressure below its critical pressure, it passes through distinct, well-defined stages. Understanding this sequence is essential before reading the P-V diagram. | # | Stage | Heat Added | Temperature | Description | |---|-------|-----------|-------------|-------------| | 1 | **Subcooled / Compressed Liquid** | Sensible heat | Rises | Liquid below saturation temperature | | 2 | **Saturated Liquid** | Sensible heat (final) | At $T_{sat}$ | First bubble of vapor appears | | 3 | **Wet Steam / Liquid-Vapor Mixture** | Latent heat | Constant | Two phases coexist | | 4 | **Saturated Vapor** | Latent heat (final) | At $T_{sat}$ | Last drop of liquid vaporizes | | 5 | **Superheated Vapor** | Sensible heat | Rises again | Vapor above saturation temperature | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sensible heat** changes the *temperature* of the substance. **Latent heat** changes the *phase* without any temperature change at constant pressure. # 2.1 Deep Theoretical Analysis — Phase Transitions & Properties ## 2.1.1 Saturation Temperature and Saturation Pressure For a given pressure, the temperature at which a pure substance begins to vaporize is uniquely fixed — this is called the **saturation temperature** $T_{sat}$. Conversely, for a given temperature, the pressure at which vaporization begins is the **saturation pressure** $P_{sat}$. These two variables are **not independent**; they are linked through the substance's saturation curve: $$ P_{sat} = f(T_{sat}) $$ This relationship is empirically tabulated in **steam tables** and is one of the most frequently used look-ups in KTU numerical problems. ## 2.1.2 The Five Distinct States of Steam **(a) Compressed / Subcooled Liquid** - Temperature is *below* the saturation temperature corresponding to the existing pressure. - Properties approximate those of saturated liquid at the same temperature. **(b) Saturated Liquid** - The substance is at the verge of vaporization. - Quality $x = 0$ (definition). - Denoted by subscript $f$ (e.g., $h_f$, $v_f$, $u_f$). **(c) Wet Steam (Liquid + Vapor Mixture)** - Two phases coexist in thermodynamic equilibrium. - Characterized by **quality** (dryness fraction) $x$: $$ x = \frac{m_{vapor}}{m_{vapor} + m_{liquid}} $$ - Properties are obtained by linear interpolation using the lever rule (see Section 3). **(d) Saturated Vapor (Dry Saturated Steam)** - Last trace of liquid has just evaporated. - Quality $x = 1$. - Denoted by subscript $g$ (e.g., $h_g$, $v_g$, $u_g$). **(e) Superheated Vapor** - Temperature is *above* the saturation temperature at the given pressure. - Properties are read from the **superheated steam table**. ## 2.1.3 Latent Heat of Vaporization The amount of energy required to convert 1 kg of saturated liquid completely into saturated vapor at the same pressure/temperature is the **latent heat of vaporization** $h_{fg}$. $$ h_{fg} = h_g - h_f $$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** $h_{fg}$ *decreases* as pressure rises and becomes **zero** at the critical point. This is because the difference between liquid and vapor phases vanishes at the critical point. ## 2.1.4 Why and How of the Saturation Dome The saturation dome is a visual representation of the two-phase coexistence region on a P-V or T-V diagram. The dome's left limb (saturated liquid line) and right limb (saturated vapor line) meet at the **critical point**. Inside the dome, the substance exists as a mixture. Outside the dome, it exists as a single phase (compressed liquid on the left, superheated vapor on the right). ## 2.1.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table lists every formula you must memorize for the KTU University Exam on this topic.** Master these and you can solve any Part B question. | # | Property | Formula | Units | Phase Valid | |---|----------|---------|-------|-------------| | 1 | Quality | $x = \dfrac{m_v}{m_v + m_l}$ | dimensionless | Wet steam only | | 2 | Specific Volume (wet) | $v = v_f + x \, v_{fg}$ | m³/kg | Wet steam | | 3 | Internal Energy (wet) | $u = u_f + x \, u_{fg}$ | kJ/kg | Wet steam | | 4 | Enthalpy (wet) | $h = h_f + x \, h_{fg}$ | kJ/kg | Wet steam | | 5 | Entropy (wet) | $s = s_f + x \, s_{fg}$ | kJ/(kg·K) | Wet steam | | 6 | Latent Heat | $h_{fg} = h_g - h_f$ | kJ/kg | Wet → Sat vapor | | 7 | Specific Volume Difference | $v_{fg} = v_g - v_f$ | m³/kg | At saturation | | 8 | Internal Energy Difference | $u_{fg} = u_g - u_f$ | kJ/kg | At saturation | | 9 | Entropy Difference | $s_{fg} = s_g - s_f$ | kJ/(kg·K) | At saturation | | 10 | Saturation Relation | $P_{sat} \leftrightarrow T_{sat}$ | MPa, °C | All phases | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Pitfall Alert:** When $x$ is **not given** and the substance is in the two-phase region, ALWAYS assume $x$ is needed as the unknown. When $x > 1$, the substance is **superheated** (use superheated table). When $x < 0$, the substance is **subcooled** (use saturated liquid table at given $T$). ## 2.1.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Rankine Cycle Power Plants:** Engineers design boilers to maximize enthalpy gain ($h_g - h_f$) at the operating pressure. - **Distillation Columns:** Wet steam properties determine tray hydraulics and heat transfer rates. - **Cogeneration Systems:** Superheated steam properties are required to size turbines and prevent blade erosion. - **Process Heating Industries:** Saturated steam is preferred because its temperature is constant at a given pressure, providing uniform heating. # 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivations & Property Relations ## 3.1.1 Derivation of Wet-Region Property Formula (Lever Rule) Consider 1 kg of wet steam with mass $m_l$ of liquid and $m_v$ of vapor: $$ m_l + m_v = 1 \text{ kg} $$ The specific volume $v$ of the mixture equals the total volume divided by total mass: $$ v = \frac{m_l \, v_f + m_v \, v_g}{m_l + m_v} $$ Since $m_l + m_v = 1$, this simplifies to: $$ v = m_l \, v_f + m_v \, v_g $$ Substituting $m_v = x$ and $m_l = 1 - x$: $$ v = (1 - x) v_f + x \, v_g $$ Expanding: $$ v = v_f - x \, v_f + x \, v_g $$ $$ v = v_f + x(v_g - v_f) $$ Since $v_{fg} = v_g - v_f$, we obtain the final lever-rule formula: $$ \boxed{v = v_f + x \, v_{fg}} $$ > [!NOTE] > The exact same algebraic structure applies to internal energy $u$, enthalpy $h$, and entropy $s$. This is because all of these are **extensive** (proportional to mass) and **additive** between phases. ## 3.1.2 Derivation of Enthalpy for Superheated Steam For superheated vapor, $x$ does not apply. The total enthalpy consists of three sequential components: $$ h = h_f + h_{fg} + c_{p,\text{avg}} (T_{sup} - T_{sat}) $$ The first term is the enthalpy of saturated liquid at the given pressure. The second term is the energy added during phase change. The third term is the **sensible heat** added during superheating, where $c_{p,\text{avg}}$ is the mean specific heat at constant pressure over the temperature range $T_{sat}$ to $T_{sup}$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Convention:** In numerical problems, ALWAYS prefer direct table lookup (steam tables) over this formula. The formula is for understanding and for derivation-based questions. ## 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example — Property of Wet Steam **Problem:** 3 kg of wet steam at 1.0 MPa contains 2.4 kg of dry saturated vapor. Determine: 1. Dryness fraction 2. Specific volume 3. Specific enthalpy 4. Specific internal energy 5. Specific entropy **Given:** $P = 1.0 \text{ MPa}$, $m = 3 \text{ kg}$, $m_v = 2.4 \text{ kg}$ **From Steam Tables at 1.0 MPa:** $$ v_f = 0.001127 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}, \quad v_g = 0.19436 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg} $$ $$ u_f = 761.39 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad u_g = 2582.8 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ $$ h_f = 762.52 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad h_g = 2777.1 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ $$ s_f = 2.1381 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}, \quad s_g = 6.5850 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)} $$ ### Step 1 — Dryness Fraction $$ x = \frac{m_v}{m_v + m_l} = \frac{2.4}{3.0} = 0.800 $$ ### Step 2 — Specific Volume Compute $v_{fg}$ first: $$ v_{fg} = v_g - v_f = 0.19436 - 0.001127 = 0.193233 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg} $$ Then: $$ v = v_f + x \, v_{fg} $$ $$ v = 0.001127 + (0.800)(0.193233) $$ $$ v = 0.001127 + 0.154586 $$ $$ \boxed{v = 0.155713 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}} $$ ### Step 3 — Specific Enthalpy Compute $h_{fg}$ first: $$ h_{fg} = h_g - h_f = 2777.1 - 762.52 = 2014.58 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ Then: $$ h = h_f + x \, h_{fg} $$ $$ h = 762.52 + (0.800)(2014.58) $$ $$ h = 762.52 + 1611.664 $$ $$ \boxed{h = 2374.18 \text{ kJ/kg}} $$ ### Step 4 — Specific Internal Energy Compute $u_{fg}$ first: $$ u_{fg} = u_g - u_f = 2582.8 - 761.39 = 1821.41 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ Then: $$ u = u_f + x \, u_{fg} $$ $$ u = 761.39 + (0.800)(1821.41) $$ $$ u = 761.39 + 1457.128 $$ $$ \boxed{u = 2218.52 \text{ kJ/kg}} $$ ### Step 5 — Specific Entropy Compute $s_{fg}$ first: $$ s_{fg} = s_g - s_f = 6.5850 - 2.1381 = 4.4469 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)} $$ Then: $$ s = s_f + x \, s_{fg} $$ $$ s = 2.1381 + (0.800)(4.4469) $$ $$ s = 2.1381 + 3.55752 $$ $$ \boxed{s = 5.69562 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification (Quality Check):** $0 \leq x = 0.8 \leq 1$ — confirmed wet steam. ✓ ## 3.1.4 Worked Numerical Example — Superheated Steam Property via Interpolation **Problem:** Determine the specific enthalpy of steam at $P = 2.0 \text{ MPa}$ and $T = 350 \text{ °C}$. **From Superheated Steam Table at 2.0 MPa:** | $T$ (°C) | $h$ (kJ/kg) | |----------|-------------| | 300 | 3023.5 | | 350 | 3137.0 | | 400 | 3247.6 | Since $T = 350 \text{ °C}$ is a direct table entry, no interpolation is needed: $$ \boxed{h = 3137.0 \text{ kJ/kg}} $$ **If** the problem had asked for $T = 320 \text{ °C}$, we would interpolate between 300 °C and 350 °C: $$ h_{320} = h_{300} + \frac{(320 - 300)}{(350 - 300)} (h_{350} - h_{300}) $$ $$ h_{320} = 3023.5 + \frac{20}{50} (3137.0 - 3023.5) $$ $$ h_{320} = 3023.5 + (0.4)(113.5) $$ $$ h_{320} = 3023.5 + 45.4 $$ $$ \boxed{h_{320} = 3068.9 \text{ kJ/kg}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner Pattern:** Numerical questions almost always require TWO steps of interpolation — first for saturation properties at the given pressure (if between table entries), then for superheated properties. Practice both. # 4.1 Structural Diagrams — Steam Formation & P-V Architecture ## 4.1.1 Block-Functional Flow of the Steam Formation Process The following block diagram illustrates the sequential thermodynamic states of water as heat is added at constant pressure below the critical pressure. Each block represents a thermodynamic state, and arrows represent energy transfer (heat addition) at constant pressure. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[STATE 1: COMPRESSED LIQUID
T less than Tsat at given P] -->|SENSIBLE HEAT Q1| B[STATE 2: SATURATED LIQUID
x equals 0, T equals Tsat] B -->|LATENT HEAT hfg at constant T and P| C[STATE 3: WET STEAM MIXTURE
0 less than x less than 1] C -->|LATENT HEAT hfg continued| D[STATE 4: DRY SATURATED VAPOR
x equals 1, T equals Tsat] D -->|SENSIBLE HEAT Q2| E[STATE 5: SUPERHEATED VAPOR
T greater than Tsat at given P] style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 style B fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 style C fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000 style D fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000,color:#000000 style E fill:#ff9999,stroke:#660000,color:#000000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** > - Blocks **A → B** and **D → E** represent *sensible* heating (temperature changes). > - Blocks **B → C → D** represent *latent* heating (temperature constant; phase change occurs). > - The total heat added per kg to go from A to E is $Q_{total} = (h_f - h_1) + h_{fg} + (h_5 - h_g)$. ## 4.1.2 P-V Diagram with Saturation Dome (Functional Block Schematic) The P-V diagram is the single most important graphical tool for steam thermodynamics. The block schematic below maps every region, line, and critical point of the P-V diagram to its engineering significance. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PVDIAGRAM[P-V DIAGRAM OF A PURE SUBSTANCE] subgraph DOME[SATURATION DOME - TWO PHASE REGION] LIQ[SATURATED LIQUID LINE
Left limb of dome
x equals 0] WET[WET STEAM REGION
0 less than x less than 1
Liquid plus Vapor mixture] VAP[SATURATED VAPOR LINE
Right limb of dome
x equals 1] end subgraph LEFTREG[COMPRESSED LIQUID REGION] SUBL[SUBCOOLED OR COMPRESSED LIQUID
T less than Tsat] end subgraph RIGHTREG[SUPERHEATED REGION] SUPR[SUPERHEATED VAPOR
T greater than Tsat] end CRIT[CRITICAL POINT
P equals 22.064 MPa
T equals 374 deg C
Phases become identical] end ISOBAR[ISOBAR LINE AT CONSTANT PRESSURE BELOW PCRIT
Passes through all 5 states] -.-> SUBL ISOBAR -.-> LIQ ISOBAR -.-> WET ISOBAR -.-> VAP ISOBAR -.-> SUPR style DOME fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366 style LEFTREG fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style RIGHTREG fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000 style CRIT fill:#ffff66,stroke:#996600,color:#000000 style ISOBAR fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,color:#000000 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insights from the P-V Diagram:** > 1. The **saturated liquid line** (left limb) is nearly vertical because $v_f$ changes very little with pressure. > 2. The **saturated vapor line** (right limb) is steeply curved, showing that $v_g$ drops sharply as pressure approaches $P_{cr}$. > 3. The **critical point** is the apex of the dome — beyond it, no distinction between liquid and vapor exists. > 4. **Three single-phase regions** exist: compressed liquid (left), superheated vapor (right), and the two-phase wet region (inside the dome). > 5. Any **horizontal line** inside the dome represents a constant-pressure (isobaric) phase-change process. ## 4.1.3 T-V Diagram Companion Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TVDIAG[T-V DIAGRAM REGIONS] SL[SATURATED LIQUID LINE] --- WETR[WET STEAM TWO PHASE REGION] --- SV[SATURATED VAPOR LINE] CL[COMPRESSED LIQUID - LEFT OF SAT LIQUID LINE] SH[SUPERHEATED VAPOR - RIGHT OF SAT VAPOR LINE] CPT[CRITICAL POINT - APEX OF DOME] end style SL fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#000000 style WETR fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366 style SV fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000 style CL fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style SH fill:#ff9999,stroke:#660000 style CPT fill:#ffff66,stroke:#996600 ``` > [!NOTE] > The T-V diagram is the most convenient for plotting the Rankine cycle, since most processes in power cycles are isobaric (horizontal lines) on this diagram. # 5.1 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank ## PART A — 3 Mark Questions ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model] **Define a pure substance. Give two examples other than water.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A pure substance is one which is **homogeneous** and has a **fixed (invariant) chemical composition** throughout its mass. Although it may exist in more than one phase, every phase has the same chemical composition. Examples other than water: 1. **N₂** (nitrogen gas in a cylinder, including its liquid phase) 2. **CO₂** (carbon dioxide, which can exist as solid "dry ice," liquid, or gas) > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Tip:** A mixture of gases like air is NOT a pure substance because the composition varies. Pure substances can have multiple phases, but not multiple chemical compositions. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model] **Distinguish between saturated liquid and compressed liquid.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Saturated Liquid | Compressed (Subcooled) Liquid | |---------|------------------|-------------------------------| | **Temperature** | Equals $T_{sat}$ at the given pressure | Lower than $T_{sat}$ at the given pressure | | **State** | On the verge of vaporization (first bubble forms) | Below saturation; no vaporization possible yet | | **Pressure relative to $P_{sat}$ at its $T$** | $P = P_{sat}(T)$ | $P > P_{sat}(T)$ | | **Energy** | Higher enthalpy (ready to vaporize) | Lower enthalpy | | **Example** | Water at 100 °C and 101.325 kPa | Water at 80 °C and 101.325 kPa | --- ## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In wet-steam problems, students most commonly lose marks by: > 1. Forgetting to compute $v_{fg}$, $h_{fg}$, $u_{fg}$, or $s_{fg}$ before applying the lever rule. > 2. Using $v_g$ directly instead of $v_f + x \, v_{fg}$ — this is a 1–2 mark penalty. > 3. Mixing up units between kJ and J (1 kJ = 1 kJ; the tables give kJ/kg — keep them in kJ/kg for $u$, $h$, $s$). > 4. Not stating the saturated temperature explicitly in superheated steam problems. > 5. Forgetting to mention the quality $x$ when the substance is wet — partial mark loss. --- ### QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model] **(a)** Define the term *dryness fraction*. A closed rigid vessel of volume 0.05 m³ contains 0.5 kg of wet steam at a pressure of **0.5 MPa**. Determine: 1. The dryness fraction of the steam. 2. The specific volume, specific enthalpy, specific internal energy, and specific entropy. **[(a) is for 7 marks]** #### Model Solution for (a) **Definition (2 marks):** The dryness fraction (or quality) $x$ of a wet steam is the ratio of the mass of dry saturated vapor to the total mass of the mixture: $$ x = \frac{m_v}{m_v + m_l} $$ where $m_v$ is the mass of vapor and $m_l$ is the mass of liquid in the mixture. The value of $x$ lies between **0** (saturated liquid) and **1** (dry saturated vapor). **Given Data:** - Volume $V = 0.05 \text{ m}^3$ - Mass $m = 0.5 \text{ kg}$ - Pressure $P = 0.5 \text{ MPa}$ **Specific volume of the mixture:** $$ v = \frac{V}{m} = \frac{0.05}{0.5} = 0.1 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg} $$ **Properties at 0.5 MPa from steam tables:** $$ v_f = 0.001093 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}, \quad v_g = 0.37483 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg} $$ $$ u_f = 639.54 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad u_g = 2567.4 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ $$ h_f = 640.09 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad h_g = 2553.6 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ $$ s_f = 1.8604 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}, \quad s_g = 6.8207 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)} $$ **Step 1 — Compute $v_{fg}$:** $$ v_{fg} = v_g - v_f = 0.37483 - 0.001093 = 0.373737 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg} $$ **Step 2 — Dryness fraction from lever rule:** $$ v = v_f + x \, v_{fg} $$ $$ 0.1 = 0.001093 + x(0.373737) $$ $$ x = \frac{0.1 - 0.001093}{0.373737} $$ $$ x = \frac{0.098907}{0.373737} $$ $$ \boxed{x = 0.2647} $$ **Step 3 — Specific Enthalpy:** Compute $h_{fg}$: $$ h_{fg} = h_g - h_f = 2553.6 - 640.09 = 1913.51 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ Then: $$ h = h_f + x \, h_{fg} $$ $$ h = 640.09 + (0.2647)(1913.51) $$ $$ h = 640.09 + 506.50 $$ $$ \boxed{h = 1146.59 \text{ kJ/kg}} $$ **Step 4 — Specific Internal Energy:** Compute $u_{fg}$: $$ u_{fg} = u_g - u_f = 2567.4 - 639.54 = 1927.86 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ Then: $$ u = u_f + x \, u_{fg} $$ $$ u = 639.54 + (0.2647)(1927.86) $$ $$ u = 639.54 + 510.30 $$ $$ \boxed{u = 1149.84 \text{ kJ/kg}} $$ **Step 5 — Specific Entropy:** Compute $s_{fg}$: $$ s_{fg} = s_g - s_f = 6.8207 - 1.8604 = 4.9603 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)} $$ Then: $$ s = s_f + x \, s_{fg} $$ $$ s = 1.8604 + (0.2647)(4.9603) $$ $$ s = 1.8604 + 1.3130 $$ $$ \boxed{s = 3.1734 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)}} $$ --- **(b)** Explain the formation of steam at constant pressure with the help of a **temperature vs. heat added (T-Q) diagram**. Mark clearly the regions of sensible heating, latent heating, and superheating. **[(b) is for 7 marks]** #### Model Solution for (b) **T-Q Diagram Description:** A T-Q diagram plots temperature on the vertical axis and the cumulative heat added (per unit mass) on the horizontal axis for a process at constant pressure. This diagram uniquely shows the *plateau* during phase change, which is missing on a P-V diagram. **The five distinct stages at constant pressure below $P_{cr}$:** **Stage 1 — Subcooled Liquid Heating (Point A to B):** - Heat $Q_1 = c_{p,l} (T_{sat} - T_{initial})$ is added. - Temperature rises linearly with $Q$. - The slope of the line equals $1/c_{p,l}$. **Stage 2 — Latent Heat Addition (Point B to C):** - Heat added $= h_{fg}$. - Temperature remains **constant** at $T_{sat}$ (horizontal line on T-Q diagram). - This plateau represents the conversion of saturated liquid into saturated vapor through wet steam. **Stage 3 — Superheating (Point C to D):** - Heat $Q_3 = c_{p,v} (T_{sup} - T_{sat})$ is added. - Temperature rises again with $Q$. - Slope is gentler than Stage 1 because $c_{p,v} > c_{p,l}$ typically. **Key Takeaways for KTU:** - The T-Q diagram has **two sloped regions** (sensible heating) and **one flat region** (latent heating). - The flat region length on the $Q$-axis equals $h_{fg}$, the latent heat of vaporization. - As pressure increases toward $P_{cr}$, the flat region shrinks and vanishes at the critical point. --- ### QUESTION B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model] **(a)** With the help of a neat **P-V diagram**, explain the following: 1. Saturated liquid line 2. Saturated vapor line 3. Critical point 4. Two-phase (wet) region 5. Superheated vapor region **[(a) is for 7 marks]** #### Model Solution for (a) **Description of the P-V Diagram (textual representation since P-V is graphical):** A P-V diagram of a pure substance is a plot of pressure $P$ (vertical axis) versus specific volume $v$ (horizontal axis). It consists of three distinct regions separated by a characteristic **saturation dome**. **Key Features:** **1. Saturated Liquid Line:** The left limb of the saturation dome. It is the locus of points where the substance is 100% liquid (quality $x = 0$). It is nearly vertical because liquid is nearly incompressible. **2. Saturated Vapor Line:** The right limb of the saturation dome. It is the locus of points where the substance is 100% dry saturated vapor (quality $x = 1$). It slopes downward steeply. **3. Critical Point:** The apex where the saturated liquid and saturated vapor lines meet. For water, the critical point is at: $$ P_{cr} = 22.064 \text{ MPa}, \quad T_{cr} = 374.946 \text{ °C} $$ Above this point, there is no distinction between liquid and vapor — only a single supercritical fluid phase exists. **4. Two-Phase / Wet Steam Region:** The interior of the saturation dome. Within this region, the substance exists as a liquid-vapor mixture with quality $0 < x < 1$. A horizontal line inside the dome (constant pressure) crosses from saturated liquid to saturated vapor, passing through all wet states. **5. Superheated Vapor Region:** The area to the right of the saturated vapor line, below the critical pressure. The substance exists as vapor at a temperature greater than $T_{sat}$ at the given pressure. **Additional Region — Compressed Liquid:** To the left of the saturated liquid line. The substance is liquid at a temperature below $T_{sat}$ for the existing pressure. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Significance:** In power plants, the boiler process traces a path from compressed liquid → wet steam → saturated/superheated vapor along a constant-pressure isobar inside the P-V diagram. The turbine expansion, by contrast, traces a path of decreasing pressure (nearly vertical descent) from the superheated region into the wet region. --- **(b)** A mass of **2 kg** of water at **30 °C** is heated at constant pressure of **200 kPa** until it becomes superheated steam at **150 °C**. Determine: 1. The heat added during the entire process. 2. The change in entropy. 3. The change in internal energy. **[(b) is for 7 marks]** #### Model Solution for (b) **Given:** - $m = 2 \text{ kg}$, $T_1 = 30 \text{ °C}$, $P = 200 \text{ kPa}$, $T_2 = 150 \text{ °C}$ **State 1 — Compressed Liquid at 30 °C:** Approximate using saturated liquid properties at 30 °C: $$ h_1 \approx h_{f@30°C} = 125.74 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad v_1 \approx v_{f@30°C} = 0.001004 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg} $$ $$ u_1 \approx u_{f@30°C} = 125.73 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad s_1 \approx s_{f@30°C} = 0.4365 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)} $$ **State 2 — Superheated Steam at 200 kPa, 150 °C:** From the superheated steam table at 200 kPa, 150 °C: $$ h_2 = 2768.4 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad s_2 = 7.2795 \text{ kJ/(kg·K)} $$ For internal energy, use the relation $u = h - Pv$: $$ v_2 = 0.9596 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg} \quad \text{(from table at 200 kPa, 150 °C)} $$ $$ u_2 = h_2 - P \, v_2 = 2768.4 - (200)(0.9596) $$ Note: convert $P$ from kPa to kJ/m³: $200 \text{ kPa} = 200 \text{ kJ/m}^3$. $$ u_2 = 2768.4 - 191.92 = 2576.48 \text{ kJ/kg} $$ **Step 1 — Heat Added:** Since the process occurs at constant pressure, the heat added equals the change in enthalpy: $$ Q = m (h_2 - h_1) $$ $$ Q = 2 \times (2768.4 - 125.74) $$ $$ Q = 2 \times 2642.66 $$ $$ \boxed{Q = 5285.32 \text{ kJ}} $$ **Step 2 — Change in Entropy:** $$ \Delta S = m (s_2 - s_1) $$ $$ \Delta S = 2 \times (7.2795 - 0.4365) $$ $$ \Delta S = 2 \times 6.8430 $$ $$ \boxed{\Delta S = 13.686 \text{ kJ/K}} $$ **Step 3 — Change in Internal Energy:** $$ \Delta U = m (u_2 - u_1) $$ $$ \Delta U = 2 \times (2576.48 - 125.73) $$ $$ \Delta U = 2 \times 2450.75 $$ $$ \boxed{\Delta U = 4901.50 \text{ kJ}} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall (3 marks lost here frequently):** Students often use $P$ in Pa in the formula $u = h - Pv$ without converting units. Always check unit consistency: $P$ in kPa × $v$ in m³/kg = kJ/kg (since 1 kPa·m³ = 1 kJ). --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU University Exam — Module 2 (Pure Substances & Steam)** - **Pure substance definition:** Homogeneous + invariant chemical composition across all phases. ✓ - **Five states of water:** Compressed liquid → Saturated liquid → Wet steam → Saturated vapor → Superheated vapor. ✓ - **Quality (dryness fraction) $x$:** Defined ONLY in the wet region ($0 < x < 1$). ✓ - **Lever-rule formula applies to:** $v$, $u$, $h$, $s$ — every extensive additive property. ✓ - **Critical point of water:** $P_{cr} = 22.064 \text{ MPa}$, $T_{cr} = 374.946 \text{ °C}$. ✓ - **At critical point:** $h_{fg} = 0$, $v_f = v_g$, liquid and vapor become indistinguishable. ✓ - **Constant-pressure phase change** is represented by a **horizontal line** inside the saturation dome on P-V and T-V diagrams. ✓ - **Latent heat $h_{fg}$:** Decreases with increasing pressure; vanishes at the critical point. ✓ - **Sensible heat:** Causes temperature change (subcooled heating, superheating). ✓ - **Latent heat:** Causes phase change at constant temperature (boiling/condensation). ✓ - **Steam tables are mandatory:** Saturation tables for $T_{sat} \leq T_{cr}$; superheated tables for $T > T_{sat}$. ✓ - **Internal energy of vapor from tables:** Use $u_g = h_g - P_{sat} v_g$ if not given directly. ✓ - **Process path on T-Q diagram:** Slope during sensible heating, flat plateau during phase change. ✓ - **Compressed liquid approximation:** Properties ≈ saturated liquid properties at the same temperature (not pressure). ✓ - **Superheated steam:** Properties from superheated table at given $P$ and $T$ directly. ✓ - **Unit consistency check:** $P$ (kPa) × $v$ (m³/kg) = $h - u$ (kJ/kg). ✓

topics.title: H-S Diagrams, P-V-T Surface, Determination of Dryness Fraction, Calculation of Work Done and Heat Transfer in Non-Flow and Flow Processes using Steam Table and Mollier Chart
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT502 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT ENGINES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** H-S Diagrams, P-V-T Surface, Determination of Dryness Fraction, Calculation of Work Done and Heat Transfer in Non-Flow and Flow Processes using Steam Table and Mollier Chart

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 H-S Diagram (Mollier Chart)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> The **H-S Diagram**, popularly known as the **Mollier Chart**, is a thermodynamic chart in which **specific enthalpy (h)** is plotted on the **vertical (Y) axis** and **specific entropy (s)** is plotted on the **horizontal (X) axis**. It is the most powerful graphical aid for steam-power engineering calculations, particularly for turbines, nozzles, and throttling devices.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition):**
Think of the Mollier chart as a **"Steam GPS Map"**. If you tell the map your starting location (Pressure + Temperature or Pressure + Dryness fraction), it instantly shows you two crucial "addresses" — your **enthalpy (h)** and **entropy (s)**. Any thermodynamic process (heating, expansion, compression) becomes a *journey* on this map, and the difference in addresses gives the work/heat directly. Unlike the T-s diagram where you must perform complex integration, the Mollier chart lets you **read off** the answer.

**Key Features of the Mollier Chart:**
- **Saturation Curve** dividing the diagram into **wet steam region** (below) and **superheated region** (above).
- **Critical Point** at the peak of the saturation dome.
- **Constant Pressure Lines (Isobars):** All isobars converge into the wet region and diverge in the superheated region. **Slope of isobar = Absolute Temperature (T)**.
- **Constant Temperature Lines (Isotherms):** Coincide with isobars in the wet region (since T = T_sat at given P), but become flatter in the superheated region.
- **Constant Dryness Fraction Lines:** Straight lines drawn across the wet region.
- **Constant Specific Volume Lines (Isochores):** Slope upward to the right.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Units Used in Mollier Chart:**
> - Enthalpy (h) → **kJ/kg**
> - Entropy (s) → **kJ/kg·K**
> - Pressure (P) → **bar**
> - Temperature (T) → **°C**
> - Specific Volume (v) → **m³/kg**

---

## 1.2 P-V-T Surface

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> The **P-V-T Surface** is a three-dimensional graphical representation of the simultaneous variation of **Pressure (P)**, **Specific Volume (V)**, and **Temperature (T)** of a pure substance (such as water/steam) in thermodynamic equilibrium. It is the "mother surface" from which all 2-D thermodynamic diagrams (P-V, T-V, P-T) are obtained as projections.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition):**
Imagine a **landscape of a mountain range** where the **height = Pressure**, **east-west = Volume**, and **north-south = Temperature**. The mountain top represents the **critical point** — beyond which no distinct liquid/gas boundary exists. A valley on one side (low T, low v) is the **solid phase**, a flat plateau is the **liquid phase**, and a vast plain is the **vapor phase**. The saturation dome is like a **natural basin** where liquid and vapor can coexist.

---

## 1.3 Dryness Fraction (x)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:**
> **Dryness Fraction (x)** is defined as the ratio of the **mass of dry (saturated) vapour** to the **total mass of the liquid-vapour mixture** in wet steam.
>
> $$x = \frac{m_g}{m_g + m_f}$$
>
> where $m_g$ = mass of dry steam and $m_f$ = mass of liquid water in suspension.

**Intuitive Explanation:**
If you have a beaker of boiling water with steam bubbling through it, the wet steam is a **mixture** of actual water droplets and invisible vapour. The dryness fraction tells you **"how much of this mixture is pure steam"**. A value of $x = 0.9$ means **90% of the mass is dry steam** and 10% is liquid water. A value of $x = 1$ means *saturated dry steam*, and $x = 0$ means *saturated water*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Related Term:** **Moisture Fraction (y) = 1 − x** represents the fraction by mass that is liquid water.

---

## 1.4 Work Done and Heat Transfer — Process Classification

In steam engineering, processes are classified as:

| Type | Meaning | SFEE Applicable? |
|---|---|---|
| **Non-Flow Process** | Closed system (fixed mass, e.g., cylinder-piston) | No (use First Law: Q = ΔU + W) |
| **Flow Process** | Open system / steady flow (e.g., turbine, nozzle) | Yes (SFEE: h₁ + q = h₂ + w) |

**Why this distinction matters in KTU exams:**
The formula for work and heat is *fundamentally different* in the two cases. Examiners often test the **work transfer (W or w)** and **heat transfer (Q or q)** separately, expecting students to **state the correct governing law** before solving.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of the H-S Diagram (Mollier Chart)

### 2.1.1 Why is the Mollier Chart Important?

In T-S diagrams, **work done** and **heat transfer** are obtained as **areas under curves** (∫T ds for heat, area for work). These require integration or graphical planimetry, which is **tedious and error-prone**. The Mollier chart eliminates this by:
