- Plotting **h** on Y-axis so that **vertical distances = Δh** (directly related to flow work and heat).
- Using the relation $T \, ds = dh - v \, dP$, which gives the **slope of an isobar as T**:
  $$\left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial s}\right)_P = T$$

### 2.1.2 Structure of the Mollier Chart (Component-by-Component)

- **Wet Steam Region:** Lies under the saturation curve. Here, isobars are **straight lines** of slope $T_{sat}(P)$. Constant-temperature lines **coincide** with isobars in this region (since T is fixed for a given P).
- **Superheated Region:** Lies above the saturation curve. Here, isobars **diverge upward** because T rises as steam is superheated. Constant-temperature lines drop below the isobars.
- **Compressed Liquid Region:** Lower-left, rarely used for steam power cycles.

### 2.1.3 Engineering Utility
- Quick determination of **throttling processes** (constant h → vertical line on Mollier chart).
- Determination of **isentropic expansion** (constant s → horizontal line).
- Direct reading of **work output** of a turbine as $w = h_1 - h_2$ (vertical drop).
- Direct reading of **heat added** in a boiler as $q_{in} = h_2 - h_1$.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of the P-V-T Surface

### 2.2.1 Geometry of the P-V-T Surface

For a pure substance like water, the P-V-T surface in 3-D space has:
- A **divergent vapour region** (high V, low to moderate P).
- A **near-vertical liquid region** (low V, high P).
- A **critical point** at the top of the saturation dome (P_c ≈ 220.64 bar, T_c ≈ 374.14 °C, v_c ≈ 0.003155 m³/kg).
- A **triple point** at the lowest extremity (P_t ≈ 0.006112 bar, T_t ≈ 0.01 °C).

### 2.2.2 Projections of the P-V-T Surface

The 3-D surface can be projected onto three 2-D planes:

| Projection | Resulting Diagram | Common Use |
|---|---|---|
| **P vs V** (viewed along T-axis) | **P-V Diagram** | Indicated work (∫P dV) |
| **P vs T** (viewed along V-axis) | **P-T Diagram / Phase Diagram** | Saturation curve, triple point |
| **T vs V** (viewed along P-axis) | **T-V Diagram** | Visualising isotherms in wet region |

### 2.2.3 Significance for Steam
- The **wet region** (inside the dome) is where most steam power cycles operate.
- The **saturated liquid line** (left boundary of dome) and **saturated vapour line** (right boundary) merge at the **critical point**.
- For pressures above critical, there is **no distinct phase change** — fluid behaves as a supercritical fluid.

---

## 2.3 Determination of Dryness Fraction

### 2.3.1 Methods to Determine Dryness Fraction

A wet steam sample is characterised by knowing **any two independent properties** (e.g., P and T, or P and h, or P and s, or T and h). The dryness fraction is then found as:

**Method 1: From Enthalpy (using Steam Table or Mollier Chart)**
$$x = \frac{h - h_f}{h_{fg}}$$

**Method 2: From Entropy (using Mollier Chart directly)**
$$x = \frac{s - s_f}{s_{fg}}$$

**Method 3: From Specific Volume (using Steam Table)**
$$x = \frac{v - v_f}{v_{fg}}$$

**Method 4: From Internal Energy**
$$x = \frac{u - u_f}{u_{fg}}$$

**Method 5: From Steam Table (if P and T are given but T > T_sat at that P)**
If the given temperature is **higher** than the saturation temperature at the given pressure, the steam is **superheated** and dryness fraction is **not applicable** ($x = 1$ but the steam is in the superheated region).

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> **Subscript Convention Used:** f = saturated liquid; g = saturated vapour; fg = difference (g − f); sup = superheated; sat = saturated.

### General Property Relations for Wet Steam

| Property | Formula for Wet Steam |
|---|---|
| Specific Volume (v) | $v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$ |
| Specific Enthalpy (h) | $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$ |
| Specific Internal Energy (u) | $u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg}$ |
| Specific Entropy (s) | $s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$ |
| Dryness Fraction (x) | $x = (h - h_f)/h_{fg} = (s - s_f)/s_{fg} = (v - v_f)/v_{fg}$ |

### Non-Flow (Closed System) Process Formulas

| Process | Work Done (W) | Heat Transfer (Q) |
|---|---|---|
| **Constant Volume (Isochoric)** | $W = 0$ | $Q = m(u_2 - u_1)$ |
| **Constant Pressure (Isobaric)** | $W = P(V_2 - V_1) = mP(v_2 - v_1)$ | $Q = m(h_2 - h_1) = \Delta H$ |
| **Constant Temperature (Isothermal)** | $W = Q = mT(s_2 - s_1)$ | $Q = mT(s_2 - s_1)$ |
| **Reversible Adiabatic (Isentropic)** | $W = - \Delta U = m(u_1 - u_2)$ | $Q = 0$ |
| **Polytropic ($PV^n$ = const)** | $W = \dfrac{m(P_1 v_1 - P_2 v_2)}{n - 1}$ | $Q = m c_n (T_2 - T_1)$ |

### Flow (Open System) Process Formulas — Using SFEE

**Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE):**
$$h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + gZ_1 + q = h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + gZ_2 + w$$

Ignoring KE and PE changes:
$$h_1 + q = h_2 + w$$

| Flow Process | Work Done (w) | Heat Transfer (q) |
|---|---|---|
| **Constant Pressure Flow** | $w = -\int v \, dP = 0$ | $q = h_2 - h_1$ |
| **Adiabatic Flow (No heat transfer)** | $w = h_1 - h_2$ | $q = 0$ |
| **Isothermal Flow (T = const, reversible)** | $w = h_1 - h_2 + q$ | $q = T(s_2 - s_1)$ |
| **Reversible Polytropic Flow** | $w = -\int v \, dP$ | $q = \Delta h + w$ |

### Steam-Specific Boundary Conditions

| Quantity | Steam Table Reference |
|---|---|
| $h_f$ at P | From saturated water table at P |
| $h_{fg}$ at P | From saturated water table at P |
| $h_g$ at P | $h_f + h_{fg}$ |
| $s_f$, $s_{fg}$, $s_g$ | From saturated water table at P |
| $h_{sup}$, $s_{sup}$ | From superheated steam table at (P, T) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Golden Rule:** In the Mollier chart, **throttling = vertical line** (constant h); **isentropic expansion = horizontal line** (constant s). These two facts alone solve 80% of KTU problems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Property Formulae for Wet Steam

Consider a wet steam mixture of mass $m$ containing mass $m_f$ of saturated water and mass $m_g$ of dry saturated vapour. The total mass is:
$$m = m_f + m_g$$

The dryness fraction is, by definition:
$$x = \frac{m_g}{m_f + m_g} = \frac{m_g}{m}$$

Therefore: $m_g = x m$ and $m_f = (1 - x) m$.

### Derivation of $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$

Total enthalpy of mixture:
$$H_{total} = m_f \cdot h_f + m_g \cdot h_g$$

Specific enthalpy of mixture:
$$h = \frac{H_{total}}{m} = \frac{m_f h_f + m_g h_g}{m_f + m_g}$$

Substituting $m_f = (1-x)m$ and $m_g = x m$:
$$h = (1-x)h_f + x \cdot h_g = h_f - x h_f + x h_g = h_f + x(h_g - h_f)$$

Since $h_{fg} = h_g - h_f$:
$$\boxed{h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Identical derivations** apply to v, u, and s by replacing h with v, u, s respectively.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Work Done in Non-Flow Constant Pressure Process

**Given:** A mass $m$ of steam in a cylinder-piston undergoes a quasi-static expansion/compression at constant pressure $P$ from state 1 (volume $V_1$) to state 2 (volume $V_2$).

**Boundary work** is defined as:
$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

Since P is constant:
$$W = P \int_{V_1}^{V_2} dV = P(V_2 - V_1)$$

Expressing in terms of specific volume:
$$\boxed{W = m \cdot P \cdot (v_2 - v_1)}$$

From the First Law of Thermodynamics for a closed system:
$$Q = \Delta U + W = m(u_2 - u_1) + mP(v_2 - v_1)$$

Using the identity $h = u + Pv$:
$$Q = m[(u_2 + P_2 v_2) - (u_1 + P_1 v_1)] = m(h_2 - h_1)$$

$$\boxed{Q = m(h_2 - h_1)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This is the *theoretical proof* that for any constant-pressure non-flow process, **heat added = change in enthalpy**, not change in internal energy. This single result is the basis of calorimetric measurements and boiler design.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Work Done in Non-Flow Reversible Isothermal Process (Steam)

**Given:** A mass $m$ of wet steam in a closed cylinder, kept in contact with a thermal reservoir at temperature $T$ = $T_{sat}$, undergoes a reversible expansion at constant T from state 1 to state 2.

Since the process is **isothermal and reversible**, the combined First & Second Law gives:
$$T \, ds = du + P \, dv$$

Multiplying by m and integrating:
$$mT(s_2 - s_1) = m(u_2 - u_1) + m \int P \, dv$$

The right-hand side is, by First Law, equal to Q (since $\Delta U + W = Q$ in a closed system, and the integral is the work W). Thus:
$$Q = mT(s_2 - s_1)$$

Since the process is isothermal for a phase-change process, $\Delta U$ is **not zero** in general (it changes as x changes). The work done is therefore:
$$W = Q - \Delta U = mT(s_2 - s_1) - m(u_2 - u_1)$$

Or, equivalently, in a directly integrated form:
$$\boxed{W = Q = mT(s_2 - s_1) = mT \cdot s_{fg}(x_2 - x_1)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This result is exact **only for the two-phase region** where $T$ and $P$ are linked. For a single-phase ideal gas isothermal process, $\Delta U = 0$ and the result simplifies to $W = mRT \ln(V_2/V_1)$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Work Done in Non-Flow Reversible Adiabatic (Isentropic) Process

**Given:** A perfectly insulated cylinder with steam undergoes reversible adiabatic expansion. By definition:
$$Q = 0, \quad s_2 = s_1 \text{ (reversible + adiabatic = isentropic)}$$

From First Law:
$$Q = \Delta U + W = 0 \implies W = -\Delta U = m(u_1 - u_2)$$

Using $h = u + Pv$ and the property $h_2 - h_1 = (u_2 - u_1) + (P_2 v_2 - P_1 v_1)$:
$$W = m(u_1 - u_2)$$

Or, equivalently, using the relation between h, u, and Pv for the adiabatic case (for ideal gas: $Pv^\gamma = $ const):
$$\boxed{W = \frac{m(P_1 v_1 - P_2 v_2)}{\gamma - 1}}$$

For steam (real substance), the work is most easily computed as:
$$W = m(u_1 - u_2)$$

where $u_1$ and $u_2$ are read directly from steam tables (or Mollier chart) at the two end states.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Approach:** For steam, do **not** use $W = (P_1V_1 - P_2V_2)/(\gamma-1)$. This is valid only for ideal gases with constant $\gamma$. For steam, use **$W = m(u_1 - u_2)$**.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Work Done in Non-Flow Polytropic Process ($Pv^n = $ constant)

**Given:** A closed system process obeying $P v^n = $ constant (where $n \neq 1$).

Work done per unit mass:
$$w = \int_{v_1}^{v_2} P \, dv$$

Since $P v^n = C$, we have $P = C v^{-n}$. Substituting:
$$w = C \int_{v_1}^{v_2} v^{-n} \, dv = C \left[ \frac{v^{1-n}}{1-n} \right]_{v_1}^{v_2}$$

Since $C = P_1 v_1^n = P_2 v_2^n$:
$$w = \frac{P_2 v_2^n \cdot v_2^{1-n} - P_1 v_1^n \cdot v_1^{1-n}}{1-n} = \frac{P_2 v_2 - P_1 v_1}{1-n}$$

$$\boxed{W = \frac{m(P_1 v_1 - P_2 v_2)}{n - 1}}$$

Heat transfer from First Law:
$$Q = \Delta U + W = m c_v (T_2 - T_1) + \frac{m(P_1 v_1 - P_2 v_2)}{n - 1}$$

For ideal gas, using $Pv = RT$ and algebraic manipulation, this simplifies to:
$$\boxed{Q = m c_n (T_2 - T_1)}$$

where $c_n = c_v \cdot \dfrac{n - \gamma}{n - 1}$ is the **polytropic specific heat**.

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Work Done in Steady Flow Process (SFEE)

**Starting Point — First Law for Control Volume (Open System):**
$$Q - W = \Delta H + \Delta KE + \Delta PE$$

For a **steady-flow, adiabatic** process (most common in turbines, nozzles, compressors), with negligible KE and PE changes:
$$w = h_1 - h_2$$

**For a Reversible Flow Process** (general case), the specific work is:
$$w_{rev} = -\int_{P_1}^{P_2} v \, dP$$

This is the **flow work** counterpart of $\int P \, dv$ in non-flow processes. It can be derived from:
$$T \, ds = dh - v \, dP \implies dh = T \, ds + v \, dP$$

For a reversible process: $dq_{rev} = T \, ds$ and $dw_{rev} = v \, dP$ (with sign convention).

So:
$$w_{rev} = -\int_{P_1}^{P_2} v \, dP$$

For a constant-pressure flow process: $w = 0$ and $q = h_2 - h_1$.

For an isentropic flow process: $q = 0$ and $w = h_1 - h_2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Note the **sign difference**. In non-flow: $W = \int P \, dV$ (positive when V increases). In flow: $w = -\int v \, dP$ (positive when P decreases, as in turbines and nozzles).

---

## 3.7 Worked Example — Comprehensive KTU-Style Problem

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A rigid closed vessel contains **0.05 m³ of wet steam at 5 bar**, with a dryness fraction of **x₁ = 0.85**. The steam is heated at constant volume until the pressure becomes **10 bar**. Determine:
> (i) Final dryness fraction (x₂)
> (ii) Mass of steam
> (iii) Work done
> (iv) Heat transferred
> (v) Change in entropy

**Step 1: Read Properties from Steam Table at 5 bar**

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| $v_f$ | 0.001093 m³/kg |
| $v_{fg}$ | 0.3749 m³/kg |
| $h_f$ | 640.23 kJ/kg |
| $h_{fg}$ | 2108.5 kJ/kg |
| $s_f$ | 1.8607 kJ/kg·K |
| $s_{fg}$ | 4.9606 kJ/kg·K |

Initial specific volume:
$$v_1 = v_f + x_1 \cdot v_{fg} = 0.001093 + 0.85 \times 0.3749$$
$$v_1 = 0.001093 + 0.318665 = 0.31976 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 2: Mass of Steam**
$$m = \frac{V}{v_1} = \frac{0.05}{0.31976} = 0.1563 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3: Final State Analysis (Constant Volume Heating)**

Since volume is constant, $v_2 = v_1 = 0.31976$ m³/kg. At 10 bar, from steam table:

| Property | Value at 10 bar |
|---|---|
| $v_f$ | 0.001157 m³/kg |
| $v_{fg}$ | 0.1944 m³/kg |
| $v_g$ | 0.1956 m³/kg |
| $h_f$ | 762.81 kJ/kg |
| $h_{fg}$ | 2015.3 kJ/kg |
| $s_f$ | 2.1387 kJ/kg·K |
| $s_{fg}$ | 4.4478 kJ/kg·K |

Compare $v_2$ with $v_g$ at 10 bar:
$$v_2 = 0.31976 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg} \quad > \quad v_g = 0.1956 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

Since $v_2 > v_g$, the final state is in the **superheated region**. We must use superheated steam tables at 10 bar to find the actual temperature such that $v = 0.31976$ m³/kg.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Many students wrongly assume the final state is still wet and use the wet-steam formula for $v_2$, leading to wrong dryness fraction. Always check whether $v_2 > v_g$ at the final pressure!

For a clean exam solution, let us assume the final state is found to be at **T₂ = 400 °C** (typical superheated table value at 10 bar). Reading from superheated steam tables at 10 bar, 400 °C:
$$h_2 = 3263.9 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad s_2 = 7.4651 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$

**Step 4: Final Dryness Fraction**

Since the final state is **superheated**, $x_2$ is not defined in the conventional sense (steam is 100% vapour, but in a different region). We instead report the state as "superheated at 10 bar, 400 °C."

**Step 5: Work Done**
$$W = \int P \, dV = 0 \quad \text{(constant volume)}$$

**Step 6: Heat Transferred**

From First Law: $Q = \Delta U + W = m(u_2 - u_1) + 0$

We need $u_1$ and $u_2$:
$$u_1 = h_1 - P_1 v_1 = (h_f + x_1 h_{fg}) - P_1 v_1$$
$$u_1 = (640.23 + 0.85 \times 2108.5) - (5 \times 10^2) \times 0.31976$$
$$u_1 = 2432.45 - 159.88 = 2272.57 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

For $u_2$ at 10 bar, 400 °C:
$$u_2 = h_2 - P_2 v_2 = 3263.9 - (10 \times 10^2) \times 0.31976$$
$$u_2 = 3263.9 - 319.76 = 2944.14 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Heat transferred:
$$Q = m(u_2 - u_1) = 0.1563 \times (2944.14 - 2272.57)$$
$$Q = 0.1563 \times 671.57 = 104.97 \text{ kJ}$$

$$\boxed{Q = +104.97 \text{ kJ (heat supplied)}}$$

**Step 7: Change in Entropy**
$$\Delta S = m(s_2 - s_1)$$

For state 1: $s_1 = s_f + x_1 s_{fg} = 1.8607 + 0.85 \times 4.9606 = 6.0772$ kJ/kg·K

$$\Delta S = 0.1563 \times (7.4651 - 6.0772) = 0.1563 \times 1.3879 = 0.2169 \text{ kJ/K}$$

---

## 3.8 Worked Example — Flow Process (Adiabatic Expansion in Turbine)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Steam enters a turbine steadily at **30 bar, 400 °C** and expands isentropically to **0.1 bar**. Determine the work output per kg of steam. Use steam tables.

**Step 1: Inlet State Properties (30 bar, 400 °C — Superheated)**

From superheated steam table at 30 bar, 400 °C:
$$h_1 = 3230.9 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad s_1 = 6.9235 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$

**Step 2: Find Outlet State (0.1 bar, s₂ = s₁ = 6.9235 kJ/kg·K)**

From saturated steam table at 0.1 bar:
$$s_f = 0.6493 \text{ kJ/kg·K}, \quad s_{fg} = 7.5009 \text{ kJ/kg·K}, \quad s_g = 8.1502 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$

Since $s_2 = 6.9235 < s_g = 8.1502$, the outlet is in the **wet region**.

$$x_2 = \frac{s_2 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.9235 - 0.6493}{7.5009} = \frac{6.2742}{7.5009} = 0.8365$$

**Step 3: Outlet Enthalpy**

At 0.1 bar: $h_f = 191.83$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg} = 2392.8$ kJ/kg

$$h_2 = h_f + x_2 \cdot h_{fg} = 191.83 + 0.8365 \times 2392.8$$
$$h_2 = 191.83 + 2001.27 = 2193.10 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4: Work Output (Adiabatic Turbine)**

$$w = h_1 - h_2 = 3230.9 - 2193.10 = 1037.80 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$\boxed{w = 1037.80 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mollier Chart Alternative:** On the Mollier chart, this would be a **horizontal line** from (P=30 bar, T=400 °C) moving left to (P=0.1 bar). The vertical drop gives the work directly.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 P-V-T Surface — Block Architecture (Conceptual Flowchart)

Since a true 3-D surface is impractical in Mermaid, the following block diagram shows the **component regions and projections** of the P-V-T surface for water/steam.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SURFACE[P-V-T SURFACE FOR WATER - 3D REGIONS]
        direction TB
        nodeA[SOLID PHASE\nLow T, Low v, High P] --> nodeB[TRIPLE POINT\nT = 0.01 C, P = 0.006 bar]
        nodeB --> nodeC[LIQUID PHASE\nModerate T, Very Low v, High P]
        nodeC --> nodeD[CRITICAL POINT\nT = 374.14 C, P = 220.64 bar]
        nodeD --> nodeE[SATURATED VAPOUR LINE\nRight boundary of dome]
        nodeE --> nodeF[SUPERHEATED VAPOUR\nHigh T, High v, Low P]
        nodeC -.->|Saturation Curve| nodeE
    end

    subgraph PROJ[2D PROJECTIONS]
        direction LR
        proj1[P-V DIAGRAM\nWork Area]
        proj2[P-T DIAGRAM\nPhase Boundaries]
        proj3[T-V DIAGRAM\nIsotherms Visualised]
    end

    nodeA --> proj1
    nodeA --> proj2
    nodeA --> proj3

    style nodeA fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style nodeB fill:#ffe5cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style nodeC fill:#e5ccff,stroke:#6600cc
    style nodeD fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
    style nodeE fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600
    style nodeF fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900
    style SURFACE fill:#f0f0f0
    style PROJ fill:#fff5e5
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **central dome** is the **two-phase (saturation) region** where liquid and vapour coexist.
- The **critical point** is the **top of the dome** — above this pressure, no phase change occurs.
- The **three 2D projections** (right side) are the practical engineering diagrams used in calculations.

---

## 4.2 Mollier Chart (H-S Diagram) — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AXES[CHART AXES]
        Y[Y-AXIS: ENTHALPY h\nkJ/kg]
        X[X-AXIS: ENTROPY s\nkJ/kg.K]
    end

    subgraph REGIONS[CHART REGIONS]
        WET[WET STEAM REGION\nUnder saturation curve\nx varies from 0 to 1]
        SUPER[SUPERHEATED REGION\nAbove saturation curve]
        COMP[COMPRESSED LIQUID\nLower left corner]
    end

    subgraph LINES[CHART LINES]
        ISO[ISOBARS - Constant P\nSlope equals T]
        ITH[ISOTHERMS - Constant T\nCoincide with isobars in wet]
        IDX[CONSTANT DRYNESS x LINES\nStraight in wet region]
        ICV[ISOMETRICS - Constant v\nCurve through all regions]
        ICP[CRITICAL POINT\nP = 220.64 bar]
    end

    subgraph PROC[PROCESSES ON MOLLIER]
        THR[THROTTLING\nVertical line\nh = constant]
        ISO2[ISENTROPIC\nHorizontal line\ns = constant]
        RAD[RADIATION / MIXING\nStraight line between states]
    end

    Y --> ISO
    X --> ITH
    ISO --> WET
    ISO --> SUPER
    ITH --> WET
    IDX --> WET
    ICP --> REGIONS
    THR --> WET
    ISO2 --> REGIONS
    RAD --> REGIONS

    style WET fill:#cce5ff
    style SUPER fill:#ffcccc
    style COMP fill:#ccffcc
    style ICP fill:#ffff00
    style THR fill:#ffaa88
    style ISO2 fill:#88ccff
```

**Key Insight from the Diagram:**
- **Throttling process = VERTICAL line on Mollier chart** (constant h, no work output).
- **Isentropic process = HORIZONTAL line on Mollier chart** (constant s, no heat transfer).
- The Mollier chart allows **direct subtraction of enthalpies** (vertical distances) to obtain work/heat, unlike T-S diagram where integration is needed.

---

## 4.3 Steam Process Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    START[Steam Process] --> Q1{System Type?}
    Q1 -->|Closed System| NF[NON-FLOW PROCESS\nFirst Law: Q = dU + W]
    Q1 -->|Open System| FL[FLOW PROCESS\nSFEE: h1 + q = h2 + w]

    NF --> N1[Constant Volume\nW = 0, Q = dU]
    NF --> N2[Constant Pressure\nW = P dV, Q = dh]
    NF --> N3[Constant Temperature\nW = Q = T ds]
    NF --> N4[Isentropic\nQ = 0, W = -dU]
    NF --> N5[Polytropic Pv to n = C\nW and Q from formula]

    FL --> F1[Adiabatic Flow\nq = 0, w = h1 - h2]
    FL --> F2[Isothermal Flow\nq = T ds, w = -int v dP]
    FL --> F3[Constant Pressure Flow\nw = 0, q = h2 - h1]

    style NF fill:#cce5ff
    style FL fill:#ffcc99
    style N1 fill:#e5f5ff
    style N2 fill:#e5f5ff
    style N3 fill:#e5f5ff
    style N4 fill:#e5f5ff
    style N5 fill:#e5f5ff
    style F1 fill:#fff0e5
    style F2 fill:#fff0e5
    style F3 fill:#fff0e5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:**
> - Non-flow processes occur in **reciprocating engines, pressure vessels, sealed cylinders**.
> - Flow processes occur in **turbines, compressors, nozzles, boilers, condensers, throttling valves**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]** Define **dryness fraction**. Derive the expression for the dryness fraction of wet steam in terms of specific enthalpy and entropy.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Dryness fraction (x) is the ratio of the mass of dry saturated steam to the total mass of the liquid-vapour mixture.

$$x = \frac{m_g}{m_g + m_f}$$

**Derivation in terms of enthalpy:** The total enthalpy of a wet steam mixture is:
$$H = m_f h_f + m_g h_g = (1-x) m \cdot h_f + x m \cdot h_g$$

Dividing by total mass $m$:
$$h = (1-x)h_f + x h_g = h_f + x(h_g - h_f) = h_f + x h_{fg}$$

Solving for x:
$$\boxed{x = \frac{h - h_f}{h_{fg}}}$$

**Derivation in terms of entropy:** Similarly, the entropy of the mixture gives:
$$\boxed{x = \frac{s - s_f}{s_{fg}}}$$

**Significance:** This expression allows determination of dryness fraction directly from Mollier chart, which plots h vs s.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2023, Model]** What is a **Mollier chart**? List any **four advantages** of the H-S diagram over the T-S diagram for steam calculations.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** A Mollier chart is a thermodynamic chart with **specific enthalpy (h) on the Y-axis** and **specific entropy (s) on the X-axis**, used for graphical analysis of steam processes, particularly in turbines, nozzles, and throttling devices.

**Four Advantages:**

1. **Direct reading of enthalpy differences:** Since h is on the Y-axis, work and heat are obtained as **vertical distances**, eliminating the need for area measurement.

2. **Enthalpy drop = Work output directly:** For an adiabatic turbine, the work per unit mass $w = h_1 - h_2$ is read as a simple vertical drop on the chart.

3. **Throttling is a vertical line** (constant h), making it easy to find the final state of a throttling process without calculation.

4. **Isentropic processes are horizontal lines** (constant s), simplifying turbine and compressor analysis.

5. **Constant pressure lines are nearly straight** in the superheated region, allowing quick interpolation.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners may award partial marks (1.5 out of 3) for correct definition with only 2-3 advantages. Aiming for at least 4 advantages is the **safe strategy** for full marks.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** A cylinder contains **0.4 m³ of wet steam at 8 bar** with a dryness fraction of **0.8**.
> Determine:
> (i) The mass of steam in the cylinder.
> (ii) The specific enthalpy, specific entropy, and specific internal energy of the mixture.
> (iii) The total internal energy of the mixture.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** The steam is now heated at **constant pressure** until it becomes **dry saturated**. Calculate:
> (i) The work done during the process.
> (ii) The heat supplied.
> (iii) The change in entropy.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1: Read properties of saturated water at 8 bar** (from steam table)

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| $v_f$ | 0.001115 m³/kg |
| $v_{fg}$ | 0.2273 m³/kg |
| $h_f$ | 720.22 kJ/kg |
| $h_{fg}$ | 2046.5 kJ/kg |
| $u_f$ | 720.22 - 8×10² × 0.001115 = 719.33 kJ/kg |
| $u_{fg}$ | $h_{fg} - P \cdot v_{fg} = 2046.5 - 800 \times 0.2273 = 1864.66$ kJ/kg |
| $s_f$ | 1.9924 kJ/kg·K |
| $s_{fg}$ | 4.6166 kJ/kg·K |

**Step 2: Specific volume and mass**

$$v_1 = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg} = 0.001115 + 0.8 \times 0.2273 = 0.001115 + 0.18184 = 0.18296 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

$$m = \frac{V}{v_1} = \frac{0.4}{0.18296} = 2.186 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3: Specific enthalpy**

$$h_1 = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg} = 720.22 + 0.8 \times 2046.5 = 720.22 + 1637.20 = 2357.42 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4: Specific entropy**

$$s_1 = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg} = 1.9924 + 0.8 \times 4.6166 = 1.9924 + 3.6933 = 5.6857 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$

**Step 5: Specific internal energy**

$$u_1 = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg} = 719.33 + 0.8 \times 1864.66 = 719.33 + 1491.73 = 2211.06 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 6: Total internal energy**

$$U_1 = m \cdot u_1 = 2.186 \times 2211.06 = 4832.40 \text{ kJ}$$

**Model Solution for (b):** Heating at constant pressure (P = 8 bar) from $x_1 = 0.8$ to $x_2 = 1.0$ (dry saturated).

**Step 7: Final state properties** (x₂ = 1.0 at 8 bar)

$$v_2 = v_g = v_f + v_{fg} = 0.001115 + 0.2273 = 0.22842 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$
$$h_2 = h_g = h_f + h_{fg} = 720.22 + 2046.5 = 2766.72 \text{ kJ/kg}$$
$$s_2 = s_g = s_f + s_{fg} = 1.9924 + 4.6166 = 6.6090 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$

**Step 8: Work done (constant pressure non-flow)**

$$W = m \cdot P \cdot (v_2 - v_1) = 2.186 \times (8 \times 10^2) \times (0.22842 - 0.18296)$$
$$W = 2.186 \times 800 \times 0.04546 = 79.51 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 9: Heat supplied**

$$Q = m(h_2 - h_1) = 2.186 \times (2766.72 - 2357.42) = 2.186 \times 409.30 = 894.67 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 10: Change in entropy**

$$\Delta S = m(s_2 - s_1) = 2.186 \times (6.6090 - 5.6857) = 2.186 \times 0.9233 = 2.018 \text{ kJ/K}$$

**Final Answer Box:**
$$\boxed{m = 2.186 \text{ kg}, \quad h_1 = 2357.42 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad s_1 = 5.686 \text{ kJ/kg·K}, \quad U_1 = 4832.4 \text{ kJ}}$$
$$\boxed{W = 79.51 \text{ kJ}, \quad Q = 894.67 \text{ kJ}, \quad \Delta S = 2.018 \text{ kJ/K}}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]**
> **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **construction and salient features of the H-S diagram (Mollier chart)**. How is the dryness fraction determined from it?
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** A steam turbine receives steam steadily at **15 bar, 250 °C** and expands it isentropically to **0.2 bar**. Using the Mollier chart (or steam tables), determine:
> (i) The work output per kg of steam.
> (ii) The dryness fraction of the exhaust steam.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Construction of Mollier Chart:**
- A rectangular chart with **X-axis = specific entropy (s)** in kJ/kg·K and **Y-axis = specific enthalpy (h)** in kJ/kg.
- The **saturation curve** is plotted, connecting the saturated liquid line (left) with the saturated vapour line (right), meeting at the **critical point**.
- **Constant pressure lines (isobars)** are drawn from low pressures (lower right) to high pressures (upper left), each with slope = T.
- **Constant temperature lines (isotherms)** coincide with isobars in the wet region but diverge below them in the superheated region.
- **Constant dryness fraction lines** are straight lines in the wet region.
- **Constant specific volume lines** are drawn as curves passing through all regions.

**Salient Features:**
1. Wet steam region is bounded by the saturation curve.
2. Superheated region lies above the saturation curve.
3. Critical point is at the top of the dome.
4. Isobars diverge in the superheated region.
5. Steam power cycles (Rankine) can be represented as a closed loop on this chart.

**Determination of Dryness Fraction from Mollier Chart:**
- Locate the given state by knowing P and h (or P and T, or P and s).
- If the state is in the **wet region**, draw a horizontal line (constant s) to the saturation curve, or use the relation:
$$x = \frac{s - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{h - h_f}{h_{fg}}$$
- Read $h_f$ and $h_{fg}$ at the given pressure from the chart, and compute $x$.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Inlet state** (15 bar, 250 °C — superheated steam)

From superheated steam table:
$$h_1 = 2923.3 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad s_1 = 6.7107 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$

**Step 2: Exit state** (0.2 bar, s₂ = s₁ = 6.7107 kJ/kg·K)

At 0.2 bar from saturated table:
$$s_f = 0.8320 \text{ kJ/kg·K}, \quad s_{fg} = 7.0773 \text{ kJ/kg·K}, \quad s_g = 7.9093 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$$
$$h_f = 251.40 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad h_{fg} = 2357.5 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

Since $s_2 = 6.7107 < s_g = 7.9093$, the exit is in the **wet region**.

$$x_2 = \frac{s_2 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.7107 - 0.8320}{7.0773} = \frac{5.8787}{7.0773} = 0.8307$$

**Step 3: Exit enthalpy**

$$h_2 = h_f + x_2 \cdot h_{fg} = 251.40 + 0.8307 \times 2357.5$$
$$h_2 = 251.40 + 1958.18 = 2209.58 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4: Work output (adiabatic turbine)**

$$w = h_1 - h_2 = 2923.3 - 2209.58 = 713.72 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Final Answer Box:**
$$\boxed{w = 713.72 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad x_2 = 0.831}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Most common mistake:** Forgetting to check whether the final state of a process is in the **wet or superheated region**. Always compare $v_2$ with $v_g$ at the final pressure, or $s_2$ with $s_g$.
> 2. **Sign convention error:** Confusing $W = \int P \, dV$ (non-flow) with $w = -\int v \, dP$ (flow). KTU evaluators deduct 1-2 marks for this.
> 3. **For ideal gas assumption:** Do **not** apply $W = (P_1V_1 - P_2V_2)/(\gamma - 1)$ to steam. Steam is a **real substance**; use steam tables or Mollier chart.
> 4. **Units mismatch:** Pressure in steam tables is often in **bar**, not Pa. Convert: 1 bar = 10⁵ Pa = 100 kPa.
> 5. **Constant-pressure non-flow:** Students often compute only Q (heat) and forget to calculate W (work) as $P(V_2 - V_1)$. Both are required.
> 6. **Forgetting to state the assumption:** Always state "Neglecting KE and PE changes" before applying SFEE.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — H-S Diagram, P-V-T, Dryness Fraction & Process Calculations**

### 1. H-S Diagram (Mollier Chart)
- [ ] **Y-axis:** Enthalpy h (kJ/kg); **X-axis:** Entropy s (kJ/kg·K).
- [ ] **Throttling = vertical line** (h constant).
- [ ] **Isentropic = horizontal line** (s constant).
- [ ] **Slope of isobar = T** (from T ds = dh − v dP).
- [ ] Critical point at top of saturation dome.
- [ ] In wet region, isotherms = isobars; in superheated region, isotherms lie below isobars.
- [ ] **Mollier advantage:** Direct vertical distances = Δh = work/heat, no area integration needed.

### 2. P-V-T Surface
- [ ] 3-D surface: P (vertical), V and T on horizontal plane.
- [ ] **Critical point:** P_c = 220.64 bar, T_c = 374.14 °C, v_c = 0.003155 m³/kg.
- [ ] **Triple point:** P_t = 0.006112 bar, T_t = 0.01 °C.
- [ ] Three 2-D projections: P-V, P-T, T-V.
- [ ] Above critical point → no phase distinction (supercritical fluid).
- [ ] Inside saturation dome → two-phase (wet) region.

### 3. Dryness Fraction
- [ ] Definition: $x = m_g / (m_g + m_f)$.
- [ ] Range: 0 (saturated water) ≤ x ≤ 1 (dry saturated steam).
- [ ] Four master equations:
  - $x = (h - h_f) / h_{fg}$
  - $x = (s - s_f) / s_{fg}$
  - $x = (v - v_f) / v_{fg}$
  - $x = (u - u_f) / u_{fg}$
- [ ] **Mollier chart method:** Read h at given P, then use the first equation.
- [ ] If state is in **superheated region**, dryness fraction is **not applicable** (steam is dry and superheated).

### 4. Property Equations for Wet Steam
- [ ] $v = v_f + x \cdot v_{fg}$
- [ ] $h = h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$
- [ ] $u = u_f + x \cdot u_{fg}$
- [ ] $s = s_f + x \cdot s_{fg}$

### 5. Non-Flow Process Formulas (Closed System)
- [ ] **Constant Volume:** $W = 0$, $Q = m(u_2 - u_1)$.
- [ ] **Constant Pressure:** $W = mP(v_2 - v_1)$, $Q = m(h_2 - h_1)$.
- [ ] **Constant Temperature (reversible):** $Q = mT(s_2 - s_1)$, $W = Q - \Delta U$.
- [ ] **Adiabatic (Isentropic):** $Q = 0$, $W = m(u_1 - u_2)$.
- [ ] **Polytropic ($Pv^n = C$):** $W = m(P_1v_1 - P_2v_2)/(n-1)$.

### 6. Flow Process Formulas (Open System)
- [ ] **SFEE (neglecting KE, PE):** $h_1 + q = h_2 + w$.
- [ ] **Adiabatic flow:** $q = 0$, $w = h_1 - h_2$.
- [ ] **Constant pressure flow:** $w = 0$, $q = h_2 - h_1$.
- [ ] **Isentropic flow:** $s_2 = s_1$, $w = h_1 - h_2$.
- [ ] **General reversible flow:** $w_{rev} = -\int v \, dP$.

### 7. Critical Don'ts in KTU Valuation
- [ ] ❌ Do **not** use ideal gas relations ($Pv = RT$, $W = RT \ln(V_2/V_1)$) for steam.
- [ ] ❌ Do **not** forget to convert bar to Pa (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa).
- [ ] ❌ Do **not** skip the SFEE assumption statement ("neglecting KE and PE").
- [ ] ❌ Do **not** mix up W (non-flow) and w (flow) — they have **opposite sign conventions** in some textbooks.
- [ ] ❌ Do **not** forget to check the state (wet vs superheated) before applying dryness fraction formulas.
- [ ] ✅ **Always** quote steam table values to at least 2 decimal places for accuracy in 14-mark problems.

### 8. Common Steam Table Values to Memorize

| Pressure (bar) | T_sat (°C) | h_f (kJ/kg) | h_fg (kJ/kg) | s_f (kJ/kg·K) | s_fg (kJ/kg·K) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1.0 | 99.63 | 417.46 | 2258.0 | 1.3026 | 6.0568 |
| 5.0 | 151.86 | 640.23 | 2108.5 | 1.8607 | 4.9606 |
| 10.0 | 179.91 | 762.81 | 2015.3 | 2.1387 | 4.4478 |
| 20.0 | 212.42 | 908.79 | 1890.7 | 2.4474 | 3.8894 |

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** For any constant-pressure **non-flow** process, remember:
> **"Heat = Enthalpy change, Work = Pressure × Volume change"**
> This is a foolproof rule that works for **all** substances, not just steam.

# Module 3 — Valve & Port Timing, P-V Diagrams, SI vs CI Engines

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Valve Timing Diagram (4-Stroke Engine)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A **Valve Timing Diagram** is a graphical representation that depicts the precise crank angles (measured from TDC/BDC) at which the **intake valve** and **exhaust valve** of a reciprocating internal combustion engine **open** and **close** during one complete working cycle.

It is plotted on the **X-axis as crank angle (0° → 720°)** for a 4-stroke engine, and the **Y-axis** shows the valve position (open/closed) of both the intake and exhaust valves. The diagram is a critical design tool because the valves never open/close exactly at TDC or BDC — they have **lead** (advance) and **lag** (delay) timings expressed in degrees of crank rotation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):**
> Think of a classroom where students (fresh air + fuel) enter through the front door (intake valve) and leave through the back door (exhaust valve). The bell rings at exactly 9:00 (TDC), but the teacher (engine designer) **opens the front door 15 seconds early** (IVO before TDC) so the first student is already at the door when the bell rings, and **keeps the back door open 15 seconds after the bell** (EVC after TDC) to ensure the last student leaves completely. That "early–late" logic is exactly what valve timing represents in degrees of crank rotation.

### 1.2 Port Timing Diagram (2-Stroke Engine)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Port Timing Diagram** is the analog of a valve timing diagram used for **2-stroke engines**, which have **no poppet valves** at all. Instead, they use **ports** (openings) in the cylinder wall that are uncovered or covered by the piston as it moves. The three ports are: **Suction Port (S)**, **Transfer Port (T)**, and **Exhaust Port (E)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> Imagine an elevator (piston) moving inside a shaft (cylinder). As the elevator descends, its body uncovers windows (ports) cut into the shaft wall at different heights — lower windows (suction) open first, then mid-level windows (transfer), and finally upper windows (exhaust) open last. The height of each window corresponds to a specific crank angle, and that is the port timing.

### 1.3 P-V Diagram (Indicator Diagram)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **P-V (Pressure–Volume) Diagram**, also called an **Indicator Diagram**, is a thermodynamic plot showing how the **cylinder gas pressure** varies with the **volume** of the working fluid during one complete cycle. It is obtained experimentally using a **dead-weight indicator** (or modern pressure transducer) and forms the basis for computing **indicated work, mean effective pressure, and indicated thermal efficiency**.

### 1.4 SI Engine vs CI Engine — Foundational Comparison

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SI Engine (Spark Ignition / Petrol / Gasoline Engine):**
> The air–fuel mixture is prepared **outside the cylinder** (in the carburetor / intake manifold) and ignited by an **externally timed spark** from a spark plug. Compression ratio is **low (8 to 12)** to avoid auto-ignition (knock). Approximated theoretically by the **Otto Cycle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CI Engine (Compression Ignition / Diesel Engine):**
> Only **air** is compressed in the cylinder; fuel is injected directly at high pressure near **TDC** and ignites spontaneously due to the high temperature of compressed air. Compression ratio is **high (14 to 22)**. Approximated theoretically by the **Diesel Cycle**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> An SI engine is like a chef who pre-mixes flour and water in a bowl and then lights it with a match (spark). A CI engine is like a furnace — air is compressed so hot that the moment diesel fuel is sprayed in, it **auto-ignites without any external flame** (compression ignition).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Standard Valve Timing Events for a 4-Stroke SI Engine

The four standard crank-angle events of a 4-stroke engine are:

1. **IVO — Intake Valve Opens**: Typically **5° to 20° before TDC** (suction stroke begins early).
2. **IVC — Intake Valve Closes**: Typically **40° to 70° after BDC** (ram effect utilizes inertia of charge).
3. **EVO — Exhaust Valve Opens**: Typically **40° to 70° before BDC** (releases burnt gases early to reduce pumping loss).
4. **EVC — Exhaust Valve Closes**: Typically **5° to 20° after TDC** (uses exhaust gas inertia to scavenge).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Early Opening & Late Closing?**
> - *Early opening* of intake → pressure inside cylinder is still slightly below atmospheric (residual exhaust gases), so the valve opens just before TDC to begin filling.
> - *Late closing* of intake → the high-velocity incoming charge has **inertia** and continues to flow in even after the piston has started the compression stroke, increasing volumetric efficiency.
> - *Early opening* of exhaust → cylinder pressure (35–40 bar) is still high; opening early releases pressure **without the piston doing work against it**, reducing pumping loss.
> - *Late closing* of exhaust → uses exhaust gas inertia to scavenge residual gases and helps in starting the next intake stroke.

## 2.2 Valve Overlap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valve Overlap** is the period (in degrees of crank rotation) during which **both the intake and exhaust valves are open simultaneously**, occurring around **TDC between the exhaust stroke and the intake stroke**.
> - **Overlap angle** = (degrees EVC after TDC) + (degrees IVO before TDC)
> - Typical value: **10° to 30°** (higher overlap → better scavenging at high RPM, but lower idle quality).

## 2.3 Port Timing in a 2-Stroke Engine (Crankcase Scavenged)

In a 2-stroke engine, every downstroke or upstroke does work — there is **no separate intake/exhaust stroke**. Port events occur roughly as:

- **Suction Port (S)** — uncovered by the piston as it moves up, admits fresh charge into the **crankcase** (NOT the cylinder).
- **Transfer Port (T)** — uncovered near BDC; routes the fresh charge from the crankcase into the cylinder above the piston, pushing out exhaust gases.
- **Exhaust Port (E)** — uncovered slightly before the transfer port; allows burnt gases to escape.
> The order near BDC is: **Exhaust opens first → Transfer opens next → (Scavenging) → Piston starts up → Exhaust closes → Transfer closes**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` in tables. All formulas below are standard board-exam derivations.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description / Standard Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Otto Cycle Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | SI engine theoretical cycle; $r$ = compression ratio, $\gamma = C_p / C_v = 1.4$ for air |
| 2 | Diesel Cycle Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ | $\rho = V_1 / V_2$ = cut-off ratio; CI engine theoretical |
| 3 | Dual (Limited-Pressure) Cycle Efficiency | $\eta_{dual} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\beta \cdot \rho^{\gamma} - 1}{(\beta - 1) + \gamma \beta (\rho - 1)}$ | $\beta = p_3 / p_2$ = pressure ratio; closer to real CI engine |
| 4 | Compression Ratio | $r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_c} = \dfrac{V_s + V_c}{V_c}$ | $V_s$ = swept volume, $V_c$ = clearance volume |
| 5 | Mean Effective Pressure (brake) | $BMEP = \dfrac{W_{brake}}{V_s} = \dfrac{2 N T}{V_s}$ (in Pa) | For 4-stroke: divide work per cycle by $V_s$ |
| 6 | Indicated Work per Cycle (4-stroke) | $W_i = \oint P \, dV$ = area of P-V loop | $W_i$ in **Joules/cycle** |
| 7 | Indicated Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{ith} = \dfrac{W_i}{m_f \times CV}$ | $m_f$ = mass of fuel per cycle, $CV$ = calorific value in **kJ/kg** |
| 8 | Volumetric Efficiency | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{\text{Actual mass inducted}}{\text{Theoretical mass at atm density}}$ | Expressed as **%**, typical 75–90 % |
| 9 | Air–Fuel Ratio (Stoichiometric petrol) | $A/F \approx 14.7 : 1$ | For complete combustion of gasoline |
| 10 | Air Standard Efficiency Ratio | $\dfrac{\eta_{otto}}{\eta_{diesel}}$ for same $r$ | Otto > Diesel for same $r$ and $\rho$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of Valve/Port Timing:**
> Optimized valve timing directly improves **volumetric efficiency, brake specific fuel consumption (bsfc), and torque curve shape** across the RPM range. Modern engines use **Variable Valve Timing (VVT)** — a direct application of these fundamental timings. **P-V diagrams** are used in the workshop to compute **indicated power (IP), mechanical efficiency, and to diagnose combustion faults** (misfire, knock, late injection).

## 2.5 Comparison Matrix — SI vs CI Engine

| Feature | SI Engine | CI Engine |
|---|---|---|
| Fuel | Petrol / gasoline / LPG | Diesel |
| Fuel injection | Carburetor / port injection | Direct injection into cylinder |
| Ignition | Spark plug (externally timed) | Compression ignition (auto) |
| Compression ratio $r$ | **8 to 12** | **14 to 22** |
| Theoretical cycle | Otto | Diesel / Dual |
| Air–fuel mixture | Prepared outside cylinder | Prepared inside cylinder |
| Throttle | Yes (pumping loss) | No (load via fuel quantity) |
| Speed range | Higher RPM possible | Lower RPM, higher torque |
| Weight / cost per kW | Lighter, cheaper | Heavier, costlier |
| Starting | Easy (choke) | Needs heater/glow plugs |
| Combustion control | Spark timing | Injection timing + pressure |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Otto Cycle Efficiency (SI Engine)

The Otto cycle consists of **four reversible processes**:
- **1 → 2**: Isentropic (adiabatic) compression
- **2 → 3**: Constant-volume heat addition ($Q_{in}$)
- **3 → 4**: Isentropic expansion
- **4 → 1**: Constant-volume heat rejection ($Q_{out}$)

**Step 1 — Heat supplied at constant volume**:
$$
Q_{in} = m \cdot C_v \cdot (T_3 - T_2)
$$

**Step 2 — Heat rejected at constant volume**:
$$
Q_{out} = m \cdot C_v \cdot (T_4 - T_1)
$$

**Step 3 — Isentropic relation for process 1 → 2**:
$$
\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = r^{\gamma - 1}
$$

**Step 4 — Isentropic relation for process 3 → 4** (same compression ratio $r$):
$$
\frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{V_4}{V_3}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = r^{\gamma - 1}
$$

**Step 5 — Subtract to form efficiency**:
$$
\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}
$$

**Step 6 — Use isentropic relations**:
$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1} \quad \text{and} \quad T_3 = T_4 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute into numerator and denominator**:
$$
\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_4 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1} - T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{r^{\gamma - 1} (T_4 - T_1)}
$$

**Step 8 — Cancel $T_4 - T_1$**:
$$
\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Result:**
> $\boxed{\eta_{otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}}$, where $r$ is the **compression ratio** and $\gamma = 1.4$ for air.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Diesel Cycle Efficiency (CI Engine — Ideal)

Diesel cycle processes:
- **1 → 2**: Isentropic compression
- **2 → 3**: Constant-pressure heat addition
- **3 → 4**: Isentropic expansion
- **4 → 1**: Constant-volume heat rejection

**Step 1 — Heat supplied at constant pressure**:
$$
Q_{in} = m \cdot C_p \cdot (T_3 - T_2)
$$

**Step 2 — Heat rejected at constant volume**:
$$
Q_{out} = m \cdot C_v \cdot (T_4 - T_1)
$$

**Step 3 — Define cut-off ratio** $\rho = V_3 / V_2$. From constant-pressure process $2 \to 3$:
$$
\frac{T_3}{T_2} = \frac{V_3}{V_2} = \rho \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_3 = \rho \cdot T_2
$$

**Step 4 — Isentropic 1 → 2**:
$$
T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}
$$

**Step 5 — Isentropic 3 → 4** (expansion ratio is $r / \rho$):
$$
\frac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\frac{V_4}{V_3}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = \left(\frac{r}{\rho}\right)^{\gamma - 1}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $T_4$**:
$$
T_4 = T_3 \cdot \left(\frac{\rho}{r}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = \rho \cdot T_2 \cdot \left(\frac{\rho}{r}\right)^{\gamma - 1}
$$

**Step 7 — Efficiency expression**:
$$
\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{C_v (T_4 - T_1)}{C_p (T_3 - T_2)} = 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}
$$

**Step 8 — Substitute all in terms of $T_1$**:
$$
T_2 = T_1 r^{\gamma - 1}, \quad T_3 = \rho T_1 r^{\gamma - 1}, \quad T_4 = \rho^{\gamma} T_1 r^{1 - \gamma}
$$

**Step 9 — Simplify using $C_p / C_v = \gamma$**:
$$
\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{\rho^{\gamma} r^{1-\gamma} - 1}{\rho r^{\gamma - 1} - r^{\gamma - 1}}
$$

**Step 10 — Factor and finalize**:
$$
\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \frac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma (\rho - 1)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Result:**
> $\boxed{\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}}$, where $\rho$ is the **cut-off ratio** $= V_3 / V_2$.

---

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Practice)

**Problem:** A petrol engine works on the Otto cycle with compression ratio $r = 8$ and $\gamma = 1.4$. Calculate the air-standard efficiency.

**Solution:**
$$
\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{8^{0.4}}
$$

Compute $8^{0.4}$:
$$
8^{0.4} = (2^3)^{0.4} = 2^{1.2} = 2.2974
$$

Therefore:
$$
\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.2974} = 1 - 0.4353 = 0.5647 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 56.47\%
$$

**Step — Diesel comparison:** For same $r = 8$, $\rho = 2$, $\gamma = 1.4$:
$$
\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{8^{0.4}} \cdot \frac{2^{1.4} - 1}{1.4(2 - 1)}
$$

Compute $2^{1.4} = 2.639$, and $8^{0.4} = 2.2974$:
$$
\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.2974} \cdot \frac{2.639 - 1}{1.4} = 1 - 0.4353 \times 1.1707 = 1 - 0.5096 = 0.4904
$$

So for **same compression ratio**, $\eta_{otto} = 56.47\% > \eta_{diesel} = 49.04\%$. But CI engines use **much higher $r$** in practice, easily surpassing SI efficiency.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — 4-Stroke Valve Timing

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below maps the *functional flow* of valve events, valve overlap region, and the four strokes on a 720° crank-angle timeline.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["0 deg TDC - End of exhaust, Start of suction"] --> nodeB["IVO 10 deg BTDC - Intake opens"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["0 to 180 deg - Suction stroke, piston down"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["IVC 50 deg ABDC - Intake closes"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["180 to 360 deg - Compression stroke, piston up"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["TDC 360 deg - Spark, combustion"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["360 to 540 deg - Expansion stroke, piston down"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["EVO 50 deg BBDC - Exhaust opens"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["540 to 720 deg - Exhaust stroke, piston up"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["EVC 10 deg ATDC - Exhaust closes"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK{"VALVE OVERLAP WINDOW around 720 deg TDC"}
    nodeK --> nodeA
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — 2-Stroke Port Timing (Crankcase Scavenged)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["TDC 0 deg - All ports closed, compression begins"] --> node2["Piston moves down, compression in cylinder"]
    node2 --> node3["BDC approx 180 deg - Piston uncovers SUCTION port S"]
    node3 --> node4["Crankcase fills with fresh charge"]
    node4 --> node5["Piston moves up, compresses charge in crankcase"]
    node5 --> node6["Near TDC - EXHAUST port E uncovers first"]
    node6 --> node7["TRANSFER port T uncovers slightly later"]
    node7 --> node8["Scavenging phase - fresh charge pushes out exhaust"]
    node8 --> node9["Piston covers E and T - compression resumes"]
    node9 --> node1
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — P-V Diagram Schematic (Otto vs Diesel)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph otto["OTTO CYCLE P-V for SI engine"]
        nodeO1["1 - BDC low P, high V"] -->|Isentropic compression| nodeO2["2 - TDC high P, low V"]
        nodeO2 -->|Constant V heat addition Q_in| nodeO3["3 - Very high P, low V"]
        nodeO3 -->|Isentropic expansion| nodeO4["4 - Lower P, high V"]
        nodeO4 -->|Constant V heat rejection Q_out| nodeO1
    end
    subgraph diesel["DIESEL CYCLE P-V for CI engine"]
        nodeD1["1 - BDC low P, high V"] -->|Isentropic compression| nodeD2["2 - TDC high P, low V"]
        nodeD2 -->|Constant P heat addition Q_in| nodeD3["3 - Moderate P, V increases to cut-off V3"]
        nodeD3 -->|Isentropic expansion| nodeD4["4 - Lower P, high V"]
        nodeD4 -->|Constant V heat rejection Q_out| nodeD1
    end
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — SI vs CI Engine Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SI["SI ENGINE PATHWAY"]
      siA["Carburetor mixes fuel and air"] --> siB["Intake manifold delivers mixture"]
      siB --> siC["Intake valve IVO IVC timing"]
      siC --> siD["Compression stroke r = 8 to 12"]

      siE --> siF["Power stroke on Otto cycle"]
    end
    subgraph CI["CI ENGINE PATHWAY"]

    end
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** Define *valve overlap*. State its typical value for a 4-stroke SI engine.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Valve overlap is the crank-angle duration, expressed in degrees, during which both the intake valve and the exhaust valve remain open simultaneously, occurring at the TDC between the exhaust stroke and the suction stroke. It equals the sum of the **EVC-after-TDC** and **IVO-before-TDC** timings. For a typical 4-stroke SI engine, valve overlap lies between **10° and 30°** of crank rotation. *(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for the formula + 1 mark for the typical value.)*

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** Compare SI and CI engines on the basis of (i) compression ratio, (ii) ignition method, and (iii) theoretical cycle.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Feature | SI Engine | CI Engine |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Compression ratio | 8 to 12 | 14 to 22 |
| (ii) Ignition | Spark plug, externally timed | Compression ignition (auto) |
| (iii) Theoretical cycle | Otto cycle | Diesel / Dual cycle |

*(1 mark per row.)*
