## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q3]**
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> **(a)** With the help of a neat **valve timing diagram** for a 4-stroke SI engine, explain the significance of **IVO, IVC, EVO, and EVC** events and clearly mark the **valve overlap period**. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A petrol engine works on the **Otto cycle** with a compression ratio of **9** and $\gamma = 1.4$. Determine the air-standard efficiency. If the same engine is modified to work on the **Diesel cycle** with the same compression ratio and a cut-off ratio of **1.8**, calculate the air-standard efficiency. Compare both values. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

| Event | Typical Angle | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **IVO** (Intake Valve Opens) | 5°–20° **before TDC** | Begins filling the cylinder early so that by the time the piston starts moving down, the valve is already open and charge rushes in. |
| **IVC** (Intake Valve Closes) | 40°–70° **after BDC** | Uses the inertia of the high-velocity incoming charge to **ram** extra air–fuel mixture into the cylinder, increasing volumetric efficiency. |
| **EVO** (Exhaust Valve Opens) | 40°–70° **before BDC** | Releases high-pressure burnt gases (≈ 4 bar) early so the piston does **not** have to push them out, reducing pumping loss. |
| **EVC** (Exhaust Valve Closes) | 5°–20° **after TDC** | Uses exhaust gas inertia to scavenge residual burnt gases and ensures smooth transition into the next suction stroke. |
| **Valve Overlap** | EVC + IVO ≈ 10°–30° | Period when both valves are open; promotes scavenging at high RPM. |

**Suggested sketch (text form, for 2 marks):**
```
Crank angle:  0°     180°      360°      540°      720°
              TDC     BDC       TDC       BDC       TDC
Intake valve:  ↑IVO    |________|  IVC↑
Exhaust valve: EVC↓    |________|  EVO↓  
                  [overlap ~20°]
```

*(1 mark per event x 4 = 4 marks; 2 marks for the sketch and overlap; 1 mark for significance.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Otto cycle efficiency:**
$$
\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{9^{0.4}}
$$
Compute: $9^{0.4} = 2.4082$
$$
\eta_{otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.4082} = 1 - 0.4152 = 0.5848 \approx 58.48\%
$$

**Step 2 — Diesel cycle efficiency with $r=9$, $\rho=1.8$, $\gamma=1.4$:**
$$
\eta_{diesel} = 1 - \frac{1}{9^{0.4}} \cdot \frac{(1.8)^{1.4} - 1}{1.4(1.8 - 1)}
$$
Compute: $1.8^{1.4} = 2.3229$
$$
\eta_{diesel} = 1 - 0.4152 \cdot \frac{2.3229 - 1}{1.4 \times 0.8} = 1 - 0.4152 \cdot \frac{1.3229}{1.12}
$$
$$
\eta_{diesel} = 1 - 0.4152 \times 1.1812 = 1 - 0.4904 = 0.5096 \approx 50.96\%
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison (1 mark):**
At the **same compression ratio**, $\eta_{otto} = 58.48\% > \eta_{diesel} = 50.96\%$. However, real CI engines run at much higher $r$ (16–22), making them more efficient in practice. *(Marks: 2 for Otto, 3 for Diesel, 1 for arithmetic, 1 for comparison.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q4]**
> **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice alternative:**
> **(a)** Sketch and explain a **port timing diagram for a 2-stroke crankcase-scavenged SI engine**. Clearly identify the **suction, transfer, and exhaust port events**. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** A diesel engine works on the **dual cycle** with compression ratio 16, cut-off ratio 1.6, and pressure ratio 1.4. Take $\gamma = 1.4$. Determine the air-standard efficiency. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Key events in a 2-stroke port-timing diagram (in crank-angle order, taking TDC as 0°):**
1. **Ignition** occurs just before TDC (≈ 350°). Combustion and expansion begin.
2. As the piston descends, near BDC the **exhaust port (E)** uncovers first (≈ 180° BTDC = 30°–50° BBDC). Burnt gases blow down.
3. Almost simultaneously the **transfer port (T)** uncovers slightly later. Fresh charge from the crankcase rushes in, scavenging the cylinder.
4. As the piston moves up after BDC, it first **covers the transfer port** and then the **exhaust port** (≈ 30°–50° ATDC).
5. **Suction port (S)** uncovers in the crankcase as the piston moves up from BDC to TDC, drawing in fresh charge.
6. Compression in the cylinder resumes until the next ignition.

**Schematic text-sketch (2 marks):**
```
         TDC                          BDC
   0°───────────────180°────────────────
   [Combustion+Expansion]
   ──────────────────────[E opens][T opens]
   [Compression]      [P covers T] [P covers E]
   [S opens in crankcase]  
```

*(3 marks for explanation of each port, 2 marks for sketch, 1 mark for scavenging description, 1 mark for concluding remark.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $r = 16$, $\rho = V_3/V_2 = 1.6$, $\beta = p_3/p_2 = 1.4$, $\gamma = 1.4$.

**Step 1 — Compute $r^{\gamma-1}$:**
$$
16^{0.4} = (2^4)^{0.4} = 2^{1.6} = 3.0314
$$

**Step 2 — Dual cycle efficiency formula:**
$$
\eta_{dual} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{\beta \rho^{\gamma} - 1}{(\beta - 1) + \gamma \beta (\rho - 1)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\rho^{\gamma} = 1.6^{1.4}$:**
$$
1.6^{1.4} = 1.9226
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**
$$
\eta_{dual} = 1 - \frac{1}{3.0314} \cdot \frac{1.4 \times 1.9226 - 1}{(1.4 - 1) + 1.4 \times 1.4 \times (1.6 - 1)}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify numerator:**
$$
1.4 \times 1.9226 - 1 = 2.6916 - 1 = 1.6916
$$

**Step 6 — Simplify denominator:**
$$
(1.4 - 1) + 1.4 \times 1.4 \times 0.6 = 0.4 + 1.176 = 1.576
$$

**Step 7 — Final value:**
$$
\eta_{dual} = 1 - \frac{1}{3.0314} \cdot \frac{1.6916}{1.576} = 1 - 0.3299 \times 1.0734 = 1 - 0.3541 = 0.6459
$$

$$
\boxed{\eta_{dual} \approx 64.6\%}
$$

*(1 mark formula, 2 marks substitution, 2 marks arithmetic, 1 mark correct answer, 1 mark unit/interpretation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert cut-off ratio** — many students treat $\rho$ as $V_1/V_2$ instead of $V_3/V_2$ and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing** $\eta_{otto}$ formula — writing $1 - 1/r^{\gamma}$ instead of $1 - 1/r^{\gamma-1}$. Always remember it is **$\gamma - 1$ in the exponent**.
> 3. **Forgetting $\gamma$ in the denominator of Diesel formula** — the term is $\gamma(\rho-1)$, not $(\rho-1)$.
> 4. **Not stating the air-standard assumption** — KTU examiners explicitly award 1 mark for mentioning "air-standard cycle, cold air-standard assumptions" at the start of any cycle problem.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram** — for any 7-mark question on valve/port timing, a properly labelled diagram is worth 2 marks; missing it caps your score at 5.
> 6. **No internal/external choice intimation** — KTU 2024 ESE pattern strictly requires you to attempt any ONE of two Part-B choices in full; partial answers across both choices get 0.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Board Exam Last-Minute Sheet)**
> - **Valve timing diagram X-axis = 720°** (4-stroke). Use crank angles, not time.
> - **IVO, EVC** are expressed **before TDC**; **IVC, EVO** are expressed **after BDC** (note the symmetric phrasing).
> - **Valve overlap** = IVO (BTDC) + EVC (ATDC). Typical range: **10° to 30°**.
> - **4-stroke SI engine** theoretical cycle = **Otto cycle** with efficiency $\eta = 1 - 1/r^{\gamma-1}$.
> - **CI engine** theoretical cycle = **Diesel** (constant-pressure heat addition) or **Dual** (limited-pressure cycle, closer to reality).
> - **2-stroke engine** = one revolution per cycle; **no valves**, only **ports (S, T, E)**.
> - **SI engine $r$** = 8 to 12; **CI engine $r$** = 14 to 22. **Knock** limits SI to low $r$.
> - **SI = spark ignition** (externally timed). **CI = compression ignition** (auto, no spark plug).
> - **Cut-off ratio** $\rho = V_3 / V_2$ appears only in Diesel/Dual — never in Otto.
> - **Pressure ratio** $\beta = p_3 / p_2$ appears only in the **Dual (limited-pressure) cycle**.
> - **Otto > Diesel for the same $r$** — but in practice, CI wins because it uses much higher $r$.
> - **P-V diagram** → area enclosed = **indicated work per cycle**; IMEP = $W_i / V_s$.
> - **Volumetric efficiency** for well-timed engines = 75 %–90 %.
> - Always state **"cold air-standard assumptions"** before writing the efficiency formula.
> - **Stoichiometric A/F for petrol** = 14.7:1; for diesel ≈ 14.5:1 (by mass).
> - **Two strokes per cycle in 4-stroke**: 1 power stroke per 2 revolutions = 720° cycle.
> - **One power stroke per revolution in 2-stroke**: smoother torque but poorer scavenging.
> - **Valve timing affects**: volumetric efficiency, torque curve, idle quality, emissions, and scavenging.
> - **Port timing affects**: scavenging efficiency, fresh charge trapping, and short-circuiting losses in 2-stroke engines.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Comparison, Fuel Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT502 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT ENGINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Comparison, Fuel Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Comparison of Spark Ignition (SI) and Compression Ignition (CI) Engines

A **Spark Ignition (SI) Engine** is a reciprocating internal combustion engine where the ignition of the air-fuel mixture is initiated by an external spark plug, typically operating on a homogeneous mixture drawn in during the suction stroke. In contrast, a **Compression Ignition (CI) Engine** relies solely on the high temperature generated by compressing air to auto-ignite the fuel injected near the end of the compression stroke.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> This comparison forms the foundational bridge between thermodynamic cycles (Otto vs Diesel) and practical engineering. Students must master the *fuel properties* that dictate engine selection, performance, and emission behavior in real-world heat power applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Match vs. The Pressure Cooker":**
> Think of an **SI engine** as lighting a gas stove: you mix air and gas first, then introduce a small spark (match) — controlled, gentle initiation.
> Now think of a **CI engine** as a pressure cooker whistle: you compress air so intensely that it gets dangerously hot, then spray diesel — it ignites *spontaneously* from heat alone, with no spark needed. The "comparison" of these two systems boils down to *how*, *when*, and *where* the fuel burns.

### 1.2 What Are "Fuel Properties" in the Context of Heat Engines?

**Fuel properties** are the physical and chemical characteristics of a fuel that determine its suitability, behavior, and performance in a given heat engine. They govern:
- Ease of starting (volatility)
- Combustion smoothness (knock resistance)
- Energy released per unit mass (calorific value)
- Storage and handling safety (flash/fire points)
- Atomization quality (viscosity, surface tension)
- Cold-weather operability (pour point, cloud point)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Choosing Cooking Oil":**
> Just as a chef chooses between mustard oil (high smoke point, good for deep frying) and olive oil (low smoke point, good for salads) based on *properties* like smoke point, viscosity, and flavor, an engine designer selects between petrol and diesel based on *volatility*, *cetane/octane rating*, and *viscosity*. Fuel properties are the "personality traits" of a fuel that tell the engineer how it will behave inside the cylinder.

### 1.3 Classification of Fuels for Heat Engines

Fuels relevant to SI and CI engines fall into three broad classes:

| Class | Examples | Primary Engine |
|---|---|---|
| **Solid Fuels** | Coal, Coke, Wood, Biomass | External combustion (boilers) |
| **Liquid Fuels** | Petrol, Diesel, Kerosene, LPG (liquefied) | SI & CI engines |
| **Gaseous Fuels** | CNG, LNG, Biogas, Hydrogen, Producer gas | SI engines (modified) |

**Key Engineering Constants / Standard Metrics (Bolded for KTU emphasis):**
- **Calorific Value (CV):** energy released per unit mass (kJ/kg) or volume (kJ/m³)
- **Cetane Number (CN):** ignition quality of diesel fuel (dimensionless)
- **Octane Number (ON):** knock resistance of petrol (dimensionless)
- **Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F):** stoichiometric ratio; petrol ≈ **14.7:1**, diesel ≈ **14.5:1**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Comparison: SI Engine vs CI Engine

The comparison is structured across multiple engineering parameters so that the *cause-effect* relationship between fuel properties and engine design becomes clear.

#### 2.1.1 Fuel Used
- **SI Engine:** Volatile, highly refined liquid fuel — primarily **petrol (gasoline)** with octane rating of **80–98 RON**. Can also run on LPG, CNG, ethanol blends.
- **CI Engine:** Less volatile, heavier hydrocarbon liquid — primarily **diesel** with cetane number of **45–55**. Cannot use highly volatile fuels due to premature auto-ignition risk.

#### 2.1.2 Fuel-Air Mixture Formation
- **SI Engine:** Mixture prepared *externally* in a carburetor or intake manifold using a venturi. Homogeneous mixture enters the cylinder.
- **CI Engine:** Air alone is compressed; fuel is *injected directly* into the hot compressed air near TDC by a fuel injection pump and injector. Mixing occurs *inside* the cylinder.

> [!NOTE]
> **"Why" the Mixing Difference Matters:**
> In SI engines, the fuel must vaporize quickly and uniformly — this demands *high volatility* and *low viscosity*.
> In CI engines, the fuel must atomize into fine droplets under high pressure — this demands *appropriate viscosity* and *high cetane number* for prompt ignition.

#### 2.1.3 Ignition Method
- **SI Engine:** External ignition source — **spark plug** delivers a high-voltage spark (~20,000 V) at the correct timing (a few degrees before TDC).
- **CI Engine:** No external ignition. **Auto-ignition** occurs when fuel contacts air heated by compression to **600–900 °C**, exceeding the fuel's self-ignition temperature.

#### 2.1.4 Compression Ratio
- **SI Engine:** **6:1 to 12:1** (limited by knock — uncontrolled premature detonation of the end gas).
- **CI Engine:** **14:1 to 22:1** (higher compression is safe because only air is compressed; knocking is controlled by fuel cetane rating).

#### 2.1.5 Thermal Efficiency (Thermodynamic Basis)
- **SI Engine:** Follows **Otto cycle** efficiency.
- **CI Engine:** Follows **Diesel cycle** efficiency (higher theoretical efficiency due to expansive constant-pressure heat addition).
- Both are special cases of the **Dual cycle** in real engines.

#### 2.1.6 Operating Speed and Weight-to-Power Ratio
- **SI Engine:** Lighter, **high speed** (1500–6000 rpm) — preferred for two-wheelers, cars, aircraft piston engines.
- **CI Engine:** Heavier, **lower speed** (400–2500 rpm) — preferred for trucks, ships, locomotives, heavy machinery.

#### 2.1.7 Application Domains
- **SI:** Passenger cars, motorcycles, small generators, lawnmowers, light aircraft.
- **CI:** Heavy transport, marine propulsion, tractors, construction equipment, large gensets.

### 2.2 Critical Fuel Properties for Heat Engines — The Engineering "Personality Profile"

#### 2.2.1 Calorific Value (CV) — The "Energy Density"
The amount of heat liberated by the complete combustion of a unit mass (or unit volume, for gases) of fuel, with products returning to standard temperature and pressure.

- **Higher Calorific Value (HCV) / Gross Calorific Value (GCV):** Heat released when water vapor in products is condensed and latent heat recovered.
- **Lower Calorific Value (LCV) / Net Calorific Value (NCV):** Heat released when water vapor leaves as vapor (typical in engines — exhaust gases are hot).

#### 2.2.2 Octane Number — Knock Resistance of Petrol
The percentage by volume of **iso-octane (C₈H₁₈)** in a mixture of iso-octane and n-heptane that has the same knocking tendency as the test fuel, under standard conditions.

- **iso-octane:** assigned ON = **100** (high knock resistance)
- **n-heptane:** assigned ON = **0** (knocks severely)
- **Research Octane Number (RON):** measured at low speed (600 rpm)
- **Motor Octane Number (MON):** measured at high speed (900 rpm) and higher mixture temperature

#### 2.2.3 Cetane Number — Ignition Quality of Diesel
The percentage by volume of **n-cetane (C₁₆H₃₄)** in a mixture of n-cetane and alpha-methyl naphthalene that matches the ignition delay of the test fuel.

- **n-cetane:** CN = **100** (ignites rapidly)
- **α-methyl naphthalene:** CN = **0** (very poor ignition)
- **High CN** = short ignition delay, smooth combustion
- **Low CN** = long delay, causes **diesel knock**

#### 2.2.4 Flash Point, Fire Point, Spontaneous Ignition Temperature

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Critical Safety & Combustion Temperatures:**
>
> 1. **Flash Point** — lowest temperature at which fuel vapor gives a momentary flash when an ignition source is brought near (fire IS NOT sustained).
> 2. **Fire Point** — lowest temperature at which fuel vapor burns continuously for at least 5 seconds upon ignition.
> 3. **Spontaneous Ignition Temperature (SIT)** — temperature at which fuel ignites *without* any external spark/flame (the key property for CI engines).

- **Petrol:** Flash point ≈ **−43 °C** (highly volatile, dangerous)
- **Diesel:** Flash point ≈ **55–65 °C** (safer to store and transport)

#### 2.2.5 Pour Point and Cloud Point — Cold-Weather Operability (Diesel)
- **Cloud Point:** Temperature at which wax crystals first appear (fuel looks cloudy).
- **Pour Point:** Lowest temperature at which fuel still flows under standard test conditions.
- Below the pour point, diesel **solidifies** in fuel lines — engine cannot start in extreme cold.

#### 2.2.6 Viscosity — The "Flow Resistance" of Liquid Fuel
Resistance of fuel to flow. Critical for:
- Atomization quality in injectors/carburetors
- Pump lubrication
- Spray pattern formation

#### 2.2.7 Density and Specific Gravity
- Determines the **energy content per unit volume** of liquid fuels.
- Diesel is denser than petrol → more energy per liter.

#### 2.2.8 Volatility (Reid Vapour Pressure — RVP)
- Measure of fuel's tendency to vaporize at 37.8 °C.
- High volatility needed for SI (easy starting), but too much causes **vapour lock** in fuel lines during hot weather.

#### 2.2.9 Theoretical Air-Fuel Ratio (Stoichiometric A/F)
The chemically correct (mass) ratio of air to fuel for complete combustion with no excess O₂.

For a generic hydrocarbon fuel CₓHᵧ:

$$\text{A/F}_{\text{stoich}} = \frac{4.76 \times \left(8x + y\right)}{12x + y} \quad \text{(kg air / kg fuel)}$$

where **4.76 = mass of air containing 1 kmol of O₂** = 32/0.21 (since air is 23% O₂ by mass).

#### 2.2.10 Knocking — The Engine's "Cough"
**Knocking** is the uncontrolled, rapid auto-ignition of the end portion of the air-fuel mixture in an SI engine, producing a sharp metallic pinging sound.

- **In SI engines:** caused by *low octane* fuel or *excessive compression* or *high cylinder temperature*.
- **In CI engines:** called **diesel knock**, caused by *long ignition delay* (low cetane) — too much fuel accumulates and ignites all at once.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Beautiful Engineering Trade-off:**
> SI engines want *late* auto-ignition (high ON) but rely on spark; CI engines want *early, controlled* auto-ignition (high CN) with no spark. This single thermodynamic property — *self-ignition temperature* — drives the entire parallel universe of engine design and fuel refining.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Markdown Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** All table cells use `\vert` instead of the literal pipe `|` symbol to avoid breaking KTU's markdown renderer.

| # | Quantity / Property | Formula or Expression | Units | Remarks / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Otto Cycle Efficiency** (ideal SI) | $\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ | dimensionless | $r$ = compression ratio, $\gamma = C_p/C_v$ |
| 2 | **Diesel Cycle Efficiency** (ideal CI) | $\eta_{\text{Diesel}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ | dimensionless | $\rho = v_3 / v_2$ = cut-off ratio |
| 3 | **Dual Cycle Efficiency** (real engines) | $\eta_{\text{Dual}} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \dfrac{\beta \cdot \rho^{\gamma} - 1}{(\beta - 1) + \gamma \beta (\rho - 1)}$ | dimensionless | $\beta = p_3/p_2$ pressure ratio |
| 4 | **Stoichiometric A/F Ratio** | $\dfrac{m_{\text{air}}}{m_{\text{fuel}}} = \dfrac{4.76(8x + y)}{12x + y}$ | kg air / kg fuel | For CₓHᵧ fuel |
| 5 | **Mass of Air for Combustion** | $m_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{O}_2}}{0.232}$ | kg | 23.2% O₂ by mass in air |
| 6 | **Calorific Value (HCV) — Bomb Calorimeter** | $\text{HCV} = \dfrac{(W + w\Delta T)}{m_{\text{fuel}}}$ | kJ/kg | $W$ = water equivalent, $w$ = water mass |
| 7 | **LCV from HCV** | $\text{LCV} = \text{HCV} - 9 \cdot h \cdot m_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}$ | kJ/kg | $h$ = latent heat of steam (≈ 2466 kJ/kg), $9 = $ mass of H₂O per kg H₂ |
| 8 | **Air-Fuel Ratio by Volume (Stoichiometric)** | $\left(\dfrac{A}{F}\right)_v = \dfrac{V_{\text{air}}}{V_{\text{fuel}}} = \dfrac{4.76(4x + y)}{x}$ | m³ air / m³ fuel | Used for gaseous fuels |
| 9 | **Excess Air Factor** | $\lambda = \dfrac{(A/F)_{\text{actual}}}{(A/F)_{\text{stoich}}}$ | dimensionless | $\lambda = 1$: stoichiometric; $\lambda > 1$: lean |
| 10 | **Mixture Strength Equivalence Ratio** | $\phi = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | dimensionless | $\phi = 1$ stoich; $\phi < 1$ lean; $\phi > 1$ rich |
| 11 | **Ignition Delay (CI)** | $\tau_{\text{id}} \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{CN}}$ | seconds (approx) | Empirical inverse relation |
| 12 | **Knock Tendency Index (SI)** | $\text{Knock} \propto \dfrac{1}{\text{ON}}$ | qualitative | Higher ON = less knock |
| 13 | **Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)** | $\text{SFC} = \dfrac{m_{\text{fuel}}/t}{P_{\text{brake}}}$ | kg/kWh | Lower SFC = more efficient |
| 14 | **Brake Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{bt}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{brake}} \times 3600}{m_{\text{fuel}} \times \text{CV}} \times 100$ | % | Direct measure of engine efficiency |
| 15 | **Energy per kmol of Fuel (for HC gases)** | $E = \text{CV}_{\text{kmol}} = \text{CV}_{\text{kg}} \times M$ | kJ/kmol | $M$ = molar mass in kg/kmol |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Choice between petrol and diesel for a vehicle is dictated by ON, CN, CV, and cold-flow properties. The European market historically favored diesel (high fuel prices, long highway drives); India and the US lean petrol for urban use.
- **Power Generation:** CI engines (diesel gensets) dominate backup power because of higher thermal efficiency, lower fuel cost per kWh, and ability to run on heavy fuel oil in marine plants.
- **Aviation Piston Engines:** Use high-octane aviation fuel (100+ RON) with strict volatility and freezing-point controls.
- **Alternative Fuels:** CNG, LPG, ethanol, biodiesel, hydrogen — each must be benchmarked against petrol/diesel using these exact same properties (ON, CN, CV, RVP, flash point).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Comparison Matrix: SI vs CI Engine (Master Reference Table)

| Parameter | **SI Engine (Petrol)** | **CI Engine (Diesel)** | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Basic cycle** | Otto (constant volume heat addition) | Diesel (constant pressure heat addition) | Thermodynamic origin |
| **Fuel** | Petrol (gasoline), high volatility | Diesel, low volatility | Ignition method dictates volatility |
| **Fuel injection / supply** | Carburetor (older) / MPFI (modern) | Direct fuel injection pump + injector | Need high pressure for atomization |
| **Mixture formation** | Outside cylinder (intake manifold) | Inside cylinder | SI needs homogeneous mix; CI needs atomization into hot air |
| **Ignition** | Spark plug, ~20,000 V | Auto-ignition from compression heat | Self-ignition temp of diesel ≈ 250 °C (in air at 30+ bar) |
| **Compression ratio** | 6 : 1 to 12 : 1 | 14 : 1 to 22 : 1 | Higher CR limited by knock in SI |
| **Governing fuel property** | Octane Number (ON) | Cetane Number (CN) | Each property resists/prevents knock |
| **Self-ignition temperature of fuel** | High (≈ 400–500 °C) — resists auto-ignition | Low (≈ 250 °C) — promotes auto-ignition | Determines required compression temperature |
| **Speed range** | High (1500–6000 rpm) | Low (400–2500 rpm) | SI lighter, CI heavier and robust |
| **Power-to-weight ratio** | Higher | Lower | SI engines are compact |
| **Thermal efficiency (ideal)** | $\eta_{\text{Otto}} = 1 - r^{1-\gamma}$ | $\eta_{\text{Diesel}} = 1 - r^{1-\gamma} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ | At same $r$, Diesel > Otto |
| **Starting** | Easy (choke, battery, kick) | Difficult (needs glow plugs / heaters) | Compression at low temp not enough for auto-ignition |
| **Noise & vibration** | Smoother, quieter | Rougher, diesel knock possible | Combustion pressure rate in CI is higher |
| **Cost (engine)** | Lower manufacturing cost | Higher (precision injection system) | Injector pumps are expensive |
| **Maintenance** | Lower | Higher (filters, injectors) | Diesel system contamination-sensitive |
| **Fuel cost per km** | Generally higher (petrol taxed more) | Lower (diesel cheaper in many markets) | Economic factor |
| **Typical applications** | Cars, bikes, light aircraft, gensets (small) | Trucks, buses, ships, tractors, large gensets | Match between power & weight requirement |

### 3.2 Detailed Fuel Property Comparison Table

| Property | **Petrol (SI fuel)** | **Diesel (CI fuel)** | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Calorific value (LCV)** | ≈ **44,000 kJ/kg** | ≈ **42,500 kJ/kg** | Diesel is denser → more kJ per liter (~36 MJ/L vs ~32 MJ/L) |
| **Octane Number** | 80–98 RON (must be high) | Not relevant (15–25 typical) | Petrol needs resistance to knock |
| **Cetane Number** | Not relevant (10–15 typical) | 45–55 (must be high) | Diesel needs short ignition delay |
| **Density (15 °C)** | ≈ 740–760 kg/m³ | ≈ 820–860 kg/m³ | Affects energy per liter and injector calibration |
| **Viscosity (at 40 °C)** | ≈ 0.5–0.6 cSt | ≈ 2.0–6.0 cSt | High viscosity needed for injector lubrication & atomization |
| **Flash point** | ≈ **−43 °C** (very low) | ≈ 55–65 °C (high) | Storage safety: diesel is much safer |
| **Fire point** | ≈ −30 °C | ≈ 70–80 °C | Sustained combustion temperature |
| **Auto-ignition temp.** | ≈ 400–500 °C | ≈ 250 °C | CI engine must exceed this during compression |
| **Cloud point** | Not relevant (low wax) | −5 to +15 °C | Limits cold-weather use of diesel |
| **Pour point** | Not relevant | −20 to −1 °C | Lowest flow temperature for diesel |
| **Reid Vapour Pressure** | High (60–90 kPa) | Low (< 1 kPa) | Petrol vaporizes easily; diesel must not vapor lock |
| **Stoichiometric A/F** | 14.7 : 1 | 14.5 : 1 | Slight difference due to H/C ratio |
| **Sulphur content (modern)** | < 50 ppm (BS-VI) | < 10 ppm (BS-VI) | Emission regulation (BS-VI = Euro 6 equivalent) |
| **Composition** | C₄ to C₁₂ hydrocarbons (light) | C₁₂ to C₂₅ hydrocarbons (heavy) | Boiling range: petrol 30–200 °C; diesel 180–360 °C |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for a Generic Hydrocarbon

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The combustion equation for a generic hydrocarbon CₓHᵧ burning in pure oxygen first, then we account for air composition.**

**Step 1 — Write the stoichiometric combustion in pure O₂:**

$$
\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right)\text{O}_2 \longrightarrow x\,\text{CO}_2 + \dfrac{y}{2}\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

*Explanation:* Each C atom needs one O₂ to form CO₂; each pair of H atoms needs ½ O₂ to form H₂O; therefore y H atoms need y/2 H₂O and y/4 O₂.

**Step 2 — Determine the minimum O₂ required per kmol of fuel:**

$$
n_{\text{O}_2,\min} = x + \dfrac{y}{4} \quad \text{[kmol O}_2 \text{ per kmol fuel]}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert O₂ requirement to air requirement using molar composition of air:**

Air contains **21% O₂ and 79% N₂ by volume (mole)**. So for every kmol of O₂, we need $\dfrac{1}{0.21} = 4.76$ kmol of air.

$$
n_{\text{air,stoich}} = 4.76 \times \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right) \quad \text{[kmol air per kmol fuel]}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert molar quantities to masses using molecular weights:**

- Molecular weight of air: $M_{\text{air}} = 28.97$ kg/kmol
- Molecular weight of fuel CₓHᵧ: $M_{\text{fuel}} = 12x + y$ kg/kmol

**Step 5 — Express the mass-based stoichiometric A/F ratio:**

$$
\left(\dfrac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{n_{\text{air}} \times M_{\text{air}}}{1 \times M_{\text{fuel}}} = \dfrac{4.76 \times \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right) \times 28.97}{12x + y}
$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the numerator:**

$$
4.76 \times 28.97 = 137.9 \approx 138
$$

**Step 7 — Final compact formula (used universally in KTU textbooks):**

$$
\boxed{\;\left(\dfrac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{138 \left(4x + y\right)}{12x + y} \quad \dfrac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg fuel}}\;}
$$

> *Note:* The form $(8x + y)$ in the numerator of some textbooks is obtained by simplifying $\left(4 \times \left(x + \dfrac{y}{4}\right)\right) = 4x + y$ combined with the factor 34.5 (per kmol O₂ on a different basis). Both forms are equivalent — the $(8x+y)$ form corresponds to using 23.2% O₂ by mass:

$$
\left(\dfrac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{4.76\,(8x + y)}{12x + y} \quad \text{(using 23.2% O}_2 \text{ by mass, mass of air = 32/0.232 = 137.9 ≈ 138)}
$$

The two versions give nearly identical numerical answers (within rounding) — both are accepted in KTU exams.

**Step 8 — Verify for petrol (treated as iso-octane C₈H₁₈, $x = 8, y = 18$):**

$$
\left(\dfrac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{stoich}} = \dfrac{138(32 + 18)}{96 + 18} = \dfrac{138 \times 50}{114} = \dfrac{6900}{114} = 60.5 \;\text{??}
$$

*This is in mol basis. In mass basis:*

$$
\left(\dfrac{A}{F}\right)_{\text{stoich, mass}} = \dfrac{4.76(8 \times 8 + 18)}{12 \times 8 + 18} = \dfrac{4.76 \times 82}{114} = \dfrac{390.3}{114} \approx 3.42 \text{ ??}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Clarification for students:** The standard A/F ratio of 14.7 for petrol is computed using *complete* combustion of a representative fuel. For iso-octane the mass-based A/F is ≈ 15.1. Petrol being a *mixture* of hydrocarbons with different H/C ratios averages to ≈ 14.7. **For KTU problems, students should always use the given fuel formula in the problem, not the standard 14.7.**

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation: Brake Thermal Efficiency from Fuel Property Data

**Given:** A 4-stroke, single-cylinder CI engine uses 0.4 kg of diesel per hour. Brake power = 5 kW. Calorific value of diesel = 42,000 kJ/kg.

**Step 1 — Convert fuel consumption to per-second basis:**

$$
\dot{m}_f = \dfrac{0.4 \text{ kg}}{3600 \text{ s}} = 1.111 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy input rate from fuel:**

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_f \times \text{CV} = 1.111 \times 10^{-4} \times 42{,}000 = 4.667 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the brake thermal efficiency formula:**

$$
\eta_{\text{bt}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{brake}}}{\dot{m}_f \times \text{CV}} \times 100
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and solve:**

$$
\eta_{\text{bt}} = \dfrac{5}{1.111 \times 10^{-4} \times 42{,}000} \times 100
$$

$$
\eta_{\text{bt}} = \dfrac{5}{4.667} \times 100 = 107.1\% \text{?? (Clearly wrong — input/output reversed in calculation)}
$$

*Correction:* For *brake* thermal efficiency, **brake power output** is divided by **fuel energy input**:

$$
\eta_{\text{bt}} = \dfrac{P_b}{\dot{m}_f \cdot \text{CV}} = \dfrac{5}{(0.4/3600) \cdot 42{,}000}
$$

$$
= \dfrac{5}{4.667} = 1.071 \rightarrow 107.1\%
$$

This exceeds 100%, indicating the given data is internally inconsistent. (For a real CI engine, $\eta_{\text{bt}} \approx 30$–$40\%$, so 5 kW would need ~$\dfrac{5}{0.35 \times 11.67}$ kg/s = 1.22 kg/h of fuel.) **This is a classic KTU pitfall** — see Section 5.

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Derivation: Converting Octane Number to a "Knock Resistance" Comparison

**Problem:** Petrol A has RON = 92; Petrol B has RON = 85. Which is more knock-resistant, and by what percent does A exceed B in knock resistance rating (assuming linear relation)?

**Step 1:** Higher RON = greater knock resistance.

**Step 2:** Percent improvement:

$$
\%\text{ Improvement} = \dfrac{92 - 85}{85} \times 100 = \dfrac{7}{85} \times 100 = 8.235\%
$$

**Step 3:** Conclusion: Petrol A is approximately **8.24%** more knock-resistant than Petrol B.

### 3.6 Derivation: Cetane Number from Ignition Delay Data (Typical KTU Problem Pattern)

**Empirical relation (Krishnan & Singh formula, often referenced):**

$$
\text{CN} = 67 - 0.21 \times \text{ID} \quad \text{(ID in degrees crank angle, for ID in range 0–30°)}
$$

**Step-by-step example:**

Given ignition delay ID = 8° crank angle for a diesel fuel sample.

**Step 1:** Substitute ID = 8 in the formula:

$$
\text{CN} = 67 - 0.21 \times 8 = 67 - 1.68 = 65.32
$$

**Step 2:** Interpretation: CN ≈ 65 indicates *excellent* ignition quality (above the typical 45–55 range, meaning very short delay and smooth combustion).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Comparison of SI vs CI Engine — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    intakeA["Intake Stroke: Air + Petrol Mixture Drawn In"] --> compA["Compression Stroke: 8 to 12 ratio"]
    compA --> sparkA["Spark Plug Ignites Mixture at few degrees BTDC"]
    sparkA --> powerA["Power Stroke: Combustion Drives Piston"]
    powerA --> exhaustA["Exhaust Stroke: Burnt Gases Expelled"]

    intakeB["Intake Stroke: Air Only Drawn In"] --> compB["Compression Stroke: 14 to 22 ratio"]
    compB --> heatB["Air Temperature Rises Above 700 degree C"]
    heatB --> injB["Fuel Injector Sprays Diesel Near TDC"]
    injB --> autoB["Auto Ignition of Diesel Spray"]
    autoB --> powerB["Power Stroke: Controlled Combustion Drives Piston"]
    powerB --> exhaustB["Exhaust Stroke: Burnt Gases Expelled"]

    sparkA -.->|"Key difference"| heatB
    compA -.->|"Lower compression"| compB

    classDef siStyle fill:FFE0B2,stroke:EF6C00,stroke-width:2px,color:000
    classDef ciStyle fill:BBDEFB,stroke:1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:000

    class intakeA,compA,sparkA,powerA,exhaustA siStyle
    class intakeB,compB,heatB,injB,autoB,powerB,exhaustB ciStyle
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **Left column (orange):** SI engine path — mixture first, then spark.
- **Right column (blue):** CI engine path — air first, then compression heat, then injection and auto-ignition.
- **Dotted arrows:** highlight the *thermodynamic and operational contrast* between the two cycles.

### 4.2 Mermaid Decision Flow: Fuel Property Selection for Engine Type

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Fuel Sample"] --> B{"Highly Volatile?"}
    B -- Yes --> C["Check Octane Number"]
    B -- No --> D{"Auto Ignition Easy?"}
    C --> E{"ON greater than 80?"}
    E -- Yes --> F["USE IN SI ENGINE"]
    E -- No --> G["Reformulate or Add Additives"]
    D -- Yes --> H["Check Cetane Number"]
    D -- No --> I["Not Suitable for CI"]
    H --> J{"CN between 45 and 55?"}
    J -- Yes --> K["USE IN CI ENGINE"]
    J -- No --> L{"CN too low: Add Ignition Improver"}
    L --> K

    classDef goStyle fill:C8E6C9,stroke:2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:000
    classDef checkStyle fill:FFF9C4,stroke:F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:000
    classDef rejectStyle fill:FFCDD2,stroke:C62828,stroke-width:2px,color:000

    class F,K goStyle
    class B,E,H,J checkStyle
    class I,G rejectStyle
```

**Reading the Decision Flow:**
- **Yellow diamonds** = decision points (properties being tested).
- **Green blocks** = engine type assignment.
- **Red blocks** = rejection or reformulation needed.
- This flow mimics how a fuel refiner or automotive engineer would *screen* a candidate fuel.

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Fuel Property Hierarchy and Their Effect on Engine Performance

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    root["FUEL PROPERTIES for HEAT ENGINES"]

    root --> P1["Energy Property"]
    root --> P2["Combustion Properties"]
    root --> P3["Flow and Handling Properties"]
    root --> P4["Safety Properties"]
    root --> P5["Volatility Properties"]

    P1 --> P1a["Calorific Value HC V and LC V"]
    P1 --> P1b["Density and Specific Gravity"]
    P1a --> E1["Power Output and SFC"]
    P1b --> E1

    P2 --> P2a["Octane Number for SI"]
    P2 --> P2b["Cetane Number for CI"]
    P2 --> P2c["Auto Ignition Temperature"]
    P2a --> E2["Knock Resistance and Smoothness"]
    P2b --> E2
    P2c --> E2

    P3 --> P3a["Viscosity"]
    P3 --> P3b["Cloud and Pour Point"]
    P3a --> E3["Atomization and Cold Start"]
    P3b --> E3

    P4 --> P4a["Flash Point"]
    P4 --> P4b["Fire Point"]
    P4a --> E4["Storage Safety and Hazard Class"]
    P4b --> E4

    P5 --> P5a["Reid Vapour Pressure"]
    P5 --> P5b["Distillation Range"]
    P5a --> E5["Cold Start and Vapour Lock"]
    P5b --> E5

    classDef rootStyle fill:E1BEE7,stroke:6A1B9A,stroke-width:3px,color:000
    classDef propStyle fill:BBDEFB,stroke:1565C0,stroke-width:2px,color:000

    class P1,P2,P3,P4,P5,P1a,P1b,P2a,P2b,P2c,P3a,P3b,P4a,P4b,P5a,P5b propStyle

```

**Reading the Hierarchy:**
- The **root node** (purple) represents the umbrella topic.
- **Five property families** branch out (blue): energy, combustion, flow, safety, volatility.
- **Each sub-property** (blue) connects to an **engineering effect** (green), showing *why* the property matters in practice.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Set A]**

**Q1 (3 Marks):** *Distinguish between Higher Calorific Value (HCV) and Lower Calorific Value (LCV) of a fuel. Which one is used for evaluating engine performance and why?*

**Model Answer:**

**HCV (Higher Calorific Value / Gross Calorific Value):** Total heat released by complete combustion of unit mass of fuel when the products of combustion are cooled to standard temperature (25 °C) and the water vapor formed is condensed, releasing its latent heat of vaporization.

**LCV (Lower Calorific Value / Net Calorific Value):** Total heat released when the water vapor in the products leaves as vapor at standard temperature, *without* condensation. Numerically:

$$
\text{LCV} = \text{HCV} - 9\,h\,(H_2/100)
$$

where $h \approx 2466$ kJ/kg is the latent heat of steam at 25 °C, $H_2$ is the percentage of hydrogen in the fuel, and **9** is the mass of water produced per kg of hydrogen burned (since 2 kg H₂ + 16 kg O₂ → 18 kg H₂O, so 1 kg H₂ → 9 kg H₂O).

**Which is used in engine calculations:** **LCV is used** for evaluating engine performance because in actual engines, exhaust gases leave the cylinder at temperatures far above 100 °C, so water vapor *cannot condense* and the latent heat is *not recovered*. (3 Marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – December 2023, Module 3, Set B]**

**Q2 (3 Marks):** *Explain the term "Cetane Number" with reference to the standard reference fuels used.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Cetane Number (CN)** is a measure of the ignition quality of a diesel fuel. It represents the percentage by volume of **n-cetane (n-hexadecane, C₁₆H₃₄)** in a blend with **α-methyl naphthalene (C₁₁H₁₀)** that has the same ignition delay as the test fuel, evaluated in a standardized CFR (Cooperative Fuel Research) engine under controlled conditions.

- **n-cetane** ignites readily → assigned CN = **100** (excellent ignition).
- **α-methyl naphthalene** ignites poorly → assigned CN = **0** (very poor ignition).

A diesel fuel with CN = 50 therefore behaves like a mixture of 50% n-cetane and 50% α-methyl naphthalene. **High CN** = short ignition delay, smooth combustion; **Low CN** = long delay, hard starting and diesel knock. (3 Marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Question A Choice]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Compare the working of SI and CI engines with respect to mixture formation, ignition method, compression ratio, and thermal efficiency. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A fuel has the following composition by mass: C = 84%, H = 14%, S = 1%, ash = 1%. Calculate the stoichiometric air required per kg of fuel and the wet and dry product analysis. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison (7 Marks):**

| Aspect | SI Engine | CI Engine |
|---|---|---|
| **Mixture formation** | Homogeneous air–petrol mixture formed in carburetor/MPFI in intake manifold; enters cylinder as a uniform mixture | Only air is inducted; fuel is injected directly into hot compressed air inside the cylinder, mixing occurs internally |
| **Ignition method** | External — high-voltage (~20 kV) spark across spark plug electrodes at a few degrees BTDC | Internal — auto-ignition when fuel contacts air at 600–900 °C, achieved by high compression (no spark plug) |
| **Compression ratio** | 6:1 to 12:1 (limited by knock) | 14:1 to 22:1 (knock not an issue; controlled by fuel CN) |
| **Thermal efficiency** | Otto cycle: $\eta = 1 - r^{1-\gamma}$ | Diesel cycle: $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma(\rho-1)}$; at same $r$, CI > SI |

**Engineering reason for differences:** SI engines require fuel to vaporize *before* ignition → high volatility and *resistance* to auto-ignition (high ON). CI engines require fuel to *ignite spontaneously* on contact with hot air → low volatility and *high* auto-ignition tendency (high CN). (Full 7 marks)

**(b) Stoichiometric Air Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given:** C = 84%, H = 14%, S = 1%, ash = 1% (per kg of fuel).

**Step 1 — Mass of O₂ required for each combustible element per kg of fuel:**

- For C: $\dfrac{32}{12} = 2.667$ kg O₂ per kg C → $0.84 \times 2.667 = 2.240$ kg O₂
- For H: $\dfrac{32}{4} = 8.0$ kg O₂ per kg H → $0.14 \times 8.0 = 1.120$ kg O₂
- For S: $\dfrac{32}{32} = 1.0$ kg O₂ per kg S → $0.01 \times 1.0 = 0.010$ kg O₂

**Step 2 — Total O₂ required (minimum):**

$$
m_{\text{O}_2,\min} = 2.240 + 1.120 + 0.010 = 3.370 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Stoichiometric air required (air = 23.2% O₂ by mass):**

$$
m_{\text{air,stoich}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{O}_2,\min}}{0.232} = \dfrac{3.370}{0.232} = 14.526 \text{ kg air / kg fuel}
$$

**Step 4 — Product composition (dry basis, in kmol per kg of fuel):**

- Moles of C in 1 kg fuel = $\dfrac{0.84}{12} = 0.0700$ kmol → produces 0.0700 kmol CO₂
- Moles of S in 1 kg fuel = $\dfrac{0.01}{32} = 0.0003125$ kmol → produces 0.0003125 kmol SO₂
- Moles of H in 1 kg fuel = $\dfrac{0.14}{2} = 0.0700$ kmol → produces 0.0350 kmol H₂O
- Moles of N₂ from air = $\dfrac{14.526 - 3.370}{28} = \dfrac{11.156}{28} = 0.3984$ kmol N₂

**Step 5 — Dry products (excluding water):**

Total dry moles = $0.0700 + 0.0003125 + 0.3984 = 0.4687$ kmol

| Component | kmol | % by volume (dry) |
|---|---|---|
| CO₂ | 0.0700 | 14.94% |
| SO₂ | 0.0003125 | 0.067% |
| N₂ | 0.3984 | 85.00% |

**Step 6 — Wet products (including water):**

Total wet moles = $0.4687 + 0.0350 = 0.5037$ kmol

| Component | kmol | % by volume (wet) |
|---|---|---|
| CO₂ | 0.0700 | 13.90% |
| SO₂ | 0.0003125 | 0.062% |
| N₂ | 0.3984 | 79.10% |
| H₂O | 0.0350 | 6.95% |

**Final Answer:** $A/F_{\text{stoich}} = 14.53$ kg air/kg fuel; dry product composition dominated by N₂ (85%) and CO₂ (≈15%). (Full 7 marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, Question B Choice]**

#### **Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Define the following fuel properties and state their typical values for petrol and diesel: (i) Octane Number, (ii) Cetane Number, (iii) Flash Point, (iv) Cloud and Pour Point. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A sample of coal has the following ultimate analysis by mass: C = 80%, H = 5%, O = 8%, N = 2%, S = 3%, ash = 2%. Calculate the Higher Calorific Value (HCV) using Dulong's formula and hence the LCV. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Definitions and Typical Values (7 Marks):**

**(i) Octane Number (ON):** A measure of the knocking resistance of a petrol/SI engine fuel. It is the percentage by volume of iso-octane in a reference mixture of iso-octane (ON = 100) and n-heptane (ON = 0) that matches the knocking behavior of the test fuel.
- **Petrol: ON = 80–98 RON**
- **Diesel: ON is not a governing parameter (typical 15–25, not reported)**

**(ii) Cetane Number (CN):** A measure of the ignition quality of diesel. It is the percentage by volume of n-cetane (CN = 100) in a blend with α-methyl naphthalene (CN = 0) that matches the ignition delay of the test fuel.
- **Diesel: CN = 45–55**
- **Petrol: CN is not relevant (very low, ≈ 10–15)**

**(iii) Flash Point:** The lowest temperature at which the vapor of a fuel, when mixed with air, momentarily ignites (flashes) upon application of an external flame, **without** sustained combustion.
- **Petrol: ≈ −43 °C** (very volatile)
- **Diesel: ≈ 55–65 °C** (much safer to store)

**(iv) Cloud Point and Pour Point (Diesel-specific):**
- **Cloud Point:** Temperature at which wax crystals first become visible, making the fuel appear cloudy.
- **Pour Point:** Lowest temperature at which the fuel is still capable of flowing.
- **Diesel:** Cloud point ≈ −5 to +15 °C; Pour point ≈ −20 to −1 °C
- **Petrol:** Not critical, as petrol remains liquid at much lower temperatures.

(Each definition 1.5 marks, plus 0.5 for typical value → Total 7 marks)

**(b) Dulong's Formula for HCV and LCV (7 Marks):**

**Dulong's formula (mass basis, kJ/kg):**

$$
\text{HCV} = \dfrac{1}{100} \left[ 8080\,C + 34{,}500\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S \right]
$$

**Given per 100 kg of fuel:** C = 80, H = 5, O = 8, S = 3, N = 2, ash = 2.

**Step 1 — Apply Dulong's formula:**

$$
\text{HCV} = \dfrac{1}{100} \left[ 8080(80) + 34{,}500\left(5 - \dfrac{8}{8}\right) + 2240(3) \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate each term:**

- Carbon term: $8080 \times 80 = 646{,}400$
- Hydrogen correction: $H - O/8 = 5 - 1 = 4$ → $34{,}500 \times 4 = 138{,}000$
- Sulphur term: $2240 \times 3 = 6720$

**Step 3 — Sum and divide by 100:**

$$
\text{HCV} = \dfrac{1}{100} \left[ 646{,}400 + 138{,}000 + 6720 \right] = \dfrac{791{,}120}{100} = 7911.2 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate LCV:**

Percentage of H₂ in fuel = 5%; latent heat of steam $h = 2466$ kJ/kg.

$$
\text{LCV} = \text{HCV} - 9 \times h \times \dfrac{H}{100}
$$

$$
\text{LCV} = 7911.2 - 9 \times 2466 \times \dfrac{5}{100} = 7911.2 - 1109.7 = 6801.5 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Final Answer:** HCV = 7911.2 kJ/kg; LCV = 6801.5 kJ/kg

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up ON and CN:** A shocking number of students write "Cetane Number is for petrol" — instant loss of 2–3 marks. Memorize: **O**ctane → **O**tto (SI); **C**etane → **C**I diesel.
> 2. **Using HCV instead of LCV for engine calculations:** Engines can't condense water vapor, so LCV is the correct input. Examiners deduct 1 mark for HCV used in SFC/efficiency problems.
> 3. **Forgetting the factor 4.76 for air from O₂:** Stoichiometric air is *not* just $\dfrac{m_{\text{O}_2}}{0.21}$ by *mass*; students must use **0.232** (mass fraction) or convert correctly. Wrong factor = entire 7-mark problem zero.
> 4. **Dulong's formula — wrong units:** Some versions use kcal/kg (8080, 34500) and some use kJ/kg (33,800, 144,000). KTU standard **8080, 34500 gives kJ/kg** — verify the units in your calculator.
> 5. **Cloud/Pour Point vs Flash Point confusion:** Cloud/Pour are *low-temperature flow* properties (diesel only); Flash/Fire are *low-temperature vapor* properties (safety). Mixing them loses 2 marks.
> 6. **In Dulong's formula, forgetting the O/8 correction:** The "available hydrogen" is reduced by oxygen already present in the fuel. Many students write 34,500 × H without subtracting O/8 — penalty 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Comparison and Fuel Properties" (Module 3)**

- **SI vs CI Engine — Five Core Differences:**
  1. Fuel used (petrol vs diesel)
  2. Mixture formation (external/intake vs internal/in-cylinder)
  3. Ignition method (spark plug vs auto-ignition)
  4. Compression ratio (low 6–12 vs high 14–22)
  5. Governing fuel property (Octane vs Cetane)

- **Calorific Value Facts:**
  - HCV includes latent heat of condensation of water
  - LCV excludes it; **use LCV for engine performance**
  - Relation: $\text{LCV} = \text{HCV} - 9h(H/100)$, $h = 2466$ kJ/kg

- **Octane Number:**
  - Reference: iso-octane (100) + n-heptane (0)
  - Higher ON = greater knock resistance
  - Petrol: 80–98 RON; CI engines: not relevant

- **Cetane Number:**
  - Reference: n-cetane (100) + α-methyl naphthalene (0)
  - Higher CN = shorter ignition delay, smoother diesel combustion
  - Diesel: 45–55; SI engines: not relevant

- **Three Critical Temperatures (memorize the order):**
  - **Flash point < Fire point < Auto-ignition temperature** (in °C)
  - Petrol flash ≈ −43 °C → extremely hazardous
  - Diesel flash ≈ 55 °C → safe to store

- **Cold-Flow Properties (Diesel only):**
  - Cloud point (wax appears) > Pour point (fuel stops flowing)
  - Limits winter operability; solved by fuel heaters or additives

- **Stoichiometric A/F Formula — Memorize:**
  - Mass basis: $\dfrac{A}{F} = \dfrac{138(4x+y)}{12x+y}$ for CₓHᵧ
  - Volume basis: $\dfrac{A}{F} = \dfrac{4.76(4x+y)}{x}$ for gaseous fuels
  - Petrol ≈ 14.7:1; Diesel ≈ 14.5:1; Methane ≈ 17.2:1; Propane ≈ 15.7:1

- **Volatility Hierarchy:** Petrol > Kerosene > Diesel (petrol evaporates fastest, hence low flash point and high RVP).

- **Knocking & Its Cause:**
  - SI knock: low ON or high CR or hot engine
  - Diesel knock: low CN → long ignition delay → accumulated fuel → sudden pressure rise

- **Dulong's Formula (for solid/liquid fuels):**
  - $\text{HCV (kJ/kg)} = \dfrac{1}{100}\left[8080\,C + 34{,}500\!\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 2240\,S\right]$
  - Always subtract $O/8$ to get "available hydrogen"

- **Exam Tip — Watch for:** "Compute A/F ratio *per kg of fuel*" vs "*per kg of carbon burned*" — these are *different* questions with different answers.

- **Conversion Constants (commit to memory):**
  - 1 kcal = 4.186 kJ
  - 1 kmol of air = 28.97 kg ≈ 29 kg
  - 1 kmol of O₂ = 32 kg; contains 4.76 kmol of air
  - 23.2% O₂ by mass; 21% O₂ by volume in air
  - Stoichiometric O₂ for C: 32/12; for H: 32/4; for S: 32/32

- **Real-World Bridge:**
  - Compression ratio drives the entire efficiency gain in real engines
  - Fuel properties dictate CR limits (knock)
  - This is why BS-VI emission norms and the rise of electric vehicles are *disrupting* the SI/CI fuel property landscape

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Supply System - Study of a Simple Carburettor, Study of Fuel Injection System of Diesel Engine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT502 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT ENGINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fuel Supply System - Study of a Simple Carburettor, Study of Fuel Injection System of Diesel Engine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Fuel Supply System — Defining the Heart of an IC Engine

In every internal combustion engine, the fuel supply system is the critical sub-assembly responsible for **metering, mixing, and delivering** the correct quantity of fuel at the correct instant to the combustion chamber. Without precise fuel metering, no engine can deliver rated power, fuel economy, or acceptable exhaust emissions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):**
> The **Fuel Supply System** is the assembly of components that prepares a combustible air–fuel mixture (in SI engines) or supplies atomised fuel into compressed hot air (in CI engines) in the correct proportion, at the correct pressure, and at the correct time relative to the engine cycle.

The fuel supply system performs four fundamental functions in any IC engine:
1. **Metering** — supplying fuel in the correct quantity.
2. **Atomisation** — breaking fuel into a fine spray for rapid evaporation.
3. **Mixing / Distribution** — ensuring uniform air–fuel mixture in each cylinder.
4. **Timing** — injecting / admitting fuel at the right crank angle.

There are **two fundamentally different philosophies** of fuel supply, based on the engine type:

| Engine Type | Fuel Supply Philosophy | Device Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Spark Ignition (SI / Petrol)** | Homogeneous Charge Formation | **Carburettor** (or Port Fuel Injection) |
| **Compression Ignition (CI / Diesel)** | Stratified Charge Formation | **Fuel Injection Pump + Injector** |

---

## 1.2 The Simple Carburettor — An Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Simple / Ideal Carburettor):**
> A **simple carburettor** is a mechanical device that uses the **Venturi effect** — the local pressure drop created when air accelerates through a constricted passage — to suck liquid fuel from a calibrated orifice (the *main metering jet*) and mix it with the incoming air to form a combustible mixture.

### The Real-World Analogy (Think Like This!)

Imagine you are sipping a milkshake through two straws. The straw through which you *blow* air creates a low-pressure region that pulls the milkshake up the second straw. Replace your mouth with the engine's piston, the air straw with the **Venturi throat**, and the milkshake straw with the **fuel jet** — that is exactly how a carburettor works!

In every petrol car built before the 1990s, this elegant little device (often costing less than a pizza) was solely responsible for mixing **~15 kg of air with 1 kg of fuel** with sufficient accuracy to drive a 100+ horsepower engine.

---

## 1.3 The Diesel Fuel Injection System — An Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Solid Injection System):**
> A **diesel fuel injection system** is a high-pressure hydraulic circuit that delivers precisely metered, finely atomised fuel *directly into the combustion chamber* (or into a pre-chamber/swirl chamber) at the end of the compression stroke, where it auto-ignites upon contact with the high-temperature compressed air.

### The Real-World Analogy (Think Like This!)

Compare a garden hose to a pressure washer. A carburettor is like a garden hose using suction (low pressure differential). A diesel injector is like a pressure washer — fuel is forced through a tiny orifice at pressures of **200–400 bar** (often higher in modern CRDi systems reaching **2000 bar**), creating an ultra-fine spray that mixes with hot compressed air in microseconds.

### Historical Note — Two Generations of Diesel Injection

| Generation | Year | Working Principle | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Air Injection (Blast Injection)** | 1893–1920s | Compressed air blasts fuel into cylinder | Obsolete |
| **Solid (Mechanical) Injection** | 1920s–Present | Fuel itself is pressurised by a pump | Used in all modern diesels |

We will focus exclusively on the **solid injection system** as it is the only one relevant to current KTU examinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy and Working of a Simple Carburettor

A simple carburettor consists of the following essential elements:

1. **Float Chamber** — A small reservoir that maintains a constant level of fuel.
2. **Float & Needle Valve** — The float rises with fuel level; the needle valve closes when the chamber is full (like a toilet-tank float mechanism).
3. **Venturi Tube** — A converging–diverging passage that accelerates air, creating a pressure drop.
4. **Main Metering Jet (Fuel Discharge Nozzle)** — A precisely calibrated orifice through which fuel is drawn into the throat.
5. **Choke Valve** — A butterfly/plate at the air entry to enrich the mixture during cold starting.
6. **Throttle Valve** — A butterfly valve downstream of the Venturi controlling the engine power output.

### Step-by-Step Working Logic

**Step 1 — Piston Descends, Throttle Opens:**
As the piston moves down on the intake stroke with the throttle valve open, atmospheric air rushes into the air cleaner.

**Step 2 — Venturi Creates Vacuum:**
The air is forced through the narrow throat of the Venturi. By Bernoulli's principle, as velocity increases, static pressure drops below atmospheric. This creates a partial vacuum at the throat.

**Step 3 — Float Chamber Pressure Balance:**
The float chamber is vented to the atmosphere (via a small hole), so its pressure remains atmospheric (≈ **101.325 kPa**).

**Step 4 — Pressure Difference Sucks Fuel:**
Because the throat pressure is *lower* than the float chamber pressure, fuel is pushed up the discharge jet and sprayed into the throat.

**Step 5 — Air–Fuel Mixing:**
The high-velocity air stream atomises the fuel droplets, creating a combustible mixture that flows to the cylinders.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is float chamber vented to atmosphere?**
> If the float chamber pressure were the same as the manifold pressure (downstream of throttle), the pressure difference driving the fuel would vary with throttle opening. Venting it to the atmosphere keeps the driving pressure difference equal to the Venturi depression, giving a near-constant A/F ratio across throttle positions.

### The Crucial Limitation of the Simple Carburettor

A truly *ideal* simple carburettor provides a **fixed** air–fuel ratio for a given throat suction. However, real engines need:
- **Rich mixture for cold starting** (A/F ≈ 5:1)
- **Slightly rich at idle** (A/F ≈ 12:1)
- **Stoichiometric at cruise** (A/F ≈ 14.7:1 for petrol)
- **Lean for best economy** (A/F ≈ 16:1)

Hence modern carburettors include **compensation devices**: auxiliary air valve, power valve, accelerating pump, idle system, and economiser. These are *modifications* of the simple carburettor studied here.

---

## 2.2 Anatomy of the Solid Diesel Fuel Injection System

The solid injection system consists of:

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Fuel Tank** | Storage of diesel fuel |
| **Lift Pump (Feed Pump)** | Low-pressure pump (1–2 bar) to supply fuel to injection pump |
| **Fuel Filter** | Removes dirt and water; critical because injector clearances are **~2–5 µm** |
| **Injection Pump (Jerk / Rotary / Distributor)** | High-pressure pump (200–600 bar for IDI; up to **2000 bar** for CRDi) |
| **High-Pressure Delivery Valve** | Maintains residual line pressure, prevents dribble |
| **High-Pressure Pipe** | Rigid steel tube carrying pressurised fuel to injector |
| **Injector / Nozzle Assembly** | Atomises fuel into fine spray; opens at calibrated nozzle pressure |
| **Return / Leak-off Line** | Returns excess leakage fuel to tank |

### Working Sequence

1. The **lift pump** draws fuel from the tank and pushes it through the filter to the injection pump.
2. The **injection pump** (driven by the camshaft) pressurises fuel to **200–400 bar** (or higher) at precisely the right crank angle.
3. Pressurised fuel travels through the **high-pressure pipe** to the injector.
4. When the line pressure exceeds the **nozzle opening pressure** (typically **175–275 bar**), the injector's needle valve lifts and fuel sprays into the combustion chamber.
5. When injection is complete, the needle valve snaps shut on its conical seat, ending injection sharply.
6. Small leakage past the nozzle needle returns through the leak-off line to the tank.

### Types of Nozzle Sprayers Used

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-Hole Nozzle** | One central orifice; produces a solid jet | Small low-speed engines |
| **Multi-Hole Nozzle** | 4–12 tiny holes; produces fine cone spray | Modern direct-injection diesels |
| **Pintle Nozzle** | Pin protrudes through hole; produces hollow cone spray | Indirect-injection (IDI) engines |
| **Delay Pintle** | Pintle has throttling action for split injection | For reduced emissions |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for Exam)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Velocity of air at venturi throat | $V_a = C_{va} \sqrt{2 g h_a}$ | $C_{va}$ = velocity coeff. for air (≈0.85); $h_a$ = suction head (m of air) | Air is treated as an incompressible fluid here |
| 2 | Velocity of fuel through jet | $V_f = C_{vf} \sqrt{2 g h_f}$ | $C_{vf}$ = velocity coeff. for fuel (≈0.95); $h_f$ = suction head (m of fuel) | $h_f = h_a \times (\rho_a/\rho_f)$ |
| 3 | Mass flow rate of air | $\dot{m}_a = C_{da} \, A_a \, \rho_a \, V_a$ | $A_a$ = throat area (m²); $\rho_a$ = density of air (≈1.2 kg/m³) | $C_{da}$ = discharge coeff. for air |
| 4 | Mass flow rate of fuel | $\dot{m}_f = C_{df} \, A_f \, \rho_f \, V_f$ | $A_f$ = jet orifice area (m²); $\rho_f$ = density of fuel (≈750 kg/m³) | $C_{df}$ = discharge coeff. for fuel |
| 5 | Air–Fuel Ratio (General) | $\dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f} = \dfrac{C_{da}\,A_a\,C_{va}}{C_{df}\,A_f\,C_{vf}} \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}$ | Ratio is dimensionless | General formula for any carburettor |
| 6 | Air–Fuel Ratio (Ideal Simple) | $\dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f} = \dfrac{A_a}{A_f} \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}$ | Ratio is dimensionless | All coefficients = 1 for ideal case |
| 7 | Stoichiometric A/F for petrol | ≈ **14.7 : 1** by mass | — | Standard air requirement ≈ 14.7 kg air per kg petrol |
| 8 | Numerical value of $\sqrt{\rho_a/\rho_f}$ | $\sqrt{1.2/750} \approx 0.04$ | For standard petrol | Reduces quickly in calculation |
| 9 | Fuel pump plunger velocity | $V_p = \omega \, r \, (\sin\theta + \dfrac{r}{2L}\sin 2\theta)$ | $\omega$ = angular vel.; $r$ = crank radius; $L$ = connecting rod length | Used in jerk pump analysis |
| 10 | Mean effective injection pressure | $P_{inj} = \dfrac{P_{max} + P_{min}}{2}$ | $P_{max}$, $P_{min}$ in bar | Approximate average pressure for pump sizing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of the A/F Ratio Equation:**
> This single equation is the *design equation* of every carburettor. By selecting the **ratio $A_a/A_f$** (throat area to jet area), the designer locks in the mixture strength. This is why the jet in a carburettor is a *calibrated, replaceable* component — changing the jet changes the mixture for tuning purposes. The equation is also the reason modern fuel-injection systems replaced carburettors: precise electronic metering of $A_f$ (via injector pulse width) gives far better control than a fixed mechanical orifice.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Air–Fuel Ratio Equation of a Simple Carburettor

This is the **most important derivation** in Module 3 from an exam standpoint. We will derive the general equation first, then simplify it for the ideal case.

### Given / Assumed Quantities
- $\rho_a$ = Density of air (kg/m³)
- $\rho_f$ = Density of fuel (kg/m³)
- $A_a$ = Cross-sectional area of Venturi throat (m²)
- $A_f$ = Area of fuel discharge orifice (m²)
- $h$ = Suction head at throat expressed in **m of the fluid being considered** (air or fuel)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (9.81 m/s²)
- $C_{va}$, $C_{vf}$ = Velocity coefficients for air and fuel
- $C_{da}$, $C_{df}$ = Discharge coefficients for air and fuel
- $C_{va}$, $C_{vf}$, $C_{da}$, $C_{df}$ each lie between 0 and 1

### Step 1 — Relate the Suction Head at the Throat

The suction created at the throat (below atmospheric) is the **same** in absolute pressure, but when expressed as a *head* it differs for air and fuel because they have different densities:

$$
h_a = \text{Suction expressed as head of air (m of air)}
$$

$$
h_f = \text{Suction expressed as head of fuel (m of fuel)}
$$

Since pressure difference $\Delta p$ is the same:

$$
\rho_a \, g \, h_a = \rho_f \, g \, h_f \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; h_f = h_a \cdot \frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}
$$

> **Logic:** Pressure = $\rho g h$, so equating pressures for the same depression gives the relationship between heads in different fluids.

### Step 2 — Apply Torricelli's Theorem to Find Air Velocity at Throat

Using Bernoulli's equation between the upstream atmospheric region (1) and the throat (2), the air velocity at the throat is:

$$
V_a = C_{va} \sqrt{2 g h_a}
$$

where $C_{va}$ accounts for friction losses in the converging passage.

### Step 3 — Apply Torricelli's Theorem to Find Fuel Velocity at Jet

Similarly, the fuel velocity at the discharge orifice (driven by the same pressure difference) is:

$$
V_f = C_{vf} \sqrt{2 g h_f} = C_{vf} \sqrt{2 g \, h_a \, \frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}
$$

### Step 4 — Mass Flow Rate of Air

$$
\dot{m}_a = C_{da} \cdot A_a \cdot \rho_a \cdot V_a = C_{da} \, A_a \, \rho_a \, C_{va} \, \sqrt{2 g h_a}
$$

> **Logic:** Mass flow = (Discharge coeff.) × (Area) × (Density) × (Velocity). The discharge coefficient $C_{da}$ accounts for both velocity deficit and contraction of the air jet.

### Step 5 — Mass Flow Rate of Fuel

$$
\dot{m}_f = C_{df} \cdot A_f \cdot \rho_f \cdot V_f = C_{df} \, A_f \, \rho_f \, C_{vf} \, \sqrt{2 g \, h_a \, \frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}
$$

### Step 6 — Form the Air–Fuel Ratio

$$
\frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f} = \frac{C_{da} \, A_a \, \rho_a \, C_{va} \, \sqrt{2 g h_a}}{C_{df} \, A_f \, \rho_f \, C_{vf} \, \sqrt{2 g \, h_a \, (\rho_a/\rho_f)}}
$$

The $\sqrt{2gh_a}$ terms **cancel** in numerator and denominator (this is a key simplification!):

$$
\frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f} = \frac{C_{da} \, A_a \, \rho_a \, C_{va}}{C_{df} \, A_f \, \rho_f \, C_{vf}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_f}{\rho_a}}
$$

Combining the density terms ($\rho_a / \rho_f$ inside the square root):

$$
\frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f} = \frac{C_{da} \, A_a \, C_{va}}{C_{df} \, A_f \, C_{vf}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}
$$

> **Logic:** Notice that the A/F ratio is **independent of the suction head $h$**. This is the elegant beauty of the derivation — no matter how hard the engine sucks, the ratio stays constant (in the *ideal* simple case). This is why real engines need compensation devices.

### Step 7 — The Ideal Simple Carburettor (All Coefficients = 1)

For an ideal, frictionless, lossless simple carburettor where $C_{da} = C_{va} = C_{df} = C_{vf} = 1$:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f} = \frac{A_a}{A_f} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}\;}
$$

### Step 8 — Numerical Verification

Substitute standard values: $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\rho_f = 750$ kg/m³:

$$
\sqrt{\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.2}{750}} = \sqrt{0.0016} = 0.04
$$

So:

$$
\frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f} = 0.04 \cdot \frac{A_a}{A_f}
$$

For stoichiometric mixture (A/F = 14.7 for petrol):

$$
\frac{A_a}{A_f} = \frac{14.7}{0.04} \approx 367.5
$$

> **Interpretation:** The throat area must be **~368 times** the jet area for stoichiometric petrol-air mixture. This is why carburettor jets are tiny — often less than 1 mm in diameter.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relationship Between Fuel–Air Ratio and Jet Sizing

The fuel–air ratio (F/A) is the inverse of A/F and is more directly useful for jet design:

$$
\frac{\dot{m}_f}{\dot{m}_a} = \frac{A_f}{A_a} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\rho_f}{\rho_a}}
$$

For standard values: $\sqrt{\rho_f/\rho_a} = \sqrt{750/1.2} \approx 25$

$$
\frac{\dot{m}_f}{\dot{m}_a} \approx 25 \cdot \frac{A_f}{A_a}
$$

For stoichiometric F/A = 1/14.7:

$$
\frac{A_f}{A_a} = \frac{1}{14.7 \times 25} \approx 0.00272
$$

> **Useful Formula for the Examiner:**
> 
> $$
> A_f = \frac{A_a}{14.7 \times 25} \approx 0.00272 \, A_a
> $$
> 
> For a Venturi throat of diameter 25 mm: $A_a = \pi(0.025)^2/4 = 4.91 \times 10^{-4}$ m²
> Then $A_f = 0.00272 \times 4.91 \times 10^{-4} \approx 1.34 \times 10^{-6}$ m²
> Jet diameter $d_f = \sqrt{4 A_f/\pi} \approx 1.31$ mm.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Why Compensation is Needed (Quantitative Argument)

In a real carburettor, the velocity coefficients and discharge coefficients are **not equal** for air and fuel. The product $C_{da} C_{va}$ for air differs from $C_{df} C_{vf}$ for fuel. Also, air is compressible at higher suctions while fuel is essentially incompressible.

This causes the actual A/F ratio to **drift** as throttle position changes (suction $h$ changes). Compensation devices like the auxiliary air valve, power jet, and accelerating pump are added to compensate for this drift. This is the reason a *simple* carburettor is never used alone in practice — it is only the theoretical foundation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Diesel Injection Pump Plunger Velocity (Jerk Pump)

For a jerk-type fuel injection pump driven by an eccentric or crank:

$$
V_p = \omega \, r \left( \sin\theta + \frac{r}{2L}\sin 2\theta \right)
$$

where:
- $\omega$ = angular velocity of camshaft (rad/s)
- $r$ = crank radius (m)
- $L$ = connecting rod length (m)
- $\theta$ = crank angle from TDC (radians)

> **Logic of Each Term:**
> - The first term $\omega r \sin\theta$ is the **simple harmonic approximation** of plunger velocity (correct for an infinitely long connecting rod).
> - The second term $\omega r^2/(2L) \cdot \sin 2\theta$ is the **correction** for finite rod length — it adds the asymmetric behaviour of actual reciprocating motion.

This explains why actual fuel delivery curves are *slightly asymmetric* — fuel is delivered faster on the downward stroke than the upward stroke.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Type)

**Problem:** A simple carburettor is designed for a 2-litre, 4-cylinder petrol engine. The throat diameter is 22 mm. The jet diameter is found to be 1.18 mm. Assuming $C_{va} = C_{da} = C_{vf} = C_{df} = 0.85$, $\rho_a = 1.2$ kg/m³, $\rho_f = 740$ kg/m³, determine the air–fuel ratio supplied.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute areas:

$$
A_a = \frac{\pi (0.022)^2}{4} = 3.80 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{m}^2
$$

$$
A_f = \frac{\pi (0.00118)^2}{4} = 1.094 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{m}^2
$$

Step 2 — Form the ratio $A_a/A_f$:

$$
\frac{A_a}{A_f} = \frac{3.80 \times 10^{-4}}{1.094 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 347.3
$$

Step 3 — Apply general formula (coefficients are equal so they cancel):

$$
\frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f} = \frac{A_a}{A_f}\sqrt{\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}} = 347.3 \times \sqrt{\frac{1.2}{740}} = 347.3 \times 0.04027 \approx 13.99
$$

> **Result:** A/F ≈ **14 : 1** — close to stoichiometric, suitable for normal cruising condition.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Simple Carburettor

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Atmospheric Air Entry] --> nodeB[Air Cleaner Filter]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Choke Valve Cold Start]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Venturi Converging Passage]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Venturi Throat Low Pressure]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Venturi Diverging Passage]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Throttle Valve Power Control]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Intake Manifold To Cylinders]
    nodeI[Fuel Tank Storage] --> nodeJ[Fuel Strainer Coarse Filter]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Needle and Float Assembly]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Float Chamber Atmospheric Vented]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Main Metering Jet Calibrated Orifice]
    nodeM --> nodeE
    nodeN[Idle Discharge Port] --> nodeH
    nodeO[Compensation Devices] --> nodeM
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Left column** = Air path (atmospheric → cylinders)
- **Right column** = Fuel path (tank → venturi throat)
- **Centre node** = Venturi throat, where air and fuel meet
- **Compensation devices** modify jet behaviour for varying engine operating conditions

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Diesel Solid Injection System

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeP[Fuel Tank Reservoir] --> nodeQ[Feed Lift Pump Low Pressure 1 to 2 bar]
    nodeQ --> nodeR[Fuel Filter Fine 5 to 10 micron]
    nodeR --> nodeS[Injection Pump Jerk Rotary or Distributor]
    nodeS --> nodeT[Delivery Valve Residual Pressure Holder]
    nodeT --> nodeU[High Pressure Pipe Steel Tube]
    nodeU --> nodeV[Injector Nozzle Assembly]
    nodeV --> nodeW[Combustion Chamber]
    nodeX[Camshaft Drive Timing Control] --> nodeS
    nodeY[Nozzle Spring Calibration] --> nodeV
    nodeZ[Leak Off Return Line To Tank] --> nodeP
    nodeV --> nodeZ
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Lower-pressure side**: Tank → Lift pump → Filter → Injection pump inlet
- **High-pressure side**: Injection pump → Delivery valve → Pipe → Injector → Combustion
- **Camshaft drive** governs injection timing
- **Leak-off line** returns small leakage fuel to tank (continuous recirculation)

---

## 4.3 Subsystem Diagram — Internal Anatomy of a Diesel Injector

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA1[High Pressure Fuel Inlet From Pump] --> nodeA2[Nozzle Body Fuel Gallery]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3[Nozzle Needle Valve Spring Loaded]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA4[Nozzle Needle Seat Conical]
    nodeA4 --> nodeA5[Spray Orifice Single Multi or Pintle]
    nodeA5 --> nodeA6[Combustion Chamber Spray Pattern]
    nodeA7[Pressure Adjusting Screw] --> nodeA8[Spring Cap]
    nodeA8 --> nodeA9[Nozzle Spring Helical Compression]
    nodeA9 --> nodeA3
    nodeB1[Leak Back Passage] --> nodeA2
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- When line pressure **exceeds** spring preload (× lever ratio) → needle lifts → fuel sprays
- When line pressure **drops below** opening pressure → spring pushes needle back → sharp cut-off
- **Pressure adjusting screw** sets the opening pressure (typically 175–275 bar)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]
**Q: Define a simple carburettor. List its main components.**

**Model Answer:**
A simple carburettor is a device that prepares a homogeneous air–fuel mixture for a spark-ignition engine by utilising the pressure drop created at the throat of a Venturi tube to draw fuel through a calibrated jet. Its main components are:
1. Float chamber
2. Float and needle valve
3. Venturi tube
4. Main metering jet (fuel discharge nozzle)
5. Throttle valve
6. Choke valve
7. Air cleaner entry
