## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** (Choose either A or B)

#### **(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the working of a simple carburettor used in an SI engine. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution Sketch (Verbal Description for Board Exam):**
Draw a vertical cross-section showing:
- Atmospheric air entering from top through air cleaner and choke valve
- Venturi tube with converging passage, throat, and diverging passage
- Float chamber at the side containing float, needle valve
- Main metering jet from float chamber into the throat
- Throttle valve at downstream end connected to engine intake manifold

**Working Explanation (7 Marks Allocation):**
1. **Stroke 1 — Piston suction:** During intake stroke, descending piston creates low pressure downstream of throttle. **(1 mark)**
2. **Stroke 2 — Air flow through Venturi:** Air rushes through Venturi; velocity increases at throat, pressure falls below atmospheric by an amount $h$ (mm of fuel). **(1 mark)**
3. **Stroke 3 — Float chamber pressurisation:** Float chamber is vented to atmosphere, so its pressure is atmospheric (constant). **(1 mark)**
4. **Stroke 4 — Fuel discharge:** Pressure difference between atmospheric (float chamber) and sub-atmospheric (throat) pushes fuel up through the main jet. **(1 mark)**
5. **Stroke 5 — Atomisation:** High-velocity air stream breaks fuel into fine droplets. **(1 mark)**
6. **Stroke 6 — Mixture formation:** Homogeneous A/F mixture flows past throttle to cylinders. **(1 mark)**
7. **Role of auxiliary systems:** Choke enriches mixture for cold start; throttle controls power. **(1 mark)**

---

#### **(b)** Derive the air–fuel ratio equation for an ideal simple carburettor. Show that the ratio is independent of the suction head at the venturi throat. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Apply Bernoulli's equation between the upstream air region (1) and the venturi throat (2):

$$
p_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho_a V_a^2 = p_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho_a V_{a1}^2
$$

With $V_{a1} \approx 0$ upstream and $p_1 - p_2 = \rho_a g h_a$:

$$
V_a = \sqrt{2 g h_a}
$$

Similarly, for fuel through the jet:

$$
V_f = \sqrt{2 g h_f} = \sqrt{2 g h_a \frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}
$$

Mass flow rates:

$$
\dot{m}_a = A_a \, \rho_a \, V_a, \qquad \dot{m}_f = A_f \, \rho_f \, V_f
$$

A/F Ratio:

$$
\frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f} = \frac{A_a \rho_a}{A_f \rho_f} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2 g h_a}}{\sqrt{2 g h_a (\rho_a/\rho_f)}} = \frac{A_a}{A_f}\sqrt{\frac{\rho_a}{\rho_f}}
$$

The $\sqrt{2 g h_a}$ terms cancel completely, **proving** that the A/F ratio is independent of the suction head $h_a$. (For ideal coefficients = 1 case.)

> **Marking key:**
> - Diagram / statement of Bernoulli (2 marks)
> - Velocity expressions (2 marks)
> - Mass flow rate expressions (1 mark)
> - A/F ratio simplification (1 mark)
> - Independence conclusion (1 mark)
> Total = **7 marks**

---

### **Question B** (Alternative Choice)

#### **(a)** Explain with a neat sketch, the solid injection system of a multi-cylinder diesel engine. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**System Description (Board Sketch Required):**
Show the closed loop:
- Fuel tank
- Feed (lift) pump
- Fine filter
- Jerk injection pump (driven by camshaft)
- High-pressure delivery valve
- High-pressure pipes
- Injector nozzles (one per cylinder)
- Leak-off return line back to tank

**Component-wise Explanation:**
1. **Fuel tank:** Stores diesel; vented to atmosphere. **(0.5 mark)**
2. **Lift pump:** Diaphragm/vane type; supplies fuel at 1–2 bar to the injection pump inlet; also scavenges air from the system. **(1 mark)**
3. **Fuel filter:** Replaceable paper element; filtration down to 5 µm. **(0.5 mark)**
4. **Injection pump:** Jerk type with one plunger per cylinder; pressurises fuel up to 400–600 bar; cam lobe profile determines injection duration. **(1.5 marks)**
5. **Delivery valve:** Maintains residual pressure in high-pressure pipe; prevents dribble after injection. **(0.5 mark)**
6. **High-pressure pipes:** Rigid steel tubes; identical length for all cylinders to ensure uniform injection timing. **(0.5 mark)**
7. **Injector:** Spring-loaded needle valve; opens at calibrated pressure (175–275 bar); atomises fuel. **(1.5 marks)**
8. **Return line:** Returns leakage fuel to tank. **(0.5 mark)**
9. **Timing control:** Pump-to-engine phasing ensures injection near TDC. **(0.5 mark)**

---

#### **(b)** With the help of sketches, describe the construction and working of a **multi-hole injector** and a **pintle nozzle injector** used in CI engines. Compare their spray patterns. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Multi-Hole Injector (3.5 marks):**
- **Construction:** Needle valve with a straight tapered shank; below the seat, the nozzle body has 4 to 12 small holes (typically 0.2–0.4 mm diameter) drilled at angles to the axis.
- **Working:** When pressure exceeds opening pressure, needle lifts; fuel sprays through all holes simultaneously as multiple fine jets, forming a hollow cone spray pattern.
- **Application:** Modern direct-injection (DI) diesel engines.
- **Advantage:** Excellent atomisation; precise spray targeting to bowl-in-piston geometry.
- **Sketch:** Cross-section showing needle, holes, spring, and spray cones.

**Pintle Nozzle Injector (3.5 marks):**
- **Construction:** Similar needle valve, but a small pin (*pintle*) protrudes downward from the needle through a single-hole orifice.
- **Working:** When needle lifts, fuel escapes around the pintle pin. The pintle's shape determines the spray cone angle (typically 15°–60°).
- **Application:** Indirect-injection (IDI) engines with swirl chambers.
- **Advantage:** Less sensitive to fuel pressure variation; produces hollow cone suitable for pre-chambers.

**Comparison of Spray Patterns:**

| Feature | Multi-Hole | Pintle |
|---|---|---|
| Number of orifices | 4–12 | 1 (with central pin) |
| Spray type | Solid multi-jet cone | Hollow cone |
| Atomisation | Very fine | Moderate |
| Spray angle | Fixed by hole angle | Variable via pintle profile |
| Typical use | DI engines | IDI / swirl chamber engines |

> **Marking key:** Construction (1) + Working (1) + Sketch (1) + Comparison (0.5) = 3.5 each; total 7.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the square-root of density ratio:** Many students write $\frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f} = \frac{A_a \rho_a}{A_f \rho_f}$ — this is **WRONG**. The square root is essential because velocity depends on $\sqrt{2gh}$, not $h$. Marks lost: typically 2 out of 7.
>
> 2. **Confusing $h_a$ and $h_f$:** If the suction is given in mm of *water*, convert carefully using $h_f = h_w \cdot \rho_w/\rho_f$. Marks lost: 1.
>
> 3. **Mixing up air and fuel discharge coefficients:** $C_{da}$ is for air (0.8–0.9), $C_{df}$ is for fuel (0.6–0.7). They are not equal. Marks lost: 1–2.
>
> 4. **Drawing a carburettor sketch without the float chamber:** A simple carburettor *must* show the float, needle valve, and float chamber. Drawing only the Venturi and jet will lose 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Saying "air mixes with fuel" instead of "atomisation occurs":** Be precise — high velocity air atomises fuel; mixing occurs as droplets evaporate in manifold.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning the lift pump / filter in the diesel system:** The examiner expects all components in the path. Marks lost: 1.
>
> 7. **Confusing injection pressure with nozzle opening pressure:** They are different! Injection pressure varies during the pump stroke; nozzle opening pressure is a fixed calibration.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **simple carburettor** uses the Venturi effect to meter fuel; the A/F ratio is given by $\frac{A_a}{A_f}\sqrt{\rho_a/\rho_f}$ (ideal case).
- [x] The A/F ratio of an ideal simple carburettor is **independent of the suction head** at the throat.
- [x] **Float chamber** is vented to atmosphere to keep driving pressure difference constant.
- [x] **Discharge coefficient** for air ($C_{da}$) and fuel ($C_{df}$) are **not equal** in real carburettors.
- [x] Standard values: $\rho_a \approx 1.2$ kg/m³, $\rho_f \approx 750$ kg/m³, so $\sqrt{\rho_a/\rho_f} \approx 0.04$ and $\sqrt{\rho_f/\rho_a} \approx 25$.
- [x] Stoichiometric A/F for petrol = **14.7 : 1**.
- [x] A **simple carburettor** is purely theoretical — real engines always need compensation.
- [x] **Diesel solid injection system** components: tank → lift pump → filter → injection pump → delivery valve → high-pressure pipe → injector → combustion chamber → leak-off return.
- [x] **Air injection system** is obsolete; uses compressed air to atomise fuel.
- [x] **Injection pump pressures**: 200–600 bar (IDI), up to 2000 bar (modern CRDi).
- [x] **Nozzle opening pressure** typically 175–275 bar; adjusted via spring preload.
- [x] **Multi-hole nozzle** → DI engines; **Pintle nozzle** → IDI / swirl chamber engines.
- [x] **High-pressure pipes** must be of **equal length** to all cylinders for uniform timing.
- [x] Diesel engines use **stratified charge formation** (heterogeneous mixture of fuel spray in air); SI engines use **homogeneous charge**.
- [x] Injection timing is governed by the **camshaft** of the injection pump relative to the engine crankshaft.
- [x] Key historical point: Air injection invented by **Rudolf Diesel (1892)**; solid injection developed by **Robert Bosch (1927)** for mass production.
- [x] For 14-mark questions, **always draw a labelled sketch** — it earns easy 2 marks even if the explanation is weak.
- [x] Always mention units explicitly in the final numerical answer (e.g., A/F = 14.7 : 1, *dimensionless*).
- [x] **Compensation devices** in real carburettors: auxiliary air valve, power jet, accelerating pump, idle system, economiser.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Pump and Injectors, Lubrication System - Lubricant Properties and Classification, Cooling System - Air Cooling and Water/Liquid Cooling Systems, Ignition System - Battery Ignition and Magneto Ignition Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT502 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT ENGINES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fuel Pump and Injectors, Lubrication System - Lubricant Properties and Classification, Cooling System - Air Cooling and Water/Liquid Cooling Systems, Ignition System - Battery Ignition and Magneto Ignition Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fuel Pump and Injectors (Diesel Engine)

### Formal Definition
A **Fuel Injection Pump** is a positive-displacement, high-pressure device used in Compression Ignition (CI) engines to deliver atomized diesel fuel into the combustion chamber at precisely controlled pressure, timing, and quantity. It is mechanically or electronically actuated by the camshaft, generating injection pressures ranging typically from **200 bar to 2000 bar** in modern Common-Rail systems. The **Injector (Nozzle)** is the final metering device that atomizes the pressurized fuel into a fine spray pattern to ensure rapid and complete combustion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The injector does NOT pump the fuel. It only meters and atomizes. The actual pressurization is done by the Fuel Injection Pump. This distinction is a frequent 3-mark question.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of the **fuel injection pump** as a high-pressure nurse with a calibrated syringe — it draws the exact dose of fuel and pushes it forcefully at the correct moment. The **injector** is the fine needle at the syringe tip that breaks the fuel into a misty cloud (atomization) so the engine's "lungs" (combustion chamber) can absorb it instantly. Together, they are like a perfume atomizer — the pump builds pressure, the nozzle produces the fine mist.

---

## 1.2 Lubrication System — Lubricant Properties & Classification

### Formal Definition
A **Lubricant** is a substance (typically oil or grease) introduced between two moving surfaces in relative motion to reduce **friction, wear, heat generation, and corrosion** while simultaneously cleaning, cooling, and sealing the contact zone. Lubricants are characterized by physical properties such as **viscosity, flash point, fire point, pour point, cloud point, neutralization number, saponification number, carbon residue, ash content**, and **viscosity index**.

### Intuitive Analogy
Lubricant is the **"invisible ball-bearing"** between surfaces. Imagine sliding two rough wooden planks against each other — they grind, squeak, and burn. Now smear a thin layer of butter (lubricant) and the planks glide effortlessly. Good engine oil is like premium butter that doesn't melt under heat, doesn't freeze in cold, doesn't turn into sticky tar, and even cleans dirt as it slides.

> [!NOTE]
> **Saponification Number** measures the amount of KOH (in mg) required to saponify 1 gram of oil. It indicates the presence of fatty acids (used to detect vegetable/animal oil adulteration in mineral oils).

---

## 1.3 Cooling System — Air and Water/Liquid Cooling

### Formal Definition
An **Engine Cooling System** is an arrangement designed to maintain the cylinder, piston, and cylinder head temperatures within an optimal operating range (typically **80°C – 95°C** for water-cooled engines) by rejecting excess heat generated during combustion. **Air Cooling** uses finned cylinder surfaces and a high-velocity airstream. **Water/Liquid Cooling** uses a circulating coolant (water + ethylene glycol) through jackets, radiator, water pump, and thermostat.

### Intuitive Analogy
The cooling system is the **engine's sweat and air-conditioner combined**. Just as your body sweats (water cooling) or stands in front of a fan (air cooling) to remove exercise-induced heat, the engine sheds combustion heat via either flowing air over hot fins or pumping liquid coolant around hot combustion walls. Without it, the engine would literally melt its own pistons.

---

## 1.4 Ignition System — Battery and Magneto Ignition

### Formal Definition
An **Ignition System** in a Spark Ignition (SI) engine is responsible for generating a high-voltage spark (**typically 20,000 – 30,000 V**) at the correct instant to ignite the compressed air-fuel mixture in the combustion chamber. The **Battery Ignition System** uses an external storage battery (12V) to energize the ignition coil. The **Magneto Ignition System** generates its own electrical energy using a permanent-magnet magneto coupled to the engine shaft, making it self-contained and independent of any battery.

### Intuitive Analogy
The ignition system is the **"matchstick lighter"** of the engine. The **battery system** is like a gas-lighter (you press a button → battery → small spark → big spark via coil). The **magneto system** is like a hand-cranked piezoelectric lighter (you crank → magnetic motion → spark produced internally). Both serve the same purpose: deliver a tiny, precisely-timed lightning bolt to start combustion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Magneto systems are typically used in **two-wheelers, outboard motors, lawn mowers, and small aircraft piston engines** because they are compact, lightweight, and don't need a battery.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fuel Injection Pump — Types and Operation

### A. Jerk-Type (Individual) Fuel Injection Pump
This is the traditional Bosch-type inline pump used in most medium-duty diesel engines. Each cylinder has its own pumping element consisting of a **barrel and plunger** actuated by a cam.

**Operating Principle:**
1. The cam lifts the plunger upward.
2. The plunger covers the **spill port (or helix port)**.
3. Fuel above the plunger is pressurized and forced through the delivery valve into the high-pressure line leading to the injector.
4. When the helix on the plunger uncovers the spill port, fuel escapes back to the sump, and delivery stops.
5. **Quantity control** is achieved by rotating the plunger via a rack and pinion — this changes the effective stroke where the helix covers the port.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fuel injection begins when the spill port is closed** (top of the plunger stroke) and **ends when the spill port is reopened** (by the helix). Effective stroke = geometric stroke − port opening distance.

### B. Common Rail Direct Injection (CRDi) System
In modern CRDi systems:
- A high-pressure pump (often a radial piston pump) generates pressure of **up to 2000 bar** continuously.
- Fuel is stored in a **common rail (accumulator)**.
- An **ECU (Electronic Control Unit)** actuates solenoid or piezo injectors to deliver fuel at the precise moment and quantity required.

### C. Injector / Nozzle Construction
The injector has:
- **Nozzle body** with one or more spray holes
- **Nozzle needle (valve)** held closed by a calibrated **spring**
- **Pressure chamber** where high-pressure fuel acts on the needle's differential area
- When pressure exceeds the **nozzle opening pressure (NOP)** — typically **175 – 350 bar** — the needle lifts and fuel sprays.

| Nozzle Type | Spray Pattern | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Single-hole (Pintaux)** | Single solid jet | Small, low-speed engines |
| **Multi-hole** | 4–12 fine jets | Direct Injection (DI) diesel engines |
| **Pintle** | Hollow conical spray | Indirect Injection (IDI) — pre-chamber |
| **Delay Pintle (Pintaux)** | Throttled conical spray | IDI with throttle cone for swirl |

### D. Spray Characteristics
- **Spray Penetration**: Distance the fuel jet travels before losing momentum. Too high → impinges on piston/walls. Too low → poor air utilization.
- **Atomization**: The breakup of fuel into fine droplets (**ideal droplet size: 10–25 μm**).
- **Spray Angle**: Determines the cone of dispersion (typically 150°–160° for multi-hole DI injectors).

---

## 2.2 Lubricant Properties — Detailed Analysis

### A. Viscosity
**Viscosity (μ)** is the measure of a fluid's internal resistance to flow. For engine oils, viscosity is the most critical property.
- **Dynamic Viscosity (μ)** in **Pa·s** or **poise (P)** where 1 P = 0.1 Pa·s.
- **Kinematic Viscosity (ν)** in **m²/s** or **centistokes (cSt)** where 1 cSt = 10⁻⁶ m²/s.

$$ \nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} $$

where ρ is fluid density (kg/m³).

### B. Viscosity Index (VI)
VI measures the rate of change of oil viscosity with temperature. **Higher VI = less viscosity variation with temperature.**

$$ \text{VI} = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100 $$

where:
- **L** = viscosity of reference oil with VI = 0 at 100°F
- **H** = viscosity of reference oil with VI = 100 at 100°F
- **U** = viscosity of the test oil at 100°F

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** A VI of 100 means the oil behaves like the best paraffinic-base oil. Multigrade oils (e.g., 20W-40) have effective VI > 100 due to **Viscosity Index Improver (VII) additives** (polymers like PMA or OCP).

### C. Flash Point and Fire Point
- **Flash Point (FP)**: The lowest temperature at which oil vapor gives off enough vapor to ignite momentarily when a flame is brought near.
- **Fire Point (FP_f)**: The lowest temperature at which oil vapor ignites and continues to burn for at least **5 seconds**.
- For engine oils, **FP should be > 200°C** to prevent crankcase explosions.

### D. Cloud Point and Pour Point
- **Cloud Point**: Temperature at which wax crystals first become visible (oil becomes cloudy).
- **Pour Point**: Lowest temperature at which oil will still flow under standard test conditions.
- **CFPP (Cold Filter Plugging Point)**: The temperature at which oil fails to pass through a standardized filter.

### E. Neutralization Number (Acid Number)
Mass of KOH (in mg) required to neutralize 1 g of oil. Indicates acidic contamination. Fresh oil has TAN < 0.1 mg KOH/g; used oil with TAN > 2.0 indicates oxidation.

### F. Saponification Number
Mass of KOH (in mg) required to saponify 1 g of oil. Indicates the amount of fatty acids (used to detect animal/vegetable oil in mineral oil).

### G. Carbon Residue (Conradson Test)
Percentage of carbon left after evaporating oil under specified conditions. Lower is better — indicates cleaner burning.

### H. Ash Content
Percentage of non-combustible residue. **Sulfated ash** indicates metallic additives (Zn, Ca, Mg from detergents). High ash can cause deposits in turbochargers and DPF clogging.

---

### KTU Formula Sheet — Lubrication

| Property | Formula / Definition | Typical Value (Engine Oil) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | 9.3 – 12.5 (at 100°C) | cSt (mm²/s) |
| Viscosity Index | $VI = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100$ | 90 – 120 | — |
| Flash Point | Vapor ignites momentarily | > 200 | °C |
| Fire Point | Vapor burns > 5 s | 220 – 250 | °C |
| Pour Point | Oil stops flowing | -15 to -30 | °C |
| Saponification No. | mg KOH/g of oil | 0 (pure mineral) | mg KOH/g |
| Carbon Residue | Conradson test residue | < 0.5 | % wt |
| TBN (engine oil) | mg KOH/g (basic reserve) | 7 – 12 | mg KOH/g |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TBN (Total Base Number)** is the modern indicator of oil's ability to neutralize acids. **TAN (Total Acid Number)** indicates accumulated acids. When TAN = TBN → oil must be changed.

---

## 2.3 Lubricant Classification

### By Origin
1. **Mineral Oils** (from petroleum refining)
   - Paraffinic base — high VI, good oxidation stability
   - Naphthenic base — low pour point, good solvency
   - Aromatic base — poor VI, used as plasticizers
2. **Synthetic Oils**
   - Polyalphaolefin (PAO)
   - Esters (diesters, polyolesters)
   - Polyglycols
   - Silicones
3. **Vegetable Oils** (castor, palm, coconut) — biodegradable, used in environmentally-sensitive areas.
4. **Animal Oils** (whale oil historically) — high saponification number.
5. **Blends** (semi-synthetic, synthetic blend).

### By Application
- **Engine oils** (SAE 20W-40, 15W-40)
- **Gear oils** (SAE 90, 140, EP grades)
- **Hydraulic oils** (ISO VG 32, 46, 68)
- **Turbine oils** (ISO VG 32, 46)
- **Compressor oils**
- **Greases** (calcium, sodium, lithium soap-based)

### By Performance (API Service Classification)
- **S category** (Spark ignition): SA, SB, SC, SD, SE, SF, SG, SH, SJ, SL, SM, SN, SP
- **C category** (Compression ignition): CA, CB, CC, CD, CD-II, CE, CF, CF-2, CF-4, CG-4, CH-4, CI-2, CJ-4, CK-4

### Lubrication Additives
| Additive Type | Function | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Detergents** | Clean deposits, neutralize acids | Ca-sulfonate, Mg-phenate |
| **Dispersants** | Suspend soot & sludge | PIB succinimide |
| **Anti-wear (AW)** | Form protective film | ZDDP (Zinc dialkyldithiophosphate) |
| **VI Improvers** | Reduce viscosity change with temperature | PMA (polymethacrylate) |
| **Anti-foam** | Collapse air bubbles | Silicone polymers |
| **Anti-oxidant** | Slow oxidation | ZDDP, hindered phenols |
| **Pour-point depressants** | Prevent wax crystallization | PMA, styrene esters |

---

## 2.4 Cooling System — Air and Water Cooling

### A. Air Cooling System
**Principle:** Heat is transferred from the cylinder walls directly to the surrounding air via extended surfaces (fins).

**Components:**
- Finned cylinder head and barrel
- Fan (blower) driven by the engine or separately
- Cowl/duct (directs airflow over fins)
- Baffles (in multi-cylinder engines — direct cool air to hottest cylinders first)

**Working:** Hot cylinder surface → conduction through metal → convection to fin → radiation + convection to air.

**Heat transfer from fin:**
$$ Q = hA(T_s - T_\infty) $$

where $h$ is convective heat transfer coefficient, $A$ is fin surface area, $T_s$ is fin surface temperature, $T_\infty$ is ambient air temperature.

**Advantages of Air Cooling:**
1. Lightweight (no radiator, water pump, coolant)
2. Simple construction
3. Cannot leak or freeze
4. No boiling problems at altitude
5. Quick warm-up
6. Suitable for small two-wheelers, aircraft, military vehicles

**Disadvantages of Air Cooling:**
1. Uneven cooling (non-uniform cylinder temperatures)
2. Limited heat rejection capacity
3. Noisy fan operation
4. Poor temperature control
5. Lower power output (due to thermal limits)

### B. Water/Liquid Cooling System

**Components:**
- **Water jacket** (around cylinders and head)
- **Water pump** (centrifugal type, belt-driven)
- **Radiator** (tube-and-fin heat exchanger)
- **Thermostat** (wax-pellet type, opens at ~80°C)
- **Coolant** (50:50 water + ethylene glycol)
- **Fan** (electric or belt-driven)
- **Expansion tank / Header tank**
- **Pressure cap** (raises boiling point to ~120°C at 1.3 bar)
- **Temperature gauge / sender**

**Three Subtypes:**

1. **Thermosyphon Cooling** (natural circulation)
   - No pump — relies on density difference
   - Hot water rises (less dense), cool water falls
   - Limited flow rate; rarely used in modern cars

2. **Forced (Pump) Circulation** — most common
   - Centrifugal water pump drives coolant
   - Direction: engine → thermostat → radiator → pump → engine
   - When cold, thermostat is closed → coolant recirculates in engine for fast warm-up

3. **Pressurized Cooling**
   - Radiator cap pressurizes system to **0.9 – 1.5 bar**
   - Raises boiling point: at 1 bar (gauge), BP ≈ 121°C
   - Prevents cavitation and coolant loss

**Heat balance for radiator:**
$$ Q_{rejected} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in}) $$

where $\dot{m}$ is coolant mass flow rate, $c_p$ is specific heat of coolant (~3.4 kJ/kg·K for 50/50 EG-water).

**Advantages of Water Cooling:**
1. Uniform cylinder temperature
2. Higher power output (more heat rejection possible)
3. Quieter operation
4. Cabin heating via heater core (uses waste heat)
5. Better temperature control

**Disadvantages of Water Cooling:**
1. Heavy and complex
2. Risk of leakage, corrosion, freezing
3. Coolant must be replaced periodically
4. Pump failure → overheating damage

---

## 2.5 Ignition System — Battery and Magneto

### A. Battery Ignition System

**Components:**
- **12V Lead-acid Battery** (or 6V in older vehicles)
- **Ignition Switch** (key-operated)
- **Ignition Coil** (step-up transformer: 12V → 20,000–30,000 V)
- **Contact Breaker (CB Points)** — mechanically opens/closes primary circuit
- **Condenser (Capacitor)** — absorbs back-EMF to prevent arcing at points
- **Distributor** — directs HT current to correct cylinder
- **Rotor Arm** (rotating contact inside distributor cap)
- **Distributor Cap** with HT cables
- **Spark Plug** — gap of **0.6 – 0.8 mm**

**Working Principle:**
1. Ignition switch ON → battery → primary winding of coil (~200 turns of thick wire).
2. As primary current builds, magnetic flux forms in iron core.
3. Contact breaker (driven by camshaft) OPENS the primary circuit.
4. Sudden collapse of magnetic flux induces a high voltage in the secondary winding (~20,000 turns of fine wire).
5. Voltage at spark plug = $V_s = V_p \times \frac{N_s}{N_p}$ where $N_s/N_p$ ≈ 100.

$$ V_s = V_p \times \frac{N_s}{N_p} \approx 12 \times 100 = 1200 \text{ V (induced)} $$

But the actual voltage is much higher (~25 kV) due to fast flux collapse ($\frac{d\phi}{dt}$ is very large):
$$ V_s = -N_s \frac{d\phi}{dt} $$

6. Distributor rotor directs the HT pulse to the correct cylinder's spark plug.
7. Spark ignites compressed mixture ~10°–30° BTDC.

### B. Magneto Ignition System

**Components:**
- Permanent-magnet rotating magneto (no battery needed)
- Primary and secondary windings on a laminated core
- **Cam-operated contact breaker**
- **Condenser**
- **HT distributor** (if multi-cylinder)
- **Spark plug**

**Types:**
- **Rotating Magnet Magneto** (rotating magnet, stationary coil)
- **Rotating Armature Magneto** (rotating coil, stationary magnets — older design)

**Working Principle:**
1. Engine rotation drives the magneto's permanent magnet past the primary coil.
2. **Alternating EMF** is induced in the primary winding (no battery needed).
3. The contact breaker (CB points) close during one half-cycle to build up current in the primary.
4. As points open at the precise ignition moment, primary current collapses, and the resulting change in flux induces a high voltage in the secondary.
5. This HT current is routed via the distributor to the correct spark plug.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** A magneto can produce spark only when the engine is rotating. It cannot start the spark on its own if the engine is being started externally (kick-start or push-start) unless it has a separate "impulse coupling" that snaps the magneto into the firing position at cranking speed.

### C. Comparison Table

| Feature | Battery Ignition | Magneto Ignition |
|---|---|---|
| Power Source | External battery | Self-generated (permanent magnet) |
| Voltage at Cranking | Stable 12V → strong spark | Weak at low cranking speed |
| Initial Cost | Higher (battery) | Lower (no battery) |
| Maintenance | Battery water, terminals | Contact breaker, capacitor |
| Reliability | Battery failure disables system | Self-contained, very reliable |
| Spark Quality at Low RPM | Excellent | Poor (requires impulse coupling) |
| Application | Cars, trucks, LMVs | Two-wheelers, mowers, aircraft, marine |
| Weight | Heavy (battery) | Lightweight |
| Independent Operation | Depends on battery | Truly self-contained |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Viscosity Index (VI) of an Oil Sample

**Problem:** A lubricating oil has kinematic viscosity of 73 cSt at 40°C and 9.0 cSt at 100°C. The reference oil with VI = 0 has viscosity 9.2 cSt at 100°C and 580 cSt at 40°C for the same viscosity at 100°C. The reference oil with VI = 100 has viscosity 9.2 cSt at 100°C and 270 cSt at 40°C. Calculate the Viscosity Index.

**Step 1 — Identify the given parameters:**
- Test oil viscosity at 100°C, $U_{100}$ = 9.0 cSt
- Reference oil VI = 0 viscosity at 100°C, $H_{100}$ = 9.2 cSt
- Reference oil VI = 0 viscosity at 40°C, $L$ = 580 cSt
- Reference oil VI = 100 viscosity at 40°C, $H$ = 270 cSt
- Test oil viscosity at 40°C, $U_{40}$ = 73 cSt

**Step 2 — Recall the VI formula:**

$$ VI = \frac{L - U}{L - H} \times 100 $$

**Step 3 — Apply the formula:**

$$ L = 580 \text{ cSt}, \quad H = 270 \text{ cSt}, \quad U = 73 \text{ cSt} $$

$$ VI = \frac{580 - 73}{580 - 270} \times 100 $$

$$ VI = \frac{507}{310} \times 100 $$

$$ VI = 1.6355 \times 100 $$

$$ \boxed{VI \approx 163.55 } $$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

> [!NOTE]
> A VI greater than 100 indicates the oil is **better than the paraffinic reference**. This is only possible with **VI Improver additives** (e.g., polymethacrylate, OCP). Such an oil is classified as **multigrade** (e.g., 20W-40).

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Secondary Voltage of an Ignition Coil

**Problem:** An ignition coil has 200 primary turns and 21,000 secondary turns. The primary current of 4 A is broken in 0.002 s. If the primary voltage is 12 V, calculate the secondary voltage.

**Step 1 — Write the transformer EMF equation:**

$$ V_s = -N_s \frac{d\phi}{dt} $$

$$ V_p = -N_p \frac{d\phi}{dt} $$

**Step 2 — Divide secondary by primary:**

$$ \frac{V_s}{V_p} = \frac{N_s}{N_p} $$

$$ V_s = V_p \times \frac{N_s}{N_p} $$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$ V_s = 12 \times \frac{21000}{200} $$

$$ V_s = 12 \times 105 $$

$$ \boxed{V_s = 1260 \text{ V (ideal transformer ratio)} } $$

**Step 4 — But actual secondary voltage is higher (real coil):**

In a real ignition coil, when the primary current is interrupted, the **rate of change of flux** is much higher than during steady-state operation. The actual induced voltage is:

$$ V_{s,actual} = L_p \frac{di_p}{dt} \times \frac{N_s}{N_p} $$

$$ L_p = \frac{N_p \cdot \phi}{i_p} \text{ (approx)} $$

Using $\frac{di_p}{dt} = \frac{4}{0.002} = 2000$ A/s and assuming a coupling coefficient of ~0.95 and a typical primary inductance of 10 mH:

$$ V_{s,actual} = 0.01 \times 2000 \times 105 \times 0.95 $$

$$ V_{s,actual} = 20 \times 105 \times 0.95 $$

$$ V_{s,actual} = 1995 \text{ V (approx)} $$

In actual ignition coils with high-permeability cores, the secondary voltage reaches **20,000 – 30,000 V** because the flux collapse is extremely rapid (microseconds), giving an enormous $\frac{d\phi}{dt}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Heat Rejection by Radiator

**Problem:** An engine's cooling system uses a 50/50 ethylene glycol-water mixture with mass flow rate of 60 kg/min. The coolant enters the radiator at 95°C and leaves at 80°C. The specific heat of the coolant is 3.4 kJ/kg·K. Calculate the heat rejected by the radiator.

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters:**
- Mass flow rate, $\dot{m} = 60$ kg/min = 1 kg/s
- Inlet temperature, $T_{in} = 95$°C
- Outlet temperature, $T_{out} = 80$°C
- Specific heat, $c_p = 3.4$ kJ/kg·K

**Step 2 — Write the heat rejection equation:**

$$ Q = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T $$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$ Q = 1 \times 3.4 \times (95 - 80) $$

$$ Q = 1 \times 3.4 \times 15 $$

$$ Q = 51 \text{ kW} $$

**Step 4 — Convert to kJ/min or kcal/min:**

$$ Q = 51 \text{ kW} = 51 \text{ kJ/s} = 3060 \text{ kJ/min} $$

$$ Q = \frac{3060}{4.186} \approx 731 \text{ kcal/min} $$

$$ \boxed{Q_{rejected} = 51 \text{ kW} } $$

> [!NOTE]
> A typical car engine rejects about **30–40% of fuel energy** through the cooling system. For a 100 kW engine, that's 30–40 kW — close to our computed 51 kW for a heavily loaded engine.

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Fuel Injection Timing in a Jerk Pump

**Problem:** A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder diesel engine runs at 2000 RPM. The fuel injection advance angle is 20° BTDC. The injection duration is 30° of crank rotation. Calculate (a) the time of injection start before TDC and (b) the total injection time in milliseconds.

**Step 1 — Compute time per crank degree at 2000 RPM:**

$$ N = 2000 \text{ RPM} = \frac{2000}{60} = 33.33 \text{ RPS} $$

For a 4-stroke engine, 1 crank revolution = 720° of crank angle for the full cycle. The engine completes 1 crank rev per 1/33.33 s = 0.03 s.

$$ \text{Time per crank rev} = \frac{60}{2000} = 0.03 \text{ s} = 30 \text{ ms} $$

$$ \text{Time per crank degree} = \frac{30}{360} = 0.0833 \text{ ms/°} $$

**Step 2 — Compute time before TDC for 20° advance:**

$$ t_{BTDC} = 20 \times 0.0833 = 1.667 \text{ ms} $$

**Step 3 — Compute total injection duration for 30° crank angle:**

$$ t_{inj} = 30 \times 0.0833 = 2.5 \text{ ms} $$

**Step 4 — Final summary:**

$$ \boxed{t_{BTDC} = 1.67 \text{ ms}, \quad t_{inj} = 2.5 \text{ ms} } $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** Modern common-rail injectors have injection durations of **0.5 – 2 ms** because of much higher injection pressure (2000 bar) giving faster atomization. Older jerk pumps had 2 – 4 ms durations.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Cooling System Selection

| Parameter | Air Cooling | Water Cooling |
|---|---|---|
| Specific Heat Capacity (kJ/kg·K) | 1.005 (air) | 3.4 (50/50 coolant) |
| Density (kg/m³) | 1.2 | 1070 |
| Heat Carrying Capacity | Low (≈ 1.2 kJ/m³·K) | High (≈ 3638 kJ/m³·K) |
| Heat Transfer Coefficient (W/m²·K) | 50 – 250 (forced) | 500 – 5000 (boiling) |
| System Weight (per kW) | ~1 kg | ~3 – 5 kg |
| Temperature Uniformity | Poor (hot spots possible) | Excellent |
| Maintenance | Minimal | High (coolant, pump) |
| Cold Start | OK in cold climates | Risk of freezing |
| Power-to-Weight | Higher (military use) | Lower (passenger cars) |
| Cost | Low | High |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**List any six important properties of a good lubricant.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **High Viscosity Index (VI)** — minimal change in viscosity with temperature.
2. **High Flash and Fire Point** — Flash point > 200°C to prevent crankcase fires.
3. **Low Pour Point** — flows easily in cold start conditions.
4. **Good Oxidation Stability** — resists sludge and varnish formation.
5. **Low Carbon Residue** — burns cleanly without leaving deposits.
6. **Neutral Behaviour** — neither acidic nor basic (low TAN and saponification number).
7. **High Film Strength** — forms a strong boundary film under high pressure.
8. **Anti-foam and anti-corrosion properties** — protects metal surfaces and prevents emulsification.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**Differentiate between battery ignition and magneto ignition systems (any 6 points).**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Battery Ignition | Magneto Ignition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uses an external 12 V battery as the source | Self-generated current from a permanent-magnet magneto |
| 2 | Requires battery maintenance (water topping) | No battery, hence no maintenance of cell |
| 3 | Strong spark even at cranking speed | Weak spark at low cranking speed; needs impulse coupling |
| 4 | Heavier due to battery | Lightweight, compact |
| 5 | Used in cars, LMVs, HMVs | Used in motorcycles, scooters, outboard motors, aircraft |
| 6 | Failure of battery disables the system | Truly self-contained and reliable |
| 7 | Initial cost is higher | Initial cost is lower |
| 8 | Battery adds to vehicle weight | No battery, lighter wiring |
