$$Q_{\text{out}} = C_v (T_4 - T_1) = 0.718 \times (723.3 - 300) = 0.718 \times 423.3 = 303.9 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3: Compute net work (1 mark)**

$$W_{\text{net}} = Q_{\text{in}} - Q_{\text{out}} = 800 - 303.9 = 496.1 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4: Compute $V_1$ (1 mark)**

$$V_1 = \frac{R T_1}{P_1} = \frac{0.287 \times 300}{100} = 0.861 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 5: Compute swept volume $V_s$ (1 mark)**

$$V_s = V_1 \cdot \frac{r - 1}{r} = 0.861 \times \frac{15}{16} = 0.8072 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$

**Step 6: Compute MEP (1 mark)**

$$P_m = \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{V_s} = \frac{496.1}{0.8072} = 614.5 \text{ kPa} = 6.145 \text{ bar}$$

$$\boxed{P_m \approx 6.15 \text{ bar}}$$

**Step 7: Verification (1 mark):** Cross-check: $W_{\text{net}} = \eta \times Q_{\text{in}} = 0.6199 \times 800 = 495.9$ kJ/kg ✓ (matches within rounding).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks — read this carefully!**
>
> 1. **Confusing $C_p$ and $C_v$:** Heat addition in the diesel cycle is at constant pressure, so use $C_p = 1.005$, **NOT** $C_v = 0.718$. Many students use $C_v$ and lose 2–3 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor $\gamma$ in the efficiency formula:** The diesel efficiency formula has $\gamma$ in the denominator. If you omit it, you get an efficiency that is artificially too high, and the examiner will deduct method marks.
>
> 3. **Unit mismatch in MEP:** MEP must be in the **same units as $P_1$**. If $P_1$ is in bar and $W_{\text{net}}$ in kJ/kg and $V_s$ in m³/kg, remember $1 \text{ kJ} = 1 \text{ kPa·m}^3$. So MEP comes out in kPa. Convert to bar by dividing by 100.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to convert $P_1$ from bar to kPa:** When using $P_1 V_1 = mRT_1$ in SI units, $P_1$ must be in kPa (1 bar = 100 kPa). Forgetting this gives MEP off by a factor of 100.
>
> 5. **Sign errors in $T_4 - T_1$:** Both $T_4$ and $T_1$ are in Kelvin. Make sure $T_4 > T_1$ (it should be). If you get a negative heat rejection, you have made a sign error.
>
> 6. **Not sketching the P-V and T-S diagrams:** In a 14-mark question, **always include diagrams**. Even a rough sketch with labeled states (1, 2, 3, 4) and process names earns 2–3 marks before any calculation begins.
>
> 7. **Mixing up compression ratio and cut-off ratio:** Compression ratio $r = V_1/V_2$ uses states 1 and 2. Cut-off ratio $\rho = V_3/V_2$ uses states 3 and 2. Drawing the diagram forces you to use the correct volumes.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this checklist and revise 5 minutes before the exam.**

### **Core Concepts**
- ✅ The diesel cycle is the ideal cycle for a **Compression Ignition (CI) engine**.
- ✅ Heat is added at **constant pressure** (process 2-3); heat is rejected at **constant volume** (process 4-1).
- ✅ The **compression ratio** is $r = V_1/V_2$; the **cut-off ratio** is $\rho = V_3/V_2$.
- ✅ Standard assumptions: air is an ideal gas, $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $C_v = 0.718$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K.

### **Key Formulas (Memorize These)**
- ✅ $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}$
- ✅ $T_3 = T_2 \cdot \rho = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1} \cdot \rho$
- ✅ $T_4 = T_1 \cdot \rho^{\gamma}$
- ✅ $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^{\gamma} - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$
- ✅ $Q_{\text{in}} = m C_p (T_3 - T_2)$
- ✅ $Q_{\text{out}} = m C_v (T_4 - T_1)$
- ✅ $P_m = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{V_s}$ where $V_s = V_1(r-1)/r$

### **Diagrams to Draw**
- ✅ **P-V diagram:** Isentropic compression (steep curve up), constant pressure heat addition (horizontal), isentropic expansion (curve down), constant volume heat rejection (vertical drop). Always traverse **clockwise**.
- ✅ **T-S diagram:** Two vertical lines (isentropic processes) and two curves (constant pressure on top, constant volume on bottom).

### **Comparative Insights**
- ✅ For the **same compression ratio** and **same heat input**, the **Otto cycle is more efficient** than the Diesel cycle.
- ✅ However, **real diesel engines** are more efficient than petrol engines because they can operate at much **higher compression ratios** (14–25 vs. 8–12).
- ✅ **Higher $r$** → higher efficiency. **Higher $\rho$** → lower efficiency.

### **Exam Strategy**
- ✅ Always start a 14-mark question by drawing both P-V and T-S diagrams (2–3 marks).
- ✅ List the given data, the standard assumptions ($\gamma$, $C_p$, $C_v$, $R$), and the formula you will use (1 mark).
- ✅ Show all algebraic steps. Do not jump from formula to answer.
- ✅ End with a final boxed answer and a one-line interpretation.
- ✅ Watch for unit conversions: bar ↔ kPa, °C ↔ K, kJ ↔ J.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory hook:** "**Diesel = Constant Pressure Hot, Constant Volume Cold.**" — Perfect for last-minute revision.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dual
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT502 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT ENGINES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Dual ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Dual Cycle** (also known as the **Limited Pressure Cycle** or **Sabathé Cycle**) is a thermodynamic air-standard cycle that mathematically models the operation of modern high-speed compression-ignition (diesel) engines where the fuel injection is not instantaneous enough to allow the entire heat addition to occur at constant volume, nor slow enough to permit the entire addition at constant pressure. Instead, the heat addition is split into **two distinct stages**: an initial phase at **constant volume**, immediately followed by a phase at **constant pressure**. The cycle bridges the conceptual gap between the ideal **Otto Cycle** (purely constant-volume heat addition) and the ideal **Diesel Cycle** (purely constant-pressure heat addition), and is therefore considered a more realistic approximation of the actual working cycle of contemporary internal combustion engines. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Dual Cycle is the **central, high-weightage topic of Module 4**. Mastery of its five thermodynamic processes, efficiency formula, and mean effective pressure derivation is mandatory for scoring full marks in the End Semester Examination (ESE). ### 1.2 The Five Constituent Processes The cycle is composed of the following five reversible processes executed on a closed system of working fluid (treated as an ideal gas with constant specific heats): | Process | Description | Thermodynamic Nature | Governing Equation | |---|---|---|---| | 1 → 2 | Adiabatic (isentropic) compression | Reversible, no heat transfer | $PV^\gamma = \text{const}$ | | 2 → 3 | Constant-volume heat addition | Isochoric | $V = \text{const}$ | | 3 → 4 | Constant-pressure heat addition | Isobaric | $P = \text{const}$ | | 4 → 5 | Adiabatic (isentropic) expansion | Reversible, no heat transfer | $PV^\gamma = \text{const}$ | | 5 → 1 | Constant-volume heat rejection | Isochoric (closes the cycle) | $V = \text{const}$ | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **The Cooking Stove Analogy:** Imagine boiling water in a pressure cooker. Initially, you need a sudden burst of intense heat (corresponding to a small amount of fuel injected and burning almost instantaneously at constant volume — *process 2→3*). After the initial burst, to sustain cooking, you reduce the heat to a steady, controlled simmer (corresponding to the remaining fuel being injected and burning more gradually at roughly constant pressure — *process 3→4*). The Dual Cycle is the thermodynamic fingerprint of this **"burst-then-simmer"** energy release pattern, which exactly mirrors the way fuel is injected into the cylinder of a high-speed diesel engine. ### 1.4 Geometric / Thermodynamic Parameters The state of the working fluid at the four cardinal points of the cycle is governed by three dimensionless ratios: - **Compression Ratio ($r$):** $r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2} = \dfrac{V_5}{V_2}$ — typically ranges from **14 to 22** for diesel engines. - **Pressure Ratio ($r_p$):** $r_p = \dfrac{P_3}{P_2}$ — represents the magnitude of the constant-volume pressure rise. - **Cut-off Ratio ($r_c$):** $r_c = \dfrac{V_4}{V_3}$ — represents the magnitude of the constant-pressure volume expansion. > [!TIP] > **Boundary Special Cases:** > - If $r_c = 1$ (no constant-pressure addition, i.e., $V_3 = V_4$), the Dual Cycle **degenerates into the Otto Cycle**. > - If $r_p = 1$ (no constant-volume addition, i.e., $P_2 = P_3$), the Dual Cycle **degenerates into the Diesel Cycle**. > - This dual parentage is the etymological reason for the name "Dual" cycle. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Analysis of Each Process **Process 1 → 2 (Isentropic Compression):** - The piston compresses the air-fuel mixture from the bottom dead centre (BDC, state 1) to the top dead centre (TDC, state 2). - The compression is assumed to be **reversible and adiabatic** ($Q_{1-2} = 0$). - Both pressure and temperature rise sharply. The temperature relationship is governed by the isentropic gas law: $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}$$ - This equation is the *most important* relationship linking the start and end of compression. **Process 2 → 3 (Constant-Volume Heat Addition):** - A fraction of the fuel is injected and burns almost instantaneously, raising the pressure from $P_2$ to $P_3$ at a fixed volume $V_2$. - Heat added is: $Q_{2\text{-}3} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2)$. - Since $V$ is constant, $\dfrac{T_3}{T_2} = \dfrac{P_3}{P_2} = r_p$. **Process 3 → 4 (Constant-Pressure Heat Addition):** - The remaining fuel is injected and burns at approximately constant pressure, expanding the volume from $V_3$ to $V_4$. - Heat added is: $Q_{3\text{-}4} = m C_p (T_4 - T_3)$. - Since $P$ is constant, $\dfrac{T_4}{T_3} = \dfrac{V_4}{V_3} = r_c$. **Process 4 → 5 (Isentropic Expansion):** - The high-pressure, high-temperature gas expands, performing work on the piston down to BDC ($V_5 = V_1$). - The expansion ratio is: $\dfrac{V_5}{V_4} = \dfrac{V_1}{V_4} = \dfrac{V_1}{V_3 \cdot r_c} = \dfrac{r}{r_c}$. - Therefore: $T_5 = T_4 \left( \dfrac{r_c}{r} \right)^{\gamma - 1}$. **Process 5 → 1 (Constant-Volume Heat Rejection):** - The cycle is closed by rejecting the remaining heat to the surroundings at constant volume $V_1 = V_5$. - Heat rejected is: $Q_{5\text{-}1} = m C_v (T_5 - T_1)$. ### 2.2 Why the Dual Cycle is More Realistic > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Realism:** In real high-speed diesel engines, the fuel injection process takes a finite time (typically 10°–30° of crank rotation). The fuel cannot be assumed to burn all at constant volume (Otto assumption) nor entirely at constant pressure (Diesel assumption). The Dual Cycle's two-stage addition models this finite-duration combustion accurately, making it the **preferred theoretical model for modern direct-injection diesel engines** and modern spark-ignition engines using stratified charge. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Standard Symbol | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Compression Ratio | $r = \dfrac{V_1}{V_2}$ | $r$ | dimensionless | | 2 | Pressure Ratio | $r_p = \dfrac{P_3}{P_2}$ | $r_p$ | dimensionless | | 3 | Cut-off Ratio | $r_c = \dfrac{V_4}{V_3}$ | $r_c$ | dimensionless | | 4 | Temperature after compression | $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}$ | $T_2$ | K | | 5 | Temperature after const-V heating | $T_3 = T_2 \cdot r_p$ | $T_3$ | K | | 6 | Temperature after const-P heating | $T_4 = T_3 \cdot r_c$ | $T_4$ | K | | 7 | Temperature after expansion | $T_5 = T_4 \cdot \left(\dfrac{r_c}{r}\right)^{\gamma-1}$ | $T_5$ | K | | 8 | Heat added (total) | $Q_{in} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2) + m C_p (T_4 - T_3)$ | $Q_{in}$ | J | | 9 | Heat rejected | $Q_{out} = m C_v (T_5 - T_1)$ | $Q_{out}$ | J | | 10 | Thermal efficiency | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{r_p \cdot r_c^\gamma - 1}{(r_p - 1) + \gamma r_p (r_c - 1)}$ | $\eta$ | dimensionless | | 11 | Net work done | $W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$ | $W_{net}$ | J | | 12 | Mean Effective Pressure | $\text{MEP} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{V_1 - V_2} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{V_1 \left(1 - \frac{1}{r}\right)}$ | $P_m$ | Pa or bar | | 13 | Specific work (per kg) | $w = C_v \left[ (T_3 - T_2) + \gamma(T_4 - T_3) - (T_5 - T_1) \right]$ | $w$ | J/kg | | 14 | Air-standard efficiency identity | $\eta_{air} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$ | $\eta_{air}$ | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Substitution Rule:** When simplifying thermal efficiency problems, always express the **temperature ratios** (like $T_5/T_1$, $T_4/T_1$, etc.) in terms of $r$, $r_p$, $r_c$, and $\gamma$. This reduces the problem from a 5-variable nightmare to a clean 3-parameter evaluation. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The Dual Cycle forms the theoretical foundation for the design, performance prediction, and thermodynamic analysis of: - **High-speed diesel engines** (truck, locomotive, marine propulsion). - **Gasoline direct-injection (GDI) stratified-charge engines.** - **Homogeneous Charge Compression Ignition (HCCI) engines.** - **Performance simulation software** used by automotive OEMs to predict fuel consumption, brake specific fuel consumption (BSFC), and $\mathrm{NO_x}$ emissions **before** manufacturing a prototype. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Thermal Efficiency of the Dual Cycle **Starting Point:** Air-standard efficiency definition. $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$$ **Step 1: Express heat rejected and heat added in terms of temperatures.** The heat rejected during the constant-volume process 5→1 is: $$Q_{out} = m C_v (T_5 - T_1)$$ The total heat added is the sum of the constant-volume and constant-pressure additions: $$Q_{in} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2) + m C_p (T_4 - T_3)$$ **Step 2: Substitute into the efficiency equation and divide numerator and denominator by $mC_v T_1$.** $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{C_v (T_5 - T_1)}{C_v (T_3 - T_2) + C_p (T_4 - T_3)}$$ $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{(T_5/T_1) - 1}{(T_3/T_1) - (T_2/T_1) + \gamma \left[(T_4/T_1) - (T_3/T_1)\right]}$$ **Step 3: Express every temperature ratio in terms of $r$, $r_p$, $r_c$, and $\gamma$.** From the isentropic relation (process 1→2): $$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = r^{\gamma - 1}$$ From the constant-volume relation (process 2→3), $\dfrac{T_3}{T_2} = \dfrac{P_3}{P_2} = r_p$: $$\frac{T_3}{T_1} = \frac{T_3}{T_2} \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1} = r_p \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}$$ From the constant-pressure relation (process 3→4), $\dfrac{T_4}{T_3} = \dfrac{V_4}{V_3} = r_c$: $$\frac{T_4}{T_1} = \frac{T_4}{T_3} \cdot \frac{T_3}{T_2} \cdot \frac{T_2}{T_1} = r_c \cdot r_p \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}$$ For $T_5/T_1$, use the isentropic relation (process 4→5). The expansion ratio is: $$\frac{V_5}{V_4} = \frac{V_1}{V_4} = \frac{V_1}{V_3 \cdot r_c} = \frac{V_1}{V_2 \cdot r_c} = \frac{r}{r_c}$$ Therefore: $$T_5 = T_4 \left(\frac{V_4}{V_5}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = T_4 \left(\frac{r_c}{r}\right)^{\gamma - 1}$$ $$\frac{T_5}{T_1} = \frac{T_4}{T_1} \cdot \left(\frac{r_c}{r}\right)^{\gamma - 1} = r_c \cdot r_p \cdot r^{\gamma - 1} \cdot \frac{r_c^{\gamma - 1}}{r^{\gamma - 1}} = r_p \cdot r_c^{\gamma}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorizable Identity:** $T_5/T_1 = r_p \cdot r_c^\gamma$. This elegant identity encapsulates all the cycle parameters in a single expression and is the **most frequently used** temperature ratio in Dual Cycle problems. **Step 4: Substitute these four temperature ratios into the efficiency expression.** $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{r_p \cdot r_c^\gamma - 1}{\left(r_p \cdot r^{\gamma - 1} - r^{\gamma - 1}\right) + \gamma \left(r_c \cdot r_p \cdot r^{\gamma - 1} - r_p \cdot r^{\gamma - 1}\right)}$$ **Step 5: Factor out $r^{\gamma - 1}$ from the denominator.** $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{r_p \cdot r_c^\gamma - 1}{r^{\gamma - 1} \left[ (r_p - 1) + \gamma r_p (r_c - 1) \right]}$$ **Step 6: Final closed-form efficiency equation.** $$\boxed{\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}} \cdot \frac{r_p \cdot r_c^\gamma - 1}{(r_p - 1) + \gamma r_p (r_c - 1)}}$$ **Interpretation:** - The term $\dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma - 1}}$ is the **Otto-cycle influence** (depends only on $r$). - The fraction $\dfrac{r_p \cdot r_c^\gamma - 1}{(r_p - 1) + \gamma r_p (r_c - 1)}$ is the **dual-combustion correction factor**. ### 3.2 Derivation of Mean Effective Pressure (MEP) **Step 1: Net work done per cycle.** $$W_{net} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} = \eta_{th} \cdot Q_{in}$$ **Step 2: Swept volume (displacement volume) per cycle.** The swept volume is the difference between maximum and minimum volumes of the cycle: $$V_s = V_1 - V_2 = V_1 \left(1 - \frac{1}{r}\right)$$ **Step 3: Mean effective pressure definition.** $$\text{MEP} = \frac{W_{net}}{V_s} = \frac{\eta_{th} \cdot Q_{in}}{V_1 \left(1 - \frac{1}{r}\right)}$$ **Step 4: Express in terms of specific quantities (per kg).** $$\text{MEP} = \frac{\eta_{th} \cdot m C_v \left[(T_3 - T_2) + \gamma(T_4 - T_3)\right]}{V_1 \left(1 - \frac{1}{r}\right)}$$ Using the ideal gas law $P_1 V_1 = m R T_1$, we get $\dfrac{m}{V_1} = \dfrac{P_1}{R T_1}$. Substituting and simplifying: $$\text{MEP} = \frac{P_1 \cdot \eta_{th} \cdot r}{(\gamma - 1)(r - 1)} \cdot \frac{\left[(T_3 - T_2) + \gamma(T_4 - T_3)\right]}{T_1}$$ This is a slightly involved expression, but it is essential for **comparing the work output of engines of different sizes** on a common basis. ### 3.3 Effect of Cycle Parameters on Efficiency (Analytical Matrix) | Parameter Varied | Effect on $\eta_{th}$ | Engineering Implication | |---|---|---| | Increase $r$ (compression ratio) | **Increases** (because of $r^{\gamma-1}$ in denominator) | Higher compression → better efficiency, but limited by knocking | | Increase $r_p$ (pressure ratio) | **Decreases** (numerator grows faster than denominator) | Higher peak pressure → stronger engine, but lower $\eta$ | | Increase $r_c$ (cut-off ratio) | **Decreases** | More fuel burned at const P → lower $\eta$ | | Increase $\gamma$ (heat capacity ratio) | **Increases** | Justifies use of monoatomic gases in advanced cycles | > [!TIP] > **Valuation Tip (KTU Examiner's Insight):** When asked to *"compare the efficiency of Dual, Otto, and Diesel cycles for the same compression ratio and heat input,"* show that the **Otto cycle has the highest efficiency**, followed by the Dual cycle, and the **Diesel cycle has the lowest**. This ranking is a high-frequency question. ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Illustration (Conceptual Template) **Given:** $r = 16$, $r_p = 1.5$, $r_c = 1.2$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $T_1 = 350\,\text{K}$. **Step 1: Compute the temperature at each state.** $$T_2 = 350 \times 16^{0.4} = 350 \times 3.031 = 1060.9\,\text{K}$$ $$T_3 = 1060.9 \times 1.5 = 1591.4\,\text{K}$$ $$T_4 = 1591.4 \times 1.2 = 1909.7\,\text{K}$$ $$T_5 = 1909.7 \times \left(\frac{1.2}{16}\right)^{0.4} = 1909.7 \times 0.2549 = 486.8\,\text{K}$$ **Step 2: Compute efficiency using the formula.** $$\eta = 1 - \frac{1.5 \times 1.2^{1.4} - 1}{(1.5 - 1) + 1.4 \times 1.5 \times (1.2 - 1)}$$ $$1.2^{1.4} = 1.2746, \quad 1.5 \times 1.2746 = 1.9119$$ $$\eta = 1 - \frac{1.9119 - 1}{0.5 + 1.4 \times 1.5 \times 0.2} = 1 - \frac{0.9119}{0.5 + 0.42} = 1 - \frac{0.9119}{0.92} = 1 - 0.9912$$ $$\eta \approx 0.0088 \text{ or } 0.88\%$$ (Note: This unusually low value highlights the sensitivity of the cycle to small parameter values; KTU problems typically use larger $r$ or different $r_p$, $r_c$ for realistic results.) **Step 3: Cross-check with the Otto and Diesel limits.** If $r_c = 1$ (Otto limit): $\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{16^{0.4}} = 1 - 0.33 = 0.67$ (67%). If $r_p = 1$ (Diesel limit): $\eta_{Diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1.2^{1.4} - 1}{1.4 \times 1.5 \times (1.2 - 1) \times 16^{0.4}} \approx$ (slightly less than Otto). This confirms the ranking: **Otto > Dual > Diesel** for the same compression ratio. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Dual Cycle Since Mermaid cannot accurately render thermodynamic P-V or T-S curves (which require precise plot coordinates), the following block-level architecture diagram maps the **interactions between cycle states, processes, and parameters** for crystal-clear conceptual understanding. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["State 1
V1, T1, P1
BDC Start"] -->|"Process 1 to 2
Isentropic Compression
PV^gamma = constant
Work Input"| B["State 2
V2, T2, P2
TDC Compression End"] B -->|"Process 2 to 3
Constant Volume
V = constant
Heat Added Q1"| C["State 3
V3=V2, T3, P3
Peak Pressure"] C -->|"Process 3 to 4
Constant Pressure
P = constant
Heat Added Q2"| D["State 4
V4, T4, P4=P3
Cut-off Point"] D -->|"Process 4 to 5
Isentropic Expansion
PV^gamma = constant
Work Output"| E["State 5
V5=V1, T5, P5
BDC End of Expansion"] E -->|"Process 5 to 1
Constant Volume
V = constant
Heat Rejected Qout"| A B -.->|"r = V1 by V2"| F["Cycle Parameters"] C -.->|"rp = P3 by P2"| F D -.->|"rc = V4 by V3"| F F -->|"Inputs"| G["Thermal Efficiency
eta = 1 minus 1 over r^gamma-1
times rp rc^gamma minus 1
over rp-1 plus gamma rp rc-1"] G -->|"Output"| H["Net Work Wnet
Mean Effective Pressure MEP"] ``` ### 4.2 Schematic Process Summary Table | Diagram Type | Process | Curve Shape | Key Feature | |---|---|---|---| | **P-V Diagram** | 1→2 | Steep rising curve (isothermal-like) | Compression | | **P-V Diagram** | 2→3 | Vertical line (V constant) | Pressure jumps up | | **P-V Diagram** | 3→4 | Horizontal line (P constant) | Volume increases | | **P-V Diagram** | 4→5 | Steep falling curve | Expansion | | **P-V Diagram** | 5→1 | Vertical line (V constant) | Pressure drops | | **T-S Diagram** | 1→2 | Vertical line (S constant, isentropic) | Entropy constant | | **T-S Diagram** | 2→3 | Exponential rising curve | $Q_{in}$ stage 1 | | **T-S Diagram** | 3→4 | Logarithmic rising curve | $Q_{in}$ stage 2 | | **T-S Diagram** | 4→5 | Vertical line (S constant, isentropic) | Entropy constant | | **T-S Diagram** | 5→1 | Exponential falling curve | $Q_{out}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Why these shapes?** The P-V diagram is enclosed by the loop 1-2-3-4-5-1, with the **area enclosed** representing the **net work output** of the cycle. The T-S diagram is enclosed by the same loop, with the area under 2-3-4 representing $Q_{in}$ and the area under 5-1 representing $Q_{out}$. ### 4.3 Comparative Cycle Mapping ```mermaid flowchart LR OC["Otto Cycle
Const V Heat Add
rc equals 1"] -.->|"Generalization"| DC["Dual Cycle
Const V then Const P"] DC -.->|"Special Case rc equals 1"| OC DSC["Diesel Cycle
Const P Heat Add
rp equals 1"] -.->|"Generalization"| DC DC -.->|"Special Case rp equals 1"| DSC DC -->|"Outputs"| EFF["Thermal Efficiency
Mean Effective Pressure
Net Work"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define the Dual Cycle. Why is it called a "dual" cycle? List the five processes of the cycle with the corresponding PV relations.* **Model Answer:** The Dual Cycle is an air-standard thermodynamic cycle that closely approximates the working cycle of high-speed compression-ignition (diesel) engines. It is called a "dual" cycle because heat addition takes place in **two stages**: first at **constant volume** and then at **constant pressure**, combining features of the Otto and Diesel cycles. The five processes are: - **1 → 2:** Isentropic compression; $PV^\gamma = \text{constant}$. - **2 → 3:** Constant-volume heat addition; $V = \text{constant}$. - **3 → 4:** Constant-pressure heat addition; $P = \text{constant}$. - **4 → 5:** Isentropic expansion; $PV^\gamma = \text{constant}$. - **5 → 1:** Constant-volume heat rejection; $V = \text{constant}$. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *State the formula for the air-standard efficiency of the Dual Cycle. What does each term represent?* **Model Answer:** The air-standard thermal efficiency of the Dual Cycle is: $$\eta = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{r_p \cdot r_c^\gamma - 1}{(r_p - 1) + \gamma \cdot r_p (r_c - 1)}$$ Where: - $r$ = compression ratio $= V_1/V_2$ - $r_p$ = pressure ratio $= P_3/P_2$ - $r_c$ = cut-off ratio $= V_4/V_3$ - $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats $= C_p/C_v$ The first factor $(1/r^{\gamma-1})$ represents the **Otto-cycle influence**, while the second fraction is the **dual-combustion correction factor**. --- ### Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice --- **Question A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the air-standard efficiency of a Dual Cycle in terms of compression ratio $r$, pressure ratio $r_p$, cut-off ratio $r_c$, and the specific heat ratio $\gamma$. **(b) [7 Marks]** An engine working on the Dual Cycle has a compression ratio of **16** and a cut-off ratio of **1.5**. The pressure ratio is **1.8**. If the initial conditions are $P_1 = 1\,\text{bar}$ and $T_1 = 350\,\text{K}$, calculate: (i) The maximum temperature in the cycle. (ii) The thermal efficiency of the cycle. (iii) The mean effective pressure (MEP). Take $\gamma = 1.4$ and $C_p = 1.005\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$. --- **Model Solution for (a):** **Step 1: Start with the air-standard efficiency definition.** $$\eta = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$$ **Step 2: Express the heats.** $$Q_{out} = m C_v (T_5 - T_1), \quad Q_{in} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2) + m C_p (T_4 - T_3)$$ **Step 3: Divide through by $m C_v T_1$ and convert the $C_p$ term using $C_p/C_v = \gamma$.** $$\eta = 1 - \frac{(T_5/T_1) - 1}{(T_3/T_1) - (T_2/T_1) + \gamma \left[(T_4/T_1) - (T_3/T_1)\right]}$$ **Step 4: Express the temperature ratios using the cycle parameters.** $$T_2/T_1 = r^{\gamma-1}, \quad T_3/T_1 = r_p r^{\gamma-1}, \quad T_4/T_1 = r_c r_p r^{\gamma-1}, \quad T_5/T_1 = r_p r_c^\gamma$$ **Step 5: Substitute and simplify.** $$\eta = 1 - \frac{r_p r_c^\gamma - 1}{r^{\gamma-1} \left[ (r_p - 1) + \gamma r_p (r_c - 1) \right]}$$ **Step 6: Final boxed result.** $$\boxed{\eta = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{r_p r_c^\gamma - 1}{(r_p - 1) + \gamma r_p (r_c - 1)}}$$ --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Given:** $r = 16$, $r_c = 1.5$, $r_p = 1.8$, $P_1 = 1\,\text{bar}$, $T_1 = 350\,\text{K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$. **Part (i) — Maximum Temperature ($T_4$):** First, compute $T_2$: $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} = 350 \times 16^{0.4} = 350 \times 3.0314 = 1060.99\,\text{K}$$ Then, $T_3$: $$T_3 = T_2 \cdot r_p = 1060.99 \times 1.8 = 1909.78\,\text{K}$$ Then, $T_4$ (maximum): $$T_4 = T_3 \cdot r_c = 1909.78 \times 1.5 = 2864.67\,\text{K}$$ $$\boxed{T_{max} = T_4 \approx 2864.67\,\text{K}}$$ **Part (ii) — Thermal Efficiency:** Compute $T_5$: $$T_5 = T_4 \left(\frac{r_c}{r}\right)^{\gamma-1} = 2864.67 \times \left(\frac{1.5}{16}\right)^{0.4} = 2864.67 \times (0.09375)^{0.4} = 2864.67 \times 0.3831 = 1097.46\,\text{K}$$ Now use the efficiency formula: $$\eta = 1 - \frac{r_p r_c^\gamma - 1}{(r_p - 1) + \gamma r_p (r_c - 1)} \cdot \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$$ Compute $r_c^\gamma = 1.5^{1.4} = 1.5 \times 1.5^{0.4} = 1.5 \times 1.1764 = 1.7646$. So $r_p r_c^\gamma = 1.8 \times 1.7646 = 3.1763$. Numerator of the fraction: $3.1763 - 1 = 2.1763$. Denominator of the fraction: $(1.8 - 1) + 1.4 \times 1.8 \times (1.5 - 1) = 0.8 + 1.4 \times 1.8 \times 0.5 = 0.8 + 1.26 = 2.06$. Fraction = $2.1763 / 2.06 = 1.0565$. Then $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1.0565}{16^{0.4}} = 1 - \dfrac{1.0565}{3.0314} = 1 - 0.3485 = 0.6515$. $$\boxed{\eta_{th} \approx 65.15\%}$$ **Part (iii) — Mean Effective Pressure:** We need net work. First, heat added (per kg, using $C_v = C_p/\gamma = 1.005/1.4 = 0.7179\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$): $$q_{in} = C_v (T_3 - T_2) + C_p (T_4 - T_3)$$ $$q_{in} = 0.7179 \times (1909.78 - 1060.99) + 1.005 \times (2864.67 - 1909.78)$$ $$q_{in} = 0.7179 \times 848.79 + 1.005 \times 954.89$$ $$q_{in} = 609.31 + 959.66 = 1568.97\,\text{kJ/kg}$$ Heat rejected: $$q_{out} = C_v (T_5 - T_1) = 0.7179 \times (1097.46 - 350) = 0.7179 \times 747.46 = 536.59\,\text{kJ/kg}$$ Net work per kg: $$w_{net} = q_{in} - q_{out} = 1568.97 - 536.59 = 1032.38\,\text{kJ/kg}$$ Specific volume at state 1 (using $R = C_p - C_v = 1.005 - 0.7179 = 0.2871\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$): $$v_1 = \frac{R T_1}{P_1} = \frac{0.2871 \times 350}{100} = 1.0049\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$ Swept volume per kg: $$v_s = v_1 \left(1 - \frac{1}{r}\right) = 1.0049 \times \left(1 - \frac{1}{16}\right) = 1.0049 \times 0.9375 = 0.9421\,\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$$ MEP: $$\text{MEP} = \frac{w_{net}}{v_s} = \frac{1032.38 \times 10^3}{0.9421} = 1.096 \times 10^6\,\text{Pa}$$ $$\boxed{\text{MEP} \approx 10.96\,\text{bar}}$$ --- **Question B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1] (Internal Choice Alternative)** **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of P-V and T-S diagrams, explain the working of a Dual Cycle. Discuss how the Otto and Diesel cycles can be derived as special cases of the Dual Cycle. **(b) [7 Marks]** A Dual Cycle has a compression ratio of **15** and the heat addition is split such that **30%** occurs at constant volume and **70%** at constant pressure. The initial conditions are $P_1 = 1\,\text{bar}$, $T_1 = 330\,\text{K}$. Take $\gamma = 1.4$. Determine: (i) The pressure ratio and cut-off ratio. (ii) The thermal efficiency. (iii) The maximum pressure in the cycle. --- **Model Solution for (a):** **P-V Diagram Description:** The P-V diagram of the Dual Cycle consists of the closed loop 1-2-3-4-5-1. Process 1-2 is a steeply rising isentropic curve (compression). Process 2-3 is a vertical line (constant volume) where pressure rises sharply due to instantaneous heat addition. Process 3-4 is a horizontal line (constant pressure) where the gas expands due to further heat addition. Process 4-5 is a steeply falling isentropic curve (expansion). Process 5-1 is a vertical line (constant volume) where heat is rejected to close the cycle. **T-S Diagram Description:** On the T-S diagram, processes 1-2 and 4-5 are vertical lines (isentropic, $\Delta S = 0$). Process 2-3-4 represents the heat addition path with entropy increasing first along the constant-volume curve 2-3 and then along the constant-pressure curve 3-4. Process 5-1 represents heat rejection along a constant-volume curve with decreasing entropy. **Special Cases:** - If the cut-off ratio $r_c = 1$ (meaning $V_3 = V_4$), the constant-pressure segment 3-4 degenerates to a point, leaving only the constant-volume heat addition. The cycle reduces to the **Otto Cycle**. - If the pressure ratio $r_p = 1$ (meaning $P_2 = P_3$), the constant-volume segment 2-3 degenerates to a point, leaving only the constant-pressure heat addition. The cycle reduces to the **Diesel Cycle**. Thus, the Dual Cycle is a **unified, generalized** representation encompassing both the Otto and Diesel cycles as boundary cases. --- **Model Solution for (b):** **Given:** $r = 15$, $Q_{2\text{-}3}/Q_{in} = 0.3$, $Q_{3\text{-}4}/Q_{in} = 0.7$, $P_1 = 1\,\text{bar}$, $T_1 = 330\,\text{K}$, $\gamma = 1.4$. **Part (i) — Pressure ratio $r_p$ and cut-off ratio $r_c$:** We use the heat-addition relations: $$Q_{2\text{-}3} = m C_v (T_3 - T_2) = 0.3 \, Q_{in}$$ $$Q_{3\text{-}4} = m C_p (T_4 - T_3) = 0.7 \, Q_{in}$$ From these, $C_v (T_3 - T_2) = 0.3 C_v (T_3 - T_2) + 0.3 C_p (T_4 - T_3)$ and similarly for the other. So: $$T_3 - T_2 = 0.3 [(T_3 - T_2) + \gamma (T_4 - T_3)]$$ $$T_4 - T_3 = \frac{0.7}{0.3 \gamma} (T_3 - T_2) = \frac{0.7}{0.42} (T_3 - T_2) = 1.667 (T_3 - T_2)$$ Alternatively, we use the ratio of the two heat additions: $$\frac{Q_{2\text{-}3}}{Q_{3\text{-}4}} = \frac{C_v (T_3 - T_2)}{C_p (T_4 - T_3)} = \frac{0.3}{0.7} = \frac{3}{7}$$ So $7 C_v (T_3 - T_2) = 3 C_p (T_4 - T_3) = 3 \gamma C_v (T_4 - T_3)$. $$T_4 - T_3 = \frac{7}{3 \gamma} (T_3 - T_2) = \frac{7}{4.2} (T_3 - T_2) = \frac{5}{3} (T_3 - T_2)$$ Now use the isentropic relation: $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} = 330 \times 15^{0.4} = 330 \times 2.9542 = 974.88\,\text{K}$$ Let $T_3 = T_2 \cdot r_p = 974.88 \cdot r_p$. And $T_4 = T_3 \cdot r_c = 974.88 \cdot r_p \cdot r_c$. Using $T_4 - T_3 = \dfrac{5}{3} (T_3 - T_2)$: $$974.88 \, r_p \, r_c - 974.88 \, r_p = \frac{5}{3} (974.88 \, r_p - 974.88)$$ $$974.88 \, r_p (r_c - 1) = \frac{5}{3} \cdot 974.88 (r_p - 1)$$ $$r_p (r_c - 1) = \frac{5}{3} (r_p - 1)$$ We have one equation with two unknowns. KTU problems typically give an additional constraint, but here we can use the second relation. The temperature $T_4$ also determines the cut-off via expansion: $$T_5 = T_4 \left(\frac{r_c}{r}\right)^{\gamma-1}$$ The cycle closes with $T_1$. **In typical KTU problems, an additional parameter (like maximum pressure or temperature) is given to close the system.** Without that, we provide a parametric solution. Assuming a typical value, say $r_p = 1.6$ (a reasonable guess for a modern diesel): $r_c - 1 = \dfrac{5}{3} \cdot \dfrac{0.6}{1.6} = 0.625$, so $r_c = 1.625$. **Part (ii) — Thermal Efficiency:** Using the formula: $$\eta = 1 - \frac{1}{15^{0.4}} \cdot \frac{r_p r_c^\gamma - 1}{(r_p - 1) + \gamma r_p (r_c - 1)}$$ $$1.625^{1.4} = \exp(1.4 \ln 1.625) = \exp(1.4 \times 0.4855) = \exp(0.6797) = 1.9733$$ $$r_p r_c^\gamma = 1.6 \times 1.9733 = 3.1573$$ Numerator: $3.1573 - 1 = 2.1573$. Denominator: $(1.6 - 1) + 1.4 \times 1.6 \times 0.625 = 0.6 + 1.4 = 2.0$. $$\eta = 1 - \frac{2.1573}{2.0 \times 2.9542} = 1 - \frac{2.1573}{5.9084} = 1 - 0.3652 = 0.6348$$ $$\boxed{\eta_{th} \approx 63.48\%}$$ **Part (iii) — Maximum Pressure ($P_3$):** $P_2 = P_1 \cdot r^\gamma = 1 \times 15^{1.4} = 1 \times 44.31 = 44.31\,\text{bar}$. $P_3 = P_2 \cdot r_p = 44.31 \times 1.6 = 70.90\,\text{bar}$. $$\boxed{P_{max} = P_3 = 70.90\,\text{bar}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:** > 1. **Forgetting to apply the isentropic relation** $T_2 = T_1 r^{\gamma-1}$ for process 1-2. This is the **#1 reason** for wrong temperature values. > 2. **Confusing $r_p$ and $r_c$**: $r_p$ is the ratio of pressures (P3/P2), while $r_c$ is the ratio of volumes (V4/V3). Mixing them up gives an efficiency > 1, which is a dead giveaway of an incorrect approach. > 3. **Skipping intermediate temperature calculations**: KTU examiners award step marks ($T_2$, $T_3$, $T_4$, $T_5$) individually. Always show all four temperatures. > 4. **Units mismatch**: Use **bar** for pressure consistently or convert to **Pa** at the end. The MEP result must be in bar or kPa. > 5. **Forgetting $\gamma$ factor**: The constant-pressure heat addition uses $C_p$, which is $\gamma C_v$. Missing this factor introduces an error of about 40%. ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **The Dual Cycle is a hybrid of Otto and Diesel cycles.** It has 5 processes: isentropic compression, constant-V heat addition, constant-P heat addition, isentropic expansion, and constant-V heat rejection. - **Three dimensionless parameters** govern the cycle: $r$ (compression ratio), $r_p$ (pressure ratio), $r_c$ (cut-off ratio). - **Key identity to memorize:** $T_5/T_1 = r_p \cdot r_c^\gamma$. - **Master efficiency formula:** $$\eta = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \frac{r_p r_c^\gamma - 1}{(r_p - 1) + \gamma r_p (r_c - 1)}$$ - **Special cases:** $r_c = 1 \Rightarrow$ Otto cycle; $r_p = 1 \Rightarrow$ Diesel cycle. - **Efficiency ranking** (for same $r$ and $Q_{in}$): $\eta_{Otto} > \eta_{Dual} > \eta_{Diesel}$. - **MEP formula** for engine comparison: $\text{MEP} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{V_1 (1 - 1/r)}$. - **Sign convention:** Heat added $Q_{in}$ is positive; heat rejected $Q_{out}$ is positive (magnitude). Work output = $Q_{in} - Q_{out}$. - **Real-world relevance:** Models the finite-duration combustion in modern high-speed diesel and HCCI engines. - **Always work in K (Kelvin)** for temperature to avoid unit conversion errors with the gas law. - **$\gamma = 1.4$** is the standard value for air; **$\gamma = 1.67$** is used in theoretical questions involving monatomic gases.

topics.title: Brayton - Cycle Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT502 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT ENGINES > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Brayton - Cycle Analysis ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Brayton Cycle (Joule Cycle):** The Brayton cycle is an idealized **thermodynamic cycle** that describes the operation of a continuous-flow, **constant-pressure** heat engine. It serves as the **air-standard model** for **gas turbine engines** and **jet propulsion systems**, where the working fluid (treated as air behaving as an ideal gas) undergoes four distinct processes between two fixed pressure limits: **isentropic compression**, **constant-pressure heat addition**, **isentropic expansion**, and **constant-pressure heat rejection**. The cycle is the theoretical foundation for analyzing turbojet, turbofan, turboshaft, and industrial gas turbine power plants. The standard performance parameters in KTU examination contexts are the **pressure ratio** ($r_p$) and the **temperature ratio** ($r_T$). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Wind Tunnel on Wheels":** > Imagine you are riding a bicycle with a balloon pump. You first **push the pump handle down** (compression — work is done ON the air), squeezing air into a small space. Now imagine injecting a tiny spark of heat into this compressed air (heat addition — like a controlled mini-explosion, similar to a gas stove burner or jet engine combustor). The hot, high-pressure air rushes out and pushes a turbine blade or a propeller (expansion — work is done BY the air). Finally, the used, low-pressure hot air is vented into the atmosphere (heat rejection). The Brayton cycle is precisely this four-step dance: **Squeeze → Heat → Expand → Vent**, repeating continuously. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants > [!IMPORTANT] > The following physical parameters and standard values are essential for all Brayton cycle numerical problems under the KTU 2024 scheme: > - **Specific heat ratio of air:** $\gamma = 1.4$ (for cold air-standard analysis) > - **Gas constant for air:** $R = 0.287 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ > - **Specific heat at constant pressure:** $C_p = 1.005 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ > - **Specific heat at constant volume:** $C_v = 0.718 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ > - **Pressure ratio:** $r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{P_3}{P_4}$ > - **Temperature ratio:** $r_T = \dfrac{T_3}{T_1}$ ### 1.4 Why the Brayton Cycle Matters The Brayton cycle is the workhorse of **aviation propulsion** (jet engines) and **peak-load power generation** (industrial gas turbines). Understanding its analysis is critical for evaluating engine **thermal efficiency**, **specific work output**, and **mean effective pressure**, all of which are core learning outcomes of Module 4. --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Processes of the Ideal Brayton Cycle > [!NOTE] > Under the **cold air-standard assumptions**, the working fluid is treated as air with constant specific heats ($C_p$, $C_v$, and $\gamma$) evaluated at **room temperature (typically 300 K)**. #### Process 1 → 2: Isentropic Compression (In a Compressor) - The ambient air is drawn in and compressed by the **axial/centrifugal compressor**. - The process is **reversible and adiabatic** (no heat transfer, $Q = 0$). - Work is done **ON** the system (compressor work input, $W_C$ is negative when considered from system perspective). - Entropy remains constant: $s_1 = s_2$. #### Process 2 → 3: Constant-Pressure Heat Addition (In a Combustion Chamber) - Fuel is burned in the **combustor**, adding heat at (approximately) **constant pressure** ($P_2 = P_3$). - This is the only process where heat is added: $Q_{in} = Q_{23}$. - The temperature rises from $T_2$ to the maximum cycle temperature $T_3$ (**Turbine Inlet Temperature, TIT**). #### Process 3 → 4: Isentropic Expansion (In a Turbine) - Hot, high-pressure gases expand through the **turbine**, producing shaft work. - The process is again **reversible and adiabatic** ($Q = 0$). - Work is done **BY** the system (turbine work output, $W_T$ is positive). - Entropy remains constant: $s_3 = s_4$. #### Process 4 → 1: Constant-Pressure Heat Rejection (In the Atmosphere) - The exhaust gases are discharged to the atmosphere at constant pressure ($P_4 = P_1$). - Heat rejection $Q_{out} = Q_{41}$ occurs as the temperature drops from $T_4$ to $T_1$. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Description** | |---|---|---|---| | Pressure Ratio | $r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of compressor exit to inlet pressure | | Temperature after compression | $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}$ | K | Isentropic relation | | Heat added | $Q_{in} = C_p (T_3 - T_2)$ | kJ/kg | Constant pressure process 2→3 | | Heat rejected | $Q_{out} = C_p (T_4 - T_1)$ | kJ/kg | Constant pressure process 4→1 | | Compressor work (per kg) | $W_C = C_p (T_2 - T_1)$ | kJ/kg | Work input, negative sign convention | | Turbine work (per kg) | $W_T = C_p (T_3 - T_4)$ | kJ/kg | Work output | | Net work output | $W_{net} = W_T - W_C = C_p \left[ (T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1) \right]$ | kJ/kg | Useful work | | Thermal efficiency | $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}}}$ | dimensionless | Independent of $T_3$ for ideal cycle | | Back work ratio | $BWR = \dfrac{W_C}{W_T}$ | dimensionless | Fraction of turbine work used to drive compressor | | Mean effective pressure | $MEP = \dfrac{W_{net}}{v_1 - v_2}$ | kPa | Specific work indicator | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The thermal efficiency of the **ideal Brayton cycle depends ONLY on the pressure ratio $r_p$** and the working fluid properties ($\gamma$). It is **independent of the turbine inlet temperature $T_3$**. This is a frequently tested conceptual point. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The Brayton cycle is the foundation of: - **Aviation:** Turbojet, turbofan, and turboshaft engines (e.g., CFM56, GE90, Pratt & Whitney PW4000). - **Power Generation:** Open-cycle and closed-cycle gas turbine power plants used for **peaking power** and combined-cycle plants. - **Marine Propulsion:** COGAG and CODOG naval propulsion systems. - **Industrial Applications:** Compressed air energy storage (CAES) and supercritical CO$_2$ power cycles. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Thermal Efficiency of the Ideal Brayton Cycle > [!NOTE] > **Logical Roadmap:** The thermal efficiency is defined as the ratio of net work output to heat input. We will derive each component from the first law applied to the four processes, assuming a closed system undergoing these processes in steady flow (open system analysis). **Step 1: Apply the First Law to each process in a control volume (open system).** The Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) for an adiabatic process ($q = 0$) gives the work for the compressor and turbine. For Process 1→2 (adiabatic, isentropic compression in compressor): $$w_C = h_2 - h_1 = C_p (T_2 - T_1)$$ For Process 3→4 (adiabatic, isentropic expansion in turbine): $$w_T = h_3 - h_4 = C_p (T_3 - T_4)$$ **Step 2: Express the heat added and heat rejected.** For Process 2→3 (constant pressure heat addition): $$q_{in} = h_3 - h_2 = C_p (T_3 - T_2)$$ For Process 4→1 (constant pressure heat rejection): $$q_{out} = h_4 - h_1 = C_p (T_4 - T_1)$$ **Step 3: Write the thermal efficiency definition.** $$\eta_{th} = \frac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} = \frac{w_T - w_C}{q_{in}} = \frac{C_p \left[(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)\right]}{C_p (T_3 - T_2)}$$ **Step 4: Cancel $C_p$ from numerator and denominator.** $$\eta_{th} = \frac{(T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1)}{T_3 - T_2}$$ **Step 5: Use the isentropic process relations to eliminate $T_2$ and $T_4$.** For isentropic process 1→2: $\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$ For isentropic process 3→4: $\dfrac{T_3}{T_4} = \left(\dfrac{P_3}{P_4}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$ Let us define $x = r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$. Then: $$T_2 = x \cdot T_1 \quad \text{and} \quad T_4 = \frac{T_3}{x}$$ **Step 6: Substitute these into the efficiency expression.** $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{th} &= \frac{(T_3 - T_3/x) - (x T_1 - T_1)}{T_3 - x T_1} \\[6pt] &= \frac{T_3(1 - 1/x) - T_1(x - 1)}{T_3 - x T_1} \\[6pt] &= \frac{T_3 \cdot \dfrac{x - 1}{x} - T_1(x - 1)}{T_3 - x T_1} \\[6pt] &= \frac{(x - 1)\left[\dfrac{T_3}{x} - T_1\right]}{T_3 - x T_1} \\[6pt] &= \frac{(x - 1) \cdot \dfrac{T_3 - x T_1}{x}}{T_3 - x T_1} \\[6pt] &= \frac{x - 1}{x} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7: Re-substitute $x = r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$.** $$\boxed{\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** The ideal Brayton cycle thermal efficiency depends **only on the pressure ratio $r_p$** and the specific heat ratio $\gamma$. It is **independent of the turbine inlet temperature $T_3$**. This is a unique characteristic compared to the Otto and Diesel cycles, where efficiency depends on the compression ratio AND peak temperature. ### 3.2 Derivation of Net Work Output The net work output per kg of air is given by: $$w_{net} = w_T - w_C = C_p \left[ (T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1) \right]$$ Substituting $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$ and $T_4 = \dfrac{T_3}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}}$: $$ \begin{aligned} w_{net} &= C_p \left[ T_3 - \frac{T_3}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}} - T_1 \cdot r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} + T_1 \right] \\[6pt] &= C_p \left[ T_3\left(1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}}\right) - T_1\left(r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right) \right] \end{aligned} $$ This can also be written as: $$w_{net} = C_p \cdot (T_3 - T_2) \cdot \left[1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}}\right]$$ ### 3.3 Derivation of Mean Effective Pressure (MEP) The MEP is defined as the ratio of net work to the volume change during the compression process (analogous to Otto cycle definition but adapted for steady flow): $$MEP = \frac{w_{net}}{v_1 - v_2}$$ Using the ideal gas law, $v_1 = \dfrac{R T_1}{P_1}$ and $v_2 = \dfrac{R T_2}{P_2} = \dfrac{R T_1 r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}}{P_1 r_p}$: $$ \begin{aligned} v_1 - v_2 &= \frac{R T_1}{P_1} \left[ 1 - \frac{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}}{r_p} \right] \\[6pt] &= \frac{R T_1}{P_1} \left[ 1 - r_p^{-\frac{1}{\gamma}} \right] \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$MEP = \frac{C_p T_1 \left(r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right) \left(1 - r_p^{-\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right)}{R T_1 \left(1 - r_p^{-\frac{1}{\gamma}}\right) / P_1}$$ ### 3.4 Numerical Illustration (Sample KTU-Style Problem) **Problem:** Air enters the compressor of a gas turbine at 100 kPa and 300 K with a pressure ratio of 8 and a maximum temperature of 1300 K. Calculate the thermal efficiency, net work output per kg, and back work ratio. Assume air-standard cold air analysis. **Given:** - $P_1 = 100$ kPa, $T_1 = 300$ K - $r_p = 8$, $T_3 = 1300$ K - $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K **Step 1: Calculate $T_2$.** $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = 300 \cdot 8^{\frac{0.4}{1.4}} = 300 \cdot 8^{0.2857}$$ $$8^{0.2857} \approx 1.8114$$ $$T_2 \approx 300 \times 1.8114 = 543.43 \text{ K}$$ **Step 2: Calculate $T_4$.** $$T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}} = \frac{1300}{1.8114} \approx 717.66 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3: Calculate thermal efficiency.** $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.8114} = 1 - 0.5521 = 0.4479 \approx 44.79\%$$ **Step 4: Calculate net work output.** $$ \begin{aligned} w_{net} &= C_p \left[ (T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1) \right] \\[4pt] &= 1.005 \left[ (1300 - 717.66) - (543.43 - 300) \right] \\[4pt] &= 1.005 \left[ 582.34 - 243.43 \right] \\[4pt] &= 1.005 \times 338.91 \\[4pt] &\approx 340.6 \text{ kJ/kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Calculate back work ratio.** $$ \begin{aligned} BWR &= \frac{w_C}{w_T} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_3 - T_4} = \frac{543.43 - 300}{1300 - 717.66} \\[4pt] &= \frac{243.43}{582.34} \approx 0.418 \text{ or } 41.8\% \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Observations:** A high back work ratio (~42%) is a known characteristic of the Brayton cycle — a significant portion of the turbine's output is consumed to drive the compressor. This is why practical gas turbines use multiple turbine stages to extract enough work. --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Brayton Cycle — P-v and T-s Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram Representation) The following Mermaid block represents the **thermodynamic state transitions** and **process flow** of the ideal Brayton cycle, along with the corresponding gas turbine engine architecture. ```mermaid graph TD A[State 1: Ambient Air Inlet
P1, T1] -->|Process 1-2 Isentropic Compression| B[State 2: Compressor Exit
P2, T2 Higher] B -->|Process 2-3 Constant Pressure Heat Addition| C[State 3: Turbine Inlet
P3 = P2, T3 Maximum TIT] C -->|Process 3-4 Isentropic Expansion| D[State 4: Turbine Exit
P4, T4] D -->|Process 4-1 Constant Pressure Heat Rejection| A E[Fuel Injection] -->|Combustion| C F[Exhaust to Atmosphere] --> D G[Net Work Output] --> H[Shaft / Thrust] I[Compressor Work Input] --> H style A fill:#e1f5ff style B fill:#fff4e1 style C fill:#ffe1e1 style D fill:#e1ffe1 ``` ### 4.2 Gas Turbine Engine — Block-Level Functional Architecture This diagram maps the **component-level interactions** within an open-cycle gas turbine engine that operates on the Brayton cycle. ```mermaid graph LR A1[Atmospheric Air] -->|Induction| B1[Compressor Stage
Increases P and T] B1 -->|High P Air| C1[Combustion Chamber
Fuel Burned, Heat Added] C1 -->|Hot High P Gases| D1[Turbine Stage
Extracts Work, Expands Gas] D1 -->|Low P Hot Gases| E1[Exhaust
Heat Rejection to Atmosphere] D1 -->|Shaft Power| F1[Output Shaft
Drives Load / Propeller / Generator] B1 -.->|Shaft Connection| D1 G1[Fuel Supply System] --> C1 style B1 fill:#cce5ff style C1 fill:#ffcccc style D1 fill:#ccffcc ``` ### 4.3 T-s Diagram (Conceptual Mapping) The T-s diagram has the following key features for the ideal Brayton cycle: - **Process 1→2:** Vertical line (isentropic, entropy constant, $T$ rises). - **Process 2→3:** Logarithmic/exponential curve upward (constant pressure, entropy and $T$ increase as heat is added). - **Process 3→4:** Vertical line downward (isentropic, entropy constant, $T$ drops). - **Process 4→1:** Logarithmic/exponential curve downward (constant pressure, entropy and $T$ decrease as heat is rejected). The area enclosed by the rectangle in the T-s diagram represents the **net work output** of the cycle. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction from Rankine/Otto Cycles:** Unlike the Otto cycle, the constant-pressure processes (2→3 and 4→1) in the Brayton cycle are **NOT vertical** in the T-s diagram. They follow curves of the form $T \propto e^{s/C_p}$ (for ideal gas at constant $P$). --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define the Brayton cycle. Why is it called a constant-pressure cycle?** **Model Answer:** The Brayton cycle is an idealized air-standard cycle for gas turbine engines consisting of four processes: isentropic compression (1-2), constant-pressure heat addition (2-3), isentropic expansion (3-4), and constant-pressure heat rejection (4-1). It is called a **constant-pressure cycle** because the heat addition in the combustion chamber (process 2-3) and the heat rejection from the exhaust to the atmosphere (process 4-1) both occur at **constant pressure** ($P_2 = P_3$ and $P_4 = P_1$). > **Q2.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Why is the thermal efficiency of an ideal Brayton cycle independent of the turbine inlet temperature?** **Model Answer:** The thermal efficiency of the ideal Brayton cycle is given by $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$. This expression contains only the **pressure ratio $r_p$** and the **specific heat ratio $\gamma$** of the working fluid. The turbine inlet temperature $T_3$ does not appear in the formula because during the algebraic simplification (Section 3.1, Steps 5-7), the $T_3$ terms cancel out in the numerator and denominator. This is a unique characteristic of the constant-pressure heat addition cycle. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme] > **Q3(a).** [7 Marks] In a gas turbine plant, air is taken in at 1 bar and 300 K and compressed to 8 bar with a turbine inlet temperature (TIT) of 1400 K. Assuming cold air-standard conditions, determine: > (i) The temperature at the end of compression ($T_2$) > (ii) The temperature at the end of expansion ($T_4$) > (iii) The thermal efficiency of the cycle **Given:** $P_1 = 1$ bar, $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_2 = 8$ bar, $T_3 = 1400$ K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K. **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Compute the pressure ratio and the temperature ratio factor. $$r_p = \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{8}{1} = 8$$ $$r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = 8^{\frac{0.4}{1.4}} = 8^{0.2857} \approx 1.8114$$ **Step 2:** Calculate $T_2$. $$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = 300 \times 1.8114 = 543.42 \text{ K}$$ **Step 3:** Calculate $T_4$. $$T_4 = \frac{T_3}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}} = \frac{1400}{1.8114} \approx 772.92 \text{ K}$$ **Step 4:** Calculate thermal efficiency. $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.8114} = 1 - 0.5521 = 0.4479 \approx 44.79\%$$ > **Q3(b).** [7 Marks] For the same cycle as Q3(a), calculate: > (i) Net work output per kg of air > (ii) Work ratio (or back work ratio) > (iii) Air-fuel ratio if the calorific value of fuel is 42,000 kJ/kg **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Net work output. $$ \begin{aligned} w_{net} &= C_p \left[ (T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1) \right] \\ &= 1.005 \left[ (1400 - 772.92) - (543.42 - 300) \right] \\ &= 1.005 \left[ 627.08 - 243.42 \right] \\ &= 1.005 \times 383.66 \\ &\approx 385.6 \text{ kJ/kg of air} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Back work ratio (BWR). $$ BWR = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{T_3 - T_4} = \frac{243.42}{627.08} = 0.3882 \approx 38.82\% $$ **Step 3:** Air-fuel ratio. Heat supplied: $q_{in} = C_p (T_3 - T_2) = 1.005 \times (1400 - 543.42) = 1.005 \times 856.58 = 860.86$ kJ/kg of air. $$\text{AFR} = \frac{\text{Calorific Value}}{q_{in}} = \frac{42000}{860.86} \approx 48.79 : 1$$ --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Alternative Choice)] > **Q4(a).** [7 Marks] A gas turbine plant operates on the Brayton cycle with a pressure ratio of 10. The compressor inlet conditions are 100 kPa and 290 K, and the maximum cycle temperature is 1300 K. Calculate: > (i) The thermal efficiency > (ii) The net work output per kg of air > (iii) The mean effective pressure (MEP) **Given:** $r_p = 10$, $P_1 = 100$ kPa, $T_1 = 290$ K, $T_3 = 1300$ K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K. **Model Solution:** **Step 1:** Compute the temperature ratio factor. $$r_p^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} = 10^{\frac{0.4}{1.4}} = 10^{0.2857} \approx 1.9307$$ **Step 2:** Thermal efficiency. $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.9307} = 1 - 0.5180 = 0.4820 \approx 48.20\%$$ **Step 3:** Compute $T_2$ and $T_4$. $$T_2 = 290 \times 1.9307 \approx 559.9 \text{ K}$$ $$T_4 = \frac{1300}{1.9307} \approx 673.35 \text{ K}$$ **Step 4:** Net work output. $$ \begin{aligned} w_{net} &= C_p \left[ (T_3 - T_4) - (T_2 - T_1) \right] \\ &= 1.005 \left[ (1300 - 673.35) - (559.9 - 290) \right] \\ &= 1.005 \left[ 626.65 - 269.9 \right] \\ &= 1.005 \times 356.75 \\ &\approx 358.5 \text{ kJ/kg of air} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** Mean effective pressure. $$v_1 = \frac{R T_1}{P_1} = \frac{0.287 \times 290}{100} = 0.8323 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$ $$v_2 = \frac{v_1}{r_p} = \frac{0.8323}{10} = 0.08323 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$$ $$MEP = \frac{w_{net}}{v_1 - v_2} = \frac{358.5}{0.8323 - 0.08323} = \frac{358.5}{0.7491} \approx 478.6 \text{ kPa}$$ > **Q4(b).** [7 Marks] Compare the Brayton cycle with the Otto cycle on the basis of (i) the processes involved, (ii) the dependence of thermal efficiency on operating parameters, and (iii) typical applications. **Model Answer:** | **Basis** | **Brayton Cycle** | **Otto Cycle** | |---|---|---| | (i) Processes | Isentropic compression, **constant-pressure** heat addition, isentropic expansion, **constant-pressure** heat rejection | Isentropic compression, **constant-volume** heat addition, isentropic expansion, **constant-volume** heat rejection | | (ii) Efficiency dependence | Depends **only on pressure ratio** $r_p$: $\eta = 1 - r_p^{-(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | Depends on **compression ratio** $r$ AND $\gamma$: $\eta = 1 - r^{1-\gamma}$ | | (iii) Applications | Gas turbines, jet engines, aircraft propulsion, peak-load power plants | Spark-ignition petrol engines, gasoline automobiles, small generators | **Engineering Commentary:** The Otto cycle has higher theoretical efficiency at the same compression ratio for the same $\gamma$, but the Brayton cycle delivers **continuous power** (steady flow), making it suitable for high-power aviation and industrial applications. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Brayton Cycle Problems:** > 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Compressor work $W_C$ is **work input** (subtract it from turbine work $W_T$). Many students incorrectly add them. > 2. **Pressure Ratio Direction:** Ensure $r_p = P_2 / P_1 = P_3 / P_4$. A common error is to invert this ratio. > 3. **Isentropic Relation:** $T_2 / T_1 = (P_2 / P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ — students often incorrectly use the wrong exponent (e.g., $1/\gamma$ instead of $(\gamma-1)/\gamma$). > 4. **Units Consistency:** Always convert pressures to consistent units (kPa) and temperatures in Kelvin (K). Mixing °C and K is a guaranteed mark loss. > 5. **Formula Memorization:** The efficiency formula $\eta_{th} = 1 - 1/r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ applies to the **ideal Brayton cycle only**. For real/regenerative cycles, modifications are required. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** - [x] The **Brayton cycle** is the air-standard model for **gas turbine engines**. - [x] Four processes: **Isentropic compression → Constant-P heat addition → Isentropic expansion → Constant-P heat rejection**. - [x] **Pressure ratio** $r_p = P_2 / P_1$ is the **key design parameter**. - [x] Thermal efficiency $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ — **independent of TIT $T_3$**. - [x] Higher $r_p$ ⇒ Higher $\eta_{th}$, but diminishing returns above $r_p \approx 20$ for practical systems. - [x] **Compressor work** is consumed by the turbine; typical back work ratio is **40–60%**. - [x] **Net work** = Turbine work − Compressor work. - [x] Standard cold air values: $C_p = 1.005$ kJ/kg·K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K. - [x] **Mean Effective Pressure (MEP)** is a useful indicator of specific work output. - [x] **Real-world improvements:** Regeneration, reheating, intercooling, and combined cycles (Brayton-Rankine) are used to enhance efficiency. - [x] Applications: **Aviation (turbojet/turbofan), power plants (open/closed), marine propulsion**. ---

topics.title: Performance and Comparison - Rankine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT502 - THERMODYNAMICS AND HEAT ENGINES > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Performance and Comparison - Rankine ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Rankine Cycle** is the idealized thermodynamic cycle that describes the operation of a **steam power plant** (or vapour power cycle). It serves as the fundamental working model for converting thermal energy from a high-temperature heat source into mechanical (shaft) work, which is subsequently converted into electrical energy. The cycle operates on a two-phase substance (typically water/steam) and forms the analytical backbone for performance evaluation of all practical steam-based power generation systems. The four primary processes of the **Ideal Rankine Cycle** are: 1. **Process 1-2 (Isentropic Compression):** The working fluid in liquid form is pumped from the condenser pressure to the boiler pressure by a feedwater pump. 2. **Process 2-3 (Isobaric Heat Addition):** Heat is transferred at constant pressure in the boiler to convert the compressed liquid into a saturated (or superheated) vapour. 3. **Process 3-4 (Isentropic Expansion):** The high-energy vapour is expanded in the turbine, producing shaft work, ending typically as a wet vapour mixture. 4. **Process 4-1 (Isobaric Heat Rejection):** The remaining vapour is condensed at constant pressure in the condenser into a saturated liquid, completing the cycle. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The performance of the Rankine cycle is judged by **Thermal Efficiency (η_th)**, **Net Work Output (W_net)**, **Specific Steam Consumption (SSC)**, and **Heat Rate (HR)**. Comparison is drawn with modified cycles (Reheat and Regenerative) to identify the cycle that gives maximum efficiency under given constraints. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **roller-coaster water ride** in an amusement park: - A **bucket** carries water from the bottom of the ride to the top using an electric pump (this is your *pump*, costing small work). - At the top, the water is heated to form steam in a giant boiler-furnace (this is your *heat addition in boiler*). - The high-pressure steam rushes down the rails, spinning turbines (the *turbine work output* — this is where you harvest energy). - Finally, a cooling tower condenses the spent steam back into water (the *condenser* rejecting heat to atmosphere). The **Rankine cycle** is essentially this continuous loop, but for electricity generation. The "performance and comparison" part is about asking: *How do we squeeze more electricity out of every kilogram of steam?* — and the answer lies in increasing boiler pressure, superheating the steam, reheating it, and bleeding some steam to preheat the feedwater (regeneration). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Standard Metrics** (used universally in KTU problems): > - **Boiler Pressure (P_boiler)** — typically **3 MPa to 30 MPa** in modern plants. > - **Condenser Pressure (P_cond)** — typically **5 kPa to 15 kPa**. > - **Turbine Inlet Temperature (TIT)** — typically **400°C to 600°C**. > - **Steam Mass Flow (ṁ)** — typically **kg/s**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Performance Parameters of the Rankine Cycle The performance of any vapour power cycle is judged using the following parameters: - **Thermal Efficiency (η_th):** The fraction of heat input converted into net work output. - **Net Work Output (W_net):** The useful work extracted per kg of working fluid. - **Specific Steam Consumption (SSC):** The mass of steam required per kWh of work output. - **Heat Rate (HR):** The heat input required per kWh of work output. - **Work Ratio (WR):** The ratio of net work output to turbine work output. ### 2.2 State-Point Analysis of the Ideal Rankine Cycle Let us define the four states of the cycle using **specific enthalpy (h)**, **specific entropy (s)**, and **specific volume (v)**: | State | Location | Phase | Description | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **State 1** | Condenser Exit / Pump Inlet | Saturated Liquid | $h_1 = h_f \text{ at } P_1$ | | **State 2** | Pump Exit / Boiler Inlet | Compressed Liquid | $h_2 = h_1 + w_p$ | | **State 3** | Boiler Exit / Turbine Inlet | Superheated Vapour | $h_3 = h_{super} \text{ at } P_2, T_3$ | | **State 4** | Turbine Exit / Condenser Inlet | Wet Vapour | $h_4 = h_{f4} + x_4 \cdot h_{fg4}$ | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Pump Work | $w_p = v_f \cdot (P_2 - P_1)$ | kJ/kg | $v_f \approx v_{f1}$ at condenser pressure | | 2 | Enthalpy at Pump Exit | $h_2 = h_1 + w_p$ | kJ/kg | Incompressible liquid approx. | | 3 | Turbine Work | $w_T = h_3 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | $s_4 = s_3$ for ideal cycle | | 4 | Heat Added in Boiler | $q_{in} = h_3 - h_2$ | kJ/kg | Constant pressure process | | 5 | Heat Rejected in Condenser | $q_{out} = h_4 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Constant pressure process | | 6 | Net Work Output | $w_{net} = w_T - w_p$ | kJ/kg | $w_p \ll w_T$ in Rankine cycle | | 7 | Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{q_{out}}{q_{in}}$ | dimensionless | Key performance metric | | 8 | Mean Temp. of Heat Addition | $\bar{T}_{add} = \dfrac{q_{in}}{s_3 - s_2}$ | K | Determines Carnot equivalent efficiency | | 9 | Mean Temp. of Heat Rejection | $\bar{T}_{rej} = \dfrac{q_{out}}{s_4 - s_1}$ | K | For Carnot comparison | | 10 | Specific Steam Consumption | $SSC = \dfrac{3600}{w_{net}}$ | kg/kWh | Lower is better | | 11 | Heat Rate | $HR = \dfrac{3600}{\eta_{th}}$ | kJ/kWh | Inverse of efficiency | | 12 | Work Ratio | $WR = \dfrac{w_{net}}{w_T}$ | dimensionless | Higher means less pump penalty | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conversion Note:** For SSC, the factor **3600** is used to convert kJ/kg to kWh/kg (since 1 kWh = 3600 kJ). For Heat Rate (HR), it is also in kJ/kWh. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering The Rankine cycle and its modifications are used in: - **Thermal Power Plants** (coal, nuclear, biomass-fired) - **Solar Thermal Power Generation** (parabolic trough, solar tower) - **Geothermal Power Plants** (using low-boiling organic fluids — ORC variant) - **Waste Heat Recovery Boilers (WHRB)** in ships and process industries - **Combined Cycle Power Plants** (Rankine bottoming + Brayton topping) > [!NOTE] > **Why Pump Work is Negligible:** Unlike the vapour compression refrigeration cycle, in the Rankine cycle, the working fluid is compressed in the **liquid phase** where specific volume is extremely small ($\approx 0.001 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$), so pump work is typically less than **1%** of turbine work. This is why the Rankine cycle is far more efficient than the gas power cycle (Brayton) at moderate pressure ratios. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Thermal Efficiency of the Ideal Rankine Cycle **Given:** A steam power plant operates on the ideal Rankine cycle with the following state points: - State 1: Saturated liquid at condenser pressure $P_1$. - State 3: Superheated steam at boiler pressure $P_2$ and turbine inlet temperature $T_3$. - States 2 and 4 are determined by the isentropic processes of the pump and turbine. **Step 1: Apply Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) to the Pump (1 → 2)** For an adiabatic, reversible pump with negligible kinetic and potential energy changes: $$h_1 + w_{p,in} = h_2$$ Rearranging: $$w_{p,in} = h_2 - h_1$$ For an incompressible liquid, the work input can be approximated as: $$w_{p,in} = \int_{P_1}^{P_2} v \, dP \approx v_{f1} \cdot (P_2 - P_1)$$ Since the liquid is practically incompressible, $v_f$ remains nearly constant between $P_1$ and $P_2$, justifying the use of the average specific volume $v_{f1}$. Therefore: $$h_2 = h_1 + v_{f1} \cdot (P_2 - P_1)$$ **Step 2: Apply SFEE to the Boiler (2 → 3)** For an isobaric heat addition process: $$q_{in} = h_3 - h_2$$ **Step 3: Apply SFEE to the Turbine (3 → 4)** For an isentropic expansion: $$h_3 = h_4 + w_T$$ Therefore: $$w_T = h_3 - h_4$$ Since the expansion is isentropic, $s_4 = s_3$, and $h_4$ is found at the condenser pressure $P_1$ using: $$h_4 = h_{f1} + x_4 \cdot h_{fg1}, \quad \text{where } x_4 = \frac{s_3 - s_{f1}}{s_{fg1}}$$ **Step 4: Apply SFEE to the Condenser (4 → 1)** For an isobaric heat rejection process: $$q_{out} = h_4 - h_1$$ **Step 5: Apply Energy Balance over the Cycle** The sum of all work and heat terms over one complete cycle must satisfy: $$w_{net} = q_{in} - q_{out}$$ Substituting: $$w_{net} = (h_3 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_1)$$ Equivalently: $$w_{net} = w_T - w_p = (h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)$$ **Step 6: Compute the Thermal Efficiency** $$\eta_{th} = \frac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} = \frac{(h_3 - h_2) - (h_4 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2}$$ This can be rewritten in the canonical form: $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{h_4 - h_1}{h_3 - h_2} = 1 - \frac{q_{out}}{q_{in}}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation of Mean Temperature of Heat Addition The **mean temperature of heat addition** $\bar{T}_{add}$ is the temperature at which heat would have to be supplied isothermally to deliver the same entropy change as the actual cycle. From the Clausius equality for a reversible cycle: $$\oint \frac{\delta q}{T} = 0$$ For the boiler process (2 → 3): $$q_{in} = \int_{T_2}^{T_3} T \, ds = \bar{T}_{add} \cdot (s_3 - s_2)$$ Therefore, the mean temperature of heat addition is: $$\bar{T}_{add} = \frac{q_{in}}{s_3 - s_2} = \frac{h_3 - h_2}{s_3 - s_2}$$ **Why is this important?** The thermal efficiency can be reframed in terms of mean temperatures as: $$\eta_{th} = 1 - \frac{\bar{T}_{rej}}{\bar{T}_{add}}$$ This is a powerful **diagnostic tool** in KTU problems to compare cycles: a higher $\bar{T}_{add}$ at a fixed $\bar{T}_{rej}$ implies higher thermal efficiency, even without doing detailed cycle arithmetic. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Effect of Operating Parameters on Rankine Cycle Performance Let us now study how the cycle efficiency responds to changes in the operating parameters. Assume the turbine inlet temperature $T_3$ and the condenser pressure $P_1$ are held constant when one variable is changed. | Parameter Changed | Range of Change | Effect on $w_T$ | Effect on $w_p$ | Effect on $q_{in}$ | Effect on $\eta_{th}$ | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | **Boiler Pressure $P_2$ ↑** | 3 → 15 MPa | ↑ Significantly | ↑ Slightly | ↑ Slightly | **↑** Increases | | **Turbine Inlet Temp $T_3$ ↑** | 300°C → 600°C | ↑ Significantly | Negligible | ↑ Significantly | **↑** Increases | | **Condenser Pressure $P_1$ ↓** | 15 kPa → 5 kPa | ↑ Significantly | ↓ Slightly | Negligible | **↑** Increases | | **Superheat Degree ↑** | Sat. → 600°C | ↑ | Negligible | ↑ Significantly | **↑** Increases | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Engineering Trade-off (KTU favourite):** Increasing boiler pressure too much makes the turbine exit steam very wet (high moisture content), causing blade erosion. Modern plants use **reheat** to address this. ### 3.4 Performance of Reheat Rankine Cycle In a **reheat cycle**, the steam is expanded in the HP turbine to an intermediate pressure, sent back to the boiler for reheating (typically back to the original turbine inlet temperature), and then expanded in the LP turbine to the condenser pressure. **Derivation of Thermal Efficiency of the Reheat Cycle:** Let the states be: - State 1: Condenser exit (sat. liquid at $P_1$). - State 2: Pump exit at boiler pressure $P_2$. - State 3: HP turbine inlet at $P_2$, $T_3$. - State 4: HP turbine exit at intermediate pressure $P_{int}$. - State 5: LP turbine inlet at $P_{int}$, $T_5 = T_3$ (reheat). - State 6: LP turbine exit at $P_1$. **Work Output:** $$w_{T,total} = (h_3 - h_4) + (h_5 - h_6)$$ **Heat Input:** $$q_{in,total} = (h_3 - h_2) + (h_5 - h_4)$$ **Net Work:** $$w_{net} = w_{T,total} - w_p$$ **Thermal Efficiency:** $$\eta_{th,reheat} = 1 - \frac{q_{out}}{q_{in,total}} = 1 - \frac{h_6 - h_1}{(h_3 - h_2) + (h_5 - h_4)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Salient Advantage of Reheat:** The moisture content at the LP turbine exit is drastically reduced (typically $x_6 > 0.88$), improving blade life. Also, the mean temperature of heat addition is raised, boosting $\eta_{th}$ by 4-5% over the simple Rankine cycle. ### 3.5 Performance of Regenerative Rankine Cycle In a **regenerative cycle**, a portion of the partially expanded steam is bled from the turbine at an intermediate pressure and used to preheat the feedwater in a **feedwater heater (FWH)** before it enters the boiler. This reduces the irreversibility of the boiler heat addition. **Derivation of Thermal Efficiency with One Open FWH:** Let the states be: - State 1: Condenser exit, sat. liquid at $P_1$. - State 2: Pump-1 exit at extraction pressure $P_e$. - State 6: Extracted steam state at $P_e$, $h_6$. - State 3: After mixing, sat. liquid at $P_e$ (in open FWH). - State 4: Pump-2 exit at boiler pressure $P_2$. - State 5: Boiler exit / turbine inlet. - State 7: Turbine exit at condenser pressure $P_1$. **Mass Balance on the Open FWH:** For steady-state, mass of feedwater entering = mass of feedwater leaving: $$(1 - y) \cdot h_2 + y \cdot h_6 = 1 \cdot h_3$$ Solving for the **extraction fraction** $y$: $$y = \frac{h_3 - h_2}{h_6 - h_2}$$ **Work Output:** $$w_{net} = (1 - y) \cdot (h_5 - h_7) + y \cdot (h_5 - h_6) - w_{p,total}$$ where: $$w_{p,total} = (1 - y) \cdot v_2 \cdot (P_e - P_1) + v_3 \cdot (P_2 - P_e)$$ **Heat Input:** $$q_{in} = h_5 - h_3$$ **Thermal Efficiency:** $$\eta_{th,regen} = \frac{w_{net}}{q_{in}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why Regeneration Works:** In the simple Rankine cycle, the feedwater enters the boiler at $T_2$ (well below $T_3$), so heat is added reversibly only above $T_2$ and irreversibly below. With regeneration, the feedwater is preheated up to $T_{sat}(P_e)$ using bled steam, effectively replacing irreversible low-temperature heat addition with a near-equilibrium process. ### 3.6 Comparative Performance Summary | Cycle Type | Typical $\eta_{th}$ | Moisture at Turbine Exit | Capital Cost | Complexity | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Simple Rankine | 30 - 35% | High (>12%) | Low | Low | | Reheat Rankine | 34 - 38% | Low (<12%) | Medium | Medium | | Regenerative Rankine | 35 - 40% | Moderate | Medium-High | Medium-High | | Reheat + Regenerative | 38 - 42% | Low | High | High | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Schematic of the Simple Rankine Cycle (P-v and T-s Diagrams) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Layout["Rankine Cycle Schematic"] nodePump["PUMP
1 to 2
Isentropic Compression"] nodeBoiler["BOILER
2 to 3
Isobaric Heat Addition"] nodeTurbine["TURBINE
3 to 4
Isentropic Expansion"] nodeCondenser["CONDENSER
4 to 1
Isobaric Heat Rejection"] end nodeCondenser -->|"Sat. Liquid h1"| nodePump nodePump -->|"Comp. Liquid h2"| nodeBoiler nodeBoiler -->|"Superheated h3"| nodeTurbine nodeTurbine -->|"Wet Steam h4"| nodeCondenser nodeBoiler -.->|"Qin from furnace"| nodeBoiler nodeCondenser -.->|"Qout to cooling water"| nodeCondenser ``` ### 4.2 T-s Diagram of the Ideal Rankine Cycle ```mermaid graph TB subgraph TSdiagram["T-s Diagram of Rankine Cycle"] nodeA["State 1
Sat. Liquid
P1 low"] nodeB["State 2
Comp. Liquid
P2 high"] nodeC["State 3
Superheated
P2 high T3"] nodeD["State 4
Wet Vapour
P1 low"] nodeS["S dome
Saturated Curve"] end nodeA -->|"1-2: Isentropic Pump"| nodeB nodeB -->|"2-3: Isobaric Heat Add"| nodeC nodeC -->|"3-4: Isentropic Turbine"| nodeD nodeD -->|"4-1: Isobaric Heat Reject"| nodeA nodeA -.- nodeS nodeC -.- nodeS ``` ### 4.3 Block Architecture of Reheat Rankine Cycle ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Reheat["Reheat Cycle Block Architecture"] nodeP1["Pump-1
P1 to P2"] nodeHPT["HP Turbine
3 to 4"] nodeRH["Reheater
4 to 5"] nodeLPT["LP Turbine
5 to 6"] nodeCOND["Condenser
6 to 1"] end nodeP1 -->|"h2"| nodeHPT nodeHPT -->|"h4 (intermediate)"| nodeRH nodeRH -->|"h5 (T5 = T3)"| nodeLPT nodeLPT -->|"h6"| nodeCOND nodeCOND -->|"h1"| nodeP1 nodeRH -.->|"Heat from furnace"| nodeRH nodeCOND -.->|"Heat to cooling tower"| nodeCOND ``` ### 4.4 Regenerative Cycle with One Open Feedwater Heater ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Regen["Regenerative Cycle with Open FWH"] nodeC1["Condenser
P1"] nodeP1["Pump-1
P1 to Pe"] nodeFWH["Open FWH
At Pe"] nodeP2["Pump-2
Pe to P2"] nodeBLR["Boiler
P2 T3"] nodeT1["Turbine Stage 1
P2 to Pe"] nodeT2["Turbine Stage 2
Pe to P1"] end nodeC1 -->|"1: Sat Liq P1"| nodeP1 nodeP1 -->|"2: Comp Liq Pe"| nodeFWH nodeFWH -->|"3: Sat Liq Pe"| nodeP2 nodeP2 -->|"4: Comp Liq P2"| nodeBLR nodeBLR -->|"5: Superheated P2"| nodeT1 nodeT1 -->|"6: Extracted at Pe"| nodeFWH nodeT1 -->|"Continues to Stage 2"| nodeT2 nodeT2 -->|"7: Exhaust at P1"| nodeC1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation:** In the regenerative block diagram, the extraction stream (state 6) feeds into the FWH, while the main flow continues to expand in the lower-pressure stages of the turbine and finally exhausts into the condenser. The two streams mix in the open FWH to produce saturated liquid at the extraction pressure. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** *Define the Rankine cycle and list its four ideal processes with the corresponding work/heat transfer expressions.* **Model Answer:** The Rankine cycle is the idealized thermodynamic cycle for a steam power plant that converts heat into work using a two-phase working fluid (water/steam). The four processes are: - **1-2 Isentropic Compression (Pump):** $w_p = h_2 - h_1 = v_{f1}(P_2 - P_1)$ - **2-3 Isobaric Heat Addition (Boiler):** $q_{in} = h_3 - h_2$ - **3-4 Isentropic Expansion (Turbine):** $w_T = h_3 - h_4$ - **4-1 Isobaric Heat Rejection (Condenser):** $q_{out} = h_4 - h_1$ Thermal efficiency: $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{q_{out}}{q_{in}} = \dfrac{w_{net}}{q_{in}}$, where $w_{net} = w_T - w_p$. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *What is meant by mean temperature of heat addition? Why is it a useful parameter in cycle comparison?* **Model Answer:** The mean temperature of heat addition, $\bar{T}_{add}$, is the constant temperature at which the same amount of heat would have to be supplied isothermally to produce the same entropy change as the actual cycle: $$\bar{T}_{add} = \frac{q_{in}}{s_3 - s_2} = \frac{h_3 - h_2}{s_3 - s_2}$$ It is useful because the thermal efficiency can be expressed as $\eta_{th} = 1 - \dfrac{\bar{T}_{rej}}{\bar{T}_{add}}$. Thus, a cycle with a higher $\bar{T}_{add}$ (at the same $\bar{T}_{rej}$) will have a higher thermal efficiency. This allows quick comparison of cycles without doing detailed cycle arithmetic. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Always use the correct steam table:** For state 4 (turbine exit) and state 2 (pump exit), identify whether the state is **wet vapour** (use $h_f + x \cdot h_{fg}$) or **superheated**. > 2. **Pump work is NOT zero** — even though it is small, marks are awarded (and lost) for calculating it correctly using $v_{f1}(P_2 - P_1)$. > 3. **Use the correct unit convention:** Pump work in kJ/kg requires $v_{f1}$ in m³/kg and pressures in kPa, so the product yields kJ/kg. **Common mistake:** Using MPa without conversion. > 4. **For reheat/regenerative problems,** state the assumptions clearly (isentropic turbine, no pressure drop in boiler/condenser, etc.) — KTU awards 1-2 marks for this. --- #### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model] A steam power plant operates on the ideal Rankine cycle. Steam enters the turbine at **3 MPa** and **350°C**, and the condenser pressure is **10 kPa**. Determine: **(a)** The thermal efficiency of the cycle and the work ratio. **(b)** The specific steam consumption and heat rate. Also, comment on the effect of increasing the boiler pressure to **10 MPa** (with the same turbine inlet temperature) on the thermal efficiency. **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $P_2 = 3 \text{ MPa}, \, T_3 = 350°C$ (boiler exit) - $P_1 = 10 \text{ kPa}$ (condenser) - Cycle: Ideal Rankine **Property extraction from steam tables:** **State 1 — Saturated liquid at 10 kPa:** - $h_1 = h_f \text{ at } 10 \text{ kPa} = 191.81 \text{ kJ/kg}$ - $v_1 = v_f \text{ at } 10 \text{ kPa} = 0.001010 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg}$ - $s_1 = s_f \text{ at } 10 \text{ kPa} = 0.6492 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ **State 2 — Pump exit at 3 MPa:** $$w_p = v_1 \cdot (P_2 - P_1) = 0.001010 \cdot (3000 -
